Consumer buying behavior varies based on **involvement level** and **perceived brand differentiation**. Henry Assael proposed four types:

| Type | Involvement | Differentiation | Example | Marketer Strategy *(1.5 marks each)* |
|------|-------------|-----------------|---------|-------------------------------------|
| **Complex** | High | High | Buying a car, house | Detailed brochures, expert salespeople, comparison websites |
| **Dissonance-Reducing** | High | Low | Buying a refrigerator, mattress | Post-purchase reassurance, warranty cards, follow-up service |
| **Habitual** | Low | Low | Buying salt, milk, sugar | Mass advertising, attractive packaging, shelf placement at eye-level |
| **Variety-Seeking** | Low | High | Buying snacks, ice cream | Frequent flavor launches, promotional pricing, multi-pack offers |

*(Any 4 types with example and marketer strategy = 7 marks)*

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Set 2 (Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Question]**

**(a)** What is **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)**? Calculate the CLV for a beauty brand where the average order value is ₹2,000, purchase frequency is 6 times per year, customer lifespan is 4 years, and profit margin is 25%. The customer acquisition cost is ₹3,000. Comment on the ratio. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Explain the **four psychological factors** that influence consumer behavior with suitable examples. *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution (a) — 7 Marks**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) is the **total net profit** a company expects to earn from a customer over the entire duration of their relationship. It is a forward-looking metric that helps businesses decide how much to spend on customer acquisition and retention.

**Given Data:**
$$AOV = ₹2,000,\quad F = 6/year,\quad L = 4\ years,\quad M = 0.25,\quad CAC = ₹3,000$$

**Step 1 — Calculate CLV using simplified formula:**
$$CLV = AOV \times F \times L \times M$$
$$CLV = 2,000 \times 6 \times 4 \times 0.25$$

Stepwise multiplication:

$$\begin{aligned}
(i)\ & 2,000 \times 6 = 12,000 \\
(ii)\ & 12,000 \times 4 = 48,000 \\
(iii)\ & 48,000 \times 0.25 = 12,000
\end{aligned}$$

$$\boxed{CLV = ₹12,000}$$

**Step 2 — Compute CLV : CAC Ratio:**
$$Ratio = \frac{12,000}{3,000} = 4 : 1$$

**Step 3 — Strategic Comment (2 marks):**
A CLV : CAC ratio of **4 : 1** falls within the **industry-healthy range of 3 : 1 to 5 : 1**. This indicates:
- The marketing spend is **efficient** — not over-investing or under-investing.
- The brand has a **sustainable acquisition model**.
- It can marginally increase ad spend to capture more market share without losing profitability.

---

**Model Solution (b) — 7 Marks**

The four psychological factors that influence consumer behavior are:

**1. Motivation (1.75 marks):**
Motivation is the internal drive that pushes a consumer to take action to satisfy a need. *Abraham Maslow's Hierarchy of Needs* arranges human needs in a pyramid: physiological, safety, social, esteem, and self-actualization. Marketers identify which level their product appeals to and craft messaging accordingly.
*Example:* *Maggi* advertisements target *physiological* (hunger) and *social* (mom's love) needs simultaneously.

**2. Perception (1.75 marks):**
Perception is the process by which a consumer selects, organizes, and interprets information. Two consumers exposed to the same ad may perceive it entirely differently due to *selective attention, distortion, and retention*.
*Example:* A premium coffee brand may be perceived as "luxurious" by urban youth but "overpriced" by budget-conscious consumers.

**3. Learning (1.75 marks):**
Learning describes changes in consumer behavior arising from experience. Consumers learn through *trial, repetition, and conditioning*.
*Example:* A child who associates Cadbury Dairy Milk with festive joy (through repeated festive ads) grows up to prefer it for celebratory gifting.

**4. Beliefs and Attitudes (1.75 marks):**
Beliefs are descriptive thoughts a consumer holds about something; attitudes are consistent evaluations, feelings, and tendencies. Both are difficult to change and heavily influence brand preference.
*Example:* The belief that "Apple products are premium" creates a positive attitude driving iPhone purchases despite higher prices.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Areas in Consumer Marketing Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing B2C with B2B** — Many students fail to clearly differentiate consumer marketing from industrial marketing in definition questions. Always include the phrase *"for personal/household use"* in your B2C definition.
> 2. **Skipping stages in the 5-stage model** — Writing only 3 or 4 stages instead of all 5 is the most common error in 7-mark questions. Use a numbered list, not paragraphs.
> 3. **Forgetting interpretation in CLV numericals** — A CLV answer without the *strategic comment* on the CLV : CAC ratio loses 1.5 marks.
> 4. **No real-world examples** — KTU examiners reward students who connect theory to **Indian brands** (HUL, Nestlé, Amul, Flipkart, Zomato). Generic textbook examples fetch fewer marks.
> 5. **Mixing up Maslow levels** — Self-actualization is the *highest* (not lowest) level. Getting this wrong costs 1 mark in definitions.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **B2C vs B2B:** Consumer marketing serves individuals buying for personal use; business marketing serves organizations.
- **4Cs Framework (Modern View):** Customer Solution, Customer Cost, Convenience, Communication — replaces the 4Ps in a consumer-centric paradigm.
- **5-Stage Decision Process:** Need Recognition → Information Search → Evaluation → Purchase → Post-Purchase. **Memorize all 5 in order.**
- **Buyer Behavior Stimulus-Response Model:** Marketing Stimuli + Other Stimuli → Buyer's Black Box → Buyer Response.
- **Four Buying Behavior Types:** Complex, Dissonance-Reducing, Habitual, Variety-Seeking — based on *involvement* and *differentiation* axes.
- **Four Influencing Factor Categories:** Cultural, Social, Personal, Psychological (memorize the order — cultural is strongest).
- **Maslow's Hierarchy:** Physiological → Safety → Social → Esteem → Self-Actualization (bottom-up).
- **CLV Formula (Simplified):** $CLV = AOV \times F \times L \times M$
- **Healthy CLV : CAC Ratio:** Between **3 : 1 and 5 : 1**.
- **Key Indian Brand Examples:** HUL (FMCG), Zomato (digital consumer), Maruti (complex buying), Tata Cliq (omnichannel).
- **Exam Tip:** Every theoretical answer must include **at least one real-world brand example** for full marks.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Services Marketing
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Services Marketing

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Service** is an intangible offering that provides value to customers without resulting in ownership of any physical commodity. A service is an act, performance, or process that is essentially intangible and does not result in the transfer of ownership of any physical product. Services are economic activities offered by one party to another, typically using skills, expertise, and knowledge to deliver an experience, performance, or solution that satisfies a customer need.

**Services Marketing** is the branch of marketing that focuses on the marketing of services rather than physical products. It involves understanding, creating, communicating, and delivering customer value through intangible offerings. Services marketing applies the traditional marketing principles but extends them with additional elements specifically tailored to address the unique characteristics of services such as intangibility, inseparability, variability, and perishability.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Standard Reference):**
> "A service is any act or performance that one party offers to another that is essentially intangible and does not result in the ownership of anything. Its production may or may not be tied to a physical product." — KTU Marketing Management Module 2 Reference Framework

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are hungry and have two options:

**Option A — Buying a Product:** You walk into a supermarket, pick up a packet of biscuits, pay at the counter, take it home, and consume it. The product is physical, you own it, you can see it, touch it, store it for months, and resell it.

**Option B — Buying a Service:** You walk into a restaurant, order a meal, the chef prepares it, the waiter serves you, you consume it at the table, pay the bill, and leave. There is no physical product you can take home. The experience is consumed the moment it is produced.

**The Restaurant is a Service. The Biscuit Packet is a Product.**

Now imagine the chef uses different ingredients each time, the waiter is in a bad mood one day, the table is dirty, and the food is cold. As a customer, your experience varies every time. This is exactly what services marketing has to deal with: **inconsistency, intangibility, and the moment-of-truth experience**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Core Intuition for KTU Students:**
> In goods marketing, you sell a **thing**. In services marketing, you sell a **promise, an experience, and a performance**. This single shift changes the entire marketing strategy, mix, and measurement system.

## 1.3 The Three Service Domains

| Domain | Description | Example |
|--------|-------------|---------|
| **Pure Services** | Entirely intangible, no physical component | Consulting, education, healthcare |
| **Major Services with Minor Goods** | Primarily service, supported by a small physical element | Air travel (the seat and meal support the service) |
| **Major Goods with Minor Services** | Primarily a product, supported by ancillary services | Car purchase (with warranty, maintenance, financing) |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Point:** Pure services like banking, insurance, and education form the core of services marketing studies. Most KTU questions test your understanding of how marketing principles differ when applied to pure or major services.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Core Characteristics of Services (IIVP)

Services differ from physical products because of four inherent characteristics. KTU examiners frequently test these characteristics and their marketing implications.

### 2.1.1 Intangibility
- Services **cannot be seen, tasted, felt, heard, or smelled** before purchase.
- A customer cannot "test-drive" a haircut or a legal consultation before buying.
- **Marketing Implication:** Marketers must use tangible cues such as branding, physical evidence, testimonials, and imagery to make the intangible feel real.

### 2.1.2 Inseparability
- Services are **produced and consumed simultaneously**.
- The service provider (e.g., a doctor, teacher, or barber) and the customer must usually be present at the same time.
- **Marketing Implication:** The service provider becomes a part of the service itself. Managing employee training and customer interaction is critical.

### 2.1.3 Variability (Heterogeneity)
- Service quality **varies from provider to provider, customer to customer, and time to time**.
- Two haircuts at the same salon, by the same stylist, can be very different.
- **Marketing Implication:** Standardization through training, scripting, automation, and quality control is essential.

### 2.1.4 Perishability
- Services **cannot be stored, inventoried, or resold**.
- An empty airline seat, an unsold hotel room, or an unused appointment slot is lost revenue forever.
- **Marketing Implication:** Demand forecasting, capacity management, and dynamic pricing become critical marketing strategies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The IIVP Mnemonic:** **I**ntangibility, **I**nseparability, **V**ariability, **P**erishability. This is the single most tested concept in KTU Module 2 services marketing.

## 2.2 The 7 Ps of Services Marketing Mix

The traditional 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) are insufficient for services. Three additional Ps were introduced by **Booms and Bitner (1981)** to handle the unique service characteristics.

| Element | Description | Service Example |
|---------|-------------|-----------------|
| **Product** | The core service offering, augmented with supporting services | A bank offering savings account + ATM access + mobile banking |
| **Price** | Customer cost, fees, pricing strategies, value-based pricing | Hourly consultation fee, subscription pricing |
| **Place** | Service delivery location, distribution channels, digital reach | Branches, mobile app, online portal |
| **Promotion** | Advertising, sales promotion, PR, personal selling, digital marketing | Service ads, social media campaigns |
| **People** | All human actors involved in service delivery — employees, customers | Trained staff, well-mannered front-desk executives |
| **Process** | The procedure, mechanism, flow of activities by which the service is delivered | Step-by-step customer onboarding, queue management |
| **Physical Evidence** | Tangible cues that signal service quality — building, equipment, brochures | Clean clinic, modern equipment, branded uniforms |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** The 3 additional Ps — People, Process, and Physical Evidence — are introduced specifically because services are intangible, variable, and inseparable. Each additional P directly counters one of the IIVP challenges.

## 2.3 The GAPS Model of Service Quality (Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry — 1985)

The GAPS Model is a diagnostic framework that identifies **five gaps** that cause service delivery failures. KTU frequently asks students to draw or explain this model.

| Gap | Name | Description |
|-----|------|-------------|
| **Gap 1** | Knowledge Gap | Difference between **customer expectations** and **management perception** of those expectations |
| **Gap 2** | Standards Gap | Difference between **management perception** and **service quality specifications** set by the firm |
| **Gap 3** | Delivery Gap | Difference between **service specifications** and **actual service delivery** by employees |
| **Gap 4** | Communication Gap | Difference between **actual service delivery** and **what is communicated externally** to customers |
| **Gap 5** | Service Gap (Total) | Difference between **expected service** and **perceived service** (the customer-experienced gap) |

**Mathematical Expression of the GAPS Model:**

$$\text{Gap 5} = f(\text{Gap 1}, \text{Gap 2}, \text{Gap 3}, \text{Gap 4})$$

$$\text{Gap 5} = \text{Expected Service} - \text{Perceived Service}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Closing Gap 5 is the ultimate goal of services marketing.** Gaps 1 to 4 are organizational-internal gaps that the firm can control. Gap 5 is the customer-facing result.

## 2.4 SERVQUAL Model (Service Quality Framework)

SERVQUAL is a **22-item instrument** that measures service quality across **5 dimensions** by comparing customer expectations with perceptions.

| Dimension | Meaning | Example |
|-----------|---------|---------|
| **Tangibles** | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance | Clean hospital, modern diagnostic equipment |
| **Reliability** | Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately | On-time delivery of banking service |
| **Responsiveness** | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service | Quick customer support response |
| **Assurance** | Knowledge and courtesy of employees, ability to inspire trust and confidence | Trained doctor explaining diagnosis clearly |
| **Empathy** | Caring, individualized attention provided to customers | Doctor remembering a patient's history |

**SERVQUAL Quality Score Formula:**

$$\text{SQ} = \sum_{i=1}^{22} (P_i - E_i)$$

Where:
- $SQ$ = Service Quality score
- $P_i$ = Customer perception of service on item $i$
- $E_i$ = Customer expectation of service on item $i$
- $i$ ranges from $1$ to $22$ (the 22 measurement items)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** If $SQ = 0$, expectations are met. If $SQ < 0$, expectations exceed perceptions (poor service). If $SQ > 0$, perceptions exceed expectations (excellent service). KTU may give a numerical problem based on this.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of the Services Marketing Framework

- **Hospitality Industry (Hotels, Tourism):** Uses People, Process, and Physical Evidence to create memorable experiences.
- **Banking and Financial Services:** Uses SERVQUAL to measure and improve customer satisfaction.
- **Healthcare:** Uses GAPS Model to diagnose why patient satisfaction falls short.
- **Information Technology Services:** Uses standardized Processes and People training to reduce Variability.
- **Education:** Uses Physical Evidence (campus, labs) and People (faculty) to overcome Intangibility.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis of the IIVP Characteristics

### Step 1: Understanding Intangibility
A customer cannot physically inspect a service before purchase. To overcome this, the firm creates tangible surrogates:

$$\text{Customer Confidence} = f(\text{Brand Reputation}, \text{Physical Evidence}, \text{Testimonials})$$

**Explanation in Words:**
Since the service itself cannot be seen, the customer relies on surrogate indicators. A well-known brand name, a clean and well-designed office, and visible customer reviews all serve as tangible proxies that reduce the perceived risk of buying an intangible offering.

### Step 2: Understanding Inseparability
Because the service is produced and consumed at the same moment, the customer becomes a co-producer:

$$\text{Service Outcome} = f(\text{Provider Skill}, \text{Customer Participation}, \text{Interaction Quality})$$

**Explanation in Words:**
In a classroom, the teacher's effectiveness depends not only on the teacher's preparation but also on the student's attentiveness and engagement. The output cannot be separated from the interaction. This is why services marketing places enormous emphasis on training front-line employees who directly interact with the customer.

### Step 3: Understanding Variability
Since each service encounter is unique, firms must design systems to reduce variation:

$$\text{Service Consistency} = g(\text{Training Intensity}, \text{Process Standardization}, \text{Quality Audits})$$

**Explanation in Words:**
A McDonald's burger in Kochi should taste the same as a McDonald's burger in Tokyo. This is achieved by rigorous training of staff, strict process standardization (recipe books, automated systems), and periodic quality audits. The variability challenge is what makes services marketing far more complex than goods marketing.

### Step 4: Understanding Perishability
Unsold service capacity is permanently lost. Revenue management solves this:

$$\text{Revenue Recovered} = \int_{0}^{T} [P(t) \cdot D(t)] \, dt$$

Where:
- $P(t)$ = Price charged at time $t$
- $D(t)$ = Demand realized at time $t$
- $T$ = Total selling window

**Explanation in Words:**
Airlines dynamically lower prices as the departure date approaches to fill empty seats, because an unsold seat at takeoff is revenue that can never be recovered. Hotels, restaurants, and salons all use this perishability-driven logic to maximize capacity utilization.

## 3.2 Exhaustive Step-by-Step Analysis of the GAPS Model

The relationship between the gaps can be expressed as:

$$\text{Gap 5} = \text{Gap 1} + \text{Gap 2} + \text{Gap 3} + \text{Gap 4}$$

Where each gap is defined below with its root cause and closure strategy.

### Step A: Identifying Gap 1
$$\text{Gap 1} = \text{Customer Expectation} - \text{Management Perception of Expectation}$$

**Root Cause:** Lack of market research, upward communication filtering, too many management layers.

**Closure Strategy:** Conduct regular customer surveys, install customer feedback systems, implement management-by-walking-around (MBWA).

### Step B: Identifying Gap 2
$$\text{Gap 2} = \text{Management Perception} - \text{Service Quality Specification}$$

**Root Cause:** Inadequate commitment to service quality, vague goal-setting, lack of resources.

**Closure Strategy:** Set SMART service quality goals, define clear service standards, allocate budget for quality improvement.

### Step C: Identifying Gap 3
$$\text{Gap 3} = \text{Service Specification} - \text{Actual Service Delivery}$$

**Root Cause:** Role ambiguity, role conflict, poor employee training, poor technology support.

**Closure Strategy:** Provide clear standard operating procedures, conduct skill development programs, invest in service-enabling technology.

### Step D: Identifying Gap 4
$$\text{Gap 4} = \text{Actual Service Delivery} - \text{External Communication}$$

**Root Cause:** Tendency to over-promise in advertising, lack of coordination between marketing and operations.

**Closure Strategy:** Ensure marketing communications reflect actual service capacity; integrate marketing and operations teams.

### Step E: The Customer-Facing Gap 5
$$\text{Gap 5} = \text{Expected Service} - \text{Perceived Service}$$

**Root Cause:** Cumulative effect of Gaps 1 through 4.

**Closure Strategy:** Systematic closure of all upstream gaps.

## 3.3 Exhaustive SERVQUAL Calculation (Worked Example)

**Problem Setup (KTU Typical Question Style):**
A restaurant uses SERVQUAL to measure service quality across 5 dimensions. On a scale of 1 to 7, the average customer expectation ($E_i$) and perception ($P_i$) for each dimension are as follows:

| Dimension | Expectation ($E_i$) | Perception ($P_i$) |
|-----------|--------------------|--------------------|
| Tangibles | 6 | 5 |
| Reliability | 7 | 4 |
| Responsiveness | 6 | 6 |
| Assurance | 6 | 7 |
| Empathy | 5 | 4 |

**Step 1: Calculate the difference $P_i - E_i$ for each dimension.**

$$\text{Tangibles: } P - E = 5 - 6 = -1$$

$$\text{Reliability: } P - E = 4 - 7 = -3$$

$$\text{Responsiveness: } P - E = 6 - 6 = 0$$

$$\text{Assurance: } P - E = 7 - 6 = +1$$

$$\text{Empathy: } P - E = 4 - 5 = -1$$

**Step 2: Calculate the overall Service Quality Score.**

$$SQ = (-1) + (-3) + (0) + (+1) + (-1) = -4$$

**Step 3: Interpretation.**

Since $SQ < 0$, customer expectations exceed perceptions overall. The restaurant is under-performing. **Reliability** is the worst dimension (gap of -3) and must be the top priority for improvement.

**Step 4: Strategic Recommendation.**

The restaurant should focus first on **Reliability** (most negative gap), then on **Tangibles** and **Empathy**, while maintaining **Assurance** (currently exceeding expectations) and **Responsiveness** (meeting expectations).

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Goods vs. Services Marketing

| Dimension | Goods Marketing | Services Marketing |
|-----------|---------------|-------------------|
| **Nature of Offering** | Tangible, physical | Intangible, experiential |
| **Inventory** | Can be stored | Cannot be stored (perishable) |
| **Customer Involvement** | Low at production, high at consumption | High at both production and consumption |
| **Quality Control** | Easy to standardize | Difficult, varies with provider |
| **Marketing Mix** | 4 Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) | 7 Ps (adds People, Process, Physical Evidence) |
| **Evaluation Before Purchase** | Possible (test, inspect) | Difficult (must rely on cues) |
| **Distribution** | Through intermediaries, warehouses | Direct provider-to-customer or digital delivery |
| **Pricing Flexibility** | Fixed price tags, discounts | Dynamic, customized, value-based |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The 7 Ps Services Marketing Mix Framework

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Services Marketing Mix - 7 Ps Framework] --> B1[Product]
    A --> B2[Price]
    A --> B3[Place]
    A --> B4[Promotion]
    A --> C1[People]
    A --> C2[Process]
    A --> C3[Physical Evidence]

    B1 --> D1[Core Service plus Augmented Offering]
    B2 --> D2[Value Based plus Dynamic Pricing]
    B3 --> D3[Direct plus Digital Channels]
    B4 --> D4[Advertising plus PR plus Digital]
    C1 --> E1[Trained Employees plus Co Creating Customer]
    C2 --> E2[Standard SOPs plus Workflow Design]
    C3 --> E3[Ambience plus Equipment plus Branding]
```

## 4.2 The GAPS Model of Service Quality

```mermaid
graph LR
    cust1[Customer Expectations] -->|Gap 1 Knowledge Gap| mgmt[Management Perception]
    mgmt -->|Gap 2 Standards Gap| spec[Service Quality Specifications]
    spec -->|Gap 3 Delivery Gap| del[Actual Service Delivery]
    del -->|Gap 4 Communication Gap| comm[External Communications]
    cust1 -->|Gap 5 Service Gap Total| cust2[Customer Perceived Service]
    comm --> cust2
```

## 4.3 The SERVQUAL Service Quality Triangle

```mermaid
graph TD
    S[SERVQUAL Service Quality Model] --> T1[Tangibles]
    S --> T2[Reliability]
    S --> T3[Responsiveness]
    S --> T4[Assurance]
    S --> T5[Empathy]

    T1 --> U1[Physical Facilities plus Equipment plus Appearance]
    T2 --> U2[Dependable plus Accurate Service Performance]
    T3 --> U3[Prompt Service plus Willingness to Help]
    T4 --> U4[Knowledge plus Trust plus Confidence]
    T5 --> U5[Caring plus Individualized Customer Attention]
```

## 4.4 Service Marketing Process Flow (Block-Level Functional Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TD
    P1[Stage 1 Identify Customer Need] --> P2[Stage 2 Design Service Offering]
    P2 --> P3[Stage 3 Set Price and Channels]
    P3 --> P4[Stage 4 Promote Service Promise]
    P4 --> P5[Stage 5 Deliver Service with People]
    P5 --> P6[Stage 6 Capture Customer Feedback]
    P6 --> P7[Stage 7 Measure SERVQUAL and Close Gaps]
    P7 -->|Loop Back| P1
```

## 4.5 IIVP Characteristics vs Marketing Solutions Map

```mermaid
graph TD
    ch1[Intangibility] --> sol1[Solution: Physical Evidence plus Branding]
    ch2[Inseparability] --> sol2[Solution: People Training plus Customer Co Creation]
    ch3[Variability] --> sol3[Solution: Process Standardization plus SOPs]
    ch4[Perishability] --> sol4[Solution: Dynamic Pricing plus Capacity Management]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define services and explain any two characteristics of services. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A service is an intangible act or performance offered by one party to another that does not result in the transfer of ownership of any physical product. Services are consumed at the point of production and are characterized by four inherent features collectively known as IIVP.

**Characteristic 1: Intangibility**
Services cannot be seen, touched, tasted, or stored before purchase. For example, a patient cannot pre-experience a surgery. Marketers address this through physical evidence, branding, and testimonials.

**Characteristic 2: Perishability**
Services cannot be inventoried. An unsold airline seat at takeoff is permanently lost revenue. Firms use dynamic pricing and demand forecasting to manage this.

*(Any two characteristics with explanation and example fetch full 3 marks.)*

---

### Question 2: List the 7 Ps of services marketing mix. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

The 7 Ps of services marketing, introduced by Booms and Bitner (1981), extend the traditional 4 Ps to address the unique nature of services:

1. **Product** — Core service offering with augmented features
2. **Price** — Customer cost and pricing strategy
3. **Place** — Service delivery location and channels
4. **Promotion** — Communication and promotion mix
5. **People** — Employees and customers involved in delivery
6. **Process** — Procedures and workflow of service delivery
7. **Physical Evidence** — Tangible cues that signal service quality

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A: Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q(A)(a): Explain the GAPS Model of Service Quality in detail with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer:**

The GAPS Model, developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1985)**, is a diagnostic framework to identify and close gaps that cause service delivery failures. It identifies **five gaps**.

**Gap 1 — Knowledge Gap:**
The difference between **customer expectations** and **management's perception** of those expectations. Example: A hospital management assumes patients care only about treatment cost, but patients actually value short waiting time more.

**Gap 2 — Standards Gap:**
The difference between **management's perception** of customer expectations and the **service quality standards** actually set. Example: The hospital decides to reduce wait time but sets a vague target of "shorter wait" instead of a measurable 15-minute standard.

**Gap 3 — Delivery Gap:**
The difference between **service standards** set and **actual service performance** by employees. Example: Front-desk staff are untrained, and patients still wait 45 minutes despite the 15-minute standard.

**Gap 4 — Communication Gap:**
The difference between **actual service delivered** and **what is promised in external communications**. Example: The hospital brochure advertises "instant appointments," but actual delivery takes 30 minutes.

**Gap 5 — Service Gap (Total Quality Gap):**
The difference between **expected service** and **perceived service** as experienced by the customer. This is the cumulative effect of Gaps 1 to 4.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$\text{Gap 5} = \text{Gap 1} + \text{Gap 2} + \text{Gap 3} + \text{Gap 4}$$

**Diagram:**

```
Customer  →Gap 1→  Management  →Gap 2→  Service     →Gap 3→  Actual      →Gap 4→  External
Expectation        Perception            Specification          Delivery              Communication
                                                                                          ↓
                                                                                Customer
                                                                                Perceived
                                                                                Service
                                                                                          ↑
                                                                            ←←←Gap 5←←←
```

**Conclusion:**
The GAPS Model provides a structured approach to diagnose service quality problems. By systematically closing Gaps 1 to 4, the firm minimizes Gap 5 and improves overall service quality.

---

### Question A: Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**Q(A)(b): Differentiate between goods marketing and services marketing. Explain the additional 3 Ps of services marketing mix.**

**Model Answer:**

**Differences Between Goods Marketing and Services Marketing:**

| Basis | Goods Marketing | Services Marketing |
|-------|----------------|-------------------|
| **Nature** | Tangible product | Intangible performance |
| **Storage** | Can be stored and inventoried | Cannot be stored (perishable) |
| **Customer Involvement** | Low at production stage | High at both production and consumption |
| **Quality Variation** | Standardized through manufacturing | Variable due to human involvement |
| **Evaluation** | Can be tested before purchase | Cannot be pre-tested |
| **Marketing Mix** | 4 Ps | 7 Ps (extended) |

**The Additional 3 Ps of Services Marketing:**

**1. People:**
All human actors involved in service delivery — employees and customers. Since services are inseparable from their providers, employee training, attitude, and skill directly determine service quality. Example: A trained cabin crew transforms an ordinary flight into a memorable experience.

**2. Process:**
The procedure, flow, and sequence of activities through which the service is delivered. Standardized processes reduce variability and ensure consistency. Example: A step-by-step onboarding process in a bank ensures every customer receives the same minimum standard of service.

**3. Physical Evidence:**
The tangible cues that signal service quality and reduce the perception of intangibility. Includes building, equipment, layout, signage, brochures, websites, and uniforms. Example: A clean, well-lit, modern hospital with visible equipment reassures patients about the quality of healthcare they will receive.

**Conclusion:**
The 3 additional Ps are essential because they directly address the four unique characteristics of services — intangibility, inseparability, variability, and perishability. Together with the original 4 Ps, they form the complete services marketing mix.

---

### Question B: Part (a) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q(B)(a): What is SERVQUAL? Explain its five dimensions with examples.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:**
SERVQUAL (Service Quality) is a 22-item instrument developed by **Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry (1988)** to measure service quality by comparing customer **expectations** (E) with customer **perceptions** (P) on a Likert scale (typically 1 to 7).

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$SQ = \sum_{i=1}^{22} (P_i - E_i)$$

**Five Dimensions of SERVQUAL:**

**1. Tangibles:**
The appearance of physical facilities, equipment, personnel, and communication materials. *Example:* A five-star hotel with elegant lobby, clean rooms, and uniformed staff scores high on tangibles.

**2. Reliability:**
The ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately. *Example:* An ATM that dispenses cash correctly every time, or a courier that delivers packages on the promised date.

**3. Responsiveness:**
The willingness to help customers and provide prompt service. *Example:* A customer support team that responds to complaints within 24 hours.

**4. Assurance:**
The knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust and confidence. *Example:* A financial advisor who clearly explains complex investment products builds customer confidence.

**5. Empathy:**
The provision of caring, individualized attention to customers. *Example:* A family doctor who remembers each patient's medical history and personal concerns.

**Conclusion:**
SERVQUAL helps firms identify the specific dimensions where customer expectations are not being met, enabling targeted improvement strategies.

---

### Question B: Part (b) — 7 Marks `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**Q(B)(b): Explain the four characteristics of services. How do they create marketing challenges?**

**Model Answer:**

**The Four Characteristics of Services (IIVP):**

**1. Intangibility:**
Services cannot be seen, touched, tasted, smelled, or heard before purchase. *Marketing Challenge:* Customers find it difficult to evaluate quality beforehand. Firms must use **physical evidence** (branding, ambience, testimonials) to make the intangible feel real.

**2. Inseparability:**
Services are produced and consumed simultaneously. The service provider and customer are often present together. *Marketing Challenge:* The provider becomes part of the product. Firms must invest heavily in **employee training** and **customer management** because every interaction is a "moment of truth."

**3. Variability (Heterogeneity):**
Service quality varies from provider to provider, customer to customer, and from time to time. *Marketing Challenge:* Standardization is difficult. Firms must invest in **process standardization**, **automation**, and **quality audits** to ensure consistency.

**4. Perishability:**
Services cannot be stored, inventoried, or saved for later use. *Marketing Challenge:* Unsold capacity is lost forever. Firms must use **demand forecasting**, **capacity management**, and **dynamic pricing** to optimize utilization.

**Summary Table:**

| Characteristic | Core Challenge | Strategic Response |
|----------------|----------------|--------------------|
| Intangibility | Cannot pre-evaluate | Physical evidence, branding |
| Inseparability | Provider = Product | Employee training |
| Variability | Inconsistent quality | Process standardization |
| Perishability | Lost unsold capacity | Dynamic pricing, forecasting |

**Conclusion:**
These four characteristics fundamentally differentiate services from goods and require a customized marketing mix — the 7 Ps — to address them effectively.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse SERVQUAL dimensions with the 7 Ps.** SERVQUAL has 5 dimensions (Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy). The 7 Ps are a marketing mix (Product, Price, Place, Promotion, People, Process, Physical Evidence). Examiners deduct marks for mixing these.
> 2. **Gap 5 is NOT just the sum of Gaps 1 to 4.** It is a function of all four gaps, but the relationship is conceptual, not strictly additive. State it carefully.
> 3. **Always provide an example** when explaining the four IIVP characteristics. A definition without example loses at least 1 mark in a 7-mark question.
> 4. **For SERVQUAL numerical problems**, show the formula $SQ = \sum (P_i - E_i)$ explicitly. Marks are awarded for the formula, the calculation, AND the interpretation.
> 5. **Do NOT list the 4 Ps and call it services marketing.** Always include People, Process, and Physical Evidence in any services marketing mix question.
> 6. **Diagram is mandatory in GAPS Model questions.** Even a simple hand-drawn box-and-arrow diagram earns you 2 additional marks.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- **Service Definition:** Intangible act, performance, or process — no transfer of ownership.
- **IIVP Mnemonic:** **I**ntangibility, **I**nseparability, **V**ariability, **P**erishability — the four pillars of services.
- **Intangibility is countered by Physical Evidence and Branding.**
- **Inseparability is countered by People (employee training) and Customer Co-Creation.**
- **Variability is countered by Process Standardization and Automation.**
- **Perishability is countered by Dynamic Pricing and Capacity Management.**
- **4 Ps (Goods):** Product, Price, Place, Promotion.
- **7 Ps (Services) — Booms and Bitner, 1981:** Product, Price, Place, Promotion + **People, Process, Physical Evidence**.
- **GAPS Model — Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry, 1985:** Five gaps — Knowledge, Standards, Delivery, Communication, and Total Service Gap.
- **Gap 5 = Expected Service − Perceived Service.**
- **SERVQUAL — Parasuraman, Zeithaml, Berry, 1988:** 22 items, 5 dimensions — Tangibles, Reliability, Responsiveness, Assurance, Empathy.
- **SERVQUAL Formula:** $SQ = \sum (P_i - E_i)$. Negative = poor service; Zero = expectations met; Positive = excellent service.
- **Three Service Domains:** Pure services, Major services with minor goods, Major goods with minor services.
- **Service Triangle Players:** Company, Employees, Customers — all three must be aligned for service quality.
- **Marketing Mix for Services MUST include the 3 additional Ps** in any KTU 7-mark or 14-mark answer.
- **Always draw a diagram** for GAPS Model and 7 Ps questions to secure full marks.
- **Real-world examples** (hospitality, banking, education, healthcare, IT) are mandatory in KTU descriptive answers.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Competition Analysis
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Competition Analysis # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] **Competition Analysis (KTU 2024 Definition)** > Competition Analysis is the systematic and continuous process of **identifying, evaluating, and monitoring** an organization's current and potential rivals in the marketplace to understand their **strategic intent, capabilities, strengths, weaknesses, and likely future actions**. It is a critical component of the marketing strategy formulation process that enables a firm to develop a sustainable **competitive advantage** and craft effective positioning strategies. In the KTU 2024 Scheme Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework, Competition Analysis maps directly to: - **CO2**: Analyze the marketing environment and competition to formulate marketing strategies. - **CO3**: Design competitive positioning and differentiation strategies. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] **Plain English Analogy — "The Chess Match on the Market Board"** > Imagine a **chess tournament** where multiple players sit around a board. To win, a player must: > 1. **Identify** all opponents across the table (competitor identification). > 2. **Study** each opponent's playing style — aggressive, defensive, or unpredictable (competitor profiling). > 3. **Predict** their next moves based on their captured pieces and past behavior (strategic intent analysis). > 4. **Counter** their strategy by positioning pieces smartly (competitive positioning). > 5. **Watch** for new players entering the tournament (new entrants / substitute threats). > > Competition Analysis is the marketing equivalent of this chess preparation — knowing the board, the pieces, and the rules before making your strategic move. --- ## 1.3 Why Competition Analysis Matters in Marketing > [!IMPORTANT] **Core Syllabus Highlights** > - **Zero competition = Zero strategy** — A firm cannot craft marketing strategy in a vacuum. > - Competition is one of the **5 Cs of Marketing** (Company, Customers, Collaborators, Competitors, Context). > - It is the **backbone of STP (Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning)** — your position is defined relative to competitors. > - KTU emphasizes **Porter's Five Forces**, **Competitor Mapping**, **Benchmarking**, and **Competitive Intelligence Systems (CIS)** as the four pillars of competition analysis. --- ## 1.4 Standard Metrics & Terminology (Bold Notation) The following **standard competitive metrics** are critical for KTU examinations: - **Market Share (%)** — The firm's sales as a percentage of total industry sales. - **Relative Market Share (RMS)** — A firm's market share divided by its largest competitor's market share. - **Competitive Parity** — Matching competitor's offerings feature-for-feature. - **Share of Voice (SOV)** — Proportion of advertising spend relative to competitors. - **Brand Equity Score** — Customer-perceived brand value differential. - **Benchmarking Index** — Quantitative comparison of performance against industry best. --- ## 1.5 Types of Competitors (KTU Taxonomy) | **Competitor Type** | **Definition** | **Example** | |---|---|---| | **Direct Competitor** | Offers the same product to the same target market with similar features. | Pepsi vs. Coca-Cola | | **Indirect Competitor** | Offers different products that solve the same underlying need. | Tea vs. Coffee (wakefulness) | | **Substitute Competitor** | Offers a product that replaces the use of another entirely. | Streaming services vs. Cable TV | | **Future / Potential Competitor** | Firms not yet in the market but likely to enter due to high margins. | EV startups threatening petrol OEMs | | **Aspirational Competitor** | A brand the firm benchmarks against despite not directly competing. | A regional brand benchmarking Tata | --- # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four-Pillar Framework of Competition Analysis (KTU 2024 Module 2 Spine) ### Pillar 1 — Competitor Identification Before analyzing, a firm must answer: *"Who is competing with us?"* This is done through: - **Customer-based mapping**: Ask customers what alternatives they considered. - **Market-based mapping**: List all players in the same product category. - **Resource-based mapping**: Identify firms with similar resources/capabilities. - **Strategic group mapping**: Group competitors pursuing similar strategies. ### Pillar 2 — Competitor Analysis (The 4-Factor Profiling System) For each identified competitor, analyze: 1. **Objectives** — What are they trying to achieve? (Profit, growth, market leadership) 2. **Strategies** — How are they trying to achieve it? (Cost leadership, differentiation, focus) 3. **Strengths** — What are their core competencies? (Brand, distribution, R&D, capital) 4. **Weaknesses** — Where are they vulnerable? (Poor service, high cost, weak brand) 5. **Response Patterns** — How do they react to competitive moves? (Aggressive, defensive, passive) ### Pillar 3 — Competitive Positioning Positioning is the act of designing the firm's offering and image to occupy a **distinctive place in the target customer's mind** relative to competitors. Two dominant frameworks: - **Porter's Generic Strategies**: Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus. - **Value Curve Analysis** (from Blue Ocean Strategy): Identify factors the industry competes on and reconstruct buyer value elements. ### Pillar 4 — Competitive Intelligence System (CIS) A CIS is a **systematic process** for gathering, analyzing, and disseminating competitive information to decision-makers. It has three tiers: - **Strategic Intelligence** (long-term, top management) - **Tactical Intelligence** (mid-term, middle management) - **Operational Intelligence** (short-term, frontline employees) --- ## 2.2 Porter's Five Forces Model (KTU High-Yield Framework) Michael Porter's Five Forces framework is the **cornerstone of competition analysis** in the KTU syllabus. It defines the **structural attractiveness** of an industry. > [!IMPORTANT] **Porter's Five Forces — KTU High-Yield Framework** > 1. **Threat of New Entrants** — How easy is it for new firms to enter? > 2. **Bargaining Power of Suppliers** — How much leverage do suppliers have? > 3. **Bargaining Power of Buyers** — How much leverage do customers have? > 4. **Threat of Substitutes** — How likely are alternative products to replace yours? > 5. **Rivalry Among Existing Competitors** — How intense is the direct competition? Each force is governed by **structural barriers and determinants** (see Section 2.3). --- ## 2.3 Determinants of Each Force (Detailed Breakdown) | **Force** | **Key Determinants** | **High-Force Indicators** | |---|---|---| | **Threat of New Entrants** | Economies of scale, capital requirements, product differentiation, switching costs, government policy, expected retaliation | Low barriers, low capital needs, weak patents | | **Supplier Power** | Supplier concentration, switching costs, unique inputs, threat of forward integration, importance of buyer to supplier | Few suppliers, high switching cost, differentiated inputs | | **Buyer Power** | Buyer concentration, buyer switching costs, product differentiation, brand identity, price sensitivity | Few large buyers, low switching costs, undifferentiated products | | **Threat of Substitutes** | Relative price performance of substitutes, buyer propensity to substitute, switching costs | Better price-performance ratio, low switching costs | | **Rivalry Intensity** | Industry concentration, growth rate, fixed costs, product differentiation, exit barriers, diversity of competitors | Many competitors, slow growth, high fixed costs, low differentiation | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Formula / Concept** | **Equation** | **Description** | |---|---|---| | **Market Share (MS)** | $$MS = \frac{\text{Firm Sales}}{\text{Total Industry Sales}} \times 100$$ | % share of total market captured by firm | | **Relative Market Share (RMS)** | $$RMS = \frac{\text{Firm's MS}}{\text{Largest Competitor's MS}}$$ | Comparison against the market leader | | **Market Growth Rate (MGR)** | $$MGR = \frac{\text{Current Market Size} - \text{Previous Market Size}}{\text{Previous Market Size}} \times 100$$ | Annualized industry growth rate | | **Competitive Parity Index (CPI)** | $$CPI = \frac{\text{Firm's Marketing Spend}}{\text{Competitor Avg. Marketing Spend}}$$ | >1 = spending more than average | | **Share of Voice (SOV)** | $$SOV = \frac{\text{Firm's Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$$ | Proportion of category advertising | | **Competitive Intensity Score (CIS)** | $$CIS = \sum_{i=1}^{5} w_i F_i$$ | Weighted sum of the 5 Porter Forces | | **Porter's Average Industry Profit** | $$\pi_{avg} = f(\text{5 Forces structure})$$ | Industry profitability is a function of the 5 forces | | **BCG Growth-Share Quadrant** | $\text{Star/Horse/Cow/Dog} = f(\text{MS}, \text{MGR})$ | Strategic classification by MS and MGR | | **Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI)** | $$HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^2$$ | Market concentration measure; $s_i$ = firm $i$'s market share | | **Concentration Ratio (CR4)** | $$CR_4 = \sum_{i=1}^{4} s_i$$ | Combined market share of top 4 firms | > [!NOTE] **Why these formulas matter in KTU exams** > KTU frequently tests 3-mark questions asking for the definition and application of these metrics. The Porter Five Forces framework is the most heavily tested concept — expect at least one 7-mark or 14-mark question on it. --- ## 2.5 Real-World Engineering / Business Utility > [!IMPORTANT] **Where Competition Analysis is Applied in Practice** > - **Startups & Product Strategy**: Validating product-market fit by analyzing existing players. > - **B2B Sales & Tendering**: Firms analyze competitor bids to win contracts. > - **Innovation & R&D Direction**: Identifying gaps in competitor offerings to develop differentiated products. > - **Mergers & Acquisitions (M&A)**: Due diligence of acquisition targets requires deep competitive profiling. > - **Pricing Strategy**: Dynamic pricing models use real-time competitor price intelligence. > - **KTU Capstone Projects**: Mandatory competitive analysis for all B.Tech project pitches. --- # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Step-by-Step Process of Competition Analysis (Detailed Workflow) The KTU 2024 Module 2 prescribes a **sequential 7-step process** for conducting competition analysis. Each step is expanded with logic and conversion rationale below. ### **Step 1: Define the Market Scope** **Logic**: Before identifying competitors, clearly define the boundary of the market you operate in. **Practical Expansion**: - Define the **product category boundaries** (e.g., smartphones vs. feature phones). - Define the **geographic scope** (local, national, global). - Define the **customer segment scope** (B2B vs. B2C, premium vs. mass). > *Example*: A Kerala-based spice brand first defines its market as "premium packaged spices in South India" — not all packaged foods. ### **Step 2: Identify Competitors** **Logic**: List all relevant competitors operating in the defined market. **Methods Used**: - Secondary research (industry reports, Google Trends). - Customer surveys ("What other brands did you consider?"). - Trade publications and competitor websites. - Mystery shopping and field observation. > *Output*: A list of 8–15 direct and indirect competitors. ### **Step 3: Gather Competitor Data (Information Collection)** **Logic**: Collect qualitative and quantitative data on each identified competitor. **Data Categories**: 1. **Marketing Mix (4Ps)**: Product, Price, Place, Promotion. 2. **Financials**: Revenue, profit margins, marketing spend. 3. **Strategy**: Long-term goals, target segments, positioning. 4. **Capabilities**: R&D strength, distribution network, brand equity. 5. **Customer Perception**: NPS, reviews, social media sentiment. > *Tools Used*: SWOT analysis, perceptual maps, financial ratio analysis. ### **Step 4: Analyze Competitors (4-Factor Profiling)** **Logic**: Apply the 4-Factor Profile to each competitor. | **Factor** | **Analytical Question** | |---|---| | **Objectives** | What is the competitor trying to achieve? Market share, profit, growth? | | **Strategies** | How are they achieving it? Cost leadership or differentiation? | | **Strengths** | What are they exceptionally good at? | | **Weaknesses** | Where are they vulnerable or lagging? | | **Response Patterns** | How do they typically react to competitive moves? | ### **Step 5: Estimate Competitor Response Patterns** **Logic**: Predict how each competitor will react to your planned move. Porter identified **4 response patterns**: 1. **Laying Low** — Competitor does not react; treats your move as a non-threat. 2. **Strong and Immediate Reaction** — Aggressive retaliation (price war, marketing blitz). 3. **Strong but Delayed Reaction** — Competitor observes, then strikes back after careful analysis. 4. **No Reaction** — Indifferent due to different strategic priorities. **Conversion Logic**: $$\text{Response Intensity} = f(\text{Threat Perception}, \text{Resource Availability}, \text{Strategic Fit})$$ ### **Step 6: Select Competitors to Attack and Avoid** **Logic**: Not all competitors are worth attacking. Use the **Customer Value Analysis (CVA)** matrix: | **Customer Value** | **Competitor Strength** | **Action** | |---|---|---| | High | High | **Attack**: Differentiate on the value dimension | | High | Low | **Improve**: Strengthen where competitor is weak | | Low | High | **Avoid**: Do not attack on this dimension | | Low | Low | **Divest**: Reduce focus here | ### **Step 7: Design Competitive Strategy and Monitor** **Logic**: Implement the chosen strategy and continuously monitor the competitive environment using a **Competitive Intelligence System (CIS)**. --- ## 3.2 Detailed Derivation: Market Share Calculation Let us derive the **Market Share** formula step-by-step from first principles. **Step 1**: Define the variable for total industry sales in period $t$ as $S_t^{industry}$. **Step 2**: Define the variable for a specific firm's sales in the same period as $S_t^{firm}$. **Step 3**: The market share is the ratio of firm sales to industry sales: $$MS_{firm} = \frac{S_t^{firm}}{S_t^{industry}}$$ **Step 4**: To express as a percentage, multiply by 100: $$MS_{firm} \ (\%) = \frac{S_t^{firm}}{S_t^{industry}} \times 100$$ **Conversion Logic**: This percentage represents the firm's share of the total category sales. A higher MS indicates greater dominance. > *Example*: If $S_t^{firm} = 240$ crore and $S_t^{industry} = 1200$ crore, then $MS = 240 / 1200 \times 100 = 20\%$. --- ## 3.3 Detailed Derivation: Relative Market Share (RMS) **Step 1**: Identify the market leader — the firm with the highest market share — and denote its share as $MS_{leader}$. **Step 2**: The RMS of the focal firm is the ratio of its market share to the leader's: $$RMS = \frac{MS_{firm}}{MS_{leader}}$$ **Step 3**: If $RMS > 1$, the firm is the market leader. If $RMS = 1$, it is tied. If $RMS < 1$, it is a challenger or follower. **Conversion Logic**: A challenger with $RMS = 0.5$ has half the market share of the leader — it needs to **double its share** to match the leader. > *Example*: Firm A has $MS = 15\%$, leader has $MS = 30\%$. $RMS = 15/30 = 0.5$. --- ## 3.4 Detailed Derivation: Herfindahl-Hirschman Index (HHI) **Step 1**: Let there be $N$ firms in the industry, with market shares $s_1, s_2, \ldots, s_N$ expressed as percentages (not decimals). **Step 2**: The HHI is the sum of the squared market shares: $$HHI = \sum_{i=1}^{N} s_i^2$$ **Step 3**: Interpretation ranges (per U.S. DOJ guidelines, also commonly referenced in KTU case studies): $$ \begin{aligned} HHI < 1500 &\Rightarrow \text{Competitive / Fragmented Industry} \\ 1500 \leq HHI \leq 2500 &\Rightarrow \text{Moderately Concentrated Industry} \\ HHI > 2500 &\Rightarrow \text{Highly Concentrated Industry} \end{aligned} $$ > *Example*: 3 firms with $s_1 = 50\%$, $s_2 = 30\%$, $s_3 = 20\%$. $HHI = 50^2 + 30^2 + 20^2 = 2500 + 900 + 400 = 3800$ → **Highly Concentrated**. --- ## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: BCG Growth-Share Matrix (Strategic Tool for Competitor Analysis) | **Quadrant** | **Market Growth** | **Relative Market Share** | **Strategic Action** | |---|---|---|---| | **Star** (Stars) | High | High | Invest aggressively, expand market | | **Cash Cow** (Cows) | Low | High | Harvest cash, maintain share | | **Question Mark** (Children) | High | Low | Invest selectively or divest | | **Dog** (Pets) | Low | Low | Liquidate, divest, or reposition | **Conversion Logic**: This matrix helps firms **classify their product portfolio** relative to competitors and decide **resource allocation**. --- # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Diagram 1: Porter's Five Forces — Complete Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A1["Threat of New Entrants"] --> A5["Competitive Rivalry Intensity"] A2["Bargaining Power of Suppliers"] --> A5 A3["Bargaining Power of Buyers"] --> A5 A4["Threat of Substitute Products"] --> A5 A5 --> A6["Industry Attractiveness and Profit Potential"] A1 -- "Determined by Entry Barriers" --> A1 A2 -- "Determined by Supplier Concentration" --> A2 A3 -- "Determined by Buyer Concentration" --> A3 A4 -- "Determined by Substitute Availability" --> A4 ``` > [!NOTE] **Interpretation**: The four forces (New Entrants, Suppliers, Buyers, Substitutes) collectively shape the **central competitive rivalry**, which determines industry attractiveness and average profitability. --- ## 4.2 Diagram 2: Seven-Step Process of Competition Analysis (KTU Workflow) ```mermaid flowchart TD S1["Step 1: Define Market Scope"] --> S2["Step 2: Identify Competitors"] S2 --> S3["Step 3: Gather Competitor Data"] S3 --> S4["Step 4: Analyze via 4-Factor Profile"] S4 --> S5["Step 5: Estimate Response Patterns"] S5 --> S6["Step 6: Select Attack and Avoid Strategy"] S6 --> S7["Step 7: Implement and Monitor via CIS"] S7 -.->|Continuous Feedback| S2 ``` --- ## 4.3 Diagram 3: Competitive Intelligence System (CIS) Architecture ```mermaid graph LR A["Field Intelligence
Sales People and Frontline"] --> B["Operational Intelligence
Middle Management"] B --> C["Tactical Intelligence
Senior Marketing"] C --> D["Strategic Intelligence
Top Management and Board"] A -- "Daily Market Data" --> B B -- "Weekly Reports" --> C C -- "Monthly Strategy Briefs" --> D D -- "Strategic Direction" --> A ``` --- ## 4.4 Diagram 4: Perceptual Mapping (Positioning Map Example for Smartphone Brands) ```mermaid graph LR A["Apple
Premium and High Tech"] --> B["Samsung
Premium and Moderate Tech"] A --> C["Xiaomi
Affordable and High Tech"] B --> D["Nokia
Affordable and Moderate Tech"] C --> D A -- "Differentiation Axis" --> D B -- "Differentiation Axis" --> C ``` > [!NOTE] **Interpretation**: The perceptual map positions brands on two dimensions (Price vs. Technology). Empty quadrants represent **untapped positioning opportunities** — a classic Blue Ocean strategy insight. --- ## 4.5 Diagram 5: Customer Value Analysis (CVA) Decision Matrix ```mermaid flowchart TD Q1{"High Customer Value and High Competitor Strength?"} -->|Yes| A1["Attack: Differentiate Aggressively"] Q1 -->|No| Q2{"High Customer Value and Low Competitor Strength?"} Q2 -->|Yes| A2["Improve: Close the Gap Quickly"] Q2 -->|No| Q3{"Low Customer Value and High Competitor Strength?"} Q3 -->|Yes| A3["Avoid: Do Not Challenge"] Q3 -->|No| A4["Divest: Withdraw Resources"] ``` --- ## 4.6 Diagram 6: Competitor Response Patterns (Porter's Model) ```mermaid graph TD R["Competitor's Reaction to Your Move"] --> R1["Laying Low
Indifferent Response"] R --> R2["Strong and Immediate
Price War and Marketing Blitz"] R --> R3["Strong but Delayed
Strategic Retaliation"] R --> R4["No Reaction
Different Strategic Priorities"] ``` --- # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, PECBT412]` **Define Competition Analysis. List any two types of competitors with examples.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1.5 Marks)**: Competition Analysis is the systematic process of identifying, evaluating, and monitoring an organization's current and potential rivals to understand their strategies, strengths, weaknesses, and likely future actions. It enables the firm to craft a sustainable competitive advantage and effective positioning. - **Types of Competitors (1.5 Marks)**: 1. **Direct Competitor** — Offers the same product to the same target market. *Example: Pepsi vs. Coca-Cola.* 2. **Indirect Competitor** — Offers different products that satisfy the same underlying need. *Example: Tea vs. Coffee (both provide wakefulness).* --- ### **Question 2** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, PECBT412]` **What is a Competitive Intelligence System (CIS)? Mention its three levels.** **Model Answer (3 Marks):** - **Definition (1.5 Marks)**: A Competitive Intelligence System (CIS) is a systematic process for gathering, analyzing, and disseminating competitive information about rivals, customers, products, and market trends to support strategic and operational decision-making. - **Three Levels (1.5 Marks)**: 1. **Strategic Intelligence** — Long-term, used by top management. 2. **Tactical Intelligence** — Mid-term, used by middle management. 3. **Operational Intelligence** — Short-term, used by frontline employees. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each with Internal Choice) ### **Question A** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Module 2, PECBT412]` **(a)** Explain Porter's Five Forces Model of competition analysis. Discuss the determinants of each force. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Differentiate between Market Share and Relative Market Share. Calculate the RMS of a firm whose market share is 18% in an industry where the market leader has a share of 36%. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks** **Introduction (1 Mark)**: Porter's Five Forces Model, developed by Michael E. Porter of Harvard Business School, is a strategic framework for analyzing the **competitive structure** of an industry. It identifies **five competitive forces** that collectively determine the **intensity of competition** and the **average profitability** of the industry. **Explanation of the Five Forces (4 Marks)**: 1. **Threat of New Entrants (0.8 Marks)**: New firms entering the industry increase capacity, intensify competition, and may erode prices and margins. Determined by **entry barriers** such as economies of scale, capital requirements, product differentiation, switching costs, and government policy. 2. **Bargaining Power of Suppliers (0.8 Marks)**: Powerful suppliers can extract higher prices or restrict supply, squeezing industry profitability. Determined by supplier concentration, switching costs, unique inputs, and threat of forward integration. 3. **Bargaining Power of Buyers (0.8 Marks)**: Powerful buyers can demand lower prices, higher quality, or better service. Determined by buyer concentration, switching costs, product differentiation, and price sensitivity. 4. **Threat of Substitute Products (0.8 Marks)**: Substitutes perform the same function using a different approach. They limit the prices firms can charge. Determined by relative price-performance and buyer switching costs. 5. **Rivalry Among Existing Competitors (0.8 Marks)**: The central force — competitors engage in price competition, advertising battles, and product launches. Determined by industry concentration, growth rate, fixed costs, and exit barriers. **Strategic Implication (1 Mark)**: - The collective strength of these five forces determines the **industry structure** and **average profitability**. Firms must position themselves where the forces are weakest, or use strategy to alter the forces in their favor (e.g., building entry barriers, locking in buyers, etc.). **Application Example (1 Mark)**: - The **Indian smartphone industry** has high rivalry (Samsung, Xiaomi, Vivo, OnePlus, Apple) and high threat of new entrants, but moderate supplier power (Qualcomm, MediaTek dominate). It is therefore a **low-to-moderate margin industry** with intense innovation race. --- #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks** **Conceptual Difference (3 Marks)**: | **Basis** | **Market Share (MS)** | **Relative Market Share (RMS)** | |---|---|---| | **Definition** | Firm's sales as a percentage of total industry sales | Firm's market share relative to the largest competitor's market share | | **Reference** | Entire industry | Single largest competitor | | **Range** | 0% to 100% | 0 to infinity (1.0 = parity with leader) | | **Purpose** | Indicates overall market presence | Indicates competitive position vs. leader | | **Formula** | $MS = \frac{S_{firm}}{S_{industry}} \times 100$ | $RMS = \frac{MS_{firm}}{MS_{leader}}$ | **Numerical Calculation (4 Marks)**: **Given**: - Market share of the focal firm: $MS_{firm} = 18\%$ - Market share of the market leader: $MS_{leader} = 36\%$ **Step 1**: State the formula: $$RMS = \frac{MS_{firm}}{MS_{leader}}$$ **Step 2**: Substitute the values: $$RMS = \frac{18\%}{36\%}$$ **Step 3**: Simplify: $$RMS = 0.5$$ **Step 4**: Interpret the result (1 Mark): - An $RMS = 0.5$ means the firm has **half** the market share of the industry leader. The firm is a **challenger** and would need to **double its market share** to match the leader. This calls for aggressive growth strategies — new product launches, market expansion, or competitive pricing. --- ### **Question B** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Module 2, PECBT412]` **(a)** Explain the Seven-Step Process of Competition Analysis. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** What is a Perceptual Map? Construct a hypothetical 2x2 perceptual map for the **soft drink industry in Kerala**, positioning brands on the dimensions of **Price (Low to High)** and **Taste Profile (Traditional to Modern)**. **(7 Marks)** --- #### **Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks** **Introduction (1 Mark)**: Competition Analysis is not a one-time event but a **structured, sequential process**. The KTU 2024 Module 2 prescribes a **seven-step process** that guides marketers from defining scope to implementing strategy. **Step-by-Step Explanation (6 Marks — 0.85 each approx.)**: 1. **Step 1 — Define the Market Scope**: Clearly delineate the product category, geographic boundary, and customer segment. *Example: "Premium packaged tea in Kerala."* 2. **Step 2 — Identify Competitors**: Use customer surveys, secondary research, trade publications, and field observation to list 8–15 direct and indirect competitors. 3. **Step 3 — Gather Competitor Data**: Collect data on the **4Ps (Marketing Mix)**, financials, strategies, capabilities, and customer perception of each competitor. 4. **Step 4 — Analyze Competitors (4-Factor Profile)**: Profile each competitor on **Objectives, Strategies, Strengths, and Weaknesses** plus **Response Patterns**. 5. **Step 5 — Estimate Competitor Response Patterns**: Predict how each competitor will react — *Laying Low, Strong Immediate, Strong Delayed, or No Reaction.* 6. **Step 6 — Select Competitors to Attack and Avoid**: Use the **Customer Value Analysis (CVA) Matrix** to identify which competitors to attack (where customer value is high and competitor is strong), improve against, avoid, or divest from. 7. **Step 7 — Design Strategy and Monitor via CIS**: Implement the chosen competitive strategy and continuously monitor the environment using a **Competitive Intelligence System (CIS)**. **Conclusion (1 Mark)**: - This process is **iterative** — Step 7 feeds back into Step 2, ensuring continuous adaptation to a dynamic competitive environment. --- #### **Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks** **Definition of Perceptual Map (2 Marks)**: - A **perceptual map** (also called a positioning map) is a **two-dimensional visual tool** that plots brands in the minds of consumers based on **two key attributes or dimensions**. It helps identify **competitive positioning**, **market gaps**, and **opportunities for differentiation**. **Constructing the 2x2 Map for Kerala Soft Drinks (3 Marks)**: ``` HIGH PRICE | Fanta | Kingfisher Premium Mountain | Soda (Modern) Dew | ---------+--------- MODERN TASTE (Modern) | (Modern) Coke | Paper Boat (Modern) | Premium ---------+--------- TRADITIONAL TASTE | Bovril | Limca (Trad.) | (Trad.) | LOW PRICE ``` **Brand Positioning Summary (2 Marks)**: | **Brand** | **Price** | **Taste Profile** | **Quadrant** | |---|---|---|---| | Fanta / Mountain Dew | Medium | Modern | Upper-Left | | Kingfisher Premium Soda | High | Modern | Upper-Right | | Coke | Medium-Low | Modern | Lower-Left | | Paper Boat Premium | High | Modern | Lower-Right (Modern-High) | | Bovril | Low | Traditional | Lower-Left (Traditional) | | Limca | Low-Medium | Traditional | Lower-Right | **Strategic Insight (1 Mark)**: - The **upper-left quadrant (high price, traditional taste)** is **empty** — this represents an **untapped Blue Ocean opportunity** for a brand that can offer a **premium traditional taste** (e.g., a premium tender coconut water with nostalgic branding). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Competition Analysis Questions** > 1. **Listing Porter's Five Forces without explaining determinants** — KTU examiners award full marks only when you discuss the *determinants* of each force. Simply naming the forces fetches only **3 out of 7 marks**. > 2. **Confusing Market Share with Relative Market Share** — MS is calculated against the *entire industry*; RMS is calculated against the *largest competitor*. Many students mix these up. > 3. **Skipping examples in definitions** — KTU 2024 OBE rubrics reward **application-level thinking**. Every definition must be supported with at least one real-world example. > 4. **Not drawing the perceptual map** — In 14-mark questions, a **diagram is compulsory**. Marks are explicitly reserved for visual representation (typically 2–3 marks). > 5. **Forgetting the iterative nature of CIS** — Competition analysis is **continuous**, not a one-time activity. Always mention that Step 7 loops back to Step 2. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] **Last-Minute Revision Checklist for KTU Exam** > - **Definition**: Competition Analysis = Identifying + Evaluating + Monitoring rivals. > - **5 Cs of Marketing**: Company, Customers, Collaborators, **Competitors**, Context. > - **Porter's Five Forces**: New Entrants, Suppliers, Buyers, Substitutes, Rivalry. > - **Porter's Generic Strategies**: Cost Leadership, Differentiation, Focus. > - **4-Factor Competitor Profile**: Objectives, Strategies, Strengths, Weaknesses (+ Response Patterns). > - **4 Response Patterns**: Laying Low, Strong Immediate, Strong Delayed, No Reaction. > - **CVA Matrix**: Attack, Improve, Avoid, Divest. > - **CIS Levels**: Strategic, Tactical, Operational. > - **Key Formulas**: $MS$, $RMS$, $MGR$, $SOV$, $CPI$, $HHI$, $CR_4$. > - **HHI Thresholds**: <1500 Competitive, 1500–2500 Moderate, >2500 Highly Concentrated. > - **BCG Matrix**: Star, Cash Cow, Question Mark, Dog. > - **Perceptual Map**: A 2D positioning tool — always draw it visually in exams. > - **Tools**: SWOT, CVA, BCG Matrix, Perceptual Map, CIS. > - **Iterative Nature**: Step 7 of the process loops back to Step 2 — analysis never ends. > - **Real-world examples to memorize**: Apple vs. Samsung, Pepsi vs. Coke, Tata vs. Reliance, Kerala-specific brands (Eastern, Nyle, etc.). ---

topics.title: Analysis of Consumer and Industrial Markets
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Analysis of Consumer and Industrial Markets

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Two Fundamental Markets in Marketing Management

In the discipline of **Marketing Management**, every transaction that occurs in any economy belongs to one of two foundational market structures. Understanding the behavioral, structural, and psychological differences between these two markets is the prerequisite to designing any effective marketing strategy.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition — Consumer Market**
> A **Consumer Market** is the aggregate of all individuals and households who purchase goods and services for **personal, family, or household consumption** — and not for resale, profit generation, or business use. The end-user is a human being satisfying a personal need or want.

> [!NOTE]
> **Formal KTU Definition — Industrial Market (B2B Market)**
> An **Industrial Market** (also called the **Business-to-Business (B2B) Market** or **Organizational Market**) comprises all organizations — firms, institutions, government bodies, and NGOs — that acquire goods and services to be used as **inputs for the production of other goods/services**, for operational purposes, or for resale to other businesses or final consumers.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Mango Shop vs. The Caterer"

Imagine you walk into a fruit shop and buy **two mangoes for yourself** to eat at home — you are acting in the **Consumer Market**. You pay cash, you are moved by personal taste, your mood matters, and you may even be influenced by a friend's Instagram reel.

Now imagine a **catering company** buying **500 kilograms of mangoes** to make mango desserts for a wedding — they are acting in the **Industrial Market**. They will inspect quality certificates, negotiate bulk discounts, demand supply contracts, evaluate vendor reliability, and possibly tender the order across multiple suppliers.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The single most important shift:** the consumer is moved by **emotion, lifestyle, and identity**; the industrial buyer is moved by **logic, ROI (Return on Investment), and operational necessity**. This is the heart of Module 2.

### 1.3 Quantitative Snapshot — Market Scale (Indicative Global Metrics)

> [!TIP]
> While exact figures fluctuate yearly, in mature economies the **B2B Industrial Market is roughly 2× to 3× the size of the consumer market in monetary value**, although it involves **far fewer buyers**. This is the classic "**many consumers, few industrial giants**" paradox.

| Parameter | Consumer Market (B2C) | Industrial Market (B2B) |
|---|---|---|
| **Typical Number of Buyers** | Millions to Billions | Hundreds to Thousands |
| **Average Order Value** | Low (unit-based) | Very High (bulk/lot-based) |
| **Decision Logic** | Emotional + Functional | Rational + ROI-driven |
| **Buyer Type** | Individual / Household | Trained Professional / Buying Center |
| **Geographic Spread** | Universal (mass market) | Concentrated (regional/cluster) |
| **Demand Nature** | Derived + Inelastic (sometimes) | Strongly **Derived** from consumer demand |
| **Relationship Length** | Short-term / transactional | Long-term / partnership |

### 1.4 Why This Topic Carries High Weightage in KTU ESE

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Pattern Observation:** Questions on "**differences between consumer and industrial markets**" appear almost every semester in either **Part A (3 marks)** or as a **14-mark descriptive question** in **Part B**. Additionally, the **industrial buying process** and the **consumer buying behavior model** are direct, high-scoring questions when students structure their answers using the official models (Stimulus-Response Model, Buying Center concept).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Stimulus–Response Model of Buyer Behavior (Foundation Model)

Philip Kotler's classical framework treats the buyer's mind as a "**black box**" — marketers can observe the inputs (stimuli) and the outputs (responses), but must infer the internal decision process.

$$\boxed{\text{Marketing \& Other Stimuli} \longrightarrow \underbrace{\text{Buyer's Black Box}}_{\text{Culture, Social, Personal, Psychological}} \longrightarrow \text{Buyer's Response (Choice / Purchase / Brand)}}$$

### 2.2 Detailed Breakdown — The Four Lenses of the Black Box

#### A. Cultural Factors (the deepest and most pervasive)

| Sub-factor | Description | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Culture** | Set of basic values, perceptions, wants, and behaviors learned from family and institutions | Vegetarianism in Kerala shapes food-buying |
| **Subculture** | Smaller groups sharing belief systems — religion, caste, region, ethnicity | Onam Sadhya demand spikes during festival |
| **Social Class** | Relatively permanent, ordered divisions of society whose members share similar values/interests | Middle-class preference for EMI-led purchases |

> [!NOTE]
> Cultural factors operate at the **subconscious** level — buyers cannot easily articulate them, yet they dictate the largest portion of preference.

#### B. Social Factors

- **Reference Groups:** peer groups, aspirational groups, family. Example: a teenager buys a sneaker brand worn by their cricketer idol.
- **Family:** including the **Family of Orientation** (parents) and **Family of Procreation** (spouse/children).
- **Roles & Status:** position in society dictates product choice (CEO chooses executive car model).

#### C. Personal Factors

- **Age & Stage in Life Cycle**
- **Occupation & Economic Situation** (income, savings, assets)
- **Lifestyle** (AIO — Activities, Interests, Opinions)
- **Personality & Self-Concept**

#### D. Psychological Factors (the four "O" + Motivation framework)

1. **Motivation** — Maslow's Hierarchy drives needs.
2. **Perception** — selective attention, distortion, retention.
3. **Learning** — experience-driven behavior change.
4. **Beliefs & Attitudes** — long-held mental positioning.

### 2.3 The Industrial Buying Process (Buygrid Model — Robinson, Faris & Wind)

The **Eight Stages of Industrial Buying Decision Making** is the most-tested industrial model in KTU:

$$\boxed{\text{Stage 1} \rightarrow \text{Stage 2} \rightarrow \text{Stage 3} \rightarrow \text{Stage 4} \rightarrow \text{Stage 5} \rightarrow \text{Stage 6} \rightarrow \text{Stage 7} \rightarrow \text{Stage 8}}$$

| Stage # | Stage Name | KTU Key Point |
|---|---|---|
| **1** | Problem Recognition | Triggered by internal or external event |
| **2** | General Need Description | Specifying the requirement |
| **3** | Product Specification | Detailed technical specs |
| **4** | Supplier Search | Identifying potential vendors |
| **5** | Proposal Solicitation | RFP / RFQ issuance |
| **6** | Supplier Selection | Multi-attribute evaluation |
| **7** | Order-Routine Specification | Final order terms |
| **8** | Performance Review | Post-purchase evaluation |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial buying is "derived demand."** The demand for steel exists only because consumers buy cars, fridges, and buildings. If consumer demand for cars falls, the industrial demand for steel **automatically contracts**, even though steel factories themselves never lost a customer directly.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!TIP]
> Marketing Management is largely conceptual. The "formulas" below are the **structural relationships** you must memorize for derivations and descriptive answers.

| # | Relationship / Formula | Units / Logic | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{Derived Demand} = f(\text{Consumer Demand})$ | Functional dependence | Explains B2B demand elasticity |
| 2 | $\text{Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{Profit}_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Monetary units; $r$ = discount rate | Measures long-term buyer worth |
| 3 | $\text{Number of Customer Touchpoints} = f(\text{Buying Center Size})$ | Count | Determines B2B marketing intensity |
| 4 | $\text{Demand Volatility Index} = \dfrac{\sigma_{\text{Industrial}}}{\sigma_{\text{Consumer}}}$ | Ratio (typically $> 1$) | Industrial markets fluctuate more |
