3. **Personal Selling:** Direct, person-to-person persuasive selling.
4. **Public Relations (PR):** Earned media, press releases, events, sponsorships.
5. **Direct Marketing:** Email, SMS, catalogs, telemarketing, direct mail.
6. **Digital & Social Media Marketing:** SEO, SEM, influencer marketing, content, viral campaigns.

**Step 3 — Strategic Combination for a Consumer-Electronics Launch (2 marks):**

For a new smartphone launch, an integrated campaign might use:

- **Pre-Launch (Tease):** PR + social-media teasers + influencer leaks.
- **Launch Day:** Advertising (TV + digital) + Personal Selling (retail) + PR event.
- **Post-Launch Sustain:** Sales Promotion (exchange offers, cashback) + Direct Marketing (email to existing customers) + Social Media (UGC, reviews).

**Step 4 — Conclusion (1 mark):**

> IMC ensures that whether a customer encounters the brand on Instagram, on TV, in a store, or on a billboard, the **core message, tone, and visual identity are consistent** — building cumulative brand equity.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Mistakes (Read Before Your Exam):**
> 1. **Listing the 4Ps/7Ps without explanation** = 0 strategy marks. Always *justify* each P with **strategic logic** (STP alignment, competitive differentiation).
> 2. **Treating Price as the most important P** = partial marks. KTU values *integration* — show that all Ps interact.
> 3. **Forgetting the 7th and 8th Ps in a service-marketing question** = −2 to −3 marks. Always check: *Is the example a service?* If yes, mention **People, Process, Physical Evidence**.
> 4. **Writing generic real-world examples** (e.g., "Coca-Cola uses good marketing") = poor marks. Use **specific** examples with specifics: *Coca-Cola's "Share a Coke" personalization campaign in 2014 increased US sales by 2% after a decade of decline.*
> 5. **No diagrams in 14-mark answers** = −1 to −2 marks. Always include a **2×2 matrix, a comparison table, or a flow diagram**.
> 6. **Ignoring the word "Strategic"** in the question = the biggest trap. KTU 2024 explicitly tests whether students understand that the mix is a *strategic, integrated system*, not a checklist.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist — Print and Revise:**

- ✅ **Marketing Mix = 4Ps (Product, Price, Place, Promotion) + 3 extended Ps (People, Process, Physical Evidence).**
- ✅ Coined by **E. Jerome McCarthy (1960)**, refined by **Philip Kotler**.
- ✅ The 7Ps were added by **Booms & Bitner (1981)** for **service marketing**.
- ✅ The mix is **strategic**, not tactical — must align with **STP outputs**.
- ✅ **Product** strategies: BCG Matrix, PLC, Product Line/Mix decisions.
- ✅ **Price** strategies: Skimming, Penetration, Competitive, Value-based, Cost-plus, Dynamic.
- ✅ **PED formula:** $E_p = \frac{\% \Delta Q_d}{\% \Delta P}$ — guides pricing decisions.
- ✅ **Break-even volume:** $Q^* = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$ — sets the pricing floor.
- ✅ **Place** strategies: Intensive (FMCG), Selective (Durables), Exclusive (Luxury); Channel length = Direct vs. Indirect.
- ✅ **Promotion** strategies: Advertising, Sales Promo, Personal Selling, PR, Direct, Digital — coordinated via **IMC**.
- ✅ **People** = customer-facing employees and other customers.
- ✅ **Process** = the choreography of service delivery.
- ✅ **Physical Evidence** = the tangible signals of service quality.
- ✅ **Ansoff Matrix** = Market Penetration, Market Development, Product Development, Diversification.
- ✅ Always include **at least one diagram/table** in 14-mark answers.
- ✅ Real-world examples must be **specific, dated, and strategic** — not generic.

---

**End of Module 2 — Strategic Marketing Mix Components | KTU 2024 Scheme Study Notes**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Planning and Development
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Product Planning and Development

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Product? (The Foundation)

In the context of **KTU 2024 Scheme – Marketing Management (PECBT412)**, a product is formally defined as anything that can be offered to a market for attention, acquisition, use, or consumption that might satisfy a want or need. It includes physical objects, services, persons, places, organizations, and ideas.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Kotler & Keller):** A product is *anything that can be offered to a market to satisfy a want or need, including physical goods, services, experiences, events, persons, places, properties, organizations, information, and ideas.*

The **5 Levels of a Product** (Mandatory KTU Concept):

| Level | Name | Meaning | Example (iPhone) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Core Benefit** | Fundamental need being satisfied | Communication & Connectivity |
| 2 | **Basic Product** | Basic version of the actual product | Touchscreen smartphone |
| 3 | **Expected Product** | Attributes buyers normally expect | Battery, charger, decent camera |
| 4 | **Augmented Product** | Exceeds expectations, differentiates | Free cloud storage, AppleCare |
| 5 | **Potential Product** | Future enhancements and directions | AR integration, AI assistant |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition 🍦

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a Product like an Ice-Cream Sundae!**
> 
> Imagine you walk into an ice-cream parlor.
> - The **core benefit** is "I am hungry for dessert" (the underlying need).
> - The **basic product** is "a scoop of vanilla ice-cream" (the literal item).
> - The **expected product** is "a clean bowl and a spoon" (things you assume come with it).
> - The **augmented product** is "chocolate sauce, sprinkles, and a free cherry on top" (the delightful extras).
> - The **potential product** is "next time, they might offer liquid nitrogen-frozen ice-cream" (future vision).
> 
> **In KTU exams, the product is NEVER just the physical object — it is the *complete bundle of value*.**

---

## 1.2 What is Product Planning?

**Product Planning** is the systematic process of making decisions about what products a company will offer, how it will develop them, manage them throughout their life, and eventually phase them out — all aligned with the firm's marketing strategy and customer needs.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition:** Product planning is the continuous process of identifying, defining, evaluating, and selecting product-market opportunities, and the coordinated execution of all activities that bring new or modified products to the market.

## 1.3 What is Product Development (New Product Development – NPD)?

Product development refers to the **complete process of bringing a new product idea from concept to market launch and beyond**. It transforms market opportunities into commercially viable products.

### Conceptual Analogy 🚗

> [!NOTE]
> **Product Development = Building a Custom House**
> 
> You don't just pour concrete and hope for the best. You:
> 1. First **imagine** the house (Idea Generation).
> 2. Then **sketch** blueprints (Concept Development).
> 3. **Check** if the design is buildable (Business Analysis).
> 4. Build a **miniature model** (Prototype).
> 5. Let a few people **test-drive** the model (Test Marketing).
> 6. Finally **construct the full house** and open the doors (Commercialization).
> 
> Skip a step, and the house collapses. Same with products!

## 1.4 Importance of Product Planning and Development

- **Survival:** Approximately **30,000 new products** are launched globally every year; without innovation, firms die.
- **Growth Driver:** New products contribute to **25-30% of total revenue** in most large firms.
- **Competitive Edge:** First-mover advantage can capture **40-60% market share**.
- **Resource Optimization:** Prevents wastage of capital on unviable ideas.
- **Customer Loyalty:** Continuous innovation keeps customers engaged.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Highlight:** A study cited in standard marketing textbooks reveals that out of every **60 new product ideas**, only **1** becomes a commercial success. This is why structured NPD is critical.

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The New Product Development Process (Kotler's 8-Stage Model)

This is the **most important model** for KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3. Examiners frequently ask for explanations of any stage with examples, OR they ask the complete sequence.

### Stage 1: Idea Generation (Idea Screening)
- **What:** Systematic search for new product ideas.
- **Sources:** Internal (R&D, employees), External (customers, competitors, distributors, trade shows).
- **Tools:** Brainstorming, focus groups, customer surveys, suggestion boxes.
- **Output:** A large pool of raw ideas (often **hundreds**).

### Stage 2: Idea Screening
- **What:** Filter out bad ideas early to avoid sunk costs.
- **Evaluation criteria:** Market size, technical feasibility, profitability, strategic fit.
- **Output:** A shortlist of viable ideas.

### Stage 3: Concept Development and Testing
- **What:** Convert raw ideas into detailed product **concepts** (a clear, meaningful consumer-oriented version).
- **Concept Testing:** Present concept to a sample of target customers; gather reactions.
- **Output:** Refined concept with features, benefits, and positioning.

### Stage 4: Marketing Strategy Development
- **What:** Design an initial marketing strategy for the new product.
- **Components:** Target market, positioning, sales, market share, profit goals, pricing, distribution, marketing mix for Year 1, Year 2, and Year 3.

### Stage 5: Business Analysis
- **What:** Detailed financial and commercial evaluation.
- **Tools:** Sales forecast, cost estimate, break-even analysis.
- **Output:** Go / No-Go decision by management.

### Stage 6: Product Development
- **What:** Convert concept into an actual physical product.
- **R&D:** Engineering, design, prototyping, multiple iterations.
- **Output:** Working prototype ready for testing.

### Stage 7: Test Marketing
- **What:** Launch product in a small, representative geographic area to gauge real-world response.
- **Methods:** Sales-wave research, simulated test marketing, controlled test marketing.
- **Output:** Real data on sales, usage, and re-purchase intent.

### Stage 8: Commercialization
- **What:** Full-scale market launch.
- **Decisions:** Timing (first-strike, parallel, late entry), geography (single city, region, national, international), target market, marketing budget.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** In the exam, when asked to "Explain the NPD process," write each stage in this exact order. Stage names must be precise — mixing "Idea Generation" with "Idea Screening" marks results in loss of 2 marks.

---

## 2.2 Product Life Cycle (PLC) — The Strategic Blueprint

The PLC is a **biological metaphor** showing how a product evolves from birth to death in the market. It consists of **4 distinct stages**.

### The Four Stages

| Stage | Sales | Profit | Customers | Marketing Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Introduction** | Low | Negative / None | Innovators (2.5%) | Build awareness; heavy promotion |
| **Growth** | Rapidly Rising | Rising sharply | Early Adopters (13.5%) | Differentiate; expand distribution |
| **Maturity** | Peaks then stabilizes | Peak | Early + Late Majority (68%) | Defend market share; line extensions |
| **Decline** | Falling | Falling | Laggards (16%) | Harvest, maintain, or withdraw |

### Why 'Why' and 'How' Behind Each Stage

- **Introduction:** High costs (launch, promotion) vs. low revenue → losses are normal.
- **Growth:** Word-of-mouth kicks in, competitors enter, profits soar.
- **Maturity:** Saturation; competition is intense; the firm must **modify** the product, **find new uses**, or **find new markets** (Market Modification, Product Modification, Marketing Mix Modification).
- **Decline:** Caused by technological change, shifting tastes, or superior substitutes. Firms must decide whether to **harvest, divest, or drop** the product.

---

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Formula / Metric | Equation | Meaning | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Market Penetration Rate** | $MPR = \frac{\text{New Customers Acquired}}{\text{Total Target Market}} \times 100$ | % of target market that bought the product | Percentage (%) |
| **Sales Forecast Accuracy** | $SFA = 1 - \frac{\vert \text{Forecast} - \text{Actual Sales} \vert}{\text{Actual Sales}}$ | How close forecast was to reality | Index (0 to 1) |
| **Break-Even Sales (units)** | $BES = \frac{\text{Fixed Costs}}{\text{Unit Price} - \text{Unit Variable Cost}}$ | Units needed to cover costs | Units |
| **Break-Even Sales (₹)** | $BEV = \frac{\text{Fixed Costs}}{1 - \frac{\text{Variable Cost}}{\text{Unit Price}}}$ | Revenue needed to break even | Rupees (₹) |
| **Product Adoption Rate** | $PAR(t) = L \cdot \frac{1 - e^{-(p+q)t}}{1 + \frac{q}{p} e^{-(p+q)t}}$ | Bass Diffusion Model for innovation spread | Cumulative adopters |
| **Market Share** | $MS = \frac{\text{Company Sales}}{\text{Total Market Sales}} \times 100$ | Firm's slice of the total market | Percentage (%) |
| **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \frac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \cdot r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | Long-term value of a customer | Rupees (₹) |
| **Product Success Ratio** | $PSR = \frac{\text{Successful Products}}{\text{Total Products Launched}} \times 100$ | % of products that succeeded | Percentage (%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** The Bass Diffusion Model formula is usually **not** asked for derivation, but is highly valued in 14-mark questions on "diffusion of innovation." Understand the role of $p$ (coefficient of innovation) and $q$ (coefficient of imitation).

### Real-World Utility of These Formulas

- **BES / BEV** → Used by finance teams before product launch to set minimum sales targets.
- **Bass Model** → Used by tech giants like Apple, Tesla, and Samsung to predict iPhone, EV, and Galaxy adoption curves.
- **CLV** → Drives marketing budget allocation; high CLV justifies higher customer acquisition costs.
- **PSR** → Benchmark for measuring R&D effectiveness; benchmark industry average is **1 in 60** ideas succeeding.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Step-by-Step Derivation: Break-Even Point for a New Product

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this derivation matters:** KTU frequently gives a 14-mark question asking to "calculate the break-even point" of a new product launch. This is the most-tested quantitative method in Module 3.

### Problem Statement
A company is planning to launch a new mixer-grinder priced at **₹3,000** per unit. The variable cost per unit is **₹1,800** and total fixed costs are **₹48,00,000** per year. Calculate:
(a) Break-even point in units.
(b) Break-even point in revenue.
(c) Sales needed to earn a target profit of **₹12,00,000**.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Given Information:**
- Unit Selling Price (SP) = ₹3,000
- Unit Variable Cost (VC) = ₹1,800
- Total Fixed Cost (FC) = ₹48,00,000
- Target Profit (TP) = ₹12,00,000

**Step 1: Calculate Contribution Margin per Unit**

The contribution margin is what each unit contributes toward covering fixed costs.

$$
CM_{unit} = SP - VC
$$

Substituting values:

$$
CM_{unit} = 3000 - 1800 = 1200
$$

So, each unit sold contributes **₹1,200** toward fixed costs.

**Step 2: Calculate Break-Even Point in Units**

The BEP (units) is the number of units needed such that total contribution equals total fixed cost.

$$
BEP_{units} = \frac{FC}{CM_{unit}}
$$

Substituting values:

$$
BEP_{units} = \frac{4800000}{1200}
$$

$$
BEP_{units} = 4000 \text{ units}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Break-Even Point in Revenue (Rupees)**

$$
BEP_{revenue} = BEP_{units} \times SP
$$

$$
BEP_{revenue} = 4000 \times 3000
$$

$$
BEP_{revenue} = 1,20,00,000 \text{ (₹1.2 Crore)}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Sales Units for Target Profit**

To earn a specific profit, we add the target profit to fixed costs in the formula.

$$
Q_{target} = \frac{FC + TP}{CM_{unit}}
$$

$$
Q_{target} = \frac{4800000 + 1200000}{1200}
$$

$$
Q_{target} = \frac{6000000}{1200}
$$

$$
Q_{target} = 5000 \text{ units}
$$

### Final Analytical Matrix

| Metric | Value | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|
| Contribution Margin / unit | ₹1,200 | 40% of price |
| **BEP (units)** | **4,000** | Sales below this = loss |
| **BEP (₹)** | **₹1,20,00,000** | Revenue below this = loss |
| **Target Sales (units)** | **5,000** | To earn ₹12,00,000 profit |
| **Margin of Safety** | *(see below)* | Cushion before loss |

**Step 5: Calculate Margin of Safety (Extension)**

If expected sales = 6,000 units:

$$
MoS = \text{Expected Sales} - BEP_{units}
$$

$$
MoS = 6000 - 4000 = 2000 \text{ units}
$$

In percentage:

$$
MoS\% = \frac{2000}{6000} \times 100 = 33.33\%
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Insight:** Always show units (₹, units) in the final answer. Also mention that above 4,000 units, every additional unit generates ₹1,200 in profit (since fixed costs are already covered). Examiners reward this insight with bonus marks.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Product Line vs. Product Mix

| Dimension | Product Line | Product Mix |
|---|---|---|
| **Definition** | Group of products within a class that are closely related | Total set of all products offered by a company |
| **Example (HUL)** | Surf Excel, Rin, Wheel → "Detergent Line" | Surf, Clinic Plus, Dove, Bru, Kissan → All HUL products |
| **Width** | — | Number of different product **lines** |
| **Length** | Number of items in a line | Total number of items across all lines |
| **Depth** | Variants of each item (size, flavor, color) | — |
| **Consistency** | — | How closely related the lines are |

### Product Mix Decisions (KTU 4 Key Decisions)

1. **Product Line Stretching:** Add items above/below the current range.
   - **Downward Stretch:** Premium brand → budget version (e.g., Samsung Galaxy S series → Galaxy A series).
   - **Upward Stretch:** Budget brand → premium (e.g., Xiaomi Mi → Mi Premium).
   - **Two-way Stretch:** Both ends simultaneously.

2. **Product Line Filling:** Add more items within the existing range (e.g., Maggi adds new flavors).

3. **Product Line Modernization:** Update existing products (e.g., iPhone gets a new design yearly).

4. **Product Line Pruning:** Drop unprofitable items to focus on profitable ones (e.g., HUL discontinued "Rin Refresh" to focus on core Rin variants).

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 New Product Development (NPD) — Sequential Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1: Idea Generation] --> B[Stage 2: Idea Screening]
    B --> C[Stage 3: Concept Development and Testing]
    C --> D[Stage 4: Marketing Strategy Development]
    D --> E[Stage 5: Business Analysis]
    E --> F{Go or No-Go Decision}
    F -->|No-Go| Z[Reject Project]
    F -->|Go| G[Stage 6: Product Development - R&D and Prototyping]
    G --> H[Stage 7: Test Marketing]
    H --> I{Commercialization Viable?}
    I -->|No| Z
    I -->|Yes| J[Stage 8: Commercialization - Full Market Launch]
    J --> K[Stage 9: Post Launch Review and PLC Tracking]
    K --> L{Product in Growth or Maturity?}
    L -->|Yes| M[Continue and Modify]
    L -->|No, in Decline| N[Harvest or Withdraw]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Diagram:** This Mermaid flowchart shows the **logical decision points** in NPD. Notice the two **diamond-shaped decision nodes** (Go/No-Go and Viability Check) — these are the critical filter stages where most ideas are killed.

## 4.2 Product Life Cycle (PLC) — Sales vs. Profit Curve

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph PLC_Stages[Product Life Cycle Curve]
        direction LR
        P1[Introduction Stage] --> P2[Growth Stage]
        P2 --> P3[Maturity Stage]
        P3 --> P4[Decline Stage]
    end

    subgraph Stage_Marketing_Strategies[Stage-Wise Marketing Strategy]
        direction LR
        S1[Heavy Promotion - Build Awareness] --> S2[Differentiate and Expand Distribution]
        S2 --> S3[Defend Market Share - Line Extensions]
        S3 --> S4[Harvest or Withdraw]
    end

    PLC_Stages ~~~ Stage_Marketing_Strategies
```

## 4.3 Product Level Hierarchy (Five Levels)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[LEVEL 1: Potential Product - Future Vision] --> B[LEVEL 2: Augmented Product - Differentiators]
    B --> C[LEVEL 3: Expected Product - Default Attributes]
    C --> D[LEVEL 4: Basic Product - The Core Physical Item]
    D --> E[LEVEL 5: Core Benefit - Fundamental Customer Need]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Although the "Core Benefit" is the most fundamental, the **Augmented Product** is what customers actually **pay a premium for**. Always highlight this when answering 14-mark questions on product strategy.

## 4.4 Product-Market Strategies (Ansoff Matrix)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Ansoff Matrix - Growth Strategy] --> B[Market Penetration: Existing Product and Existing Market]
    A --> C[Market Development: Existing Product and New Market]
    A --> D[Product Development: New Product and Existing Market]
    A --> E[Diversification: New Product and New Market]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Strategic Risk Mapping:** Market Penetration = **Lowest Risk**, Diversification = **Highest Risk**. KTU examiners often ask: "Which Ansoff strategy should a startup use?" — Answer: Begin with **Market Penetration**, then gradually diversify as resources and capability grow.

---
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**"Differentiate between a product and a service with two relevant examples."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Basis | Product | Service |
|---|---|---|
| **Tangibility** | Tangible, physical | Intangible |
| **Inventory** | Can be stored | Cannot be stored |
| **Transfer of Ownership** | Yes (e.g., selling a phone) | No (e.g., salon haircut) |
| **Example 1** | iPhone 15 (Apple) | Banking loan processing |
| **Example 2** | Maggi Noodles Packet | Uber ride service |

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for table, 1 mark for examples. State that a product is a bundle of physical + augmented attributes.)*

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**"Briefly explain the concept of 'Product Life Cycle' and state its stages."**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**
The Product Life Cycle (PLC) is a marketing concept that describes the progression of a product through **four distinct stages** from introduction to withdrawal, based on sales and profit trends over time.

The four stages are:
1. **Introduction** — Low sales, negative profits, heavy promotion.
2. **Growth** — Rapid sales growth, rising profits, competitors enter.
3. **Maturity** — Sales peak then stabilize, intense competition, focus on differentiation.
4. **Decline** — Sales and profits fall due to changing customer preferences or technology.

*(Examiner's Note: 1 mark for definition, 2 marks for clearly naming and explaining the four stages.)*

---

## PART B — 14 Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:**
> - Do NOT write generic points like "product is important" — every sub-question must link to a *specific* stage of NPD or PLC.
> - Always support with **Indian examples** (Maggi, Amul, Flipkart) — purely foreign examples (Coca-Cola, Apple) often lead to partial marking.
> - For derivations, **show every algebraic step**. Skipping steps results in 0 marks for calculation questions.
> - Cite **Kotler & Keller** or **Philip Kotler** wherever a definition appears. KTU expects textbook-specific terminology.

---

### 📌 QUESTION A (14 Marks) — **NPD Process + Real-World Case**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 3]**

**"Explain in detail the eight stages of the New Product Development (NPD) process with an example of a recent product launch in India."** [14 Marks]

#### Part (a) — Stages 1 to 4 of NPD (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Idea Generation (1 Mark):** The systematic process of collecting ideas from internal sources (R&D, employees) and external sources (customers, competitors, distributors, trade fairs). For example, **Chai Point** generated its instant chai mix idea from observing office workers' lack of time for tea breaks.

**2. Idea Screening (1 Mark):** Filtering the idea pool to remove unviable concepts early. The company evaluates each idea on parameters like market size, technical feasibility, profit potential, and strategic fit. **Fifty** chai-related ideas may be screened down to **5**.

**3. Concept Development and Testing (2 Marks):** The chosen idea is converted into a **detailed customer-oriented concept** — e.g., "A premium ready-to-mix chai sachet for working professionals that delivers barista-quality tea in 30 seconds at home." This concept is then tested with a sample of **100 target customers** to gauge intent to buy, perceived value, and improvement areas.

**4. Marketing Strategy Development (2 Marks):** A preliminary marketing plan is drafted, including:
- **Target market:** Urban professionals aged 25-40 in metros.
- **Positioning:** "Authentic barista-style chai at home, in 30 seconds."
- **Pricing:** ₹150 for a 10-sachet pack.
- **Distribution:** E-commerce (Amazon, Flipkart) + modern retail (D-Mart, Reliance).
- **Year 1 sales target:** ₹2 crore.

#### Part (b) — Stages 5 to 8 of NPD (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**5. Business Analysis (2 Marks):** Detailed financial projections are computed. Estimated first-year sales: ₹2 crore; total cost: ₹1.4 crore; expected profit: ₹60 lakh. **Break-even point = 18,000 units.** Management reviews and approves.

**6. Product Development (2 Marks):** R&D team creates the actual formulation, packaging design, and branding. Multiple prototypes are tested for taste, shelf life, and dissolution speed. Regulatory approvals (FSSAI) are obtained.

**7. Test Marketing (1 Mark):** Product is launched in **Bangalore** (a representative urban market) for 3 months. Real-world data is collected: 12,000 units sold, 60% re-purchase rate, 4.2/5 customer rating. Feedback reveals need for sugar-free variant.

**8. Commercialization (1 Mark):** Based on positive test results, the company launches pan-India via e-commerce platforms, with a marketing budget of ₹50 lakh spent on Instagram influencers, Google Ads, and in-store displays at supermarkets.

**Conclusion (1 Mark):** Following the structured NPD process reduces failure risk from **80% to under 20%**, making it a critical strategic discipline for any firm.

---

### 📌 QUESTION B (14 Marks) — **PLC Strategies + Mix Modification**

**[KTU University Exam – Sample Pattern, Module 3]**

**"Discuss the four stages of the Product Life Cycle in detail. Using the example of a real Indian product, explain the strategy adopted at each stage."** [14 Marks]

#### Part (a) — Stages 1 and 2 of PLC (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Introduction Stage (3.5 Marks):** The product is launched; sales are **low** due to limited awareness, distribution issues, and customer hesitation. Costs are high due to heavy promotional spending, while revenue is low — leading to **negative or minimal profits**. Customers at this stage are **innovators (2.5%)** who are willing to take risks.

> **Indian Example:** When **Maggi** was first launched in India in 1983, Nestlé had to spend heavily on awareness campaigns, free samples, and trial drives. Sales were low, and profitability was negligible in Year 1.

**Growth Stage (3.5 Marks):** Once the product gains traction, sales **rise rapidly** as word-of-mouth spreads. Profits climb sharply due to economies of scale. **Early adopters (13.5%)** enter, and competitors begin to enter the market with imitations. The firm should focus on **differentiation, distribution expansion, and brand-building**.

> **Indian Example:** Between 1985 and 1995, Maggi became a household name. Sales surged. Competitors like **Top Ramen** entered the market. Maggi responded with **Masala Maggi** (variant extension) and expanded distribution to every kirana store in India.

#### Part (b) — Stages 3 and 4 of PLC (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**Maturity Stage (3.5 Marks):** Sales reach a **peak and then stabilize**. Profits remain high initially but begin to plateau as market saturation sets in. The market is dominated by the **early majority and late majority (68%)** — conservative buyers. Competition is intense, with price wars and feature differentiation. Strategies here include:
- **Market Modification:** Find new users or new uses.
- **Product Modification:** Improve quality, features, or styling.
- **Marketing Mix Modification:** Adjust price, promotion, or placement.

> **Indian Example:** Maggi's maturity phase saw the launch of **Maggi Special Masala, Maggi Cuppa Mania, and Maggi Veg Atta Noodles**. Nestlé also promoted Maggi as a "quick breakfast" rather than just noodles, expanding its usage occasions.

**Decline Stage (3.5 Marks):** Sales and profits **fall steadily** due to technological change, shifting consumer preferences, or superior substitutes. Customers are **laggards (16%)** who switch only when forced. The firm has three strategic options:
- **Harvest:** Reduce marketing spend, milk the brand for cash flow.
- **Maintain:** Continue at current levels in hope of a turnaround.
- **Withdraw:** Drop the product from the market.

> **Indian Example:** In **2015**, Maggi faced a severe **ban by FSSAI** due to alleged lead content. Sales dropped to nearly zero — a textbook decline scenario. Nestlé's strategy: withdraw, reformulate, and rebuild trust. After rigorous lab testing, Maggi was relaunched in **November 2015**, eventually reclaiming its leadership position — a rare case of *revival from decline*.

**Conclusion (Bonus Mark):** The PLC is not a strict life-or-death trajectory; products can be **repositioned, relaunched, or revived** with strategic interventions, as Maggi's story proves.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Module 3: Product Planning and Development**

### 🔑 Core Definitions
- **Product:** Anything offered to a market to satisfy a need (Kotler).
- **Product Mix:** Total set of products a firm offers; measured by **width, length, depth, and consistency**.
- **NPD:** New Product Development — the 8-stage process from idea to launch.
- **PLC:** Product Life Cycle — 4 stages: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline.
- **Augmented Product:** The differentiating extras that customers value most.

### 🔑 The 8 NPD Stages (Memorize in Order)
1. Idea Generation
2. Idea Screening
3. Concept Development and Testing
4. Marketing Strategy Development
5. Business Analysis
6. Product Development (R&D / Prototyping)
7. Test Marketing
8. Commercialization

### 🔑 PLC Stage Characteristics
- **Introduction:** Innovators (2.5%) | Loss-making
- **Growth:** Early Adopters (13.5%) | Rapid profit growth
- **Maturity:** Early + Late Majority (68%) | Peak sales
- **Decline:** Laggards (16%) | Falling sales

### 🔑 The 5 Product Levels
- Core Benefit → Basic → Expected → Augmented → Potential

### 🔑 Key Product Line Decisions
- **Stretching** (Downward / Upward / Two-way)
- **Filling** (add within range)
- **Modernization** (update)
- **Pruning** (drop unprofitable)

### 🔑 Ansoff Matrix
- **Market Penetration** → Existing Product + Existing Market (Lowest Risk)
- **Market Development** → Existing Product + New Market
- **Product Development** → New Product + Existing Market
- **Diversification** → New Product + New Market (Highest Risk)

### 🔑 Critical Metrics to Remember
- **Break-Even Units** $BEP = \frac{FC}{SP - VC}$
- **Break-Even Revenue** $BEP_R = \frac{FC}{1 - \frac{VC}{SP}}$
- **Bass Diffusion Model** for innovation adoption
- **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** for long-term customer worth

### 🔑 Exam Strategy Tips
- ✅ Use **Indian examples** (Maggi, Amul, Flipkart, Tata, HUL).
- ✅ Always **show calculations step-by-step**.
- ✅ Cite **Kotler & Keller** in definition questions.
- ✅ Mention the **5 product levels** whenever defining "product."
- ✅ Use a **table or diagram** in 14-mark answers — examiners reward visual clarity.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Product Life Cycle
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Product Life Cycle

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is a foundational marketing management concept that describes the **systematic progression of a product through four distinct stages** from the moment it is launched into the market until it is eventually withdrawn or discontinued. Introduced in 1965 by **Theodore Levitt** in his seminal Harvard Business Review article *"Exploit the Product Life Cycle,"* the PLC model has become a cornerstone of strategic marketing planning.

$$PLC = f(\text{Sales, Profits, Competition, Customers, Technology})$$

Mathematically, it can be expressed as a function of five interdependent variables that evolve over time:

$$S(t), \quad P(t), \quad C_c(t), \quad C_u(t), \quad T(t)$$

where $S(t)$ = Sales volume, $P(t)$ = Profit, $C_c(t)$ = Competitor count, $C_u(t)$ = Customer awareness, and $T(t)$ = Technological maturity, all measured as functions of time $t$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (Syllabus-Approved):** The Product Life Cycle is a strategic framework that outlines the sequential stages — **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline** — through which a product-class, product-form, or individual brand passes during its commercial existence in the marketplace.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuition

Imagine a **human being's life journey**:

| Human Life Stage | Equivalent PLC Stage | Parallel Meaning |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Birth & Infancy** | Introduction | Newborn, fragile, needs nurturing (heavy marketing investment) |
| **Childhood & Youth** | Growth | Rapid learning, gaining strength, learning to walk fast (sales accelerate) |
| **Adulthood** | Maturity | Peak performance, stable career, maximum earnings (saturation of market) |
| **Old Age & Death** | Decline | Retirement, fading relevance, eventual exit (product discontinuation) |

Just as a human needs different nutrition, education, and lifestyle at different ages, a **product requires different marketing strategies** at each stage of its life. A newborn baby needs intensive parental care (high promotional spend), while an adult breadwinner needs investment and growth planning (market expansion).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Insight for Students:** The PLC is **NOT** the life cycle of the *company* — it is the life cycle of a *product* in the *market*. A company like Apple has many products, each at different PLC stages simultaneously (iPhone 16 in Growth, iPhone 12 in Maturity, iPhone 8 in Decline).

---

## 1.3 Scope of the PLC Concept

The PLC can be applied at **three distinct levels of aggregation**:

1. **Product-Class Level** (e.g., "all automobiles" — longest lifecycle, spans decades)
2. **Product-Form Level** (e.g., "electric sedans" — medium lifecycle, spans several years)
3. **Brand Level** (e.g., "Tesla Model 3" — shortest lifecycle, often just a few years)

> [!TIP]
> **Remember:** Marketers apply different PLC strategies depending on which level they are managing. A decline at the *brand* level does not mean the *product-class* is dying.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Four Canonical Stages — Exhaustive Breakdown

### **Stage 1: Introduction Stage**

This is the **launch phase** where the product is first introduced to the marketplace.

**Strategic Characteristics:**
- **Sales Volume:** Very low, often unprofitable
- **Growth Rate:** Slow, often erratic
- **Competition:** Minimal (pioneering advantage)
- **Pricing:** Skimming ($P_{high}$) or Penetration ($P_{low}$)
- **Distribution:** Selective, limited outlets
- **Promotion:** Heavy, awareness-building campaigns
- **Costs:** High per-unit cost due to low economies of scale
- **Customers:** Innovators (~2.5% of market, Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation)

**The fundamental sales equation during Introduction:**

$$S_{intro} = N_{innovators} \times f_p \times R_{repeat}$$

where $S_{intro}$ = Total introduction sales, $N_{innovators}$ = Number of innovators in the market, $f_p$ = First-purchase rate, and $R_{repeat}$ = Repeat purchase ratio.

---

### **Stage 2: Growth Stage**

The product gains **market acceptance and rapid sales acceleration**.

**Strategic Characteristics:**
- **Sales Volume:** Rapidly increasing, often exponential
- **Profit:** Rising sharply as economies of scale kick in
- **Competition:** New entrants attracted by success; market share becomes contested
- **Pricing:** Maintained or slightly reduced
- **Distribution:** Intensive, expanded to more outlets
- **Promotion:** Shift from awareness to *preference* and *conviction*
- **Customers:** Early Adopters (~13.5% of market) join the Innovators
- **Cash Flow:** Becomes positive; reinvestment opportunities emerge

**Growth rate formula:**

$$g = \frac{S_{t+1} - S_t}{S_t} \times 100\%$$

where $g$ = Growth rate percentage, $S_{t+1}$ = Sales in next period, $S_t$ = Sales in current period.

> [!NOTE]
> In the Growth stage, the **Strategic Objective** shifts from "creating awareness" to *"creating brand preference and capturing market share"*.

---

### **Stage 3: Maturity Stage**

Sales reach a **plateau**, and the market becomes **saturated**.

**Strategic Characteristics (Three Sub-Phases):**
1. **Growth Maturity** — Sales growth begins to slow
2. **Stable Maturity** — Sales plateau, sales volume stable
3. **Decaying Maturity** — Absolute sales start to decline

- **Profit:** Peaks and then stabilizes or begins to decline
- **Competition:** Intense, with marginal players squeezed out
- **Pricing:** Often reduced; competitive pricing wars may erupt
- **Distribution:** Maximum intensity
- **Promotion:** Focus on *reminder* and *defensive* advertising
- **Customers:** Early Majority (~34%) and Late Majority (~34%) — bulk of consumers
- **Product Modifications:** Line extensions, feature upgrades, repackaging

**Market Saturation Point (Penetration Ceiling):**

$$M_{max} = P_{total} \times A_{rate} \times U_{index}$$

where $M_{max}$ = Maximum market potential, $P_{total}$ = Total potential population, $A_{rate}$ = Awareness/adoption rate, $U_{index}$ = Usage index per customer.

---

### **Stage 4: Decline Stage**

Sales and profits fall as the product loses market relevance.

**Strategic Characteristics:**
- **Sales:** Falling steadily; may drop to zero
- **Profit:** Declining; eventually negative
- **Competition:** Weakening; firms exit the market
- **Pricing:** Often deeply discounted to clear inventory
- **Distribution:** Selective reduction; less shelf space
- **Promotion:** Minimal, mostly informational
- **Customers:** Laggards (~16%) — final holdouts
- **Strategic Options:** Harvesting, Divesting, or Pivoting

**Decline Velocity Equation:**

$$D_v = \frac{S_{peak} - S_{current}}{S_{peak}} \times 100\%$$

where $D_v$ = Decline velocity percentage, $S_{peak}$ = Sales at peak (maturity), $S_{current}$ = Current sales.

---

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Comprehensive)

| Formula Name | Mathematical Expression | Description | Units |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sales Growth Rate** | $g = \frac{S_{t+1} - S_t}{S_t} \times 100$ | Percentage change in sales between two periods | % |
| **Market Share** | $MS = \frac{S_{brand}}{S_{total}} \times 100$ | Brand's portion of total market sales | % |
| **Decline Velocity** | $D_v = \frac{S_{peak} - S_{now}}{S_{peak}} \times 100$ | Rate of sales fall from peak | % |
| **Break-Even Volume** | $BEV = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$ | Units needed to cover fixed + variable costs | Units |
| **Contribution Margin** | $CM = P - VC$ | Profit contribution per unit | Currency |
| **Penetration Index** | $PI = \frac{A_{actual}}{A_{potential}} \times 100$ | Adoption vs. theoretical maximum | % |
| **Repeat Purchase Ratio** | $RPR = \frac{C_{repeat}}{C_{total}} \times 100$ | Loyal customers as % of total customers | % |
| **Adoption Speed (Rogers)** | $A_t = A_{max}(1 - e^{-bt})$ | Cumulative adopters over time $t$ | Persons |
| **Profit Margin** | $PM = \frac{\pi}{R} \times 100$ | Net profit as % of revenue | % |
| **Price Elasticity** | $E_d = \frac{\Delta Q / Q}{\Delta P / P}$ | Demand sensitivity to price change | Unitless |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL KTU Rule:** All formulas above use **standard marketing variables**. When solving KTU numerical problems, always define each variable before substitution to earn full marks in the *"Define"* step.

---

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering & Industry Utility

In **engineering and product management** contexts, the PLC framework is essential for:

1. **R&D Investment Decisions** — Engineering teams allocate budgets based on whether a technology is in Introduction (heavy R&D) or Decline (minimal R&D).
2. **Manufacturing Capacity Planning** — Production lines are scaled up during Growth and scaled down during Decline.
3. **Software Versioning** — Tech products like Android versions follow PLC: Android 14 (Growth), Android 10 (Maturity), Android 7 (Decline).
4. **Semiconductor Industry** — Chip architectures (e.g., 14nm, 7nm, 5nm) each have their own PLC.
5. **Automotive Engineering** — Car models have 5-7 year PLCs with mid-cycle facelifts to extend Maturity.

> [!TIP]
> **Real Engineering Example:** Intel's *Core i7* processor line has gone through multiple PLCs. Each new generation (12th Gen, 13th Gen, 14th Gen) re-enters the Introduction stage, even though the *product-class* (x86 CPUs) remains in Maturity.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations, Analytical Matrix & Strategic Framework

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Marketing Mix Across the PLC

A critical KTU 2024 question type asks students to **map the 4Ps across all four PLC stages**. The exhaustive analytical matrix is presented below with the rationale for each strategic choice.

| Marketing Mix Element | Introduction | Growth | Maturity | Decline |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Product** | Basic, single variant; may have quality issues | Improved versions, added features; warranty extensions | Full line with multiple sizes, models, flavors | Phased deletion of weak variants; retain only profitable ones |
| **Price** | High skimming OR low penetration | Slight reductions to capture more customers | Competitive pricing; discounts and deals | Deep discounts to clear stock |
| **Place (Distribution)** | Selective, exclusive outlets | Intensive distribution via multiple channels | Maximum saturation; extensive coverage | Selective pruning; retain only profitable outlets |
| **Promotion** | Heavy awareness advertising; samples, demos | Preference-building; comparative ads | Reminder and reinforcement; loyalty programs | Minimal informational ads; targeted to loyalists |
| **Sales Volume Trend** | $\text{Very Low} \rightarrow \text{Rising Slowly}$ | $\text{Exponentially Rising}$ | $\text{Peak then Plateau then Declining}$ | $\text{Steadily Falling} \rightarrow \text{Near Zero}$ |
| **Profit Trend** | $\text{Negative or Zero}$ | $\text{Rising Rapidly}$ | $\text{Peaks then Slowly Declines}$ | $\text{Falling Sharply} \rightarrow \text{Negative}$ |
| **Customer Type (Rogers)** | Innovators ($2.5\%$) | Early Adopters ($13.5\%$) | Early + Late Majority ($68\%$) | Laggards ($16\%$) |
| **Competitor Count** | Few (pioneer) | Rapidly Increasing | Maximum (intense rivalry) | Decreasing (exits) |
| **Strategic Focus** | Build Awareness | Build Preference | Defend Market Share | Harvest or Divest |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Break-Even Analysis in PLC Stages

One of the most commonly tested KTU numerical problems involves calculating the **break-even volume (BEV)** at different PLC stages. Let us work through it completely.

### **Problem Setup:**
A company launches a new electric scooter with the following parameters:
- Fixed Costs (FC) = ₹50,00,000
- Variable Cost per unit (VC) = ₹30,000
- Selling Price per unit (P) = ₹50,000

**Step 1: Compute the Contribution Margin per Unit**

$$CM = P - VC$$

$$CM = 50{,}000 - 30{,}000$$

$$CM = 20{,}000 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 2: Compute the Break-Even Volume (BEV)**

$$BEV = \frac{FC}{CM}$$

$$BEV = \frac{50{,}00{,}000}{20{,}000}$$

$$BEV = 250 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Interpretation Across PLC Stages**

| PLC Stage | Implication for BEV | Marketing Action |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Introduction | Sales $< 250$ units initially → Loss | Spend heavily on advertising to push past BEV |
| Growth | Sales $\gg 250$ units → Substantial profit | Reinvest profits into expansion |
| Maturity | Sales stabilize near peak → Maximum absolute profit | Defend BEV with cost efficiencies |
| Decline | Sales $< 250$ units again → Loss returns | Harvesting strategy; minimize further FC |

**Step 4: Compute Profit at any Sales Level $S$**

$$\pi = (P - VC) \times S - FC$$

$$\pi = CM \times S - FC$$

For example, if $S = 1000$ units in the Growth stage:

$$\pi = 20{,}000 \times 1000 - 50{,}00{,}000$$

$$\pi = 2{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000$$

$$\pi = 1{,}50{,}00{,}000 \text{ (i.e., ₹1.5 Crore profit)}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Adoption Curve (Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation)

The PLC's customer adoption follows a **bell curve** derived from Rogers' theory. The cumulative number of adopters over time is given by:

$$A(t) = A_{max} \cdot \left(1 - e^{-bt}\right)$$

where $A_{max}$ = total potential market, $b$ = adoption coefficient, $t$ = time.

**Step-by-Step Analysis:**

**Step 1: Initial Condition Check**
At $t = 0$ (product launch):

$$A(0) = A_{max} \cdot (1 - e^{0}) = A_{max} \cdot (1 - 1) = 0$$

Confirms zero customers at launch (matches Introduction stage).

**Step 2: Long-Run Limit**
As $t \rightarrow \infty$:

$$\lim_{t \to \infty} A(t) = A_{max} \cdot (1 - 0) = A_{max}$$

Confirms market saturation at Maturity.

**Step 3: Inflection Point (Maximum Growth Rate)**
The maximum adoption rate $\frac{dA}{dt}$ occurs when:

$$\frac{d^2 A}{dt^2} = 0$$

$$A_{max} \cdot b^2 \cdot e^{-bt} = 0 \quad \Rightarrow \quad t = \infty$$

This shows that adoption rate is **maximum at the start** and decays, which corresponds to the **early Growth stage** having the steepest sales ramp.

**Step 4: Categorical Customer Counts (Rogers' Bell Curve)**
Using the standard normal distribution, customer categories as percentages:

| Category | Cumulative Adoption | Description |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Innovators | $0\% \text{ to } 2.5\%$ | Venturesome, risk-takers |
| Early Adopters | $2.5\% \text{ to } 16\%$ | Opinion leaders, social respect |
| Early Majority | $16\% \text{ to } 50\%$ | Deliberate, careful |
| Late Majority | $50\% \text{ to } 84\%$ | Skeptical, traditional |
| Laggards | $84\% \text{ to } 100\%$ | Tradition-bound, resistant |

---

## 3.4 Variations and Extensions of the PLC Concept

KTU 2024 syllabus explicitly expects students to know these **advanced PLC patterns**:

### **3.4.1 Fad PLC (Crackle Pattern)**
- Sharp spike in sales followed by rapid decline
- Examples: Pet Rocks, Cabbage Patch Kids, certain fashion crazes
- Duration: Weeks to months
- Marketing Implication: Quick profit harvesting, no reinvestment

### **3.4.2 Fashion PLC (Recurring Cycles)**
- Multiple peaks and troughs over decades
- Examples: Bell-bottom pants, miniskirts, vinyl records
- Marketing Implication: Re-launch campaigns timed to cyclical demand

### **3.4.3 Product Life Cycle Extension Strategies**
1. **Product Modifications** — New features, design changes
2. **Market Modification** — Finding new users or new uses
3. **Marketing Mix Modification** — Adjusting 4Ps to extend Maturity

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Example:** The **Boeing 737** aircraft has been in production since 1967. Through continuous modifications (737-100, -200, -300, ..., -800, MAX), Boeing has successfully *extended the Maturity stage* for over **55+ years** — one of the longest commercial PLCs in history.

### **3.4.4 Theoretical Critiques of PLC**
- **Not all products follow the S-curve** — Some show erratic patterns
- **Stage boundaries are subjective** — Hard to pinpoint exact transitions
- **PLC is descriptive, not predictive** — Cannot forecast the *length* of each stage

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Canonical PLC S-Curve (Block-Level Functional Flow)

The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the **complete functional architecture of the Product Life Cycle**, showing all four stages, their characteristic sales trajectories, and the strategic marketing actions at each phase.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["STAGE 1: INTRODUCTION\nLow Sales | Negative Profit\nHigh Costs | Few Customers\nStrategy: Build AWARENESS"] --> A2["STAGE 2: GROWTH\nRapid Sales Rise | Rising Profit\nMore Competitors | Early Adopters\nStrategy: Build PREFERENCE"]
    A2 --> A3["STAGE 3: MATURITY\nPeak Sales | Plateau | Intense Competition\nMaximum Customers\nStrategy: DEFEND Market Share"]
    A3 --> A4["STAGE 4: DECLINE\nFalling Sales | Profit Erosion\nCustomer Attrition\nStrategy: HARVEST or DIVEST"]
    A4 --> A5["END OF LIFE CYCLE\nProduct Discontinuation\nResource Reallocation"]

    B1["Marketing Input:\nHeavy Promotion"] -.-> A1
    B2["Marketing Input:\nDistribution Expansion"] -.-> A2
    B3["Marketing Input:\nLoyalty Programs"] -.-> A3
    B4["Marketing Input:\nPrice Discounts"] -.-> A4

    C1["Customer Type:\nInnovators (2.5%)"] -.-> A1
    C2["Customer Type:\nEarly Adopters (13.5%)"] -.-> A2
    C3["Customer Type:\nEarly and Late Majority (68%)"] -.-> A3
    C4["Customer Type:\nLaggards (16%)"] -.-> A4

    A1 -.-> D1["Output: Market\nPositioning"]
    A2 -.-> D2["Output: Brand\nEquity"]
    A3 -.-> D3["Output: Market\nSaturation"]
    A4 -.-> D4["Output: Cash\nRecovery"]

    style A1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

```

## 4.2 Sales & Profit Trajectory Architecture

This diagram visualizes the **twin trajectory** of sales and profit across the four PLC stages, which is the most commonly asked KTU visual.

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    PHASE1 --> PHASE2
    PHASE2 --> PHASE3
```

## 4.3 PLC Extension Strategies Decision Tree

This flowchart helps students choose the correct **PLC extension strategy** when a product enters Maturity or Decline.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    N0["Product Reaches\nMaturity or Decline?"] --> N1{"Is Market\nstill viable?"}
    N1 -->|"YES"| N2["Option 1:\nPRODUCT MODIFICATION\nNew features, design upgrade"]

    N3 -->|"NO"| N5{"Can 4Ps be\nrevised effectively?"}

    N6 --> N8
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Product Life Cycle" and list its four stages.**

**Model Answer:**

The **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** is a marketing management concept that describes the sequence of stages a product passes through from its launch in the market to its eventual withdrawal. It was introduced by **Theodore Levitt in 1965** to help marketers formulate stage-specific strategies.

The four stages of the PLC are:
1. **Introduction** — Product is launched; low sales, high costs
2. **Growth** — Rapid sales increase; rising profits
3. **Maturity** — Sales peak and plateau; intense competition
4. **Decline** — Sales and profits fall; product may be discontinued

**Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):**
- Definition: 1 mark
- Four stages listed: 1 mark
- Brief explanation: 1 mark

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
**Differentiate between the Introduction stage and the Growth stage of the PLC.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Introduction Stage | Growth Stage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Sales Volume** | Very low, slow growth | Rapidly increasing, exponential |
| **Profit** | Negative or negligible | Rising sharply |
| **Competition** | Few competitors (pioneer monopoly) | Many new entrants join |
| **Customers** | Innovators only (2.5%) | Innovators + Early Adopters (16%) |
| **Pricing Strategy** | Skimming or Penetration | Maintained or slight reduction |
| **Promotion Focus** | Building awareness | Building preference and conviction |
| **Distribution** | Selective, limited | Intensive, expanding |
| **Strategic Objective** | Create product awareness | Capture market share |

**Marks Distribution:** Comparison table with 4-5 key differences earns full 3 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** In Part B questions, students typically lose marks by (1) **failing to mention Theodore Levitt as the originator**, (2) **confusing PLC levels** (product-class vs. brand vs. form), and (3) **omitting marketing strategies** for each stage. Always include the **strategic marketing action** for each stage discussed. Also, write **at least 8-10 lines per sub-part** to ensure full marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **four stages of the Product Life Cycle** with suitable **sales and profit curves**. Describe the **characteristics** of each stage in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **marketing strategies** that a company should adopt at each stage of the PLC. Illustrate with a **real-world example**.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (a):**

The Product Life Cycle, conceptualized by **Theodore Levitt (1965)**, describes how a product evolves through four distinct stages from market entry to exit.

**Stage 1: Introduction**
- Product is launched; sales are very low and the firm usually operates at a loss
- Heavy promotional expenditure to build awareness
- Distribution is selective; pricing follows skimming or penetration strategy
- Customers are *innovators* (2.5% of market)
- Example: When Tesla launched the original Roadster in 2008

**Stage 2: Growth**
- Sales rise rapidly as the product gains market acceptance
- New competitors enter attracted by success
- Profits rise due to economies of scale
- Customers include *early adopters*; distribution becomes intensive
- Example: Tesla Model S sales acceleration in 2014-2016

**Stage 3: Maturity**
- Sales peak and then plateau; market becomes saturated
- Competition is maximum; weak competitors are squeezed out
- Profits stabilize at a high level before slowly declining
- Strategy: Defend market share via product modifications, loyalty programs
- Example: Toyota Camry in the global sedan market

**Stage 4: Decline**
- Sales and profits fall steadily due to technology change, taste shift, or saturation
- Competitors exit; firm may harvest, divest, or discontinue
- Promotional spend is cut; loyal laggards remain
- Example: BlackBerry phones, Nokia feature phones

**Sales and Profit Curves (ASCII Schematic):**

```
Sales ^
      |          ___________
      |        /             \
      |      /                  \
      |    /                      \
      |   /                         \
      |  /                            \
      | /                               \
      |/__________________________________> Time
      Intro  Growth  Maturity  Decline
```

**Marks:** Stage description (4) + Curves (1) + Examples (1) + Conclusion (1) = **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-A (b):**

**Marketing Strategies at Each Stage of the PLC**

**1. Introduction Stage Strategies:**
- **Product:** Offer a basic version; focus on quality
- **Price:** Use *price skimming* (high initial price for premium markets) or *penetration pricing* (low price for mass adoption)
- **Place:** Selective distribution through specialized outlets
- **Promotion:** Heavy *informative advertising*; free samples, demonstrations, introductory offers
- **Example:** Apple uses penetration pricing for new iPhone launches in emerging markets like India

**2. Growth Stage Strategies:**
- **Product:** Introduce improved versions, additional features, wider model range
- **Price:** Maintain or slightly reduce to capture more market share
- **Place:** Expand distribution to mass outlets
- **Promotion:** Shift to *persuasive advertising* highlighting differentiation
- **Example:** Coca-Cola adds new flavors (Cherry, Vanilla) during growth phases

**3. Maturity Stage Strategies:**
- **Product:** Add new features, packaging variations, quality improvements
- **Price:** Competitive pricing; introduce discounts and bundling
- **Place:** Maximize distribution; explore new geographies
- **Promotion:** Emphasize brand differences; loyalty rewards programs
- **Example:** Maggi noodles' reformulation and repositioning to extend maturity

**4. Decline Stage Strategies:**
- **Product:** Phase out weak SKUs; retain only profitable variants
- **Price:** Deep discounting to liquidate inventory
- **Place:** Reduce distribution to profitable outlets only
- **Promotion:** Minimal; focused on loyal customers
- **Example:** Parle-G reducing distribution to rural-only markets as urban demand shifts

**Marks:** Strategies (5) + Real-world example (1) + Conclusion (1) = **7 marks**

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam - May 2023] — *Alternative Choice*

**(a) [7 Marks]** What is the **Product Life Cycle (PLC)**? Explain the **characteristics of the Maturity stage** in detail. What strategies can a firm use to **extend the Maturity stage**?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of **Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation** and its relevance to the PLC. Describe the **five categories of adopters** with their psychological characteristics.

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (a):**

**Definition:** The PLC is a marketing model that describes the four-stage evolution of a product: Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline. (1 mark)

**Characteristics of the Maturity Stage:**

1. **Sales Plateau:** After rapid growth, sales reach a peak and then plateau. The market becomes saturated.
2. **Maximum Competition:** Many competitors fight for market share; the industry becomes oligopolistic.
3. **Profit Stabilization:** Profits peak and then stabilize; weaker firms experience declining margins.
4. **Customer Base:** Comprises the Early Majority and Late Majority (~68% of total market).
5. **Slowdown in Growth:** Growth rate drops to near zero.
6. **Cost Pressures:** Firms seek cost efficiencies to maintain profitability.

**Sub-Phases of Maturity:**
- **Growth Maturity** — Sales growth begins to slow
- **Stable Maturity** — Sales remain flat at peak
- **Decaying Maturity** — Sales begin to decline absolutely

**Strategies to Extend the Maturity Stage:**

1. **Product Modification:**
   - Quality improvements (e.g., iPhone camera upgrades)
   - Feature additions (e.g., smartphones adding 5G)
   - Style changes (e.g., car facelifts every 3-4 years)
   - Repackaging (premium packaging at no extra cost)

2. **Market Modification:**
   - Finding new users (e.g., marketing electric shavers to women)
   - Finding new uses (e.g., baking soda as a refrigerator deodorizer)
   - Increasing usage frequency (e.g., "have a Coke with meals" campaigns)

3. **Marketing Mix Modification:**
   - **Price:** Discounts, bundling, volume rebates
   - **Distribution:** New channels, international expansion
   - **Promotion:** Reminder advertising, comparative ads

**Example:** Coca-Cola has used all three strategies to keep its product in Maturity for over 130 years.

**Marks:** Definition (1) + Maturity characteristics (3) + Sub-phases (1) + Extension strategies (1.5) + Example (0.5) = **7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for Q-B (b):**

**Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation Theory (1962):**

Everett Rogers' theory explains **how, why, and at what rate** new products spread through a population. The rate of adoption follows a **bell-shaped normal distribution** when plotted over time.

**The Five Categories of Adopters:**

| # | Category | % of Market | Psychological Characteristics | PLC Stage |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Innovators** | 2.5% | Venturesome, risk-takers, financially well-off, cosmopolitan | Introduction |
| 2 | **Early Adopters** | 13.5% | Opinion leaders, social respect, locally integrated, educated | Growth |
| 3 | **Early Majority** | 34% | Deliberate, careful, deliberate decision-makers | Maturity (start) |
| 4 | **Late Majority** | 34% | Skeptical, traditional, lower income, cautious | Maturity (end) |
| 5 | **Laggards** | 16% | Tradition-bound, isolated, resistant to change, oldest | Decline |

**Relevance to PLC:**

1. **Stage-Customer Mapping:** Each PLC stage corresponds to a specific adopter category. Targeting Innovators in Introduction and Early Adopters in Growth accelerates adoption.
2. **Marketing Communication:** Messages must differ for each segment. Innovators respond to technical specs; Early Majority responds to social proof.
3. **Forecasting:** Total adoption at any time = sum of customers from each category that has entered the market.
4. **Critical Mass:** Once 10-25% adoption is reached (after Early Adopters), the product gains *critical mass* and the Growth stage accelerates.
5. **Chasm:** Geoffrey Moore's extension notes a "chasm" between Early Adopters and Early Majority that some products fail to cross.

**Mathematical Expression:**

$$A(t) = \sum_{i=1}^{5} A_i(t)$$

where $A_i(t)$ is the cumulative number of adopters from category $i$ at time $t$.

**Marks:** Theory introduction (1) + Five categories with characteristics (4) + PLC relevance (2) = **7 marks**

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Exam:**

### **Must-Know Facts:**
- ☐ PLC introduced by **Theodore Levitt (1965)** in HBR
- ☐ Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation: **1962**
- ☐ Four stages: **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, Decline**
- ☐ Five adopter categories: **2.5% / 13.5% / 34% / 34% / 16%** (sums to 100%)
- ☐ PLC applies at three levels: **Product-Class, Product-Form, Brand**
- ☐ Maturity has **three sub-phases**: Growth, Stable, Decaying

### **Commonly Confused Concepts:**
- ⚠️ PLC ≠ Product Mix (Mix is the portfolio of products a company sells)
- ⚠️ Decline ≠ Obsolescence (Decline is gradual; obsolescence is sudden)
- ⚠️ Sales Peak occurs in **Maturity**, not Growth
- ⚠️ Profit Peak often occurs *just before* sales peak (late Growth)

### **High-Frequency Exam Triggers:**
- 🎯 *"Compare Introduction and Growth stages"* — Always make a **2-column comparison table**
- 🎯 *"Discuss marketing strategies at Maturity stage"* — Always include all **three extension strategies** (Product, Market, Marketing Mix)
- 🎯 *"Explain Rogers' theory"* — Always mention **all five categories** with **percentages**
- 🎯 *"PLC is not applicable to all products"* — Mention **Fad and Fashion variations** as exceptions

### **Numerical Formulas to Memorize:**
- 📐 $BEV = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$
- 📐 $g = \frac{S_{t+1} - S_t}{S_t} \times 100$
- 📐 $MS = \frac{S_{brand}}{S_{total}} \times 100$
- 📐 $\pi = (P - VC) \times S - FC$

### **Real-World Examples for Viva/Interviews:**
- 🍎 Apple iPhone (Growth with continuous reinvention)
- 🚗 Toyota Camry (Sustained Maturity)
- ☕ Coca-Cola (130+ years in Maturity)
- 📟 BlackBerry (Classic Decline case study)
- ⌚ Smartwatches (Currently in Growth)
- 🎮 Nintendo Wii (Introduction → Growth → Sharp Decline)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: New Product Development and Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** New Product Development and Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 What is a "New Product" in Marketing Management?

In the formal KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, a **New Product** is any good, service, or idea that is perceived by a potential customer or target market as being substantially different from existing offerings in the market. It is **not limited to inventions** — a product can be "new" if it is new to the firm, new to the market, new to the customer, or new in its positioning.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition Highlight (Boothroyd, Philip Kotler framework):**
> "A new product is a product that opens up an entirely new market, replaces an existing product, or significantly broadens the market of an existing product."

**Six Categories of New Products (Kotler):**
1. **New-to-the-World Products** (true innovations — e.g., iPhone, drones)
2. **New Product Lines** (firm enters an established market for the first time)
3. **Additions to Existing Product Lines** (line extensions — e.g., new flavor of Maggi)
4. **Improvements/Revisions** (enhanced performance or perceived value)
5. **Repositioned Products** (targeted at new markets or uses)
6. **Cost Reductions** (new products performing similar functions at lower cost)

### 1.2 New Product Development (NPD) — Formal Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **New Product Development (NPD)** is the structured, cross-functional process by which firms conceive, design, develop, test, and commercialize new products. It is a sub-process of the broader Product Life Cycle Management (PLM) and is governed by the **Stage-Gate® (Cooper) model** in modern practice.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!TIP]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "Cooking a New Dish" Metaphor:**
> Imagine you run a restaurant. Creating a new dish is just like NPD:
> - **Idea Generation** = Brainstorming new recipes (what would customers love?)
> - **Screening** = Throwing out recipes that are too costly, too weird, or already exist.
> - **Concept Testing** = Asking a few trusted foodies to taste and rate it.
> - **Business Analysis** = Estimating ingredient cost vs. selling price.
> - **Product Development** = Actually cooking, perfecting, plating, and packaging.
> - **Test Marketing** = Offering it as a "Chef's Special" in one branch for 2 weeks.
> - **Commercialization** = Adding it to the permanent menu across all branches.
>
> Skip any step, and you risk launching a flop — **just like how 80% of new products fail in the real market** (Harvard Business Review data, persistent across decades).

### 1.4 Why NPD is Critical — The Innovation Imperative

| Reason | Strategic Logic |
|---|---|
| **Customer Needs Evolve** | Tastes, technology, and demographics change continuously |
| **Product Life Cycle Erosion** | Sales of every product eventually decline; renewal is mandatory |
| **Competitive Pressure** | Rivals copy successful products rapidly — **first-mover advantage** matters |
| **Revenue Growth** | New products drive **30–40% of total revenue** in mature FMCG firms |
| **Brand Equity Reinforcement** | Innovation signals a "living" brand (Apple, Tesla effect) |
| **Survival** | The 80/20 rule: **80% of new products fail**, so firms must launch many to survive |

### 1.5 Key Performance Metrics in NPD

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Metrics (memorize):**
> - **New Product Success Rate (NPSR)** = $\frac{\text{Successful New Products}}{\text{Total New Products Launched}} \times 100\%$
> - **Time-to-Market (TTM)** — months from concept to launch
> - **Adoption Rate** = % of target market that buys within a given period
> - **Trial Rate vs. Repeat Purchase Rate** — distinguishes hype from loyalty
> - **Cannibalization Rate** = % of new product sales taken from firm's own existing products

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Eight-Stage New Product Development Process

The KTU 2024 syllabus mandates a deep understanding of the **Boothroyd-Kotler 8-stage model**. Each stage has a Go/No-Go gate, mirroring the modern **Cooper Stage-Gate® process**.

#### Stage 1: Idea Generation (Ideation)
- **Definition:** Systematic search for new product ideas, both internally and externally.
- **Sources of Ideas:**
  - *Internal:* R&D labs, employees, intrapreneurship programs, executive brainstorming
  - *External:* Customers (voice of customer — VoC), competitors (reverse engineering), suppliers, distributors, patents, university research, social media trends
- **Key Tools:** Brainstorming, SCAMPER, Mind Mapping, Crowdsourcing, Ethnographic Observation
- **Outcome:** A large **idea pool** (typically 50–100 raw ideas)

#### Stage 2: Idea Screening
- **Definition:** Separating good ideas from poor ones to reduce R&D costs.
- **Decision Criteria:** Strategic fit, market size, technical feasibility, ROI potential, brand alignment
- **Common Mistake:** Premature discarding of breakthrough ideas (Kodak missed digital cameras in 1975!)
- **Tools:** Idea screening matrix, weighted scoring model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula — Weighted Score of an Idea:**
> $$\text{Weighted Score} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} w_i \cdot s_i$$
> where $w_i$ = weight of criterion $i$ (sum of all weights = 1) and $s_i$ = score (0–10) of idea on criterion $i$. A score **> 7.0** typically advances the idea to the next stage.

#### Stage 3: Concept Development and Testing
- **Concept Development:** Transforming an idea into a **product concept** (a detailed version expressed in consumer-meaningful terms).
- **Concept Statement Format:**
  > "[Product name] is a [category] for [target market] that [key benefit] unlike [main competitor] it [key differentiation]."
- **Concept Testing:** Presenting the concept to 30–50 target consumers via surveys, focus groups, or in-depth interviews.
- **Measurement:** **Purchase Intent Score** (1 = definitely would not buy, 5 = definitely would buy). Average ≥ 3.5 is a positive signal.

#### Stage 4: Marketing Strategy Development
- **Three-Part Strategy Document (Boothroyd Framework):**
  1. **Target Market:** Demographics, psychographics, usage rate
  2. **Product Positioning:** The unique value proposition and value proposition canvas
  3. **Marketing Mix (4Ps/7Ps):** Product, Price, Place, Promotion (+ People, Process, Physical Evidence for services)

#### Stage 5: Business Analysis
- **Objective:** Financial viability check before committing to heavy investment.
- **Tools:**
  - **Sales Forecast** (three scenarios: optimistic, pessimistic, expected)
  - **Break-Even Analysis**
  - **Return on Investment (ROI)**
  - **Net Present Value (NPV)**
  - **Payback Period**
- **Decision Rule:** Proceed if **NPV > 0** and **Payback Period < firm's threshold** (e.g., 3 years for FMCG).

#### Stage 6: Product Development (Engineering Phase)
- R&D translates the concept into a physical product or prototype.
- Iterative prototype testing, design for manufacturability (DFM), packaging design.
- Lab tests for quality, safety, regulatory compliance (BIS, FSSAI, FDA, CE).

#### Stage 7: Test Marketing (Market Testing)
- **Definition:** Launching the product in a limited geographic area to gauge real consumer response before full rollout.
- **Methods:**
  - *Sales Wave Testing* (free samples to repeated test groups)
  - *Controlled Test Marketing* (via retail panels like Nielsen)
  - *Simulated Test Marketing* (virtual test, e.g., BASES IRI)
  - *Full-Scale Test Markets* (cities like Hyderabad, Pune in India)
- **Cost vs. Insight Trade-off:** Full-scale is most accurate but very expensive.

#### Stage 8: Commercialization (Launch)
- **Definition:** Full-scale market introduction.
- **Key Decisions:**
  - **When** to launch (timing — first, parallel, late entrant)
  - **Where** to launch (geographic rollout — city → region → national → international)
  - **To Whom** (target customer segment)
  - **How** (marketing budget, distribution intensity, launch event)
- **KTU Statistic:** Launch budgets typically range from **10% to 30%** of expected first-year sales.

---

### 2.2 Product Life Cycle (PLC) — The Strategic Context for NPD

The **Product Life Cycle** is the bell-shaped curve that maps the sales of a product through four/five distinct stages. **It is the single most tested diagram in KTU marketing papers.**

| Stage | Sales | Profits | Cash Flow | Marketing Focus | Competitor Count |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Introduction** | Low | Negative | Negative | Awareness, trial | Few |
| **Growth** | Rapidly rising | Rising fast | Improving | Differentiation, distribution | Growing |
| **Maturity** | Peaks & plateaus | High but stabilizing | Positive high | Defending market share | Maximum (saturated) |
| **Decline** | Falling | Falling | Lowering | Harvest or divest | Declining |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Formula — Profit Maximization Timing:**
> A firm typically maximizes profit when **Marginal Revenue (MR) = Marginal Cost (MC)**. In PLC terms, this usually occurs **near the peak of the Maturity stage**, after which marketing efforts must shift to harvesting or revitalization.

---

### 2.3 Diffusion of Innovation Theory (E.M. Rogers)

> [!NOTE]
> The Diffusion of Innovation explains **how, why, and at what rate** new products spread through a market. It is the foundation of **Adoption Curve theory** — a KTU favorite.

**The Five Adopter Categories (in sequence of adoption):**

| Category | % of Adopters | Characteristic | Marketing Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Innovators** | 2.5% | Venturesome, risk-takers, technology enthusiasts | Technical specs, early access |
| **Early Adopters** | 13.5% | Opinion leaders, socially respected | Reviews, testimonials, PR |
| **Early Majority** | 34% | Deliberate, mainstream-pragmatic | Mass advertising, case studies |
| **Late Majority** | 34% | Skeptical, price-sensitive | Discounts, peer reassurance |
| **Laggards** | 16% | Traditional, change-resistant | Heavy discounts, simplicity |

> [!TIP]
> **Intuition:** The "Crossing the Chasm" gap (Moore, 1991) lies between Early Adopters and Early Majority. Most new tech products fail here because Early Adopters buy vision but Early Majority wants a complete, reliable solution.

**KTU Formula — Cumulative Adoption:**
> $$A(t) = L \cdot \left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)$$
> where $A(t)$ = cumulative adopters at time $t$, $L$ = ultimate market potential (saturation level), $k$ = adoption rate constant, $e$ = Euler's number $\approx 2.71828$.

---

### 2.4 Product Portfolio Models

#### Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **BCG Matrix** classifies a firm's products into 4 quadrants based on two axes. KTU frequently asks for "categorize these products into Stars, Cash Cows, Question Marks, Dogs."

- **Market Growth Rate** (vertical axis)
- **Relative Market Share** (horizontal axis)

| Quadrant | Growth | Share | Cash Flow | Strategic Action |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Stars** | High | High | Net neutral | Invest aggressively |
| **Cash Cows** | Low | High | Cash generator | Harvest |
| **Question Marks** | High | Low | Cash drain | Invest selectively or divest |
| **Dogs** | Low | Low | Cash drain | Divest or phase out |

#### GE-McKinsey Matrix
A 9-cell matrix using **Business Strength** vs. **Industry Attractiveness** — provides more nuance than BCG. Three zones: Invest, Selectively Invest, Harvest/Divest.

---

### 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Quick Reference — All Formulas for NPD & PLC (KTU 2024 Scheme):**

| Concept | Formula | Meaning of Variables | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| New Product Success Rate | $\text{NPSR} = \dfrac{N_{success}}{N_{total}} \times 100$ | $N_{success}$ = successful launches, $N_{total}$ = total launches | % |
| Weighted Idea Score | $S = \sum w_i \cdot s_i$ | $w_i$ = weight, $s_i$ = score | Dimensionless (0–10) |
| Break-Even Units | $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | $FC$ = fixed cost, $P$ = price, $VC$ = variable cost per unit | Units |
| Return on Investment | $\text{ROI} = \dfrac{\text{Net Profit}}{\text{Cost of Investment}} \times 100$ | Net profit, investment cost | % |
| Payback Period | $PP = \dfrac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$ | Initial outlay, annual cash inflow | Years |
| Diffusion (Bass/Exponential) | $A(t) = L(1 - e^{-kt})$ | $L$ = market potential, $k$ = adoption constant, $t$ = time | Adopters |
| Cannibalization Rate | $C\% = \dfrac{\text{Sales Lost in Existing Product}}{\text{Sales of New Product}} \times 100$ | Numerator from old product | % |
| Market Penetration | $\text{MP} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Buying Product}}{\text{Total Potential Customers}} \times 100$ | Customer base, market size | % |
| Trial-to-Repeat Ratio | $\text{TRR} = \dfrac{\text{Repeat Purchases}}{\text{Trial Purchases}}$ | Repeat sales, trial sales | Ratio |
| Price Elasticity | $E_p = \dfrac{\%\Delta Q}{\%\Delta P}$ | % change in quantity, % change in price | Dimensionless |

---

### 2.6 Real-World Utility

NPD frameworks are used by:
- **Apple** — Secretive Stage-Gate process; 3-year forward planning
- **Tesla** — Sequential launches (Model S → 3 → X → Y → Cybertruck)
- **P&G** — "Connect + Develop" open innovation model (50% of new products involve external partners)
- **FMCG Firms in India** — HUL, ITC, Nestlé use test marketing in Mumbai/Pune for pan-India rollout decisions
- **Pharma** — Clinical trials are a regulated Stage-Gate (Phase I → IV)
