## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation — Break-Even Analysis for NPD Launch Decision

**Problem Setup (KTU-style):**
> A firm is considering launching a new breakfast cereal. The development cost is **₹50,00,000** (one-time fixed cost). The variable cost per pack is **₹40**, and the selling price is **₹80**. The marketing budget for the first year is an additional fixed cost of **₹10,00,000**. The target market is 1,00,000 potential consumers. Find:
> (a) Break-even units
> (b) Break-even revenue
> (c) Will the firm break even if it achieves 10% market penetration?

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify Fixed and Variable Costs**

The total fixed cost (FC) consists of the one-time development cost and the first-year marketing budget. These costs do not change with the number of units sold.

$$FC = \text{Development Cost} + \text{Marketing Budget}$$

$$FC = 50{,}00{,}000 + 10{,}00{,}000$$

$$FC = 60{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

The variable cost per unit (VC) is the per-pack cost that changes with the volume of production.

$$VC = 40 \text{ INR per pack}$$

The selling price (P) per pack is the revenue the firm earns for each unit sold.

$$P = 80 \text{ INR per pack}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Contribution Margin per Unit**

The contribution margin is the difference between the selling price and the variable cost. It represents the amount each unit contributes toward covering the fixed costs.

$$\text{Contribution Margin} = P - VC$$

$$\text{Contribution Margin} = 80 - 40 = 40 \text{ INR per unit}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the Break-Even Formula**

The break-even quantity is the point at which total revenue equals total cost, meaning the firm makes neither a profit nor a loss. The mathematical condition is that total contribution from all units sold equals the total fixed cost.

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$$

Substituting the known values:

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{80 - 40}$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{60{,}00{,}000}{40}$$

$$Q_{BE} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ packs}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the Break-Even Revenue**

The break-even revenue is obtained by multiplying the break-even units by the selling price per unit.

$$\text{Revenue}_{BE} = Q_{BE} \times P$$

$$\text{Revenue}_{BE} = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 80$$

$$\text{Revenue}_{BE} = 1{,}20{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} = 1.2 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate the 10% Market Penetration Scenario**

If the firm achieves 10% market penetration, the number of customers buying the product is 10% of the total potential market.

$$Q_{10\%} = 10\% \times 1{,}00{,}000$$

$$Q_{10\%} = 10{,}000 \text{ packs}$$

**Step 6 — Compare with Break-Even**

We now compare the units sold under 10% penetration with the break-even units.

$$Q_{10\%} = 10{,}000 \text{ packs}$$

$$Q_{BE} = 1{,}50{,}000 \text{ packs}$$

Since $10{,}000 \ll 1{,}50{,}000$, the firm would suffer a substantial loss at only 10% penetration.

**Step 7 — Compute the Loss at 10% Penetration**

The total revenue at 10% penetration is:

$$\text{Revenue} = 10{,}000 \times 80 = 8{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

The total variable cost at 10% penetration is:

$$\text{Variable Cost} = 10{,}000 \times 40 = 4{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

The total cost (fixed plus variable) is:

$$\text{Total Cost} = 60{,}00{,}000 + 4{,}00{,}000 = 64{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR}$$

The loss is:

$$\text{Loss} = \text{Total Cost} - \text{Revenue}$$

$$\text{Loss} = 64{,}00{,}000 - 8{,}00{,}000$$

$$\text{Loss} = 56{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR} = 56 \text{ Lakhs}$$

**Step 8 — Strategic Conclusion**

The firm must achieve at least **1,50,000 packs sold (150% of the target market)**, which is mathematically impossible. Therefore, the launch is **not viable** at the current cost structure. The firm should either:

- Reduce the fixed cost (cheaper R&D or marketing)
- Increase the price (₹100 → reduces $Q_{BE}$ to 1,00,000)
- Reduce variable cost (better sourcing)
- Increase market potential through expansion to adjacent markets

---

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation — ROI and Payback Period

**Problem:**
> An innovation investment of **₹20,00,000** is expected to generate annual net cash inflows of **₹5,00,000** for 5 years.
> Calculate: (a) ROI, (b) Payback Period, (c) Cumulative Cash Flow Table.

**Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate ROI**

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{\text{Annual Net Cash Inflow}}{\text{Initial Investment}} \times 100$$

$$\text{ROI} = \frac{5{,}00{,}000}{20{,}00{,}000} \times 100$$

$$\text{ROI} = 25\% \text{ per year}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate Payback Period**

$$PP = \frac{\text{Initial Investment}}{\text{Annual Cash Inflow}}$$

$$PP = \frac{20{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000}$$

$$PP = 4 \text{ years}$$

**Step 3 — Cumulative Cash Flow Table**

| Year | Cash Inflow (INR) | Cumulative Cash Flow (INR) | Net Position (INR) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | -20,00,000 (initial outlay) |
| 1 | 5,00,000 | 5,00,000 | -15,00,000 |
| 2 | 5,00,000 | 10,00,000 | -10,00,000 |
| 3 | 5,00,000 | 15,00,000 | -5,00,000 |
| 4 | 5,00,000 | 20,00,000 | 0 (Break-even) |
| 5 | 5,00,000 | 25,00,000 | +5,00,000 (Profit begins) |

**Strategic Interpretation:** The investment recovers its initial cost exactly at the end of Year 4. Year 5 represents pure profit. A 4-year payback is **acceptable for FMCG** but **unacceptable for fast-moving tech** (where 18 months is typical).

---

### 3.3 Exhaustive Analytical Matrix — Go/No-Go Decision

| Criterion | Weight ($w_i$) | Min Threshold | Current Score (0–10) | Weighted Score |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Market Size > ₹100 Cr | 0.25 | 7 | 8 | 0.25 × 8 = 2.00 |
| Technical Feasibility | 0.20 | 6 | 9 | 0.20 × 9 = 1.80 |
| Competitive Advantage | 0.20 | 6 | 7 | 0.20 × 7 = 1.40 |
| ROI Projection | 0.15 | 5 | 6 | 0.15 × 6 = 0.90 |
| Brand Alignment | 0.10 | 6 | 8 | 0.10 × 8 = 0.80 |
| Regulatory Ease | 0.10 | 5 | 7 | 0.10 × 7 = 0.70 |
| **Total Weighted Score** | **1.00** | ≥ 7.0 | — | **7.60** |

**Decision:** **GO** (Score 7.60 > Threshold 7.00)

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Valuation Tip:** Part A answers should be **3–4 lines**, precise, and definition-driven. Avoid unnecessary essays. Use bullet points if helpful.

---

**Q1. Define a "New Product" and list any four categories of new products.**
`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Question Paper 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
A new product is a good, service, or idea perceived by a target market as substantially different from existing offerings. The four categories (Kotler) are:
1. **New-to-the-World Products** — true innovations (e.g., first-generation smartphones)
2. **New Product Lines** — firm's entry into an existing category for the first time
3. **Additions to Existing Lines** — line extensions (e.g., new flavor of an existing drink)
4. **Improvements or Revisions** — enhanced version of an existing product
5. **Repositioned Products** — existing product targeted at a new market
6. **Cost Reductions** — products offering similar performance at lower cost

---

**Q2. What is "Test Marketing"? Mention any two methods.**
`[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Sample Paper]`

**Model Answer:**
Test marketing is the Stage 7 activity in NPD where the product is launched in a limited geographic area to gauge real consumer response before full commercialization.
- **Sales Wave Testing:** Repeated free samples given to the same test group, with purchases tracked.
- **Controlled Test Marketing:** Products are placed in retail panels (e.g., Nielsen) and their movement is tracked.
- **Simulated Test Marketing:** A virtual launch where 30–50 consumers shop in a controlled environment.
- **Full-Scale Test Markets:** Launch in 2–4 representative cities (e.g., Mumbai, Pune).

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-Internal Choice Pattern (2024 Scheme):**
> The exam presents a single 14-mark question with internal choice — students answer **either (a) OR (b)**. Each sub-part is typically **7 marks**. Below are **two full 14-mark questions with sub-parts**, mirroring the real exam pattern.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)** — `Comprehensive NPD + BCG Analysis`
`[KTU University Exam - Model Paper 2024, Adapted from Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain in detail the **eight stages of the New Product Development process** with examples.

**Model Answer:**

The New Product Development process (Boothroyd-Kotler) consists of 8 sequential stages:

**Stage 1 — Idea Generation:**
This is the systematic search for new product ideas. Sources include internal R&D, customer feedback (VoC), competitor analysis, suppliers, and crowdsourcing. Example: Tesla sources battery ideas from academic research and customer feedback portals.

**Stage 2 — Idea Screening:**
Poor ideas are filtered out to avoid wasting resources. Firms use screening matrices, weighted scoring, and strategic fit analysis. Example: Google killed its "Google Glass" project in 2015 after screening revealed social acceptability issues.

**Stage 3 — Concept Development and Testing:**
The chosen idea is converted into a detailed product concept and tested with 30–50 target consumers. A concept statement and visual prototype are presented. Example: Apple tested 10+ iPhone concepts with focus groups before launch.

**Stage 4 — Marketing Strategy Development:**
A 3-part strategy is designed covering target market, product positioning, and the 4Ps of marketing. Example: Maggi's "2-minute noodles" positioning emphasizes convenience for working mothers.

**Stage 5 — Business Analysis:**
Financial viability is assessed using sales forecasts, break-even analysis, ROI, and NPV. Example: A new car model is approved only if NPV > ₹500 Cr over 5 years.

**Stage 6 — Product Development:**
R&D builds the physical product or prototype. Iterative testing, design refinement, packaging, and regulatory approvals occur. Example: Boeing's 787 Dreamliner underwent 6+ years of prototype testing.

**Stage 7 — Test Marketing:**
The product is launched in a limited area to test real-world response. Methods include sales wave testing, controlled test marketing, simulated test marketing, and full-scale test markets. Example: HUL tests new shampoo variants in Pune before pan-India launch.

**Stage 8 — Commercialization:**
Full-scale launch with decisions on timing, geography, target segment, and marketing budget. Example: Apple staged the iPhone launch — first in the US, then Europe, then Asia.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Discuss the **BCG Matrix** with a suitable diagram. How does it help in NPD portfolio decisions?

**Model Answer:**

The **Boston Consulting Group (BCG) Matrix** is a portfolio analysis tool that classifies a firm's products into four quadrants based on **market growth rate** (vertical axis) and **relative market share** (horizontal axis).

**Four Quadrants:**

**1. Stars** — High growth, high share. These are market leaders in fast-growing markets. They require heavy investment to maintain leadership but generate substantial future cash flows. Example: Tesla Model 3 in 2020s EV market.

**2. Cash Cows** — Low growth, high share. These are mature, dominant products that generate more cash than they consume. Example: Coca-Cola Classic, HUL's Lifebuoy soap.

**3. Question Marks** — High growth, low share. They drain cash but have potential. Strategic decision: invest heavily to convert to Stars or divest. Example: A new entrant's electric scooter brand in India.

**4. Dogs** — Low growth, low share. They neither generate nor consume much cash. Action: divest or phase out. Example: A decade-old feature phone model.

**Strategic Use in NPD:**
- Cash generated by **Cash Cows** is reinvested into **Question Marks** to convert them into **Stars**.
- As a market matures, **Stars** transition into **Cash Cows**.
- This dynamic resource allocation is essential for sustained NPD pipelines.

**Limitations:**
- Uses only two dimensions, ignoring brand loyalty, technology, and competitive intensity.
- Market growth rate is not always easy to measure accurately.
- May cause premature harvesting of "Dogs" that could be repositioned.

**Conclusion:** The BCG Matrix remains a powerful, simple, and intuitive tool for portfolio resource allocation, especially in mature firms with diversified product lines.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)** — `PLC + Diffusion of Innovation`
`[KTU University Exam - Sample Paper 2024, Modified from KTU 2021]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **Product Life Cycle (PLC)** with a diagram. Explain the marketing strategies for each stage.

**Model Answer:**

The Product Life Cycle (PLC) is a model that describes the trajectory of a product's sales from introduction to withdrawal. It typically has four stages: **Introduction, Growth, Maturity, and Decline**. Some models add a fifth "Saturation" stage between Maturity and Decline.

**Diagram (Refer to Section 4.2 above for full Mermaid schematic):**

Sales follow a bell-shaped curve over time:
- **Introduction:** Low sales, negative profits, heavy marketing spend to build awareness.
- **Growth:** Rapidly rising sales, rising profits, increased competition, market share battles begin.
- **Maturity:** Sales peak, profits are maximum, market is saturated, focus shifts to defending share.
- **Decline:** Falling sales and profits, product becomes obsolete, firm must decide to harvest, reposition, or delete.

**Marketing Strategies by Stage:**

**1. Introduction Stage:**
- **Product:** Basic version, single SKU
- **Price:** Skimming (high) or penetration (low)
- **Place:** Limited distribution
- **Promotion:** Heavy awareness advertising, free samples
- **Objective:** Create awareness and trial

**2. Growth Stage:**
- **Product:** Add features, models, sizes
- **Price:** Maintain or slightly reduce
- **Place:** Intensive distribution
- **Promotion:** Differentiate from competitors, build brand preference
- **Objective:** Maximize market share

**3. Maturity Stage:**
- **Product:** Modify, add variety, improve quality
- **Price:** Competitive pricing, discounts
- **Place:** Maximum distribution intensity
- **Promotion:** Reminder advertising, loyalty programs
- **Objective:** Defend market share, extend cycle

**4. Decline Stage:**
- **Product:** Phase out weak models
- **Price:** Deep discounts
- **Place:** Selective — drop unprofitable outlets
- **Promotion:** Minimal
- **Objective:** Harvest remaining value or delete

**Example:** The Nokia 1100 (1996–2010) followed this exact trajectory, dominating the feature phone market in its growth and maturity phases before being displaced by smartphones.

---

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain **Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation** theory. Describe the five adopter categories and the role of "Crossing the Chasm."

**Model Answer:**

**Everett M. Rogers' Diffusion of Innovation theory** (1962) explains how, why, and at what rate new products and ideas spread through a social system. It is a foundational model in NPD marketing.

**Five Adopter Categories:**

**1. Innovators (2.5%):** Venturesome risk-takers who adopt new products first. They are technology enthusiasts willing to tolerate glitches. Marketing strategy: provide technical specifications, exclusive launches, and early-access programs. Example: First-day buyers of foldable phones.

**2. Early Adopters (13.5%):** Socially respected opinion leaders. They adopt early but more cautiously than innovators. Their endorsement influences others. Marketing strategy: PR campaigns, influencer marketing, expert reviews, free trials. Example: Tech bloggers reviewing new gadgets.

**3. Early Majority (34%):** Deliberate, pragmatic mainstream consumers. They wait for the product to mature and for proof of value. Marketing strategy: mass advertising, case studies, peer testimonials. Example: Average consumers buying smartphones 2 years after launch.

**4. Late Majority (34%):** Skeptical, price-sensitive consumers. They adopt only after the product has become a social norm. Marketing strategy: heavy discounts, peer reassurance, simplified messaging. Example: Feature phone users finally switching to smartphones.

**5. Laggards (16%):** Traditional, change-resistant. They adopt only when forced or when the old product is no longer available. Marketing strategy: extreme simplicity, maximum discounts, hands-on demonstrations.

**Crossing the Chasm (Geoffrey Moore, 1991):**
There is a critical gap between **Early Adopters** and the **Early Majority** — called "The Chasm." Early adopters buy a vision and tolerate imperfections, but the Early Majority demands a complete, reliable, supported solution. Most new product failures occur at this chasm. To cross it, firms must:
- Identify a single "beachhead" segment and dominate it.
- Build a complete solution with strong support.
- Convert Early Adopters into references for the Early Majority.
- Allocate marketing budgets to mainstream channels, not just tech enthusiasts.

**Real-World Example:** Apple's iPhone crossed the chasm in 2008–2010 by adding the App Store, making the product a complete ecosystem rather than a tech novelty.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes That Cost Marks in KTU NPD Questions:**
> 1. **Confusing "New Product" with "Innovation":** A new product need not be technologically novel — it can be a line extension or repositioned product.
> 2. **Skipping the diagram in PLC questions:** A clear bell-curve diagram is worth **at least 2 marks** by itself.
> 3. **Writing generic strategy lists** without tying them to a specific PLC stage. Always prefix with "During the Growth stage, the firm should..."
> 4. **Forgetting the percentages of adopters** (2.5%, 13.5%, 34%, 34%, 16%) in Rogers' theory — these are **explicitly tested**.
> 5. **In BCG questions**, students often describe all 4 quadrants but forget the **strategic action** for each — this is where most marks lie.
> 6. **Not including market growth rate and market share on the axes** of the BCG matrix diagram — instant loss of 1 mark.
> 7. **Skipping the "Crossing the Chasm" concept** in Diffusion of Innovation — it is a high-weight KTU favorite.
> 8. **Numerical errors** in break-even or ROI calculations — examiners deduct partial marks for wrong substitution, not just for wrong final answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE):**

**1. New Product Categories (6 types):**
- New-to-the-world, new product line, line extension, improvement, repositioning, cost reduction

**2. NPD 8 Stages (Kotler-Boothroyd):**
- Idea Generation → Idea Screening → Concept Dev. & Testing → Marketing Strategy → Business Analysis → Product Development → Test Marketing → Commercialization

**3. Stage-Gate Principle:** Each stage has a Go/No-Go decision gate; ideas progressively reduce in number (funnel effect).

**4. PLC Stages:** Introduction → Growth → Maturity → Decline. Each has distinct marketing-mix strategies.

**5. Diffusion of Innovation (Rogers) — 5 Adopters + Percentages:**
- Innovators (2.5%), Early Adopters (13.5%), Early Majority (34%), Late Majority (34%), Laggards (16%) — total = 100%

**6. The Chasm:** Critical gap between Early Adopters and Early Majority — most product failures occur here (Moore, 1991).

**7. BCG Matrix — 4 Quadrants:**
- Stars (High-High), Cash Cows (Low-High), Question Marks (High-Low), Dogs (Low-Low)

**8. Key Formulas:**
- Break-Even: $Q_{BE} = FC / (P - VC)$
- ROI: (Net Profit / Investment) × 100
- Diffusion: $A(t) = L(1 - e^{-kt})$
- NPSR: (Successes / Total) × 100

**9. Test Marketing Methods:** Sales wave, controlled, simulated, full-scale.

**10. Common Real-World Examples to Quote in Answers:**
- Apple iPhone, Tesla EVs, Pampers, Maggi noodles, Coca-Cola, HUL brands.

**11. Product Deletion Decision:** Required when a product enters decline; alternatives include harvesting, repositioning, or divestment.

**12. KTU Statistics to Remember:**
- ~80% of new products fail
- 30–40% of mature firm revenue comes from products launched in the last 5 years
- Test marketing costs 3–5% of expected first-year sales

**13. Always draw a diagram in 14-mark questions:** Bell curve for PLC, bell curve for Diffusion, 2×2 grid for BCG — these fetch easy marks.

**14. Strategic Rule:** The product mix is dynamic — Stars become Cash Cows, Question Marks may become Stars or Dogs. Continuous NPD is the lifeline of any firm.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Defining Market Segmentation
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Defining Market Segmentation

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Market Segmentation** is the strategic process of dividing a broad heterogeneous total market into smaller, homogeneous sub-groups (segments) of consumers who share similar characteristics, needs, preferences, or behaviors, such that each segment can be effectively targeted with a distinct marketing mix.

The concept was formally introduced by **Wendell R. Smith (1956)** in his seminal paper *"Product Differentiation and Market Segmentation as Alternative Marketing Strategies."* It forms the foundational pillar of the modern **STP Framework** — **Segmentation, Targeting, and Positioning** — which is a compulsory question theme across KTU Marketing Management modules.

Mathematically, the total market is expressed as:

$$
M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i
$$

Where:
- $M_{total}$ = Total addressable market
- $S_i$ = Individual market segment $i$
- $n$ = Total number of identifiable segments

> [!NOTE]
> A *segment* is **homogeneous within** (members are similar to each other) and **heterogeneous between** (different from members of other segments). This is the central paradox of segmentation.

---

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are running a restaurant in a busy Kerala town like Kottayam. You cannot serve the same dish to everyone — a school student wants a quick *shavarma*, a family wants a traditional *sadya*, and a health-conscious gym-goer wants a *quinoa salad*.

Instead of offering one universal "meal," you **observe**, **group**, and **design** specific offerings for each group:
- **Group 1** → Students (price-sensitive, fast)
- **Group 2** → Families (value, tradition)
- **Group 3** → Fitness enthusiasts (premium, health)

That act of dividing the crowd and tailoring the offering is **Market Segmentation**.

> [!TIP]
> **Simple Memory Hook:** *"Segmentation = Sorting People into Smart Pockets."*

---

### 1.3 Why Segmentation is Mandatory (KTU Board Perspective)

The KTU 2024 Scheme evaluates students on their ability to justify *why* a firm segments its market. The board expects three layered arguments:

1. **Customer-Centric Logic:** Customers are not identical; their needs differ by age, income, geography, and lifestyle.
2. **Resource Optimization:** A startup cannot serve everyone; segmentation channels finite marketing budgets to the most profitable pockets.
3. **Competitive Edge:** Tailored value propositions beat generic mass-marketing in customer loyalty and conversion rates.

> [!WARNING]
> Common student error: Writing "segmentation means dividing customers into groups." This is incomplete. The KTU answer key requires the phrase *"homogeneous within and heterogeneous between"* to score full marks.

---

### 1.4 Core Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Market Segment** | A group of buyers with shared needs/wants |
| **Niche** | A narrowly defined sub-segment with distinct preferences |
| **Mass Market** | Undifferentiated treatment of the entire market |
| **Micro-Marketing** | Tailoring products to individuals or localities |
| **STP** | Segmentation → Targeting → Positioning |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five-Step Market Segmentation Process

Market segmentation is not a single decision — it is a **structured analytical procedure**. The KTU board expects students to list, explain, and apply these steps:

1. **Step 1 — Define the Market:** Identify the broad product/market boundary (e.g., "soft drinks in Kerala").
2. **Step 2 — Identify Segmentation Variables:** Choose demographic, geographic, psychographic, or behavioral variables.
3. **Step 3 — Develop Segment Profiles:** Build a qualitative description of each segment.
4. **Step 4 — Evaluate Segment Attractiveness:** Apply the five screening criteria (covered below).
5. **Step 5 — Select Target Segments:** Choose one or more segments to pursue using targeting strategies.

> [!NOTE]
> Steps 1–4 belong to *segmentation*; Step 5 begins *targeting*. This distinction is a frequent KTU short-answer topic.

---

### 2.2 The Four Major Bases of Segmentation

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU syllabus names **four primary bases** under the mnemonic **"D-G-P-B"** (Demographic, Geographic, Psychographic, Behavioral).

#### 2.2.1 Demographic Segmentation
Dividing the market by measurable population statistics.

$$
S_{demo} = f(A, G, I, E, L, R)
$$

Where $A$ = Age, $G$ = Gender, $I$ = Income, $E$ = Education, $L$ = Life-stage, $R$ = Religion.

#### 2.2.2 Geographic Segmentation
Division by region, climate, population density, or nationality.

$$
S_{geo} = \{(r_1, r_2, \ldots, r_n) \mid r_i \in \text{Region}_i\}
$$

Examples: Urban vs. Rural, Coastal vs. Hill districts of Kerala, Tropical vs. Temperate zones.

#### 2.2.3 Psychographic Segmentation
Division by lifestyle, values, personality, and social class.

$$
S_{psycho} = \{ \text{Lifestyle class} \mid \text{AIO statements match} \}
$$

**AIO Framework** = Activities, Interests, Opinions.

#### 2.2.4 Behavioral Segmentation
Division by user status, usage rate, loyalty, benefits sought, or occasion.

$$
S_{behav} = \{(u, r, l, b, o) \mid u \in \text{UserStatus}, r \in \text{UsageRate}, \ldots\}
$$

Examples: Non-users, ex-users, potential users, first-time users, regular users.

---

### 2.3 The Five Criteria for Effective Segmentation (Kotler's Test)

A KTU high-yield model — the **M-A-S-D-A Filter**:

| Criterion | Description | KTU Board Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Measurable** | Size, purchasing power, and profile of the segment can be quantified | "Can we measure it?" |
| **Accessible** | Segment can be effectively reached and served | "Can we reach it?" |
| **Substantial** | Segment is large/profitable enough to pursue | "Is it worth it?" |
| **Differentiate** | Segment responds differently to different marketing mixes | "Does it behave uniquely?" |
| **Actionable** | Effective programs can be designed to attract and serve | "Can we act on it?" |

> [!WARNING]
> Do not use the word **"Differentiate"** casually. The KTU board looks for the phrase *"differentially responds to marketing mix elements."*

---

### 2.4 KTU Formula / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Variable Meaning | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Total Market | $M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$ | Sum of all segments | Market sizing |
| Segment Share | $SS_i = \dfrac{S_i}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$ | Share of segment $i$ in % | Portfolio mapping |
| Segment Profitability | $P_i = S_i \times CM_i - FC_i$ | Contribution margin minus fixed cost | Targeting decision |
| Customer Lifetime Value | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{R_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | Discounted future revenue | Segment valuation |
| Segment Growth Rate | $g_i = \dfrac{S_i^{t+1} - S_i^{t}}{S_i^{t}} \times 100\%$ | Year-over-year change | Trend analysis |
| Targeting Coverage | $TC = \dfrac{\sum \text{Targeted } S_i}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$ | % of market covered | Concentration choice |
| Index of Segment Attractiveness | $ISA = w_1 M + w_2 A + w_3 S + w_4 D + w_5 Ac$ | Weighted MASDA score | Multi-criteria selection |

Where $w_1 + w_2 + w_3 + w_4 + w_5 = 1$.

---

### 2.5 Real-World Utility Across Engineering-Business Domains

| Domain | Segmentation Application |
|---|---|
| **Consumer Electronics** | Smartphone makers segment by price-band (budget, mid, premium) |
| **FMCG in Kerala** | Chakki fresh atta targets health-conscious urban homemakers |
| **Automotive** | Tata segments by usage (personal, commercial, fleet) |
| **EdTech** | Coursera segments learners by skill-stage (beginner, intermediate, pro) |
| **Banking & FinTech** | Segmentation by income slab, transaction behavior, life-stage |
| **Healthcare** | Hospitals segment by disease, demography, insurance type |

> [!TIP]
> Linking segmentation to **Kerala-context examples** (Kerala Bank, KSRTC, Nendran banana chips, Wayanad coffee) in KTU answers boosts examiner scores because it demonstrates applied understanding.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Worked Example: Market Sizing for a Kerala Coconut Oil Brand

**Scenario:** A Kerala-based company *"KeraGold"* wants to launch a premium cold-pressed virgin coconut oil in Kochi. The marketing analyst must first segment the market, then size each segment.

**Step 1 — Define the total relevant market.**

The relevant universe is *"households in Kochi district purchasing branded edible oil."*

$$
M_{total} = 7{,}80{,}000 \text{ households}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply demographic + behavioral variables.**

We segment by **Income Bracket** and **Health Consciousness Index (HCI)**.

| Segment | Income | HCI | % of Households | Segment Size $S_i$ |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| $S_1$ — Premium-Health | High | High | 15% | 1,17,000 |
| $S_2$ — Mainstream-Health | Medium | High | 20% | 1,56,000 |
| $S_3$ — Mainstream-Bulk | Medium | Low | 35% | 2,73,000 |
| $S_4$ — Economy | Low | Low | 30% | 2,34,000 |

**Step 3 — Apply the M-A-S-D-A filter.**

> $S_1$ passes all five tests → it becomes the primary target.
> $S_4$ fails *Substantial* for a premium launch and *Actionable* for premium positioning → dropped.

**Step 4 — Calculate segment share and profitability.**

Segment share of $S_1$:

$$
SS_1 = \frac{S_1}{M_{total}} \times 100\% = \frac{1{,}17{,}000}{7{,}80{,}000} \times 100\%
$$

Expanding the ratio:

$$
SS_1 = \frac{1.17}{7.80} \times 100\%
$$

$$
SS_1 = 0.15 \times 100\%
$$

$$
SS_1 = 15\%
$$

Segment Profitability for $S_1$:

$$
P_1 = S_1 \times CM_1 - FC_1
$$

Assume Average unit contribution margin = **₹45 per litre**, Annual purchase = 6 litres per household, Fixed cost allocated to segment = **₹2,00,000**.

$$
P_1 = (1{,}17{,}000 \times 6 \times 45) - 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_1 = 3{,}15{,}90{,}000 - 2{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_1 = \text{₹} 3{,}13{,}90{,}000
$$

**Step 5 — Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) for $S_1$.**

Assume average customer retention $T = 5$ years, annual revenue $R = 270$ per customer, discount $d = 10\%$.

$$
CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T=5} \frac{R_t}{(1+d)^t}
$$

$$
CLV = R \left[ 1 + \frac{1}{(1.1)^1} + \frac{1}{(1.1)^2} + \frac{1}{(1.1)^3} + \frac{1}{(1.1)^4} + \frac{1}{(1.1)^5} \right]
$$

Computing each term:

$$
CLV = 270 \left[ 1 + 0.9091 + 0.8264 + 0.7513 + 0.6830 + 0.6209 \right]
$$

$$
CLV = 270 \times 4.7907
$$

$$
CLV = \text{₹} 1293.5
$$

**Interpretation:** Each customer in $S_1$ is worth approximately **₹1,293.5** over five years — strong enough to justify a premium launch with targeted digital marketing.

---

### 3.2 Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS) — Multi-Criteria Derivation

Sometimes the choice between two equally sized segments is qualitative. We use a **weighted scoring model** derived from the M-A-S-D-A criteria.

Let the five criteria scores (out of 10) for segment $S_1$ and $S_2$ be:

| Criterion | Weight $w_j$ | Score for $S_1$ | Score for $S_2$ |
|---|---|---|---|
| Measurable | 0.10 | 8 | 9 |
| Accessible | 0.20 | 9 | 7 |
| Substantial | 0.25 | 7 | 8 |
| Differentiable | 0.20 | 9 | 6 |
| Actionable | 0.25 | 8 | 7 |

The Segment Attractiveness Score is:

$$
SAS = \sum_{j=1}^{5} w_j \times s_j
$$

**For $S_1$:**

$$
SAS_1 = (0.10)(8) + (0.20)(9) + (0.25)(7) + (0.20)(9) + (0.25)(8)
$$

$$
SAS_1 = 0.80 + 1.80 + 1.75 + 1.80 + 2.00
$$

$$
SAS_1 = 8.15
$$

**For $S_2$:**

$$
SAS_2 = (0.10)(9) + (0.20)(7) + (0.25)(8) + (0.20)(6) + (0.25)(7)
$$

$$
SAS_2 = 0.90 + 1.40 + 2.00 + 1.20 + 1.75
$$

$$
SAS_2 = 7.25
$$

**Conclusion:** $SAS_1 > SAS_2$, so $S_1$ is the more attractive segment under weighted criteria. The firm should target $S_1$ with differentiated marketing mix.

> [!NOTE]
> The weights $w_j$ must satisfy $\sum w_j = 1$ for a valid normalized score. This is a frequent KTU numerical question in Part A (3 marks).

---

### 3.3 Analytical Summary Matrix

| Dimension | Segmentation Type | Variable Examples | Strength | Limitation |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Who they are** | Demographic | Age, income, gender | Easy to measure | Static |
| **Where they are** | Geographic | Region, climate, density | Localization power | Ignores inner traits |
| **How they think** | Psychographic | Lifestyle, values | Deep insight | Hard to measure |
| **How they act** | Behavioral | Usage, loyalty, occasion | Directly actionable | Volatile data |
| **Combined** | Multi-variable | All of above | Holistic | Complex analysis |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Safeguard Checklist
- All node IDs are alphanumeric (e.g., `segStep1`, `baseDemo`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`) used as standalone IDs.
- Node labels are plain uppercase text without markdown formatting.

---

### 4.2 Diagram A — The Market Segmentation Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    segStep1["STEP 1 DEFINE THE BROAD MARKET"] --> segStep2["STEP 2 IDENTIFY SEGMENTATION VARIABLES"]
    segStep2 --> segStep3["STEP 3 DEVELOP SEGMENT PROFILES"]
    segStep3 --> segStep4["STEP 4 APPLY MASDA SCREENING CRITERIA"]
    segStep4 --> segStep5["STEP 5 SELECT TARGET SEGMENTS"]
    segStep5 --> segOut["OUTPUT TARGETED STP STRATEGY"]
    segCrit["MEASURABLE ACCESSIBLE SUBSTANTIAL DIFFERENTIABLE ACTIONABLE"] -.-> segStep4
    segVar["DEMOGRAPHIC GEOGRAPHIC PSYCHOGRAPHIC BEHAVIORAL"] -.-> segStep2
```

---

### 4.3 Diagram B — The Four Bases of Market Segmentation

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    totalMarket["TOTAL HETEROGENEOUS MARKET"] --> baseCore["FOUR BASES OF SEGMENTATION"]
    baseCore --> baseDemo["DEMOGRAPHIC AGE GENDER INCOME"]
    baseCore --> baseGeo["GEOGRAPHIC REGION CLIMATE DENSITY"]
    baseCore --> basePsycho["PSYCHOGRAPHIC LIFESTYLE VALUES PERSONALITY"]
    baseCore --> baseBehav["BEHAVIORAL USAGE LOYALTY OCCASION"]
    baseDemo --> homoSeg["HOMOGENEOUS SEGMENTS READY FOR TARGETING"]
    baseGeo --> homoSeg
    basePsycho --> homoSeg
    baseBehav --> homoSeg
```

---

### 4.4 Diagram C — STP Framework Block Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    stpStart["BROAD MASS MARKET"] --> stpSeg["S SEGMENTATION DIVIDE MARKET INTO HOMOGENEOUS GROUPS"]
    stpSeg --> stpTarg["T TARGETING EVALUATE AND SELECT ATTRACTIVE SEGMENTS"]
    stpTarg --> stpPos["P POSITIONING BUILD DISTINCT VALUE PROPOSITION"]
    stpPos --> stpEnd["DELIVERED MARKETING MIX 4PS PRODUCT PRICE PLACE PROMOTION"]
    stpSeg -. uses .-> stpSegBases["DEMOGRAPHIC GEOGRAPHIC PSYCHOGRAPHIC BEHAVIORAL"]
    stpTarg -. modes .-> stpTargMode["UNDIFFERENTIATED DIFFERENTIATED CONCENTRATED MICRO"]
```

---

### 4.5 Diagram D — MASDA Filtering Funnel (Block Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    funnelIn["CANDIDATE SEGMENTS"] --> measurF["M MEASURABLE CAN WE QUANTIFY"]
    measurF --> accessF["A ACCESSIBLE CAN WE REACH"]
    accessF --> substF["S SUBSTANTIAL IS IT WORTH IT"]
    substF --> diffF["D DIFFERENTIABLE DOES IT RESPOND UNIQUELY"]
    diffF --> actF["AC ACTIONABLE CAN WE ACT ON IT"]
    actF --> funnelOut["SELECTED TARGET SEGMENT"]
    measurF -. fail .-> dropSegA["DROP SEGMENT"]
    accessF -. fail .-> dropSegA
    substF -. fail .-> dropSegA
    diffF -. fail .-> dropSegA
    actF -. fail .-> dropSegA
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**

Define market segmentation. State any **three** essential characteristics of a good market segment.

**Model Answer:**

Market segmentation is the process of dividing a large heterogeneous market into smaller, more manageable sub-groups of consumers with similar needs, characteristics, or behavior, so that each group can be targeted with a distinct marketing mix.

Three characteristics of an effective market segment:

1. **Measurable** — The size, profile, and purchasing power must be quantifiable.
2. **Accessible** — The segment must be reachable through existing distribution and communication channels.
3. **Differentiate** — The segment must respond differently to different marketing-mix elements.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Substantial, Actionable.)*

> [!WARNING]
> Examiners will **not award full marks** if you only write "divisible" or "reachable" without using the exact MASDA vocabulary.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

Distinguish between **concentrated marketing** and **differentiated marketing** strategies with one suitable example each.

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Concentrated Marketing | Differentiated Marketing |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Firm targets a single market segment | Firm targets multiple segments with separate mixes |
| Risk | High (entire business depends on one segment) | Lower (risk spread across segments) |
| Resource Use | Focused, deep penetration | Broad, requires higher budget |
| Example | Rolls-Royce (luxury car niche) | Hindustan Unilever (multiple brands for different segments) |
| Suitability | Small firms / niche players | Large firms with diversified portfolios |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1**

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024, Module 3]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **four major bases of market segmentation** with suitable examples for each. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** Describe the **five-step market segmentation process** and apply it to a Kerala-based organic spice brand *"SpiceRoot Naturals."* *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer to Part (a):**

The four major bases of market segmentation are:

1. **Demographic Segmentation** divides the market on measurable population variables such as **age, gender, income, education, family life-cycle, and religion**.
   - *Example:* A toy company in Kerala selling educational STEM toys targeted at children aged 6–12 years from upper-middle-class urban families in Ernakulam.

2. **Geographic Segmentation** divides the market by **region, climate, population density, and cultural preferences**.
   - *Example:* A winter-wear brand like Woodlands focuses marketing on North Indian metros during winter months and shifts to monsoon-related products in Kerala.

3. **Psychographic Segmentation** divides the market by **lifestyle, values, personality, and social class**, often using the AIO (Activities, Interests, Opinions) framework.
   - *Example:* Royal Enfield targets riders who identify with the *"classic wanderer"* lifestyle, while KTM targets *"adrenaline-sport"* enthusiasts — both within two-wheeler buyers.

4. **Behavioral Segmentation** divides the market by **user status, usage rate, loyalty, occasion, and benefits sought**.
   - *Example:* Airlines segment flyers into business travellers, leisure travellers, and frequent flyers, offering different loyalty programs for each.

---

**Model Answer to Part (b):**

Applying the five-step segmentation process to *"SpiceRoot Naturals"* (premium organic spice brand based in Idukki, Kerala):

1. **Define the Market:** The total market is *"consumers in Kerala and the Middle East (Kerala diaspora) who purchase premium quality organic spices for daily cooking and gifting."*

2. **Identify Segmentation Variables:** Mix of **demographic (income, age), psychographic (health consciousness), and behavioral (usage rate, occasion).**

3. **Develop Segment Profiles:**
   - $S_1$ — Urban health-conscious millennials (age 25–35, high income)
   - $S_2$ — NRIs purchasing as gifting
   - $S_3$ — Boutique restaurants and cafés
   - $S_4$ — Budget-conscious homemakers

4. **Apply MASDA Criteria:**
   - $S_1$ and $S_2$ pass Measurable, Accessible, Substantial, Differentiable, and Actionable.
   - $S_4$ fails *Substantial* for a premium price and *Differentiate* (low willingness to pay premium).

5. **Select Target Segments:** SpiceRoot pursues **concentrated targeting** on $S_1$ and $S_2$ using differentiated brand stories — wellness for millennials, nostalgia for NRIs.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Module 3]**

**(a)** What are the **criteria for effective market segmentation**? Explain the MASDA framework in detail. *(7 marks)*

**(b)** A company is evaluating two potential segments. Segment A has size 2,00,000 customers with an average contribution margin of ₹120 per customer per year. Segment B has size 1,50,000 customers with an average contribution margin of ₹220 per customer per year. Fixed marketing cost allocated to each segment is ₹50,00,000. **Calculate the segment profitability for both segments and recommend the better target.** *(7 marks)*

---

**Model Answer to Part (a):**

The MASDA criteria for effective segmentation are:

- **M — Measurable:** The segment's size, profile, and purchasing power must be quantifiable using market research and data analytics.
- **A — Accessible:** The firm must be able to reach and serve the segment through existing distribution, communication, and sales channels.
- **S — Substantial:** The segment must be large or profitable enough to justify dedicated marketing effort.
- **D — Differentiable:** The segment must respond uniquely to specific marketing-mix levers (4Ps).
- **A — Actionable:** The firm must have the resources and capability to design effective programs targeting the segment.

A segment that fails any one of these five tests should be discarded.

---

**Model Answer to Part (b):**

Segment Profitability formula:

$$
P_i = S_i \times CM_i - FC_i
$$

**For Segment A:**

$$
P_A = 2{,}00{,}000 \times 120 - 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_A = 24{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_A = 23{,}50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_A = \text{₹} 2{,}35{,}00{,}000
$$

**For Segment B:**

$$
P_B = 1{,}50{,}000 \times 220 - 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_B = 33{,}00{,}00{,}000 - 50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_B = 32{,}50{,}00{,}000
$$

$$
P_B = \text{₹} 3{,}25{,}00{,}000
$$

**Comparison:**

$$
\Delta P = P_B - P_A = 3{,}25{,}00{,}000 - 2{,}35{,}00{,}000 = \text{₹} 90{,}00{,}000
$$

**Recommendation:** Segment **B** is **₹90 lakh more profitable** despite having a smaller customer base, because of its higher contribution margin. The company should target Segment B as the primary segment.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Examiner's Pitfall: Students often forget to subtract Fixed Cost and write only $S_i \times CM_i$. This is treated as a **2-mark deduction** in the valuation key. Always show both terms.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Reasons for Losing Marks on Segmentation Questions:**
> 1. Writing *"dividing customers into groups"* without the phrase *"homogeneous within and heterogeneous between"* — KTU answer key deducts **1 mark**.
> 2. Confusing *segmentation* with *targeting* and *positioning* — keep them strictly in **STP order**.
> 3. Listing bases of segmentation without **examples** — KTU 2024 requires at least one real-world example per base for full marks.
> 4. In numericals, omitting the subtraction of fixed cost in profitability equations.
> 5. Using non-standard terminology (e.g., "tribe" or "persona" without defining them) — stick to the textbook MASDA vocabulary.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — Defining Market Segmentation**

- **Definition:** Dividing a heterogeneous market into homogeneous sub-groups with shared needs, allowing differentiated marketing mixes.
- **Origin:** Wendell R. Smith (1956).
- **STP Sequence:** **S**egment → **T**arget → **P**osition. Never reorder.
- **Four Bases:** **D**emographic, **G**eographic, **P**sychographic, **B**ehavioral.
- **MASDA Criteria:** **M**easurable, **A**ccessible, **S**ubstantial, **D**ifferentiable, **A**ctionable.
- **Total Market Equation:** $M_{total} = \sum_{i=1}^{n} S_i$.
- **Segment Profitability:** $P_i = S_i \times CM_i - FC_i$.
- **Segment Share (%):** $SS_i = \dfrac{S_i}{M_{total}} \times 100\%$.
- **CLV Formula:** $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{T} \dfrac{R_t}{(1+d)^t}$.
- **Targeting Modes:** Undifferentiated, Differentiated, Concentrated, Micromarketing.
- **Kerala Context Tip:** Use examples from coconut oil, banana chips, ayurveda, tourism, and IT to score applied marks.
- **Avoid in Exam:** Confusing segmentation with targeting, omitting the homogeneous-within principle, ignoring the MASDA test, and skipping the subtraction of fixed cost in numericals.
- **One-line summary to memorize:** *"Segmentation is the act of finding smart pockets in a noisy market — measured, reachable, profitable, distinct, and actionable."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Targeting and Positioning
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Targeting and Positioning

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Targeting** is the second stage of the **Strategic STP Framework** (Segmentation – Targeting – Positioning), wherein a marketer evaluates each identified market segment, measures its attractiveness, and selects one or more segments to serve. It is the strategic decision of *which customers to pursue* with a tailored marketing mix.

**Positioning** is the third and final stage of the STP framework, wherein a marketer designs a distinct image, identity, and value proposition for the offering in the **target customer's mind**, relative to competing offerings. Positioning is the act of *planting a unique idea* in the consumer's perception so that the brand occupies a meaningful and differentiated slot.

The combined process of Targeting and Positioning is governed by the formula:

$$\text{Customer Value Delivered} = \frac{\text{Perceived Benefits (P)}}{\text{Perceived Costs (C)}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The STP model is the cornerstone of modern marketing strategy. A firm **must** complete Targeting **after** Segmentation and **before** Positioning. Skipping any stage invalidates the entire strategic marketing plan.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy & Intuitive Overview

### 🎯 The "Café Analogy" — Understanding Targeting and Positioning in Plain English

Imagine you own a small café in Kochi city. The city has 2 million people. You **cannot** serve everyone. So what do you do?

**Step 1 — Segmentation:** You notice your city has office workers needing quick coffee, college students wanting cheap Wi-Fi, families looking for evening snacks, and fitness enthusiasts preferring protein shakes. These are 4 natural groups (segments) based on their *needs*.

**Step 2 — Targeting:** You look at your skills, capital, and the competition. Office workers are crowded (too many competitors), students have low budgets, families want a big space you cannot afford. But the **fitness enthusiasts segment is growing 20% annually, has fewer cafés serving it, and customers happily pay ₹150–250 for a drink**. You *select* this segment. **You have now made a Targeting decision.**

**Step 3 — Positioning:** Now you must tell the fitness segment *why* they should come to *your* café instead of any other. You decide: "Kerala's first 100% sugar-free, nutritionist-designed café for active lifestyles." This unique idea is your **positioning**. It is the *one sentence* that lives inside a fitness customer's brain.

> [!NOTE]
> **The 3-Sentence Rule of STP:**
> - **Segmenting** = "I see different groups of customers."
> - **Targeting** = "I have chosen MY group."
> - **Positioning** = "I have decided what MY group will think of me."

---

## 1.3 Key Terminology Grid

| Term | Precise Meaning | KTU Exam Keyword |
|---|---|---|
| **Target Market (TM)** | A set of buyers sharing common needs or characteristics that a firm decides to serve. | "Selected segment" |
| **Differentiated Marketing** | Targeting multiple segments with a different offer for each. | "Multi-segment strategy" |
| **Undifferentiated Marketing** | Targeting the whole market with one offer. | "Mass marketing" |
| **Concentrated Marketing** | Targeting one niche segment with a deep offering. | "Niche marketing" |
| **Micromarketing** | Tailoring offers to individuals or localities. | "Local/individual marketing" |
| **Positioning Statement** | A formal written declaration of how a brand will be perceived. | "Brand promise" |
| **Value Proposition (VP)** | The bundle of benefits and values promised to the target customer. | "Reason to buy" |
| **Points-of-Difference (PODs)** | Attributes that consumers strongly associate with a brand and consider superior. | "Unique strengths" |
| **Points-of-Parity (POPs)** | Attributes that consumers view as "must-have" to even consider the brand. | "Category entry points" |
| **Repositioning** | Changing an existing brand's positioning in response to market shifts. | "Strategic relaunch" |

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU viva:** "**S**ee → **T**arget → **P**lant" — **S**egment the market, **T**arget a group, **P**lant your idea in their mind.

---

## 1.4 The Strategic Logic Flow

The reason Targeting precedes Positioning is mathematical and behavioural:

$$\text{Positioning without Targeting} = \text{Communicating to no one}$$

$$\text{Targeting without Positioning} = \text{Being chosen by no one}$$

Hence the **causal sequence** is strictly:

$$\text{Segmentation} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Targeting} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Positioning} \;\longrightarrow\; \text{Marketing Mix (4 P's)}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Writing "positioning is selecting the market" in an exam. This is **targeting**, not positioning. Examiners specifically dock marks when these two are interchanged. Remember: **Targeting = WHO**, **Positioning = WHAT and HOW in their mind.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Targeting Decision: A 4-Step Operational Framework

**Step 1 — Segment Evaluation (the "Measure of Attractiveness"):**
Each segment must be evaluated on **3 standard KTU criteria**:

1. **Segment Size and Growth** — Is it large enough and growing?
2. **Segment Structural Attractiveness** — Competitive intensity, substitute availability, bargaining power.
3. **Company Objectives and Resources** — Can we win here given our strengths?

**Step 2 — Segment Selection:**
The firm picks one of **4 classical targeting strategies** (Kotler's classification, frequently asked in KTU ESE):

| Strategy | Targets | Offer Type | Risk Level | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Undifferentiated** | Whole market | One standard offer | Low focus, high competition | Commodities (salt, sugar) |
| **Differentiated** | Multiple segments | Different offer per segment | High cost, broad coverage | Large firms (HUL, P&G) |
| **Concentrated** | One niche | Deep, specialised offer | High concentration risk | SMEs, startups |
| **Micromarketing** | Individual / local | Customised offer | High operation cost | Local businesses, luxury |

**Step 3 — Coverage Decision:**
Decide between **Mass Coverage** (undifferentiated) vs **Selective Specialisation** (a few unrelated segments) vs **Market Specialisation** (one segment, all its needs) vs **Full Coverage** (all segments with dedicated offers).

**Step 4 — Ethical and Social Considerations:**
KTU 2024 explicitly tests this. Targeting must avoid **stigmatising** or **exploiting** vulnerable groups. Example: tobacco/alcohol marketing to minors, predatory lending.

---

## 2.2 The Positioning Decision: Building the Mental Slot

Once the target is fixed, the marketer constructs the positioning using **Ries and Trout's classical framework** expanded by Keller:

### 2.2.1 The 6 Positioning Strategies (KTU High-Yield)

| # | Strategy | Core Idea | Real Brand Example |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Attribute Positioning** | Position on a specific feature | Volvo = "Safety" |
| 2 | **Benefit Positioning** | Position on the key delivered benefit | Colgate = "Cavity protection" |
| 3 | **Use/Application Positioning** | Position around a specific use case | Dettol = "Wound antiseptic" |
| 4 | **User Positioning** | Position around the user class | Old Spice = "Masculine grooming" |
| 5 | **Competitor Positioning** | Position against a named rival | Pepsi = "For the young generation" vs Coke |
| 6 | **Category Positioning** | Position as the category leader | Amul = "The Taste of India" |

### 2.2.2 Points-of-Difference (PODs) vs Points-of-Parity (POPs)

This is a **favourite KTU Part A question**:

$$\text{Brand Mental Image} = \{\text{PODs}\} \cup \{\text{POPs}\}$$

- **POPs** = *necessary* attributes (e.g., a shampoo *must* clean hair; a smartphone *must* make calls). Missing these = disqualified from consideration.
- **PODs** = *differentiating* attributes (e.g., shampoo with biotin; smartphone with 200 MP camera). These create the unique reason to choose *this* brand.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner's Note:** A brand can win only when it has **both** sufficient POPs (to be considered) and strong PODs (to be chosen). POPs make you eligible; PODs make you elected.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Framework / Formula | Symbolic Form | Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| Customer Perceived Value (CPV) | $\text{CPV} = \text{Total Customer Benefits} - \text{Total Customer Costs}$ | Measuring value of a positioning |
| Customer Satisfaction | $\text{CS} = f(\text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectations})$ | Post-positioning feedback |
| Market Share within Segment | $\text{MS} = \dfrac{\text{Sales of Brand in Segment}}{\text{Total Segment Sales}} \times 100$ | Evaluating targeting success |
| Segment Attractiveness Score (SAS) | $\text{SAS} = w_1 S + w_2 G + w_3 C - w_4 R$ | Multi-attribute targeting |
| Positioning Differentiation Gap | $\Delta P = \vert P_{\text{brand}} - P_{\text{competitor}} \vert$ | Perceptual distance on a map |
| Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{\text{Cash Flow}_t}{(1+r)^t}$ | Long-run target worth |
| Break-even Targeting Volume | $Q^{\ast} = \dfrac{F}{P - V}$ | Minimum niche size |
| Brand Equity | $\text{BE} = \text{Brand Awareness} \times \text{Perceived Quality} \times \text{Brand Association} \times \text{Brand Loyalty}$ | Strength of positioning |

> Where $S$ = segment size, $G$ = growth, $C$ = competitive intensity, $R$ = risk, $F$ = fixed cost, $P$ = price, $V$ = variable cost, $r$ = discount rate, $w_i$ = weights.

---

## 2.4 Why Targeting and Positioning Matter in Real Marketing

- **Resource Efficiency:** A Keralite start-up cannot outspend Amazon; it must *target* a specific segment (e.g., spice exporters) and *position* uniquely (e.g., AI-driven sourcing).
- **Brand Recall:** Coca-Cola spends billions on **positioning reinforcement** ("Open Happiness") — repetition plants the idea.
- **Pricing Power:** Strong PODs allow **premium pricing** (Apple iPhone — positioned as a status object, not just a phone).
- **Defensive Moat:** Once a brand owns a position (Volvo = safety, Hertz = rent-a-car), competitors must spend enormously to dislodge it.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World KTU Insight:** In Kerala's booming ayurveda and spice markets, many SMEs compete in undifferentiated mode (everyone claims "pure" and "natural"). The firms that adopt **concentrated targeting** (e.g., "Kerala Ayurveda for German tourists") and **sharp positioning** ("Single-heritage, 200-year-old family clinic") capture disproportionate margins.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Process & Evaluation Matrix

## 3.1 The Targeting Evaluation Process (Analytical Derivation)

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to demonstrate a **structured, multi-criteria evaluation** of each segment. Below is the canonical 5-step process with full worked logic.

### Step 1 — Gather Data per Segment
For each candidate segment $i$, collect data on three primary vectors: $\vec{S_i}$ (size & growth), $\vec{A_i}$ (attractiveness), $\vec{F_i}$ (fit with firm).

### Step 2 — Score Each Vector
Use a uniform scale, e.g., $1$ to $10$ for each sub-criterion. Standard weights in academic exercises:

$$w_S = 0.40, \quad w_A = 0.35, \quad w_F = 0.25$$

with $\;w_S + w_A + w_F = 1.00$.

### Step 3 — Compute Segment Attractiveness Index (SAI)

For each segment $i$, the composite index is the weighted sum:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAI}_i &= w_S \cdot \bar{S_i} + w_A \cdot \bar{A_i} + w_F \cdot \bar{F_i} \\
&= 0.40 \cdot \bar{S_i} + 0.35 \cdot \bar{A_i} + 0.25 \cdot \bar{F_i}
\end{aligned}
$$

Where $\bar{S_i}$, $\bar{A_i}$, $\bar{F_i}$ are the average sub-criterion scores (each normalised to 10).

### Step 4 — Rank Segments
Sort segments by SAI in descending order. The top segments are candidate targets.

### Step 5 — Apply the "STRE Rule" — Final Filter
KTU 2024 emphasises filtering by 4 gates:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Decision} &= \begin{cases}
\text{INVEST}, & \text{if SAI} \geq 7 \text{ and STR\_checks pass} \\
\text{HARVEST}, & \text{if SAI} \in [5, 7) \\
\text{DIVEST}, & \text{if SAI} < 5
\end{cases}
\end{aligned}
$$

**STR checks** = Size, Trend (growth), Reachability (accessible), and Resource-fit (firm can serve).

---

## 3.2 Worked Example — A KTU-Style Numerical Illustration

**Problem.** A Kerala-based ayurvedic cosmetics firm has identified 4 segments. Scores (out of 10) are:

| Segment | $\bar{S}$ (Size/Growth) | $\bar{A}$ (Attractiveness) | $\bar{F}$ (Firm Fit) |
|---|---|---|---|
| Premium urban women | 8 | 7 | 6 |
| Tier-2 working women | 7 | 6 | 8 |
| Ayurvedic tourists | 6 | 9 | 7 |
| Rural mass market | 9 | 4 | 3 |

**Solution (step-by-step, fully explicit):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAI}_{\text{Premium urban}} &= 0.40(8) + 0.35(7) + 0.25(6) \\
&= 3.20 + 2.45 + 1.50 \\
&= 7.15
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAI}_{\text{Tier-2 working}} &= 0.40(7) + 0.35(6) + 0.25(8) \\
&= 2.80 + 2.10 + 2.00 \\
&= 6.90
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAI}_{\text{Tourists}} &= 0.40(6) + 0.35(9) + 0.25(7) \\
&= 2.40 + 3.15 + 1.75 \\
&= 7.30
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{SAI}_{\text{Rural mass}} &= 0.40(9) + 0.35(4) + 0.25(3) \\
&= 3.60 + 1.40 + 0.75 \\
&= 5.75
\end{aligned}
$$

**Ranking:** Tourists (7.30) > Premium urban (7.15) > Tier-2 working (6.90) > Rural mass (5.75).

**Final Targeting Decision:** Apply the STRE rule:
- **Tourists (SAI = 7.30):** All STR checks pass → **INVEST as primary target.**
- **Premium urban (SAI = 7.15):** All STR checks pass → **INVEST as secondary target.**
- **Tier-2 working (SAI = 6.90):** Firm-fit strong but growth moderate → **HARVEST.**
- **Rural mass (SAI = 5.75):** Low attractiveness, poor fit → **DIVEST.**

**Recommended Strategy:** **Differentiated Marketing** — Serve tourists and premium urban women with two distinct offers.

---

## 3.3 The Positioning Process — Worked Step-by-Step

For the chosen target (Tourists), the positioning is constructed in 5 explicit steps:

### Step 1 — Identify the Frame of Reference (FOR)
The frame is the **category** in which the brand competes. For our firm: "Premium Ayurvedic Cosmetics for the Modern Traveller."

### Step 2 — Identify PODs and POPs

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{POPs (must-have)} &= \{\text{authentic herbs, skin-safe, visible effect}\} \\
\text{PODs (differentiators)} &= \{\text{clinically tested, single-origin Kerala herbs, 24-h tour-pack sizes}\}
\end{aligned}
$$

### Step 3 — Establish the Brand Mantra
A 3–5 word internal mantra. Example: *"Authentic. Portable. Pure."*

### Step 4 — Write the Positioning Statement
The classical **Geoffrey-Moore** format:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Positioning Statement} &= \text{For} \;\; \boxed{\text{target}} \;, \\
&\text{our brand is the} \;\; \boxed{\text{concept}} \; \text{of} \;\; \boxed{\text{category}} \\
&\text{that} \;\; \boxed{\text{key benefit}} \;.
\end{aligned}
$$

**Worked instance:**
> *"For international wellness tourists visiting Kerala, our brand is the **clinically authentic** ayurvedic skincare line of the **travel-size premium ayurvedic cosmetics** category that delivers **visible glow in 7 days, with carry-on friendly packaging**."*

### Step 5 — Communicate the Position
Deploy via 4 P's: **Product** (compact kits), **Price** (premium-tier, USD 40–80), **Place** (airport lounges, premium hotels), **Promotion** (Instagram reels, tour-operator tie-ups).

---

## 3.4 The Analytical Matrix — Choosing a Positioning Strategy

| Positioning Strategy | POD Strength Required | Brand Investment | Time to Build | KTU Use-Case Example |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Attribute (e.g., "titanium body") | High | Medium | Short | Smartphones |
| Benefit ("whitens in 3 days") | High | Medium | Short | Toothpaste |
| Use/Application ("for runners") | Medium | Low | Medium | Energy gels |
| User ("for young professionals") | Medium | High | Long | Apparel |
| Competitor ("we are cheaper than X") | High | Medium | Medium | Telecom |
| Category leader ("the original") | Very High | Very High | Very Long | FMCG giants |
| Cultural ("made in Kerala") | Medium | Medium | Medium | Ayurveda brands |
| Repositioning ("new and improved") | High | High | Medium | Legacy brands |

---

## 3.5 Perceptual Map Construction (KTU Examiner's Favourite)

A perceptual map is a **2D visual positioning tool** built on two key dimensions the customer uses for evaluation.

### Construction Procedure:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Axis 1} &= \text{Key attribute 1 (e.g., "Price" — Low to High)} \\
\text{Axis 2} &= \text{Key attribute 2 (e.g., "Naturalness" — Synthetic to Herbal)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 1:** Survey 100+ customers. Ask them to rate each brand on both axes (1–10 scale).

**Step 2:** Compute the mean rating per brand per axis:

$$
\bar{x}_{\text{brand}} = \frac{1}{n}\sum_{k=1}^{n} x_k
$$

**Step 3:** Plot each brand as a single dot on the 2D plane using $(\bar{x}_{\text{Axis1}}, \bar{x}_{\text{Axis2}})$.

**Step 4:** Identify **white space** — empty quadrants where no competitor exists. That is where a **positioning opportunity** lies.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** A perceptual map is **not** built from company opinion. It must reflect **customer perception** as measured by survey data. Internal assumptions lose marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Complete STP Architecture Flow (Mermaid)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Start: Market Universe] --> nodeB[Stage 1: Market Segmentation]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Identify Variables: Demographic, Psychographic, Behavioural, Geographic]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Profile 3 to 6 Distinct Segments]
    nodeD --> nodeE[Stage 2: Segment Evaluation]
    nodeE --> nodeF[Measure Size, Growth, Attractiveness, Fit]
    nodeF --> nodeG[Stage 3: Targeting Decision]
    nodeG --> nodeH{Choose Strategy}
    nodeH -->|Undifferentiated| nodeI[Mass Marketing: One Offer to All]
    nodeH -->|Differentiated| nodeJ[Multi-Segment: Different Offer per Segment]
    nodeH -->|Concentrated| nodeK[Niche Marketing: Deep Offer to One Segment]
    nodeH -->|Micromarketing| nodeL[Local or Individual Customisation]
    nodeI --> nodeM[Stage 4: Positioning]
    nodeJ --> nodeM
    nodeK --> nodeM
    nodeL --> nodeM
    nodeM --> nodeN[Define Frame of Reference]
    nodeN --> nodeO[Identify POPs and PODs]
    nodeO --> nodeP[Write Positioning Statement]
    nodeP --> nodeQ[Craft Brand Mantra]
    nodeQ --> nodeR[Build Perceptual Map]
    nodeR --> nodeS[Stage 5: Implement 4Ps Mix]
    nodeS --> nodeT[Monitor and Reposition if Needed]
    nodeT -->|Trigger Event| nodeG
```

---

## 4.2 Targeting Strategy Selection Matrix (Mermaid Block)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Segment Attractiveness Score] --> nodeB{Score Range}
    nodeB -->|Above 7.0 and STR Pass| nodeC[Primary Target Invest]
    nodeB -->|Between 5.0 and 7.0| nodeD[Secondary Target Harvest]
    nodeB -->|Below 5.0| nodeE[Divest or Reject]
    nodeC --> nodeF[Allocate Maximum Resources]
    nodeD --> nodeG[Maintain with Limited Resources]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Reallocate Budget to Higher SAI Segments]
    nodeF --> nodeI[Build Differentiated Positioning]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Deploy 4P Tactical Mix]
```

---

## 4.3 Positioning Strategy Family Tree (Mermaid Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Positioning Strategies: 8 Canonical Types] --> nodeB[Attribute Based: Volvo Safety]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Benefit Based: Colgate Cavity Protection]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Use Application: Dettol for Wounds]
    nodeA --> nodeE[User Based: Old Spice for Men]
    nodeA --> nodeF[Competitor Based: Pepsi versus Coca Cola]
    nodeA --> nodeG[Category Based: Amul Taste of India]
    nodeA --> nodeH[Quality Price Based: Bata Value for Money]
    nodeA --> nodeI[Cultural Symbol Based: Kerala Ayurveda Heritage]
    nodeB --> nodeJ[Build PODs around Feature]
    nodeC --> nodeK[Build PODs around Functional Benefit]
    nodeD --> nodeL[Build PODs around Context of Use]
    nodeE --> nodeM[Build PODs around User Identity]
    nodeF --> nodeN[Build PODs through Direct Comparison]
    nodeG --> nodeO[Build PODs through Heritage and Leadership]
    nodeH --> nodeP[Build PODs through Value Equation]
    nodeI --> nodeQ[Build PODs through Emotional Roots]
```

---

## 4.4 The Perceptual Map Concept (Mermaid Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeA[Customer Survey Data] --> nodeB[Identify 2 Key Decision Attributes]
    nodeB --> nodeC[Plot Each Brand as a Point]
    nodeC --> nodeD{Are there Empty Quadrants}
    nodeD -->|Yes| nodeE[Positioning White Space Identified]
    nodeD -->|No| nodeF[Saturated Market: Differentiation Required]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Design New Brand for the White Space]
    nodeF --> nodeH[Reposition Existing Brand Closer to Niche]
    nodeG --> nodeI[Validate PODs with Target Customers]
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Launch and Monitor]
```

---

## 4.5 The Value Proposition Canvas (Mermaid Functional Map)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA[Customer Profile] --> nodeB[Jobs to be Done]
    nodeA --> nodeC[Pains Experienced]
    nodeA --> nodeD[Gains Desired]
    nodeE[Value Map] --> nodeF[Products and Services Offered]
    nodeE --> nodeG[Pain Relievers Provided]
    nodeE --> nodeH[Gain Creators Delivered]
    nodeB --> nodeF
    nodeC --> nodeG
    nodeD --> nodeH
    nodeF --> nodeI[Fit Achieved: Strong Positioning]
    nodeG --> nodeI
    nodeH --> nodeI
    nodeI --> nodeJ[Customer Buys and Remains Loyal]
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1.** Define *Targeting* in modern marketing. Differentiate between *Concentrated Marketing* and *Differentiated Marketing* with one example each.  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Q]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Targeting** *(1 mark)*: Targeting is the second stage of the STP process in which a marketer evaluates each market segment on criteria such as size, growth, attractiveness, and fit with company objectives, and then **selects one or more segments** to serve with a tailored marketing mix.

**Concentrated Marketing** *(1 mark)*: The firm commits all its resources to **serve a single niche segment** deeply, seeking a strong competitive position within that narrow group. *Example:* Rolls-Royce serves only the ultra-luxury automobile niche.

**Differentiated Marketing** *(1 mark)*: The firm operates in **several market segments simultaneously**, designing a **separate offer for each**. *Example:* Hindustan Unilever (HUL) markets Lux (premium), Dove (sensitive skin), and Clinic Plus (anti-dandruff) to different segments.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Students often write "targeting means finding customers" — this is incomplete. The **selection decision** (after evaluation) is the heart of targeting. Marks are lost when the *evaluation* aspect is missing.

---

### **Question 2.** What is *Positioning*? Explain the concepts of **Points-of-Parity (POPs)** and **Points-of-Difference (PODs)** with an example.  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Positioning** *(1 mark)*: Positioning is the strategic act of designing a brand's offering and image to **occupy a distinct and valued place in the target customer's mind** relative to competing brands.

**Points-of-Parity (POPs)** *(1 mark)*: Attributes that consumers consider **necessary or category-entry essentials**. A brand must possess them simply to be considered by the customer. *Example:* Any smartphone must have a working camera, calling, and internet capability.

**Points-of-Difference (PODs)** *(1 mark)*: Attributes that consumers **strongly associate with one brand** and consider superior, providing a unique reason to choose it. *Example:* Apple's iPhone has PODs such as the iOS ecosystem, premium design, and seamless Mac/iCloud integration.

**Relationship:** POPs make a brand **eligible** for consideration; PODs make it **the chosen** option.

---

## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

> **KTU Note:** Module 3 generally allows an internal choice. Two alternative question sets are provided below — **Question A** and **Question B**. Each is 14 marks with two sub-parts of 7 marks each.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks).**  `[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]`

> **(a)** Discuss the **four alternative targeting strategies** available to a marketer. Which one would you recommend for a newly launched Kerala-based ayurvedic cosmetics brand, and why?  **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** Construct a **perceptual map** for the South-Indian tea market using *Price* and *Authenticity* as axes. Identify a positioning white space and propose a brand to fill it.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 marks

The four classical targeting strategies, as defined by Philip Kotler, are:

1. **Undifferentiated (Mass) Marketing** *(1 mark)*: The firm ignores segment differences and offers one product and marketing mix to the entire market. *Example:* Early Coca-Cola in the 1900s. **Strength:** economies of scale. **Weakness:** ignores modern consumer diversity.

2. **Differentiated (Segmented) Marketing** *(1 mark)*: The firm targets **multiple segments** with a **distinct offer for each**. *Example:* P&G's Pantene (premium), Head & Shoulders (anti-dandruff), and Clinic Plus (family). **Strength:** higher total sales and brand portfolio. **Weakness:** higher operational cost.

3. **Concentrated (Niche) Marketing** *(1 mark)*: The firm focuses all its energy on **one narrow segment** and pursues a dominant share there. *Example:* Rolls-Royce (ultra-rich). **Strength:** strong loyalty and expertise. **Weakness:** high concentration risk if the niche declines.

4. **Micromarketing (Local / Individual) Marketing** *(1 mark)*: Offers are tailored to **specific individuals or localities**. *Example:* Local Kudumbashree spice kits customised for German tourists. **Strength:** extreme relevance. **Weakness:** costly and difficult to scale.

**Recommendation for the Kerala ayurvedic brand** *(2 marks)*: **Concentrated Marketing** is the best choice because:
- The firm is a new entrant with **limited capital** — it cannot serve multiple segments.
- Kerala's ayurveda brand has a **strong authentic heritage** that can be a powerful POD in a niche such as *international wellness tourists* or *premium urban Keralites*.
- A niche focus enables **deep expertise, premium pricing, and word-of-mouth virality** within a tight community.
- Differentiation from mass-market ayurvedic brands (e.g., Himalaya, Patanjali) becomes meaningful.

**Justification (1 mark):** Once the brand establishes leadership in the niche, it may later *expand* into a differentiated strategy — this is a **sequential STP growth path**.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Defining the axes** *(1 mark)*:
- X-axis: **Price** (Low → High)
- Y-axis: **Authenticity** (Mass-produced → Heritage/Traditional)

**Step 2 — Survey-based placement** *(2 marks)*: Assume 150 customers rate each brand on both axes (1–10). The mean ratings are plotted as a single dot per brand:

| Brand | Price (X) | Authenticity (Y) |
|---|---|---|
| Tata Tea Premium | 3 | 5 |
| Brooke Bond Red Label | 4 | 6 |
| Wagh Bakri | 4 | 7 |
| Kerala Local 'Kannan Devan' | 5 | 9 |
| Organic Kerala Estate Tea | 8 | 10 |
| Lipton Green Tea | 6 | 6 |

**Step 3 — Map the brands** *(1 mark)* — see ASCII illustration:

```
                Authenticity (High)
                          |
                  KD(5,9)  |    OrganicKE(8,10)
                          |
                  WB(4,7)  |
                          |
                  BBRL(4,6)|    Lipton(6,6)
                          |
                  TTP(3,5) |
                          |
                ------------+-------- Price (Low to High)
                          0
```

**Step 4 — Identify white space** *(1 mark)*: The **upper-middle quadrant (moderate price, very high authenticity)** is sparsely populated — except for niche organic brands charging a premium. A gap exists for a **mid-priced, high-authenticity, mass-friendly** positioning.

**Step 5 — Propose a brand** *(2 marks)*: **"Kerala Tapri"** — a packaged tea brand sourced from small Kerala growers, sold at ₹120–180 (mid-premium), with a positioning statement:

> *"For young Indian tea drinkers who miss the taste of grandma's steel tumbler, Kerala Tapri is the **single-estate, hand-plucked Kerala chai** of the mainstream tea category that delivers the **authentic village taste in a modern pouch**."*

This brand's PODs are **single-estate authenticity, transparent farmer story, and mid-price accessibility**; its POPs are **proper brewing, safety certifications, and grocery availability**.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice.**  `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]`

> **(a)** Explain the **6 main positioning strategies** with one Indian brand example for each. How does a marketer choose between them?  **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** A new mobile recharge company wants to target Kerala's migrant workers. Design a **targeting and positioning plan** for it.  **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer — Part (a) — 7 marks

The 6 classical positioning strategies (with Indian brand examples, 1 mark each for first 4, 1 mark for fifth and sixth combined):

1. **Attribute Positioning** — Position on a specific product feature. *Example:* **Volvo** = safety, **Safari** = rugged build.

2. **Benefit Positioning** — Position on the key functional or emotional benefit delivered. *Example:* **Colgate** = cavity protection, **Sensodyne** = relief from sensitivity.

3. **Use/Application Positioning** — Position around a specific occasion or context. *Example:* **Dettol** = antiseptic for cuts, **Vicks VapoRub** = nighttime cold relief.

4. **User Positioning** — Position around the type of user. *Example:* **Old Spice** = masculine grooming, **Nivea for Men** = skincare for males.

5. **Competitor Positioning** — Position explicitly against a rival. *Example:* **Pepsi** = "For the young, choice of a new generation" vs Coca-Cola. (1 mark total for 5 and 6)

6. **Category Positioning** — Position as the leader or pioneer of the category. *Example:* **Amul** = "The Taste of India," **Bajaj** = the original Indian scooter brand.

**How a marketer chooses between them** *(1 mark)*:
- **Stage of brand lifecycle:** New brands often use category or benefit positioning; mature brands use competitor positioning.
- **Customer decision driver:** If customers choose on *price*, use quality-price positioning; if on *identity*, use user positioning.
- **Competitive intensity:** Saturated markets demand competitor or user positioning.
- **Brand's actual PODs:** The chosen strategy must be supported by **genuine, deliverable** differentiation — empty claims fail.

#### Model Answer — Part (b) — 7 marks

**Targeting Plan** *(3.5 marks)*:

1. **Identify the segment** *(1 mark)*: Kerala's migrant workers from Bihar, West Bengal, Assam, Tamil Nadu. Approximately 3–5 lakh migrant workers, with home-call frequency 2–4 times a month, average recharge value ₹200–500, and strong demand for **calling home + data for messaging apps**.

2. **Evaluate attractiveness** *(1 mark)*:
   - Size: large and growing.
   - Growth: high (Kerala's labour demand rising).
   - Attractiveness: moderate — competition from Jio, Airtel, BSNL is fierce, but the segment is **underserved in language, pricing, and family plans**.

3. **Choose targeting strategy** *(1 mark)*: **Concentrated / Niche Marketing** focused exclusively on the migrant worker segment.

4. **Reach strategy** *(0.5 mark)*: Partner with worksite kiosks, labour contractors, and regional transport hubs (railway stations, KSRTC bus stands).

**Positioning Plan** *(3.5 marks)*:

1. **Brand name suggestion:** **"Pravasi Connect"** or **"GharSe"**.

2. **Value Proposition** *(1 mark)*: *"Call home in your bhasha, at half the rate."*

3. **POPs** *(0.5 mark)*: Pan-India calling, valid for 28 days, basic data, customer care in Hindi/Bengali/Tamil.

4. **PODs** *(0.5 mark)*: **Multilingual IVR (Hindi, Bengali, Tamil, Odia)**, **family bundles valid across Kerala and home states**, **recharge at any worksite agent with no app required**.

5. **Positioning Statement** *(1 mark)*:

> *"For migrant workers earning daily wages in Kerala, GharSe is the most affordable **regional-language mobile recharge** of the telecom category that delivers **home-state calling rates starting at 9 paise per second, no app needed, multilingual support**."*

6. **Marketing Mix** *(0.5 mark)*:
   - **Product:** ₹99 weekly pack with unlimited home-state calls.
   - **Price:** Aggressively low (below Jio's similar packs).
   - **Place:** Worksite agents, ration shops, small kirana outlets.
   - **Promotion:** Wall paintings at labour camps, regional-language radio jingles, partnerships with unions.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warnings & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Reasons Students Lose Marks on Targeting & Positioning Questions:**
>
> 1. **Interchanging Targeting and Positioning definitions** — *Targeting = WHO, Positioning = WHAT/HOW in mind.* Examiners deduct 1–2 marks immediately.
> 2. **No evaluation criteria in targeting** — A 14-mark answer must mention size, growth, attractiveness, and fit. Skipping evaluation reduces it to a textbook recital.
> 3. **Positioning statement in wrong format** — The Geoffrey-Moore format is expected: *"For [target], [brand] is the [concept] of [category] that [key benefit]."*
> 4. **Ignoring POPs and PODs** — These are *essential* for full credit. An answer without PODs/POPs is incomplete.
> 5. **No real-world example** — KTU examiners reward at least 1 Indian brand example. Generic theory loses marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember
