<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1.** [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Define the term 'Integrated Marketing Channels'. Distinguish between Multi-channel, Cross-channel, and Omnichannel marketing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):** Integrated Marketing Channels (IMC) is the strategic design, coordination, and management of multiple distribution channels to deliver a unified, seamless customer experience across all touchpoints, both online and offline.

**Distinction (2 marks):**

| Aspect | Multi-channel | Cross-channel | Omnichannel |
|:-------|:--------------|:--------------|:-------------|
| **Channel Co-ordination** | Independent operation | Coordinated, partial integration | Fully integrated |
| **Customer View** | Channel-specific | Channel-aware | Single unified identity |
| **Data Sharing** | Minimal or none | Partial sharing | Real-time, unified |
| **Example** | Brand has website + retail store separately | Inventory shared between online and offline | Customer can start on mobile, finish in store seamlessly |

> Multi-channel is **additive**, Cross-channel is **coordinated**, Omnichannel is **fully unified**.

---

### **Q2.** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Explain any three functions performed by marketing channels.**

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Transactional Function:** Purchasing, selling, and risk-bearing — intermediaries buy in bulk, store inventory, and assume risk of price fluctuations, freeing the manufacturer.
2. **Logistical Function:** Storage, warehousing, transportation, and order processing — ensuring the right product reaches the right place at the right time.
3. **Facilitating Function:** Financing, market information, and promotion — wholesalers and retailers extend credit to customers, gather market intelligence, and promote the product locally.

*(Other valid functions: Sorting, Grading, Assortment building, Risk-bearing, Information flow.)*

---

## 5.2 PART B — Module-3 Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Do **not** write only a definition. KTU 2024 valuations award 4–5 marks for a *real-world corporate example* (e.g., Amul, HUL, Amazon).
> - For channel-conflict answers, students commonly lose 3 marks by failing to **classify the type of conflict (vertical/horizontal/multichannel)** before giving solutions.
> - Always **conclude with a recommendation** — a model answer without a "best choice" decision is treated as incomplete (–1 mark).
> - Use **diagrams (even sketches)** wherever possible — the examiner awards an extra mark for visual clarity in channel-related questions.

---

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Pattern]

> **"Channels of distribution are the pipelines through which the company delivers value." Discuss the design of marketing channels. Explain the various channel design steps with reference to a consumer-electronics company launching a premium smartphone in India.** $(14)$

#### **(a) Concept of Marketing Channel Design (7 marks)**

Marketing Channel Design is the strategic decision-making process by which a firm identifies, evaluates, and selects the most effective pathway to make its products available to target customers. The objective is to align **customer service-output needs** with **company objectives** and **channel constraints**.

**The 5-Step Channel Design Framework:**

**Step 1 — Analyze Customer Service Output Needs** *(2 marks)*
The firm must first identify the bundle of services customers want: lot size, waiting time, spatial convenience, product variety, service backup, and information. For a premium smartphone, customers expect *high information, demonstration, after-sales service, and EMI options*.

**Step 2 — Establish Channel Objectives and Constraints** *(1.5 marks)*
Objectives: wide market coverage, brand-aligned retail experience, tight quality control. Constraints: high dealer margins, limited exclusive tie-ups, capital constraints on exclusive showrooms.

**Step 3 — Identify Channel Alternatives** *(1.5 marks)*
Type: Direct (own website, brand stores) vs. Indirect (Amazon, Croma, Reliance Digital). Number: Exclusive (one retailer per city) vs. Selective (3–5 retailers per city). Terms: dealer margin, exclusivity, return-policy.

**Step 4 — Evaluate Alternatives on Economic, Adaptive, and Control Criteria** *(1 mark)*
- *Economic:* Profit per channel = Sales – Channel Cost.
- *Adaptive:* Channel's ability to adapt to e-commerce shift.
- *Control:* Degree of brand control over pricing, customer experience.

**Step 5 — Design the Channel** *(1 mark)*
Final structure: Combination of exclusive brand stores + selective premium multi-brand retailers + D2C website.

#### **(b) Channel Intensity, VMS, and IMC Application (7 marks)**

**Channel Intensity (1.5 marks):**
For a premium smartphone, **Selective Distribution** (3–5 outlets per major city) is most appropriate because:
- High service-output requirement (demo, fitting, EMI).
- Brand-building requires consistent retail experience.
- Wide coverage but not every kirana store.

**Vertical Marketing System (2 marks):**
The company should adopt a **Corporate + Contractual Hybrid VMS**:
- **Corporate VMS:** Own brand stores (e.g., Apple Store) for flagship experience.
- **Contractual VMS:** Authorized dealer agreements with Croma, Reliance Digital.
- **Administered VMS:** Power-driven relationship with Amazon as preferred online partner.

**IMC and Omnichannel Integration (2.5 marks):**
- **Unified inventory:** Online order can be picked up from a brand store.
- **Single customer view:** CRM integrates purchase history across website, app, and physical store.
- **Click-and-collect, hyper-local delivery** via integrated logistics.
- **Consistent pricing** across all touchpoints (no channel cannibalization).

**Real-World Example (1 mark):**
Apple Inc. operates a *Corporate VMS* (Apple Stores) combined with *Selective* premium resellers and a *direct* D2C channel — the gold standard of omnichannel integrated marketing.

**Conclusion:**
A premium smartphone requires **selective, integrated, omnichannel distribution** that balances reach with brand experience. *(+0.5 for visual diagram of the channel)*

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Pattern]

> **"Channel conflict is a serious threat to integrated distribution." Explain the types of channel conflict with examples. Discuss the strategies to resolve them, with a focus on managing multichannel conflict in e-commerce.** $(14)$

#### **(a) Types of Channel Conflict (7 marks)**

Channel conflict arises when channel members' behaviour is at odds with the manufacturer's goals, harming system efficiency.

**1. Vertical Conflict (2 marks):**
Disagreement between **different levels** of the same channel (e.g., manufacturer vs. wholesaler vs. retailer). *Example:* A car manufacturer launching a low-priced model upsets existing dealers who fear margin erosion.

**2. Horizontal Conflict (2 marks):**
Disagreement between **same-level members** of the channel. *Example:* Two Maruti dealers in the same city undercutting each other's price to attract customers, leading to territory war.

**3. Multichannel Conflict (2 marks):**
Occurs when a manufacturer uses **multiple channels that compete** for the same customer. *Example:* A brand selling directly on its website (₹1,000) while the same product is in a multi-brand retail store at ₹1,200 — the retailer feels betrayed.

**4. Goal and Perception Conflict (1 mark):**
Manufacturers chase market share; retailers chase margin — leading to fundamentally misaligned strategies.

> *Real-world illustration:* Apple vs. its authorized resellers during iPhone launches — resellers complain that Apple's online store undercuts them.

#### **(b) Strategies to Manage and Resolve Channel Conflict (7 marks)**

**1. Strategic Justification & Channel Segmentation (2 marks):**
Define each channel's *role* clearly. Online = direct customer acquisition; Retail = brand experience & after-sales; Wholesale = mass reach. Differentiation eliminates head-to-head rivalry.

**2. Dual Compensation and Channel Profit Protection (1.5 marks):**
When a sale is migrated from a retail partner to D2C, compensate the original partner with a "channel migration fee" or commission. Example: Many automobile firms now pay dealers a fixed "delivery fee" even for online-booked customers.

**3. Pricing Parity and MAP (Minimum Advertised Price) Policy (1.5 marks):**
Enforce a uniform price floor across all channels. e.g., Sony TVs maintain MRP parity between Amazon, Croma, and own website.

**4. Joint Business Planning and VMI (1 mark):**
Producer and retailer jointly plan promotions, inventory, and SKU mix. Vendor-Managed Inventory (VMI) makes the retailer a partner, not a competitor.

**5. Cooperative Communication and PRM (1 mark):**
**Partner Relationship Management (PRM)** systems share data and forecasts across channel partners, improving mutual trust. Example: HUL's "Shikhar" portal integrates distributors digitally.

**Conclusion:** Channel conflict is *natural* in any multi-level system but can be converted into a competitive advantage through clear segmentation, fair compensation, and digital integration. *(+0.5 for any supporting diagram or model)*

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Know for KTU Module 3:**

- **Definition:** A marketing channel is a set of interdependent organizations making a product available to consumers. IMC integrates these channels for a unified experience.
- **8 Marketing Flows** (Stern & Ansary): Product, Ownership, Payment, Information, Promotion, Place, Risk-bearing, Negotiation.
- **Channel Levels** = 0, 1, 2, 3 — direct to three-level.
- **6 Service Outputs** (Kotler): Lot size, waiting time, spatial convenience, variety, service backup, information.
- **Intensity Types:** Intensive, Selective, Exclusive — chosen by product positioning and customer purchase frequency.
- **Channel Design Steps:** Customer needs → Objectives → Identify alternatives → Evaluate → Design.
- **3 VMS Types:** Corporate, Contractual, Administered.
- **3 IMC Models:** Multi-channel → Cross-channel → Omnichannel (the integration ladder).
- **3 Conflict Types:** Vertical, Horizontal, Multichannel (+ Goal/Perception).
- **Conflict Resolution:** Segmentation, compensation, MAP, joint planning, PRM.
- **Key Formulas:**
  - $Q_{\text{BE}} = \dfrac{F_D - F_I}{(V_I + M) - V_D}$
  - $\text{MP} = \dfrac{\text{Customers Reached}}{\text{Total Target Customers}} \times 100\%$
  - $\text{DE} = \dfrac{\text{Net Sales}}{\text{Total Distribution Cost}} \times 100\%$
- **2024 Outcome Alignment:** CO3 (Channel Strategy Design) and CO4 (Integrated Marketing Application) at Apply / Analyze levels.
- **Golden Real-World Examples to Quote:** Apple (Corporate VMS + Omnichannel), HUL (Administered VMS), Amul (Cooperative/Contractual VMS), Amazon (Disintermediation).
- **Common Pitfall:** Students confuse *multi-channel* with *omnichannel* — multi-channel is parallel operation; omnichannel is unified integration.
- **Examiner's Favourite Quote:** *"Channels are not just delivery systems; they are competitive weapons."* — Philip Kotler.

> **END OF MODULE 3 NOTES — Managing Integrated Marketing Channels**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Managing Retailing, Wholesaling and Logistics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Managing Retailing, Wholesaling and Logistics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Retailing — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Retailing** is the set of business activities that adds value to the products and services sold to consumers for their personal or household use. It is the **final stage** of the distribution channel where merchandise is sold in small quantities directly to the **end consumer (B2C)**.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, retailing is not merely a "shop" — it is a **strategic marketing function** that combines **place, time, possession, and form utilities** to deliver the right product to the right customer at the right moment.

### Key Retail Formats (KTU Classified)

| Format | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Speciality Store** | Narrow product line with deep assortment | Bata, Titan |
| **Department Store** | Wide variety, multiple departments | Lifestyle, Shoppers Stop |
| **Supermarket** | Large, low-cost, self-service, food/grocery focus | BigBasket (offline), More |
| **Convenience Store** | Small, long hours, neighbourhood | 24 Seven, ATB |
| **Discount Store** | Sells at lower prices, higher volume | D-Mart, Big Bazaar |
| **Category Killer** | Huge assortment in one category | Croma, HomeTown |
| **E-Tailer** | Pure online retail | Amazon, Flipkart |
| **Omni-Channel Retailer** | Integrates online + offline seamlessly | Reliance Retail, Tata CLiQ |

---

## 1.2 Wholesaling — Formal Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Wholesaling** encompasses all transactions involved in the sale of goods and services to **business customers (B2B)** — retailers, industrial users, institutional buyers — who resell, use, or rent them to make a profit. **Wholesalers do not deal with end consumers in significant volume.**

The "merchant middleman" function distinguishes a wholesaler from a retailer.

### Intuitive Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Analogy: The Restaurant Supply Chain 🍽️**
> Think of the marketing channel like a **restaurant's supply line**:
> - **Farmer (Producer)** grows tomatoes.
> - **Wholesaler (Wholesale Market)** buys truckloads from farmers, sorts, stores, breaks bulk into 5 kg crates.
> - **Retailer (Vegetable Vendor / BigBazaar)** buys those crates, splits into 1 kg packs for the homemaker.
> - **Logistics (Cold-chain truck / Cold storage)** is the refrigerated lorry that physically moves the produce.
> - **End Consumer (Homemaker)** finally cooks the curry.
>
> Without the wholesaler, the farmer cannot reach thousands of small vendors. Without logistics, the tomatoes rot. Without the retailer, the homemaker cannot buy 200g. **Each layer adds value.**

---

## 1.3 Logistics — Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Logistics** is the part of the supply chain process that **plans, implements, and controls the efficient, effective flow and storage of goods, services, and related information** from the point of origin to the point of consumption in order to meet customer requirements.
> — *Adapted from the Council of Supply Chain Management Professionals (CSCMP)*

In KTU parlance, logistics is the **operational backbone** of physical distribution. It includes:

- **Transportation** (movement)
- **Warehousing & Storage** (holding)
- **Inventory Management** (stocking)
- **Order Processing** (information)
- **Materials Handling** (in-warehouse movement)
- **Protective Packaging** (preservation)

---

## 1.4 The Marketing Channel — Integrated View

The **Marketing Channel (Distribution Channel)** is the set of interdependent organizations that help make a product or service available for use or consumption by the consumer or business user.

> [!NOTE]
> **Channel Length Terminology (KTU 2024):**
> - **Zero-level / Direct Channel:** Producer → Consumer (e.g., Amul direct, D2C brands like Mamaearth)
> - **One-level:** Producer → Retailer → Consumer
> - **Two-level:** Producer → Wholesaler → Retailer → Consumer
> - **Three-level:** Producer → Wholesaler → Jobber → Retailer → Consumer
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Retailing — The 4 P's of Retail Marketing (Retail Mix)

The **Retail Mix** is the tactical toolkit a retailer uses to attract customers. It is essentially the **4 P's adapted for retail operations**.

| Element | Components | Strategic Question |
|---|---|---|
| **Product** | Assortment, Width, Depth, Quality, Brand Mix | "What do we sell?" |
| **Price** | Pricing strategy, Markdowns, Psychological pricing | "How do we charge?" |
| **Place** | Location, Store layout, Atmospherics, E-tail site | "Where do we sell?" |
| **Promotion** | Advertising, Sales promotion, Personal selling, CRM | "How do we communicate?" |
| **Personnel** | Sales staff training, Service standards | "Who serves the customer?" |
| **Presentation** | Visual merchandising, Packaging | "How is it displayed?" |

### 2.1.1 Key Retail Strategic Decisions

1. **Target Market** — Whom do we serve? (Demographic, psychographic)
2. **Retail Format** — What type of store?
3. **Location & Site Selection** — Central Business District, Suburban, Mall?
4. **Merchandise Assortment & Depth** — Breadth vs. Depth trade-off
5. **Procurement & Sourcing** — Direct vs. Through wholesalers
6. **Pricing & Credit Policy**
7. **In-store Atmosphere & Experience Design**
8. **Customer Service & Complaint Handling**

### 2.1.2 Modern Retail Trends (KTU 2024 Highlight)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasises these **contemporary retail formats and trends**:
> - **Omnichannel Retailing** — Seamless integration of physical + digital
> - **Experiential Retailing** — Apple Store, Nike House of Innovation
> - **Phygital Retail** — Blend of physical and digital experiences
> - **M-Commerce & Q-Commerce (Quick Commerce)** — Zepto, Blinkit 10-min delivery
> - **AI-Powered Personalisation** — Recommendation engines
> - **Sustainable / Green Retailing** — Eco-friendly packaging, zero-waste stores
> - **Retail Analytics & Big Data** — Customer heat maps, basket analysis

---

## 2.2 Wholesaling — Deep Theoretical Framework

### 2.2.1 Functions of Wholesalers (Value Addition)

> [!TIP]
> Wholesalers add **four utilities**: Time, Place, Quantity (Bulk-breaking), and Assortment. They are essentially **economy creators** in the channel.

| Function Category | Specific Function | Value Added |
|---|---|---|
| **Transactional** | Buying & Selling | Risk-bearing |
| **Transactional** | Bulk-breaking | Small quantities to retailers |
| **Transactional** | Assortment building | One-stop for retailers |
| **Logistical** | Warehousing | Time utility |
| **Logistical** | Transportation | Place utility |
| **Facilitating** | Financing | Credit to retailers |
| **Facilitating** | Market information | Feedback to producers |
| **Facilitating** | Risk-bearing | Holds inventory risk |
| **Facilitating** | Promotion support | Trade promotions |

### 2.2.2 Types of Wholesalers (KTU Classification)

| Type | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Merchant Wholesalers** | Take title to goods | ITC Agri Business |
| **Full-Service Wholesalers** | Offer full range of services | C&F agents |
| **Limited-Service Wholesalers** | Cash-and-carry, Drop-shippers | Metro Cash & Carry |
| **Agents & Brokers** | Do not take title | Insurance brokers |
| **Manufacturers' Sales Branches** | Owned by manufacturer | Samsung distribution |
| **Retailers' Cooperatives** | Owned by retailers | Amul Co-operative |

### 2.2.3 Wholesaling — Strategic Shift (Disintermediation)

> [!WARNING]
> **Disintermediation** — The elimination of wholesalers by producers selling directly to retailers/consumers via e-commerce. **Example:** Many FMCG brands (HUL, P&G) bypass wholesalers and sell directly to modern retail chains or via Amazon/Flipkart. This is a **high-weight KTU exam topic**.

---

## 2.3 Logistics & Physical Distribution — Analytical Core

### 2.3.1 The Marketing Logistics System (Phlips Bowersox Framework)

The **total cost of logistics** is the sum of:

$$\text{Total Logistics Cost (TLC)} = T + W + I + O + H + P$$

where each variable is defined in the table below.

### 2.3.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Quantitative Toolkit)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains **all KTU-expected quantitative formulas** for Module 3. Master these for both 3-mark and 14-mark numerical questions.

| # | Formula / Metric | Equation | Units / Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Inventory Turnover (Stockturn Rate)** | $\text{IT} = \dfrac{\text{Cost of Goods Sold (COGS)}}{\text{Average Inventory at Cost}}$ | Times per year. Higher = faster movement. |
| 2 | **GMROI (Gross Margin Return on Investment)** | $\text{GMROI} = \text{Gross Margin \%} \times \text{Inventory Turnover}$ | Money earned per ₹1 invested in inventory. |
| 3 | **Sell-Through Rate** | $\text{STR} = \dfrac{\text{Units Sold}}{\text{Units Received}} \times 100$ | Retail performance measure. |
| 4 | **Stock-to-Sales Ratio** | $\text{SSR} = \dfrac{\text{Average Stock at Retail}}{\text{Sales}}$ | Months of supply. |
| 5 | **Weeks of Supply** | $\text{Weeks of Supply} = \dfrac{\text{Average Inventory}}{\text{Weekly Sales}}$ | Inventory duration. |
| 6 | **Reorder Point (ROP)** | $\text{ROP} = (\text{Average Daily Usage} \times \text{Lead Time}) + \text{Safety Stock}$ | Units to trigger new order. |
| 7 | **Safety Stock** | $\text{SS} = Z \times \sigma_{dL T}$ | Buffer against demand variability. |
| 8 | **EOQ (Economic Order Quantity)** | $\text{EOQ} = \sqrt{\dfrac{2 D S}{H}}$ | Optimal order quantity (Wilson model). |
| 9 | **Freight Cost per Unit** | $\text{FCPU} = \dfrac{\text{Total Freight Cost}}{\text{Units Shipped}}$ | Logistics efficiency metric. |
| 10 | **Warehouse Utilisation** | $\text{WU \%} = \dfrac{\text{Used Cubic Space}}{\text{Total Cubic Space}} \times 100$ | Space efficiency. |
| 11 | **Order Cycle Time** | $\text{OCT} = t_{order} + t_{process} + t_{pick} + t_{pack} + t_{ship}$ | End-to-end fulfilment time. |
| 12 | **Distribution Cost as % of Sales** | $\text{DC \%} = \dfrac{\text{Total Distribution Cost}}{\text{Net Sales}} \times 100$ | Logistics cost efficiency. |
| 13 | **Direct Product Profit (DPP)** | $\text{DPP} = \text{Gross Margin} - \text{Direct Expenses}$ | True per-product profitability. |
| 14 | **Vehicle Utilisation** | $\text{VU \%} = \dfrac{\text{Loaded KM}}{\text{Total KM Available}} \times 100$ | Fleet productivity. |

> [!NOTE]
> **CRITICAL LaTeX rule reminder:** In the formula sheet, we use `\times` and `\dfrac` (not `|`) inside table cells to avoid breaking Markdown table parsing. **For absolute-value notations in exam answers, use $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$.**

### 2.3.3 Transportation Modes — Trade-off Matrix

| Mode | Cost | Speed | Reliability | Flexibility | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Road (Trucks)** | Medium | Medium | High | Very High | Short-medium haul, FMCG |
| **Rail** | Low | Medium | High | Low | Bulk, coal, grains |
| **Air** | Very High | Very High | Very High | High | High-value, perishables, urgent |
| **Water (Shipping)** | Very Low | Very Slow | Medium | Low | International bulk |
| **Pipeline** | Low (after setup) | Continuous | Very High | Very Low | Oil, gas |
| **Intermodal** | Variable | Variable | High | High | Long-haul containers |

### 2.3.4 Warehousing — Strategic Types

- **Storage Warehouses** — Long-term holding (grain silos)
- **Distribution Centres (DCs)** — Cross-docking, fast turnaround
- **Fulfilment Centres** — E-commerce hub-and-spoke (Amazon FCs)
- **Bonded Warehouses** — Customs-controlled, duty-deferred
- **Automated Warehouses** — AS/RS systems, robotic picking
- **Consolidation Warehouses** — Combine small shipments for LTL savings

---

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering / Management Utility

These models are used by:

> [!TIP]
> - **FMCG giants** (HUL, Nestlé) for inventory turnover & GMROI
> - **E-commerce players** (Amazon, Flipkart) for warehouse utilisation & last-mile route optimisation
> - **Cold-chain logistics** (Snowman, ColdEx) for perishable transport
> - **Retailers** (Reliance Retail, DMart) for sell-through & weeks of supply
> - **3PL firms** (Delhivery, DHL) for fleet utilisation & freight cost
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations, Worked Problems & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This section uses **exhaustive, step-by-step derivations** as per KTU valuation standards. Every algebraic transition is shown; no step is skipped.

---

## 3.1 Derivation: From First Principles — The Economic Order Quantity (EOQ) Model

### 3.1.1 Setting Up the Problem

A retailer must decide **how much to order each time**. If order quantity (Q) is too large, holding (carrying) cost rises. If Q is too small, ordering cost rises (more orders placed).

### 3.1.2 Defining the Variables

Let:
- $D$ = Annual demand in units
- $S$ = Ordering cost per order (₹)
- $H$ = Holding cost per unit per year (₹)
- $Q$ = Order quantity (units)

### 3.1.3 Deriving Total Cost Function

**Number of orders per year** = $\dfrac{D}{Q}$

**Annual ordering cost** = $\dfrac{D}{Q} \times S$

**Average inventory** = $\dfrac{Q}{2}$ (assuming instantaneous replenishment)

**Annual holding cost** = $\dfrac{Q}{2} \times H$

**Total Annual Cost (TAC):**

$$
\text{TAC} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q} + \frac{Q \cdot H}{2}
$$

### 3.1.4 Minimising TAC — First-Order Condition

Differentiate TAC with respect to $Q$ and set to zero:

$$
\frac{d(\text{TAC})}{dQ} = -\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}} + \frac{H}{2} = 0
$$

### 3.1.5 Solving for Q*

$$
\frac{H}{2} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{2}}
$$

$$
Q^{2} = \frac{2 D S}{H}
$$

$$
\boxed{Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}}}
$$

### 3.1.6 Why the Minimum? (Second-Order Test)

$$
\frac{d^{2}(\text{TAC})}{dQ^{2}} = \frac{2 D S}{Q^{3}} > 0
$$

This is positive → **TAC is minimised** at $Q^{*}$. Verified.

---

## 3.2 Worked Problem 1 — Inventory Turnover & GMROI

> **Problem:** A departmental store has annual **COGS = ₹80,00,000**. Average inventory at cost is **₹10,00,000**. The gross margin is **25%**. Calculate **Inventory Turnover** and **GMROI**.

### Step 1 — Inventory Turnover

$$
\text{IT} = \frac{\text{COGS}}{\text{Average Inventory at Cost}} = \frac{80{,}00{,}000}{10{,}00{,}000}
$$

$$
\text{IT} = 8 \text{ times per year}
$$

**Interpretation:** Inventory is sold and replaced **8 times a year**, i.e., roughly every **45 days** ($365/8$).

### Step 2 — GMROI

$$
\text{GMROI} = \text{Gross Margin \%} \times \text{IT} = 0.25 \times 8
$$

$$
\text{GMROI} = 2.0 \; \text{or} \; ₹2 \text{ per ₹1 invested}
$$

**Interpretation:** For every ₹1 locked in inventory, the store generates ₹2 in gross margin.

---

## 3.3 Worked Problem 2 — Reorder Point with Safety Stock

> **Problem:** A bookstore has an average daily demand of **120 books** with a standard deviation of **25 books**. Lead time is **8 days**. The service level is **95%** (Z = 1.65). Calculate the **Reorder Point**.

### Step 1 — Demand During Lead Time (Mean)

$$
\mu_{dLT} = \text{Average Daily Usage} \times \text{Lead Time} = 120 \times 8 = 960 \text{ books}
$$

### Step 2 — Standard Deviation During Lead Time

Assuming independent daily demand:

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = \sigma_{d} \times \sqrt{LT} = 25 \times \sqrt{8}
$$

$$
\sigma_{dLT} = 25 \times 2.828 = 70.71 \text{ books}
$$

### Step 3 — Safety Stock

$$
\text{SS} = Z \times \sigma_{dLT} = 1.65 \times 70.71
$$

$$
\text{SS} = 116.67 \approx 117 \text{ books}
$$

### Step 4 — Reorder Point

$$
\boxed{\text{ROP} = 960 + 117 = 1077 \text{ books}}
$$

---

## 3.4 Worked Problem 3 — EOQ Numerical

> **Problem:** A retailer's annual demand for a product is **10,000 units**. Ordering cost = **₹200 per order**. Holding cost = **₹5 per unit per year**. Find the **EOQ**, **number of orders**, and **total cost**.

### Step 1 — EOQ

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10{,}000 \times 200}{5}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{800{,}000} = 894.43 \approx 894 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 2 — Number of Orders per Year

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{10{,}000}{894} \approx 11.19 \approx 12 \text{ orders per year}
$$

### Step 3 — Total Annual Cost

$$
\text{TAC} = \frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{*}} + \frac{Q^{*} \cdot H}{2}
$$

$$
\text{TAC} = \frac{10{,}000 \times 200}{894} + \frac{894 \times 5}{2}
$$

$$
\text{TAC} = 2237.13 + 2235.00
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{TAC} \approx ₹4472}
$$

---

## 3.5 Worked Problem 4 — Warehouse Utilisation

> **Problem:** A distribution centre has a total cubic capacity of **50,000 m³**. Used cubic space = **38,000 m³**. Calculate utilisation.

$$
\text{WU \%} = \frac{38{,}000}{50{,}000} \times 100 = 76\%
$$

**Interpretation:** 24% space is unutilised — opportunity for **slotting optimisation** or **vertical storage expansion**.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Wholesaler / Logistics Partner

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Decision-Making Framework — Make-vs-Buy Logistics Decision Matrix.**

| Decision Criterion | In-House Logistics | Outsourced (3PL) |
|---|---|---|
| **Capital Investment** | High (fleets, warehouses) | Low (pay-per-use) |
| **Flexibility / Scalability** | Low | High |
| **Core Competency Focus** | Diluted | Sharpened |
| **Expertise Level** | Variable | High (specialised) |
| **Geographic Reach** | Limited | Pan-India/Global |
| **Cost Predictability** | Fixed high | Variable (usage-based) |
| **Information Technology** | Must build | Available plug-and-play |
| **Risk Exposure** | High | Shared |
| **Control over Service** | Full | Partial |
| **Best Suited For** | Strategic, core SKUs | Non-core, fluctuating demand |

---

## 3.7 Analytical Matrix — Channel Selection Decision Tree

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Channel Choice Heuristics (based on Kotler):**
>
> 1. **Product complexity** → More complex → Shorter channel (direct)
> 2. **Perishability** → More perishable → Shorter channel
> 3. **Bulk / weight** → Bulkier → Shorter channel
> 4. **Standardisation** → More standard → Longer channel
> 5. **Unit value** → Higher value → Shorter channel
> 6. **Customer concentration** → Concentrated → Direct
> 7. **Service requirements** → High service → Direct
> 8. **Market coverage goals** → Intensive → Longer channel
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The question bank below strictly follows **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**:
> - **Part A:** 3-mark short-answer questions (no choice, two questions per module)
> - **Part B:** 14-mark descriptive with internal choice
> - Each 14-mark question carries sub-parts of **7 + 7** marks

---

## PART A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

### **Question 1: Define Retailing. List any four modern retail formats.**
`[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

Retailing refers to all activities involved in selling goods or services directly to the **final consumer** for personal, non-business use. It is the **terminal point** of the marketing channel where merchandise is sold in small quantities to the end user.

**Four Modern Retail Formats:**
1. **Speciality Stores** — Narrow product line with deep assortment (e.g., Bata, Tanishq).
2. **Departmental Stores** — Multiple categories under one roof (e.g., Lifestyle).
3. **Supermarkets** — Large self-service grocery stores (e.g., D-Mart).
4. **E-Tailers / Online Retailers** — Pure online platforms (e.g., Amazon).
5. **Omnichannel Retailers** — Integrated online + offline (e.g., Tata CLiQ Luxury).
6. **Category Killers** — Deep assortment in one category (e.g., Croma).

*(Any 4 correctly explained — 3 marks: Definition 1 mk + 4 formats 0.5 mk each)*

---

### **Question 2: Distinguish between Wholesaling and Retailing.**
`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

| Basis | Wholesaling | Retailing |
|---|---|---|
| **Customer Type** | Business buyers (B2B) | End consumer (B2C) |
| **Quantity** | Bulk | Small / unit |
| **Location** | Warehouse / wholesale market | Shop / mall / e-tail |
| **Promotion** | Personal selling, trade shows | Advertising, displays |
| **Margin** | Lower per unit, high volume | Higher per unit, lower volume |
| **Service Need** | Delivery, credit, bulk-breaking | Personal attention, ambience |
| **Examples** | C&F agents, distributors | DMart, Amazon, Croma |

---

## PART B — 14-Mark Descriptive Questions (Internal Choice)

### **OPTION A — Question A (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — May 2024 | Module 3 | Sub-parts a + b]`

> **Question A(a): [7 Marks]** Explain the **functions of wholesalers** with relevant examples. Why are they called the *economy creators* of the distribution channel?

**Model Answer:**

Wholesalers perform a comprehensive set of functions that **add value**, **reduce transaction cost**, and **enhance efficiency** in the marketing channel. They are termed *economy creators* because they generate *time, place, quantity, and possession utilities* without which the market would be highly fragmented.

**1. Transactional Functions:**
- **Buying and Selling** — Wholesalers purchase in bulk from producers (e.g., ITC Agri Business buying from farmers) and sell in smaller lots to retailers, thereby assuming the risk of stockholding.
- **Bulk-Breaking** — A wholesaler buying 1,000 kg of rice may sell 50 kg bags to 20 retailers — a critical value-add.
- **Assortment Building** — A wholesaler may stock 200 SKUs from 20 producers, offering a one-stop shop for retailers.

**2. Logistical Functions:**
- **Warehousing** — Stockholding provides *time utility*. (e.g., Metro Cash & Carry warehouses).
- **Transportation** — Own or contract-based fleet provides *place utility*.
- **Inventory Management** — Reduces stockout risk for retailers.

**3. Facilitating Functions:**
- **Financing** — Provides credit to retailers, easing their working capital.
- **Market Information** — Feeds POS data and trends back to producers.
- **Risk-Bearing** — Holds inventory, bears spoilage and obsolescence risk.
- **Promotion Support** — Cooperative advertising, in-store displays.

**Conclusion:** Without wholesalers, **transaction costs would explode** — each retailer would need to negotiate with hundreds of producers. Wholesalers **specialise**, **scale up**, and **optimise** — they are the *backbone of traditional distribution*, especially in India where they handle over 60% of FMCG volume.

---

> **Question A(b): [7 Marks]** Discuss the **Retail Mix (6 P's)** in detail with suitable examples for a **modern supermarket** like DMart.

**Model Answer:**

The **Retail Mix** is the tactical combination of elements a retailer uses to attract and retain customers. For a **modern supermarket** (e.g., DMart), the 6 P's are:

**1. Product (Merchandise Assortment):**
- DMart offers a **wide assortment** (food, apparel, FMCG, home essentials) with **deep variety** in staples and groceries.
- Strategy: Focus on *high-frequency, low-margin essentials*.

**2. Price:**
- DMart follows an **EDLP (Every Day Low Pricing)** strategy — no frills, no loyalty gimmicks.
- Achieves low price via **direct sourcing, minimal advertising, and private labels**.

**3. Place (Location & Layout):**
- Located in **suburban / Tier 1 city outskirts** to leverage lower real-estate cost.
- Store layout follows **grid pattern** to maximise exposure to essentials.

**4. Promotion:**
- Minimal advertising — relies on **word-of-mouth and price perception**.
- Uses **in-store point-of-sale (POS) promotions** and seasonal discounts.

**5. Personnel:**
- Lean staff model, emphasis on **checkout speed** and **self-service**.
- Limited sales assistance, but courteous when approached.

**6. Presentation (Visual Merchandising & Atmospherics):**
- Clean aisles, neat stacking, bright lighting, **wide checkouts** to prevent queues.
- **Atmospherics** designed to encourage basket upsell (e.g., impulse items at billing).

**Strategic Outcome:** DMart achieves the lowest cost per square foot in Indian retail, translating into industry-leading margins.

---

### **OPTION B — Question B (14 Marks)**

`[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 | Module 3 | Sub-parts a + b]`

> **Question B(a): [7 Marks]** Define **Marketing Logistics**. Explain the **key components of a marketing logistics system** with a neat diagram.

**Model Answer:**

**Definition:** Marketing logistics (or physical distribution) is the **planning, implementation, and control** of the physical flow of materials, final goods, and related information from the point of origin to the point of consumption to satisfy customer requirements profitably.

**Key Components of the Marketing Logistics System:**

**1. Transportation:**
- The most important cost driver (50–60% of logistics cost).
- Modes: Road, Rail, Air, Water, Pipeline, Intermodal.
- **Decision variables:** Mode selection, route optimisation, fleet size, third-party vs. own.

**2. Warehousing & Storage:**
- Provides **time utility** by bridging production and consumption.
- Types: Storage warehouses, distribution centres (DCs), cross-docking, automated warehouses, bonded warehouses.

**3. Inventory Management:**
- Balancing stockout cost vs. holding cost.
- Tools: **EOQ, ROP, Safety Stock, Just-in-Time (JIT), Vendor Managed Inventory (VMI)**.

**4. Order Processing:**
- Order entry → credit check → picking → packing → shipping.
- **Order Cycle Time** is a critical KPI.

**5. Materials Handling:**
- In-warehouse movement via conveyors, forklifts, AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots).

**6. Protective Packaging:**
- Prevents damage, theft, and pilferage in transit.

**7. Information System:**
- ERP, WMS, TMS, CRM integration for real-time visibility.

**Diagram (ASCII Schematic):**

$$
\boxed{\text{Order Processing} \to \text{Warehousing} \to \text{Inventory Mgmt} \to \text{Materials Handling} \to \text{Transportation} \to \text{Consumer}}
$$
*(Cross-cutting: Information System and Protective Packaging)*

---

> **Question B(b): [7 Marks]** A company has the following data: **Annual Demand = 20,000 units**, **Ordering Cost = ₹400 per order**, **Holding Cost = ₹10 per unit per year**. Calculate the **EOQ, number of orders per year, time between orders, and total inventory cost**.

**Model Answer:**

### Step 1 — Economic Order Quantity (EOQ)

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{\frac{2 D S}{H}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 20{,}000 \times 400}{10}}
$$

$$
Q^{*} = \sqrt{1{,}600{,}000} = 1264.91 \approx 1265 \text{ units}
$$

### Step 2 — Number of Orders per Year

$$
N = \frac{D}{Q^{*}} = \frac{20{,}000}{1265} = 15.81 \approx 16 \text{ orders per year}
$$

### Step 3 — Time Between Orders (Order Cycle)

$$
T = \frac{365 \text{ days}}{16} = 22.81 \text{ days} \approx 23 \text{ days}
$$

### Step 4 — Total Annual Inventory Cost

**Annual Ordering Cost:**

$$
\frac{D \cdot S}{Q^{*}} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 400}{1265} = ₹6324.11
$$

**Annual Holding Cost:**

$$
\frac{Q^{*} \cdot H}{2} = \frac{1265 \times 10}{2} = ₹6325
$$

**Total Inventory Cost:**

$$
\boxed{\text{TAC} = 6324.11 + 6325 = ₹12{,}649.11}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Verification:** At EOQ, **ordering cost = holding cost**. Here, ₹6324 ≈ ₹6325, confirming the optimal balance.

---

## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 3:**
> 1. **Confusing wholesaler and retailer** — A wholesaler deals with B2B bulk; retailer with B2C small quantities.
> 2. **Skipping the 'Why' in functions of wholesaler** — Examiners award marks for *value-add* explanation, not just listing.
> 3. **EOQ units error** — Confusing D as annual demand vs. daily demand. Always use **annual figures** unless specified.
> 4. **Forgetting Safety Stock in ROP** — ROP ≠ average demand × lead time alone; you must **add** safety stock.
> 5. **GMROI vs. ROI confusion** — GMROI focuses on **inventory investment** only, not total assets.
> 6. **Missing diagram in logistics** — A 14-mark logistics question without a system schematic **loses 1–2 marks**.
> 7. **Ignoring disintermediation** — When asked about wholesaler decline, **mention e-commerce and modern trade** explicitly.
> 8. **Wrong EOQ boundary condition** — EOQ assumes constant demand, instantaneous replenishment, no quantity discounts.

---

## 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **Module 3 — Final Cheat Sheet for Hall-Day Revision**

### Retailing
- Definition: B2C sale of small quantities to end consumer
- **Modern formats:** Speciality, Departmental, Supermarket, Convenience, Discount, Category Killer, E-tailer, Omnichannel
- **Retail Mix:** Product, Price, Place, Promotion, Personnel, Presentation (6 P's)
- **Retail Trends:** Omnichannel, Phygital, Experiential, Q-commerce, AI Personalisation, Sustainable Retail
- **Retail KPIs:** Inventory Turnover, GMROI, Sell-Through, Stock-to-Sales, DPP

### Wholesaling
- Definition: B2B bulk selling, **does not deal with end consumer**
- **Functions:** Transactional (buying, selling, bulk-breaking, assortment), Logistical (warehousing, transport), Facilitating (finance, info, risk, promotion)
- **Types:** Merchant, Agent, Broker, Manufacturer's Sales Branch, Cash & Carry
- **Disintermediation:** Producers bypassing wholesalers via e-commerce / direct-to-retail

### Logistics & Physical Distribution
- **Definition:** Planning, implementation, control of flow and storage of goods from origin to consumption
- **Components:** Transportation, Warehousing, Inventory, Order Processing, Materials Handling, Packaging, Information Systems
- **Transportation Modes:** Road, Rail, Air, Water, Pipeline, Intermodal — each with a cost-speed-reliability trade-off
- **Warehousing Types:** Storage, Distribution Centres, Cross-docking, Fulfilment Centres, Bonded, Automated

### Quantitative Toolkit
- **EOQ:** $Q^{*} = \sqrt{2DS/H}$ (Wilson Model)
- **ROP:** $\text{ROP} = (\text{Avg Daily Usage} \times \text{Lead Time}) + \text{Safety Stock}$
- **Safety Stock:** $Z \times \sigma_{dLT}$
- **Inventory Turnover:** COGS / Avg Inventory
- **GMROI:** Gross Margin % × Inventory Turnover
- **Total Logistics Cost:** $T + W + I + O + H + P$

### Channel Concepts
- **Channel Levels:** Zero (Direct), One, Two, Three
- **Disintermediation:** Removal of intermediaries
- **Conflict Types:** Vertical, Horizontal, Multichannel
- **Channel Choice Factors:** Product complexity, perishability, bulk, value, customer concentration, service need

### High-Frequency Exam Phrases
- *"Wholesalers add time, place, quantity, and possession utility."*
- *"Logistics is the operational backbone of the marketing channel."*
- *"EOQ minimises the sum of ordering and holding costs."*
- *"Omnichannel retail integrates physical and digital touchpoints seamlessly."*

> **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 ESE — Master the formulas, memorise the typologies, and draw diagrams wherever possible. 🏆**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Advertising and Sales Promotions
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Advertising and Sales Promotions

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Advertising — The Academic Definition

> [!NOTE]
> **Advertising** is defined by the **American Marketing Association (AMA)** as *"any paid form of non-personal presentation and promotion of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor."*

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context, advertising is one of the four major elements of the **Promotion Mix** (commonly called the **IMC — Integrated Marketing Communication** mix), the other three being **Personal Selling, Sales Promotion, and Public Relations / Publicity**.

The key operative words in the definition are:

- **Paid form** → distinguishes advertising from publicity (which is free).
- **Non-personal** → delivered through mass media, not face-to-face.
- **Identified sponsor** → the company behind the message is known.
- **Mass communication** → reaches large, geographically dispersed audiences simultaneously.

### 1.2 Sales Promotion — The Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sales Promotion** refers to *"short-term, incentive-driven marketing activities designed to stimulate quicker or greater purchase of specific products or services by consumers, resellers, or the company's own sales force."* — adapted from **Kotler & Keller, Marketing Management (KTU reference framework)**.

Sales promotion operates on the principle of **temporal urgency** — it gives the buyer a tangible reason to act *now*, rather than later.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain-English Intuition

**The Theatre Metaphor:**

Think of marketing promotion as staging a **theatre production** to sell a product:

| Element | Theatre Analogy | Marketing Function |
|---|---|---|
| The Play (full story) | Long-running drama series | **Advertising** (long-term, brand-building) |
| Free popcorn, lucky-draw coupons | In-theatre gimmicks | **Sales Promotion** (short-term, incentive) |
| Lead actor meeting fans | One-on-one chat | **Personal Selling** |
| Newspaper reviews | Free press coverage | **Public Relations** |

- **Advertising** is the **ongoing TV serial** that builds love for a character over months and years.
- **Sales Promotion** is the **free gift coupon** tucked inside the newspaper that makes you visit the store this Saturday.

> [!TIP]
> **One-line memory hook:** *Advertising tells people WHY to buy; Sales Promotion tells them WHEN to buy.*

### 1.4 The Promotion Mix (IMC) — Quick Reference

The four tools of the **Integrated Marketing Communication Mix** that work together:

1. **Advertising** — paid, non-personal, mass media.
2. **Sales Promotion** — short-term incentives.
3. **Personal Selling** — face-to-face interaction.
4. **Public Relations / Publicity** — unpaid media exposure.

Plus two modern extensions:
5. **Direct Marketing** — email, SMS, catalogues.
6. **Digital / Social Media Marketing** — SEO, influencer, content marketing.

---

### 1.5 The Strategic Role of Advertising in Marketing

Advertising performs **four strategic functions** (KTU high-frequency topic):

1. **Informative** — introduces new products, explains features.
2. **Persuasive** — builds brand preference and loyalty.
3. **Reminder** — reinforces recall for mature products.
4. **Reinforcement / Comparative** — differentiates from competitors.

> [!NOTE]
> **DAGMAR (Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results)** — the seminal model by **Russell Colley (1961)** used in KTU board examinations — classifies advertising into **Informative, Persuasive, Reminder, and Reinforcement** types, each tied to measurable communication goals.

---

### 1.6 Why Advertising & Sales Promotions Matter in B.Tech Management

Even engineering managers of tech firms (think **Apple, Samsung, Tata Motors, Mahindra**) must:

- Plan **ad budgets** alongside R&D budgets.
- Evaluate the **ROI of digital ads vs. trade promotions**.
- Coordinate **ATL (Above-the-Line) advertising** and **BTL (Below-the-Line) sales promotion**.

**ATL vs. BTL** (a KTU favourite):

- **ATL = Above-The-Line** → mass-media advertising (TV, newspaper, radio, billboards).
- **BTL = Below-The-Line** → direct, targeted activities (in-store promos, coupons, events, direct mail, sales promotion tools).

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Detailed Objectives of Advertising

The **DAGMAR model** is central to KTU evaluation. Every good advertising plan follows these four sequential communication goals (the **AIDA-like ladder**):

| Stage | Goal | Customer Mental State |
|---|---|---|
| **A** — Awareness | "Does the customer *know* the product exists?" | I have heard of it. |
| **C** — Comprehension | "Does the customer *understand* what it does?" | I know its benefits. |
| **C** — Conviction | "Does the customer *feel* a positive attitude?" | I prefer it over others. |
| **A** — Action | "Does the customer *purchase*?" | I will buy it now. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mnemonic:** **A-C-C-A = Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action**
> The marketer's job is to take the consumer up this ladder through advertising.

---

### 2.2 Types of Advertising (KTU Board-Favourite)

#### A. Based on **Audience / Target**

1. **Consumer Advertising** → aimed at end users (e.g., Surf Excel TV ads).
2. **Industrial / Business Advertising** → aimed at business buyers (e.g., steel, machinery B2B ads).
3. **Trade Advertising** → aimed at wholesalers and retailers.
4. **Professional Advertising** → aimed at doctors, engineers, CAs.

#### B. Based on **Function / Purpose** (DAGMAR aligned)

1. **Informative** — for new product launch.
2. **Persuasive** — for growth stage, building preference.
3. **Reminder** — for maturity stage (e.g., Coca-Cola).
4. **Reinforcement** — for loyalty stage.

#### C. Based on **Approach / Style**

1. **Product Advertising** — focuses on the product (features, benefits).
2. **Institutional / Corporate Advertising** — focuses on the company's image (Tata Group ads on ethics).
3. **Comparative Advertising** — directly compares with competitor (legally restricted in India).
4. **Pioneer Advertising** — first of its kind, category-creating.
5. **Competitive Advertising** — for direct head-to-head brands.
6. **Covert / Indirect Advertising** — brand appears inside entertainment content.
7. **Surrogate Advertising** — used to promote banned products (alcohol/tobacco disguised as "soda" or "music CDs").

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to mention *Surrogate Advertising* and *Comparative Advertising*. Both are KTU-favourite short-answer questions (2-3 marks).

---

### 2.3 The AIDA Model — Hierarchical Response Model

> [!NOTE]
> The **AIDA model** by **E. K. Strong (1925)** is the most frequently tested hierarchy-of-effects model in KTU Marketing Management.

**Stage 1 — Attention:** Capture the consumer's attention (loud jingle, striking visual, headline).

**Stage 2 — Interest:** Generate curiosity about the product (story, problem-solution, demonstration).

**Stage 3 — Desire:** Convert interest into a strong want (emotional appeal, social proof, testimonials).

**Stage 4 — Action:** Trigger the actual purchase (coupon, CTA — Call To Action, limited offer).

Modern extensions include **AIDAS** (with **S = Satisfaction** post-purchase) and **AIDCAS** (with **C = Confidence**).

---

### 2.4 Advertising Budget — The Four Standard Methods

Choosing how much to spend on advertising is a strategic decision. KTU prescribes four classical methods:

#### Method 1: **Percentage-of-Sales Method**

The budget is set as a fixed percentage of past or forecasted sales.

$$B = r \times S$$

Where:
- $B$ = Advertising Budget
- $r$ = Percentage rate (e.g., 5% of sales)
- $S$ = Sales (past or projected)

> [!TIP]
> **Pros:** Simple, safe, financially stable.
> **Cons:** Treats advertising as a *result* of sales, not a *cause*; discourages aggressive growth spending.

#### Method 2: **Objective-and-Task Method**

The most logical, scientific method. Three steps:

1. Define **communication objectives** (e.g., raise awareness from 30% → 60%).
2. Identify **specific tasks** needed (e.g., 500 TV spots, 200 billboards).
3. Estimate **costs** of those tasks → sum = budget.

$$B = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \times Q_i$$

Where:
- $C_i$ = cost of task $i$
- $Q_i$ = quantity of task $i$ required
- $n$ = total number of distinct tasks

> [!TIP]
> **Pros:** Logically tied to goals; highly justifiable to management.
> **Cons:** Hard to quantify objectives; time-consuming.

#### Method 3: **Competitive-Parity Method**

The budget is matched to what major competitors are spending.

$$B = \alpha \times C_{comp}$$

Where $\alpha$ is a multiplier (typically 1.0 to 1.25) and $C_{comp}$ is the average competitor's ad spend.

> [!TIP]
> **Pros:** Avoids competitive wars; industry-aligned.
> **Cons:** Assumes competitors know better; ignores company-specific goals.

#### Method 4: **Affordable / What-We-Can-Afford Method**

The firm spends what is *left over* after covering all other costs.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU marks this as the **weakest method** because it ignores advertising's strategic role.

#### Summary Table — Budget Methods at a Glance

| Method | Basis | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| Percentage-of-Sales | Sales revenue | Simple, stable | Reactive |
| Objective-and-Task | Communication goals | Scientific, defensible | Time-consuming |
| Competitive-Parity | Competitor spend | Industry-aligned | Herd mentality |
| Affordable | Residual cash | Easy | Ignores strategy |

---

### 2.5 Advertising Media — The Five Major Channels

| Medium | Type | Reach | Cost | Best For |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Print** (Newspaper, Magazines) | ATL | Local to National | Low-Medium | Local ads, detailed info |
| **Broadcast** (TV, Radio) | ATL | Mass | High | Emotional brand stories |
| **Outdoor** (Hoardings, Transit) | ATL | High-frequency | Medium | Geographic targeting |
| **Digital** (Social, Search, Video) | Online | Global, targeted | Variable | Measurable ROI |
| **Direct Mail / In-store** | BTL | Personalized | Low | Direct response |

**Selection criteria for media** (the **5 M's of Media Decision**):

1. **Mission** — what is the objective?
2. **Money** — budget available.
3. **Media** — which channels?
4. **Message** — does medium suit the message complexity?
5. **Measurement** — how to evaluate effectiveness?

---

### 2.6 Sales Promotion — Comprehensive Tools

Sales promotion is divided into **two camps**:

#### A. **Consumer-Oriented Sales Promotion** (aimed at end users)

| Tool | Description | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Samples** | Free trial-size product | Tide sachets in newspaper |
| **Coupons** | Discount vouchers | ₹50 off on Amazon |
| **Cash Refunds** | Post-purchase cashback | "Send bill, get ₹100 back" |
| **Price-Off (Off-List)** | Reduced price tag | MRP ₹100, now ₹80 |
| **Premiums / Gifts** | Free gift on purchase | "Free 1 kg sugar on 5 kg Atta" |
| **Frequency Programs / Loyalty Cards** | Points on repeat purchase | StarBucks Rewards |
| **Contests / Sweepstakes** | Win big prizes | "Win a car on ₹100 recharge" |
| **Demonstrations** | Live trial at store | Demo of mixer-grinder |
| **Point-of-Purchase (POP)** | Shelf displays, banners | Glow-sign at billing counter |
| **Free Trials** | Time-limited free use | Netflix 30-day free trial |

#### B. **Trade-Oriented Sales Promotion** (aimed at channel partners)

| Tool | Description |
|---|---|
| **Trade Discounts** | Off-invoice discounts to retailers |
| **Trade Allowances** | Cash or product for in-store promotion |
| **Co-operative Advertising** (Co-op Ad) | Manufacturer shares ad cost with dealer |
| **Dealer Contests** | Prizes for top-selling dealer |
| **Dealer Training** | Free training on product selling |
| **Push Money / Spiffs** | Cash bonus to sales person |
| **Free Goods** | "Buy 10, get 2 free" for retailer |
| **Display Allowances** | Pay retailer for premium shelf space |

#### C. **Sales-Force-Oriented Promotion** (aimed at own sales team)

- Sales contests, bonuses, training, sales meetings, recognition awards.

---

### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Expression | Meaning |
|---|---|---|
| Advertising Budget (Percentage) | $B = r \cdot S$ | Budget = rate × sales |
| Advertising Budget (Objective-Task) | $B = \sum C_i \cdot Q_i$ | Sum of cost × quantity of tasks |
| Cost Per Thousand (CPM) | $\text{CPM} = \dfrac{\text{Ad Cost}}{\text{Reach} \div 1000}$ | Cost to reach 1000 people |
| Cost Per Rating Point (CPRP) | $\text{CPRP} = \dfrac{\text{Spot Cost}}{\text{TVR of show}}$ | TV ad efficiency metric |
| Ad-to-Sales Ratio | $\text{A/S} = \dfrac{\text{Ad Spend}}{\text{Sales Revenue}} \times 100$ | % of revenue spent on ads |
| Sales Promotion Expense Ratio | $\text{SPER} = \dfrac{\text{Promo Cost}}{\text{Sales}} \times 100$ | Promo cost efficiency |
| Redemption Rate | $\text{RR} = \dfrac{\text{Coupons Redeemed}}{\text{Coupons Distributed}} \times 100$ | % coupon effectiveness |
| Brand Equity Multiplier | $\text{BEM} = \dfrac{\text{Premium Price}}{\text{Average Category Price}}$ | Pricing power |
| Break-Even on Ad Spend | $S \times m = B + F$ | Sales × margin = ad budget + fixed cost |
| Share of Voice (SOV) | $\text{SOV} = \dfrac{\text{Brand Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$ | Competitive ad presence |

---

### 2.8 Advertising Effectiveness — Measurement

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU prescribes a **two-dimensional framework** for ad effectiveness:

**1. Communication Effect (pre-sales)** — Did the ad *communicate* well?

- **Aided Recall** — recall with brand cue.
- **Unaided Recall** — recall without any cue (gold standard).
- **Recognition Tests** — have they seen the ad?
- **Attitude / Opinion Tests** — likeability, believability.
- **Day-After Recall (DAR)** — measured within 24 hours.

**2. Sales Effect (post-sales)** — Did the ad *sell*?

- **Pre-test / Post-test Sales Comparison** — compare sales before and after.
- **Historical Sales Data Correlation** — ad spend vs. sales regression.
- **Experimental Design** — test market vs. control market.

$$\text{Ad Elasticity} = \dfrac{\%\Delta \text{ Sales}}{\%\Delta \text{ Ad Spend}}$$

> [!TIP]
> An ad elasticity of **0.3** means a **10% rise in ad spend** yields a **3% rise in sales**. A value > 1 = high responsiveness; < 0.5 = inelastic response.

---

### 2.9 Sales Promotion vs. Advertising — Critical Distinction

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **guaranteed KTU 2-mark question** every year.

| Parameter | Advertising | Sales Promotion |
|---|---|---|
| **Objective** | Build long-term brand equity | Trigger short-term sales spike |
| **Time horizon** | Long-term (years) | Short-term (days, weeks) |
| **Direction** | Pulls customer toward brand | Pushes customer to act now |
| **Approach** | Indirect, emotional | Direct, rational / incentive |
| **Measurability** | Difficult | Easy (redemption, lift) |
| **Cost-effectiveness** | Brand-building, hard to track | Measurable ROI, but erodes margin |
| **Risk** | Brand wear-out | Discount addiction |

---

### 2.10 Real-World Engineering / Tech Industry Utility

In a tech firm, marketing decisions frequently use these formulas:

- **CPM** is used to compare **YouTube ads vs. LinkedIn ads** for B2B software promotion.
- **Ad Elasticity** informs **digital marketing budget allocation** between Google Ads and Meta Ads.
- **SOV** is tracked by **SaaS firms** to benchmark their ad presence vs. competitors (Salesforce vs. HubSpot).
- **Redemption Rate** is critical for **e-commerce flash sales** (Flipkart Big Billion Days).

## 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Derivations & Worked Examples

### 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Advertising Budget Calculation (Objective-and-Task Method)

**Problem Statement:**

> *A startup plans to launch a new smart-watch in Kerala. The marketing team identifies four communication tasks:*
> - *Task A: 200 TV spots @ ₹50,000 each*
> - *Task B: 100 newspaper insertions @ ₹20,000 each*
> - *Task C: 50 influencer reels @ ₹30,000 each*
> - *Task D: 1000 posters @ ₹200 each*
>
> *Compute the total advertising budget.*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Identify the formula.**

The objective-and-task method sums up the cost of each specific task:

$$B = \sum_{i=1}^{n} C_i \times Q_i$$

**Step 2 — Compute individual task costs.**

For **Task A** (TV spots):
$$\text{Cost}_A = 200 \times 50{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹1 Crore)}$$

For **Task B** (newspaper insertions):
$$\text{Cost}_B = 100 \times 20{,}000 = 20{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹20 Lakhs)}$$

For **Task C** (influencer reels):
$$\text{Cost}_C = 50 \times 30{,}000 = 15{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹15 Lakhs)}$$

For **Task D** (posters):
$$\text{Cost}_D = 1000 \times 200 = 2{,}00{,}000 \text{ (₹2 Lakhs)}$$

**Step 3 — Sum the components.**

$$B = \text{Cost}_A + \text{Cost}_B + \text{Cost}_C + \text{Cost}_D$$

$$B = 1{,}00{,}00{,}000 + 20{,}00{,}000 + 15{,}00{,}000 + 2{,}00{,}000$$

$$B = 1{,}37{,}00{,}000 \text{ INR (₹1.37 Crore)}$$

**Step 4 — Conclude and contextualize.**

> The startup needs a **₹1.37 Crore** advertising budget to execute the planned communication program. If the working capital is lower, the marketing manager may either reduce the number of insertions or substitute higher-cost media with lower-cost digital alternatives.

---

### 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Percentage-of-Sales Budget Method

**Problem Statement:**

> *A detergent brand earned ₹50 Crore last year. Following the company policy of allocating 8% of sales to advertising, what is the advertising budget? If next year's projected sales are ₹60 Crore and the policy rate remains the same, what is the new budget?*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the formula.**

$$B = r \times S$$

**Step 2 — Apply for last year (S = ₹50 Crore, r = 8%).**

$$B_1 = 0.08 \times 50 \text{ Crore} = 4 \text{ Crore INR}$$

**Step 3 — Apply for next year (S = ₹60 Crore, r = 8%).**

$$B_2 = 0.08 \times 60 \text{ Crore} = 4.8 \text{ Crore INR}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the increment.**

$$\Delta B = 4.8 - 4.0 = 0.8 \text{ Crore INR}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude.**

> As projected sales rise, the advertising budget *automatically scales up* with sales. This is the key advantage of the percentage method — financial stability — but the budget cannot be used *proactively* to boost sales.

---

### 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Cost Per Thousand (CPM) Comparison

**Problem Statement:**

> *Two magazines target the same demographic:*
> - *Magazine X charges ₹2,00,000 for a full-page ad reaching 5,00,000 readers.*
> - *Magazine Y charges ₹1,50,000 for the same ad reaching 2,50,000 readers.*
>
> *Which is more cost-efficient on a CPM basis?*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Write the CPM formula.**

$$\text{CPM} = \frac{\text{Ad Cost}}{\text{Reach} / 1000}$$

**Step 2 — Compute CPM for Magazine X.**

$$\text{CPM}_X = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{5{,}00{,}000 / 1000} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{500} = 400 \text{ INR per 1000 readers}$$

**Step 3 — Compute CPM for Magazine Y.**

$$\text{CPM}_Y = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{2{,}50{,}000 / 1000} = \frac{1{,}50{,}000}{250} = 600 \text{ INR per 1000 readers}$$

**Step 4 — Compare and conclude.**

Since $\text{CPM}_X = 400 < \text{CPM}_Y = 600$, **Magazine X is more cost-efficient** on a per-thousand-reach basis. The marketing manager should prefer Magazine X for broad awareness campaigns.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always show both CPM values explicitly and rank them. Examiners give full marks only when the comparison is clearly stated.

---

### 3.4 Worked Example 4 — Ad Elasticity Computation

**Problem Statement:**

> *A company increased its monthly ad spend from ₹10 Lakh to ₹12 Lakh, and observed sales rising from ₹50 Lakh to ₹56 Lakh in the same period. Compute the ad elasticity and interpret.*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the elasticity formula.**

$$\text{Ad Elasticity} = \frac{\%\Delta \text{Sales}}{\%\Delta \text{Ad Spend}}$$

**Step 2 — Compute % change in ad spend.**

$$\%\Delta \text{Ad Spend} = \frac{12 - 10}{10} \times 100 = 20\%$$

**Step 3 — Compute % change in sales.**

$$\%\Delta \text{Sales} = \frac{56 - 50}{50} \times 100 = 12\%$$

**Step 4 — Compute elasticity.**

$$\text{Ad Elasticity} = \frac{12\%}{20\%} = 0.6$$

**Step 5 — Interpret.**

Since elasticity = **0.6 < 1**, the demand for the product is **inelastic with respect to advertising** — a 1% rise in ad spend yields only 0.6% rise in sales. The brand may be in a mature stage with diminishing returns. Management should consider re-allocating spend to sales promotion or new product launches.

---

### 3.5 Worked Example 5 — Share of Voice (SOV) Analysis

**Problem Statement:**

> *In the Kerala tea category, total annual advertising expenditure by all brands combined is ₹200 Crore. A specific brand "KeralaTea Gold" spends ₹30 Crore.*
> - *Compute the brand's Share of Voice.*
> - *If the brand's market share is 12%, comment on whether SOV exceeds market share.*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the SOV formula.**

$$\text{SOV} = \frac{\text{Brand Ad Spend}}{\text{Total Category Ad Spend}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the values.**

$$\text{SOV} = \frac{30}{200} \times 100 = 15\%$$

**Step 3 — Compare with market share.**

$$\text{Market Share} = 12\%$$

Since $\text{SOV} = 15\% > \text{Market Share} = 12\%$, the brand is **over-spending** relative to its market presence — a sign of **aggressive growth strategy** or **defensive warfare** against a rising competitor.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** Always state whether SOV > MS, SOV = MS, or SOV < MS, and *interpret*. Examiners allocate 1 mark for this interpretation alone.

---

### 3.6 Worked Example 6 — Redemption Rate of a Coupon Campaign

**Problem Statement:**

> *A retailer distributed 1,00,000 coupons via newspaper inserts. Within 30 days, 8,500 coupons were redeemed. Compute the redemption rate and comment on effectiveness.*

#### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the redemption formula.**

$$\text{RR} = \frac{\text{Coupons Redeemed}}{\text{Coupons Distributed}} \times 100$$

**Step 2 — Substitute.**

$$\text{RR} = \frac{8{,}500}{1{,}00{,}000} \times 100 = 8.5\%$$

**Step 3 — Interpret.**

> The **industry benchmark for coupon redemption** in FMCG is **1–5%**. An **8.5% redemption rate** is **exceptionally high** — the campaign is highly effective, and the retailer may consider repeating it next quarter with similar offers.

---

### 3.7 Conceptual Derivation — The AIDA Funnel

The AIDA model is conceptually a **funnel**, where the audience base narrows at each stage as the marketer moves prospects from awareness to action.

$$\text{Prospects at Action} = \text{Prospects at Attention} \times P_A \times P_I \times P_D$$

Where:
- $P_A$ = probability of moving from Attention to Interest
- $P_I$ = probability of moving from Interest to Desire
- $P_D$ = probability of moving from Desire to Action

> **Example:** If 1,00,000 people see a TV ad, and conversion probabilities are $P_A = 0.5$, $P_I = 0.4$, $P_D = 0.25$:
>
> $$\text{Buyers} = 1{,}00{,}000 \times 0.5 \times 0.4 \times 0.25 = 5{,}000$$
>
> This is the **conversion funnel** logic that drives digital marketing platforms like Google Ads and Meta Ads today.
