## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** *Define advertising. Mention any **four** features of advertising.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1 mark):** Advertising is any paid form of non-personal presentation and promotion of ideas, goods, or services by an identified sponsor, as defined by the American Marketing Association.
>
> **Four features (2 marks — 0.5 each):**
>
> 1. **Paid form** — unlike publicity, the sponsor pays for the message.
> 2. **Non-personal** — delivered through mass media; no face-to-face contact.
> 3. **Mass communication** — reaches a large, geographically dispersed audience.
> 4. **Identifiable sponsor** — the brand or company behind the ad is disclosed.

---

> **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — May 2024]** *Distinguish between advertising and sales promotion. Give any **three** points.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| S.No. | Basis | Advertising (1.5 marks) | Sales Promotion (1.5 marks) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Objective | Builds long-term brand equity | Triggers short-term sales spike |
| 2 | Time horizon | Long-term (years) | Short-term (days/weeks) |
| 3 | Approach | Indirect, emotional storytelling | Direct, rational, incentive-based |
| 4 | Cost-effectiveness | Brand-building, hard to measure | Measurable ROI, erodes margin |

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note (KTU 2024 Scheme Pattern):** Each Part B question carries **14 marks** and has internal choice — either **Or** between two sub-options, or two 7-mark sub-parts. Below we provide **Question A** and **Question B** as alternative choices.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** — *Conceptual + Numerical*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, Dec 2024]**

> **(a)** *Explain the **DAGMAR model** of advertising in detail. Discuss its four communication stages with examples. (7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *A FMCG company has a sales turnover of ₹80 Crore. The marketing head wants to use the **Objective-and-Task method** to set the advertising budget. The following tasks are identified:*
> - *Task 1: 150 TV commercials @ ₹60,000 each*
> - *Task 2: 200 radio spots @ ₹8,000 each*
> - *Task 3: 100 newspaper insertions @ ₹25,000 each*
> - *Task 4: 500 influencer posts @ ₹15,000 each*
>
> *Calculate the total advertising budget. If the company instead spent 6% of sales on advertising (percentage method), how would the budget compare? Comment. (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Definition (1 mark):** DAGMAR stands for **Defining Advertising Goals for Measured Advertising Results**, proposed by **Russell Colley in 1961**. It is a communication-effect model that translates advertising goals into measurable hierarchical stages.

**The Four Communication Stages (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Awareness:** The first task is to make the target audience *aware* that the product or brand exists. This is critical for new product launches. *Example:* A new electric scooter brand running heavy outdoor ads to make the city population aware of its name.

2. **Comprehension:** The customer must *understand* what the product does, its key features, and benefits. *Example:* A 60-second ad explaining how the new scooter's battery range of 120 km solves the daily commute problem.

3. **Conviction:** The customer develops a *favourable attitude* and mental preference for the brand over competitors. *Example:* Customer feels "This brand is more reliable than brand X" after seeing safety test results in the ad.

4. **Action:** The customer is motivated to *actually purchase* the product. *Example:* Ad ends with "Visit our showroom this weekend and get ₹10,000 off" — triggers immediate purchase.

**Conclusion (2 marks):** DAGMAR is a strategic framework that forces marketers to set SMART (Specific, Measurable, Achievable, Relevant, Time-bound) communication goals. The ladder must be climbed sequentially — skipping stages leads to weak brand-building. The ACCA mnemonic (Awareness, Comprehension, Conviction, Action) helps in memorising the model.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1 — Compute the Objective-and-Task budget (3 marks).**

Using $B = \sum C_i \times Q_i$:

- Task 1: $150 \times 60{,}000 = 90{,}00{,}000$
- Task 2: $200 \times 8{,}000 = 16{,}00{,}000$
- Task 3: $100 \times 25{,}000 = 25{,}00{,}000$
- Task 4: $500 \times 15{,}000 = 75{,}00{,}000$

$$B_{\text{obj}} = 90 + 16 + 25 + 75 = 206 \text{ Lakh} = ₹2.06 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Percentage-of-Sales budget (2 marks).**

$$B_{\%} = 0.06 \times 80 = ₹4.80 \text{ Crore}$$

**Step 3 — Compare and comment (2 marks).**

| Method | Budget |
|---|---|
| Objective-and-Task | ₹2.06 Crore |
| Percentage-of-Sales (6%) | ₹4.80 Crore |

**Comment:** The **percentage method gives a much larger budget (₹4.80 Crore)** than the actual task-based requirement (₹2.06 Crore). This means the percentage method may lead to **overspending** on advertising. The Objective-and-Task method is more **scientific and cost-efficient**, because it ties spending to *actual communication needs* rather than mechanical sales percentages. The marketing head should prefer the Objective-and-Task method to optimize return on investment.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** — *Alternative Choice*

**[KTU University Exam — Model Question, May 2025]**

> **(a)** *Explain the **AIDA model**. Discuss its stages and explain how it is used in designing an advertising campaign. (7 marks)*
>
> **(b)** *Discuss the various **tools of consumer-oriented sales promotion** with suitable examples. How are they different from trade-oriented sales promotion tools? (7 marks)*

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Introduction (1 mark):** The AIDA model, proposed by **E. K. Strong in 1925**, is one of the oldest and most widely used hierarchical models of consumer response to advertising. It describes the cognitive and behavioural journey a customer undergoes from first encountering an ad to making a purchase.

**Stages (4 marks — 1 mark each):**

1. **Attention:** The first and most crucial step — capture the customer's attention. Marketers use striking headlines, vibrant visuals, celebrity faces, or bold jingles. *Example:* Cadbury's "Kuch Meetha Ho Jaye" jingle grabs instant attention.

2. **Interest:** Once attention is captured, the ad must generate curiosity and interest in the product. Storytelling, problem-solution frameworks, and demonstrations are used. *Example:* Surf Excel's "Daag Acche Hain" campaign built interest by reframing stains positively.

3. **Desire:** The customer must *want* the product, not just be interested. Emotional appeals, social proof, and aspirational messaging are employed. *Example:* Apple iPhone ads build desire by showing lifestyle, not just specs.

4. **Action:** The customer is nudged to *act now* through a clear call-to-action (CTA). *Example:* "Shop now", "Use code WELCOME10 for 10% off", or "Limited stock — buy today."

**Application in Ad Campaign Design (2 marks):** Modern digital campaigns use the AIDA funnel in every micro-interaction — from Instagram reels (Attention) → product page (Interest) → reviews and testimonials (Desire) → checkout button with coupon (Action). Marketers also extend AIDA to **AIDAS** (adding **S = Satisfaction**) to ensure post-purchase loyalty.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Consumer-Oriented Sales Promotion Tools (4 marks):**

These tools are aimed at the **end consumer** to encourage immediate purchase:

1. **Samples** — Free trial-size products to introduce a new brand. *Example:* Maggi distributing free sachets at railway stations.

2. **Coupons** — Discount vouchers redeemable on next purchase. *Example:* ₹100 off coupon on first BigBasket order.

3. **Premium / Free Gifts** — Bonus item given on purchase. *Example:* "Free 1 kg sugar on buying 5 kg Atta."

4. **Cash Refunds** — Post-purchase cashback. *Example:* Send bill and get ₹200 back via UPI.

5. **Price-Off / Off-List** — Temporary price reduction. *Example:* MRP ₹500, now ₹399 sticker on the pack.

6. **Loyalty Programs** — Reward points for repeat purchases. *Example:* Amazon Pay Later, Zomato Gold.

7. **Contests / Sweepstakes** — Chance to win big prizes. *Example:* "Scratch and win a car on ₹1000 recharge."

8. **Free Trials** — Time-limited free use. *Example:* Netflix 30-day free trial.

**Trade-Oriented Sales Promotion Tools — Differences (3 marks):**

| Parameter | Consumer-Oriented | Trade-Oriented |
|---|---|---|
| **Target** | End consumer | Retailer, wholesaler, distributor |
| **Objective** | Off-the-shelf purchase | Stock-up, shelf-space, push |
| **Tools** | Coupons, samples, contests | Trade discounts, co-op ads, dealer contests |
| **Cost bearers** | Manufacturer | Manufacturer and dealer share |
| **Examples** | Free shampoo sachet | "3% off-invoice discount to retailer" |

> The two work together — **consumer promotion creates pull**, while **trade promotion creates push**. Effective IMC balances both.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU students lose marks in Advertising & Sales Promotion questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing advertising with publicity** (1-mark loss): Advertising is *paid*; publicity is *unpaid*. Mixing them loses a definition mark.
> 2. **Skipping DAGMAR stages** (1-mark loss): Always write all four — Awareness, Comprehension, Conviction, Action — with one example each.
> 3. **Forgetting the formula in numericals** (2-mark loss): Always state $B = r \times S$ or $B = \sum C_i \times Q_i$ before substituting values.
> 4. **Missing the comment / interpretation** (2-mark loss): KTU gives marks for *interpreting* the computed value. For CPM, say which is more efficient. For SOV, say whether it exceeds market share.
> 5. **One-line answers for 7-mark questions** (3-mark loss): Part (a) of 7 marks needs **at least 4-5 paragraphs** with an introduction, body, examples, and conclusion.
> 6. **Confusing ATL/BTL** (1-mark loss): Advertising is mostly ATL; Sales promotion is BTL — but digital advertising blurs the line. Use the correct tag.
> 7. **Not mentioning Surrogate Advertising** (1-mark loss): In India, this is a KTU favourite short-answer.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision — Advertising & Sales Promotions**

**Core Definitions:**
- Advertising = paid, non-personal, mass-media, identified sponsor.
- Sales Promotion = short-term incentive to accelerate purchase.

**The IMC Promotion Mix:** Advertising + Personal Selling + Sales Promotion + PR + Direct Marketing + Digital.

**DAGMAR Model:** Awareness → Comprehension → Conviction → Action (ACCA).

**AIDA Model:** Attention → Interest → Desire → Action (AIDA); modern extension AIDAS adds Satisfaction.

**Types of Advertising (must remember all):**
- By audience: Consumer, Industrial, Trade, Professional.
- By function: Informative, Persuasive, Reminder, Reinforcement (DAGMAR).
- By style: Product, Institutional, Comparative, Pioneer, Competitive, Surrogate.

**Ad Budget Methods (4 types):**
- Percentage of Sales: $B = r \cdot S$.
- Objective-and-Task: $B = \sum C_i \cdot Q_i$.
- Competitive Parity: $B = \alpha \cdot C_{comp}$.
- Affordable: residual cash.

**Key Metrics to remember:**
- $\text{CPM} = \text{Cost} / (\text{Reach}/1000)$.
- $\text{SOV} = (\text{Brand Ad Spend}/\text{Total Category Ad Spend}) \times 100$.
- $\text{Redemption Rate} = (\text{Coupons Redeemed}/\text{Coupons Distributed}) \times 100$.
- $\text{Ad Elasticity} = \%\Delta \text{Sales} / \%\Delta \text{Ad Spend}$.

**Sales Promotion Tools (Consumer):** Samples, Coupons, Premiums, Cash Refunds, Price-Off, Loyalty, Contests, Free Trials, POP Displays.

**Sales Promotion Tools (Trade):** Trade Discount, Trade Allowance, Co-op Ad, Dealer Contests, Free Goods, Display Allowances.

**Sales Promotion Tools (Sales-Force):** Contests, Bonuses, Training, Recognition.

**Critical Comparisons to remember:**
- Advertising vs. Sales Promotion (long-term vs. short-term; brand vs. incentive).
- ATL vs. BTL (mass vs. targeted).
- Push vs. Pull strategy (trade promotion vs. consumer promotion).
- CPM vs. CPRP (print vs. TV).

**Effectiveness Measurement:**
- Communication Effect: Aided Recall, Unaided Recall, DAR, Attitude Tests.
- Sales Effect: Pre-Post Sales, Regression, Experimental Design.

**Modern Extensions to know:**
- Digital Advertising, Influencer Marketing, Programmatic Ads, Social Media Ads.
- IMC (Integrated Marketing Communication) — coordinating all tools under one unified message.

> **Final Memory Hook (Golden Rule):** *Advertising builds the BRAND; Sales Promotion builds the SALE.*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Pricing Objectives, Policies and Methods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Pricing Objectives, Policies and Methods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: CORE TECHNICAL DEFINITION & INTUITIVE OVERVIEW

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Pricing** is the marketing activity wherein the organization establishes a monetary value or exchange ratio at which a product or service is offered to the target market. As a component of the **Marketing Mix (4 P's: Product, Price, Place, Promotion)**, price is the only element that generates revenue; all others represent cost. Pricing decisions are strategic in nature because they directly influence a firm's **revenue streams, profitability, market positioning, and competitive standing**.

**Pricing Objectives** are specific, measurable goals that a firm seeks to achieve through its pricing strategy over a defined planning horizon. They serve as the foundational rationale guiding all subsequent pricing decisions.

**Pricing Policies** are the broad organizational guidelines, philosophies, and decision frameworks that institutionalize the firm's approach to price-setting, ensuring consistency across product lines, market segments, and time periods.

**Pricing Methods** are the systematic, analytical, and procedural techniques used by marketers to determine the actual numerical price of a product or service, factoring in costs, demand, competition, and perceived value.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The student must clearly distinguish between *Objectives* (the "WHY" of pricing), *Policies* (the "WHAT RULES govern pricing"), and *Methods* (the "HOW" of calculating the actual price). Examiners frequently test this three-tier hierarchy.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of pricing like **selling a cup of filter coffee in Kerala** ☕:

- **Pricing Objective** = The *reason* you are selling coffee. Are you trying to make maximum profit (premium artisanal café), or are you trying to attract as many customers as possible to gain market share (the local *kalyana stall* that sells at ₹10)?
- **Pricing Policy** = The *rules* you follow. Do you keep the price *fixed* (like an airport coffee shop) or *flexible* (like a tea stall that charges less in the morning and more in the evening)? Do you give discounts to regular customers (loyalty policy)?
- **Pricing Method** = The *formula* you use to decide the number. You might add a 30% markup on the cost of milk, sugar, and powder (cost-plus method), or you might match what the shop next door charges (competition-based method).

This three-layer understanding — *Objective → Policy → Method* — is the exact flow a KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark descriptive answer.

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Marketing Metric:** Price is typically expressed as a **monetary value per unit (₹/unit, $/unit)** or as a **percentage markup over cost (%)**. **Price elasticity of demand (Ep)** is dimensionless and is the standard sensitivity metric in pricing analysis.

### 1.3 The Strategic Position of Pricing in the Marketing Mix

| Element of 4 P's | Nature | Role in Firm |
|------------------|--------|--------------|
| Product | Tangible/Offering | Creates Value |
| **Price** | **Monetary** | **Captures Value (Revenue Generator)** |
| Place | Distribution | Delivers Value |
| Promotion | Communication | Communicates Value |

**Key Insight:** Price is the **only reversible element** of the marketing mix in the short term. Product features, distribution channels, and promotional campaigns take months to alter; price can be changed overnight. This flexibility gives pricing enormous strategic leverage.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU HIGH-YIELD FORMULA SHEET

### 2.1 Pricing Objectives — The Six Classical Categories

Pricing objectives are the **end-results** a marketer wants to achieve. Philip Kotler's framework identifies the following six principal categories:

#### A. Profit-Oriented Objectives
- **Profit Maximization:** Setting price to generate the highest possible absolute profit. Marginal Revenue (MR) = Marginal Cost (MC) is the theoretical optimum.
- **Target Return on Investment (ROI):** Setting price to achieve a predetermined percentage return on the firm's investment. Common in large corporations.
- **Early Cash Recovery:** Aggressive high-volume, low-margin pricing to recover initial investment rapidly.

#### B. Sales-Oriented Objectives
- **Sales Growth:** Pricing to maximize unit sales volume or revenue growth, often at the expense of short-term profit.
- **Sales Maximization (Sales Volume Goal):** Pricing to achieve the largest possible market share, even at break-even or low margins.

#### C. Market-Share Oriented Objectives
- **Market Penetration:** Setting a low introductory price to attract a large customer base rapidly.
- **Market Skimming:** Setting a high initial price to "skim" the top of the demand curve (innovators/early adopters), then gradually lowering it.

#### D. Competitor-Oriented Objectives
- Pricing to match, undercut, or stay above competitor prices. The objective is **competitive stability** rather than market leadership.

#### E. Customer-Oriented / Status-Quo Objectives
- **Meeting Competition:** Pricing to maintain the existing competitive balance.
- **Customer Retention:** Pricing to keep existing customers loyal (loyalty discounts, bundling).

#### F. Image / Positioning Objectives
- **Premium / Luxury Image:** High price to signal quality and exclusivity.
- **Value Image:** Moderate price to signal "best value for money."

### 2.2 Pricing Policies — The Institutional Rules

Policies are the **operational guidelines** that govern day-to-day pricing decisions. They provide continuity and discipline.

#### Classification of Pricing Policies:

1. **Price-Flexibility Policy**
   - *One-Price Policy:* Same price for all customers under similar conditions. (Most common in retail — Big Bazaar, Lulu Mall).
   - *Variable-Price Policy:* Price negotiated with each customer. (B2B, automobile showrooms, real estate).

2. **Price-Level Policy (Over Customer Groups)**
   - *Differential Pricing:* Different prices for different customer segments (student discounts, senior citizen discounts, geographical pricing).

3. **Price-Level Policy (Over Products)**
   - *Product-Line Pricing:* Different prices for items within a single product line (iPhone 15 vs. iPhone 15 Pro).
   - *Optional-Product Pricing:* Pricing of accessories/attachments (printer + cartridges).
   - *Captive-Product Pricing:* Main product priced low; consumables priced high (Gillette razors).
   - *By-Product Pricing:* Selling by-products to recover waste cost (petroleum refining).

4. **Price-Change Policy**
   - *Discount Policy:* Cash, quantity, trade, seasonal discounts.
   - *Allowance Policy:* Promotional allowance, advertising allowance to dealers.
   - *Price-Adjustment Strategies:* Psychological, promotional, geographical.

5. **Psychological Pricing Policy**
   - *Odd-Pricing:* ₹99, ₹499 (perceived as much cheaper).
   - *Prestige Pricing:* Round numbers like ₹1000 (signals quality).

### 2.3 Pricing Methods — The Three Broad Approaches

Pricing methods are the **mathematical/analytical procedures** for setting the actual price.

#### A. Cost-Based Methods (Internal-Focus)
1. **Cost-Plus Pricing (Mark-up Pricing):**
   $$P = C \times (1 + m)$$
   Where: $P$ = Price, $C$ = Unit Cost, $m$ = Markup percentage.
2. **Break-Even Pricing:** Price at which Total Revenue = Total Cost (zero profit, zero loss).
3. **Target Return Pricing:** Price to achieve a specified ROI.

#### B. Demand-Based Methods (Customer-Focus)
1. **Skimming Pricing:** High initial price, lowered over time.
2. **Penetration Pricing:** Low initial price to gain market share.
3. **Perceived-Value Pricing:** Price based on customer perception of value (not cost).
4. **Price Lining:** Setting a limited number of price points (e.g., shirts at ₹500, ₹1000, ₹2000).

#### C. Competition-Based Methods (Market-Focus)
1. **Going-Rate Pricing:** Price based on prevailing market rate.
2. **Sealed-Bid Pricing (Tendering):** Price set by competitive bidding.
3. **Customary Pricing:** Price dictated by tradition/custom (₹5 toffee, ₹10 cola).

#### D. Value-Based Pricing (Modern Approach)
- Price is anchored firmly to the **value delivered** to the customer rather than to cost. Common in SaaS, luxury, and technology markets.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbol Meaning | Units / Type | When to Use |
|---|-------------------|----------------|--------------|-------------|
| 1 | $P = C \times (1 + m)$ | P: Price, C: Cost, m: Markup | ₹ per unit, % | Cost-Plus / Markup Method |
| 2 | $BE = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$ | BE: Break-Even Units, FC: Fixed Cost, VC: Variable Cost per unit | Units, ₹ | Break-Even Pricing |
| 3 | $TR = P \times Q$ | TR: Total Revenue, Q: Quantity | ₹ | Revenue Calculation |
| 4 | $Q_d = a - bP$ | Linear Demand Curve | Units | Demand-Based Analysis |
| 5 | $E_p = \dfrac{\% \Delta Q_d}{\% \Delta P}$ | Price Elasticity of Demand | Dimensionless | Demand Sensitivity |
| 6 | $P_{opt} = \dfrac{DC + (1 - b)C_{m}}{2b}$ | Optimal Price (for a demand function $Q = \frac{a}{P} - b$) | ₹ per unit | Profit Maximization |
| 7 | $ROI = \dfrac{Net\ Profit}{Investment} \times 100$ | Target Return Pricing | % | Target Return Method |
| 8 | $MC = MR$ | Marginal Cost = Marginal Revenue | ₹ | Profit Maximization Rule |
| 9 | $Q_d = f(P, I, P_r, T, A)$ | General Demand Function | — | Theoretical Demand |
| 10 | $MP = \dfrac{P - C}{P} \times 100$ | Mark-up Percentage on Selling Price | % | Mark-up Analysis |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering/Management Utility:** In B.Tech projects (especially industrial engineering and MBA cross-functional work), cost-plus pricing is the basis of **tendering and contract costing**. Break-even analysis is used in **production planning** and **plant layout decisions**. Price elasticity informs **product launch strategies** in tech and consumer goods industries.

### 2.5 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Pricing Method

The selection of a pricing method is contingent on the **firm's objective**, **stage in product life cycle**, and **competitive structure**:

- If **objective is survival** → use *Penetration Pricing*.
- If **objective is profit max** → use *Skimming* (innovative products) or *Cost-Plus* (commodities).
- If **objective is market share** → use *Going-Rate* or *Penetration*.
- If **objective is image/positioning** → use *Perceived-Value Pricing*.
- If **objective is competitive parity** → use *Going-Rate Pricing*.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## SECTION 3: STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

### 3.1 Derivation of the Cost-Plus Pricing Formula

**Problem Context:** A KTU-registered student-run startup produces hand-made circuits. Variable cost per unit is ₹40, fixed cost is ₹60,000, expected production is 6,000 units, and the firm wants a 25% markup on cost.

**Step 1: Define the variables symbolically.**

$$C = VC + \frac{FC}{Q}$$

Where $C$ = total unit cost, $VC$ = variable cost per unit, $FC$ = total fixed cost, $Q$ = expected quantity.

**Step 2: Substitute the values.**

$$C = 40 + \frac{60{,}000}{6{,}000} = 40 + 10 = 50$$

**Step 3: Apply the Cost-Plus Pricing formula.**

$$P = C \times (1 + m)$$

Where $m$ = markup fraction. With $m = 25\% = 0.25$:

$$P = 50 \times (1 + 0.25) = 50 \times 1.25 = 62.5$$

**Step 4: Conclude.**

$$\boxed{P = ₹62.5 \text{ per unit}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Examination Tip:** If the question states "markup on *selling price*" instead of "markup on *cost*", the formula becomes $m_{sp} = \dfrac{P - C}{P} \times 100$. This is a common KTU trap.

### 3.2 Derivation of the Break-Even Point

**Problem Context:** Fixed cost = ₹1,00,000. Variable cost per unit = ₹20. Selling price per unit = ₹50. Find the break-even quantity in units and in revenue.

**Step 1: State the Break-Even Condition.**

At break-even, Total Revenue = Total Cost.

$$TR = TC$$

**Step 2: Expand both sides using their cost-revenue components.**

$$P \times Q = FC + (VC \times Q)$$

Where $P$ = price per unit, $Q$ = quantity, $FC$ = fixed cost, $VC$ = variable cost per unit.

**Step 3: Rearrange the equation to solve for Q.**

$$PQ - VC \cdot Q = FC$$

$$Q(P - VC) = FC$$

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - VC}$$

**Step 4: Substitute the numerical values.**

$$Q_{BE} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{50 - 20} = \frac{1{,}00{,}000}{30} = 3{,}333.33 \text{ units}$$

**Step 5: Compute Break-Even Revenue.**

$$TR_{BE} = P \times Q_{BE} = 50 \times 3{,}333.33 = ₹1{,}66{,}666.67$$

**Step 6: Verify.**

$$TC = FC + VC \cdot Q = 1{,}00{,}000 + 20 \times 3{,}333.33 = 1{,}00{,}000 + 66{,}666.67 = ₹1{,}66{,}666.67 \;\checkmark$$

> [!NOTE]
> The contribution margin per unit is $P - VC = ₹30$. The firm recovers all fixed costs once it sells 3,334 units.

### 3.3 Derivation of Price Elasticity of Demand (Ped) — Point Method

**Problem Context:** Demand function is $Q_d = 10{,}000 - 200P$. Find the price elasticity of demand at $P = ₹20$.

**Step 1: Find quantity demanded at P = 20.**

$$Q_d = 10{,}000 - 200(20) = 10{,}000 - 4{,}000 = 6{,}000 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: Apply the Point Elasticity Formula.**

$$E_p = \frac{dQ}{dP} \times \frac{P}{Q}$$

**Step 3: Compute the derivative of the demand function.**

$$\frac{dQ}{dP} = -200$$

**Step 4: Substitute all values.**

$$E_p = -200 \times \frac{20}{6{,}000} = -200 \times 0.00333 = -0.667$$

**Step 5: Interpret.**

$$\vert E_p \vert = 0.667 < 1$$

Since elasticity is less than 1, demand is **inelastic** at this price. A price increase will *increase* total revenue.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Examiner Pattern:** Always include the **interpretation** ("elastic / inelastic / unit-elastic / perfectly elastic") along with the numerical value. A value without interpretation often attracts a 1-mark deduction.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Pricing Methods at a Glance

| Method | Key Driver | Formula | Best For | Risk |
|--------|-----------|---------|----------|------|
| Cost-Plus | Internal Cost | $P = C(1+m)$ | Commodities, manufacturing | Ignores demand & competition |
| Break-Even | Cost-Volume | $Q = \frac{FC}{P-VC}$ | Project planning, capacity decisions | Does not target profit |
| Target Return | ROI Goal | $P = \frac{FC + (ROI \times I)}{Q} + VC$ | Public utilities, capital-intensive firms | Demand may not support the price |
| Skimming | Innovator Demand | Market-led | New tech / luxury launches | Alienates mass market |
| Penetration | Mass Adoption | Cost-led low | FMCG, network-effect products | Thin margins, slow profit recovery |
| Going-Rate | Competitor Price | Match market | Oligopolistic markets | No differentiation |
| Perceived-Value | Customer Value | Survey + segment | Premium / differentiated brands | Costly market research |
| Auction / Sealed-Bid | Competitive Bid | Win-the-contract logic | Government tenders, B2B | Race-to-the-bottom pricing |

### 3.5 Worked Numerical Example — Target Return Pricing

**Problem:** A firm invests ₹5,00,000 in equipment. It wants a 20% return on investment. Fixed cost = ₹1,50,000. Variable cost per unit = ₹30. Expected sales = 10,000 units. Find the price per unit.

**Step 1: Define the Target Return formula.**

$$P = \frac{FC + (ROI \times I)}{Q} + VC$$

Where $I$ = investment, $ROI$ = required return, $Q$ = expected unit sales.

**Step 2: Compute the required absolute return.**

$$ROI \times I = 0.20 \times 5{,}00{,}000 = 1{,}00{,}000$$

**Step 3: Compute the per-unit allocation of FC + target return.**

$$\frac{1{,}50{,}000 + 1{,}00{,}000}{10{,}000} = \frac{2{,}50{,}000}{10{,}000} = 25$$

**Step 4: Add the variable cost.**

$$P = 25 + 30 = ₹55 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 5: Verify profitability.**

$$TR = 55 \times 10{,}000 = ₹5{,}50{,}000$$
$$TC = 1{,}50{,}000 + 30 \times 10{,}000 = 4{,}50{,}000$$
$$\text{Profit} = 1{,}00{,}000 = 20\% \text{ of } 5{,}00{,}000 \;\checkmark$$
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

### 4.1 Pricing Decision Hierarchy Flowchart

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Firm Identifies Pricing Objective] --> B{Primary Goal?}
    B -->|Maximize Profit| C[Profit-Oriented Pricing]
    B -->|Maximize Sales| D[Sales-Oriented Pricing]
    B -->|Build Share| E[Market-Share Pricing]
    B -->|Match Competitors| F[Competition-Oriented]
    B -->|Customer Loyalty| G[Customer-Oriented]
    B -->|Premium Image| H[Image/Positioning]
    C --> I[Set Pricing Policy Framework]
    D --> I
    E --> I
    F --> I
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{Select Pricing Method}
    J -->|Cost Focus| K[Cost-Plus / Break-Even / Target Return]
    J -->|Demand Focus| L[Skimming / Penetration / Perceived Value]
    J -->|Competition Focus| M[Going-Rate / Sealed-Bid / Customary]
    K --> N[Final Price Decision]
    L --> N
    M --> N
    N --> O[Monitor, Adjust, Re-evaluate]
```

### 4.2 Three-Tier Pricing Architecture (Objective → Policy → Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    Obj[PRICING OBJECTIVE]:::tier1 --> Pol[PRICING POLICY]:::tier2
    Pol --> Met[PRICING METHOD]:::tier3
    Met --> Fin[FINAL PRICE]:::final

    classDef tier1 fill:#FFE4B5,stroke:#FF8C00,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef tier2 fill:#B0E0E6,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef tier3 fill:#98FB98,stroke:#228B22,stroke-width:3px,color:#000
    classDef final fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#DC143C,stroke-width:4px,color:#000
```

### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Pricing Method Selection Engine

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Input: Cost Data FC and VC] --> B[Cost-Plus Module]
    A2[Input: Demand Function Qd] --> C[Demand-Based Module]
    A3[Input: Competitor Prices] --> D[Competition-Based Module]
    A4[Input: Customer Surveys] --> E[Value-Based Module]
    B --> F[Output: Markup Price]
    C --> G[Output: Skim or Penetrate Price]
    D --> H[Output: Going-Rate Price]
    E --> I[Output: Perceived-Value Price]
    F --> J[Price Evaluation Block]
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Price Acceptable?}
    K -->|Yes| L[Finalize Price]
    K -->|No| M[Adjust Inputs and Recompute]
    M --> B
    M --> C
    M --> D
    M --> E
```

### 4.4 Pricing Strategy Across Product Life Cycle

```mermaid
graph LR
    I[INTRODUCTION STAGE] -->|High Price, Low Volume| SK[SKIMMING]
    I -->|Low Price, High Volume| PN[PENETRATION]
    G[GROWTH STAGE] --> GR[Competitive Pricing as Demand Rises]
    M[MATURITY STAGE] --> MT[Discount Pricing and Loyalty Schemes]
    D[DECLINE STAGE] --> DC[Harvest or Divest Pricing]
```

### 4.5 Demand Curve and Price Elasticity Schematic (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Demand Curve Qd = a - bP] --> B{Compute Ep}
    B -->|Ep > 1| C[ELASTIC - Price Cut Increases Revenue]
    B -->|Ep = 1| D[UNIT ELASTIC - Revenue Constant]
    B -->|Ep < 1| E[INELASTIC - Price Cut Decreases Revenue]
    C --> F[Recommendation: Lower Price to Grow Sales]
    D --> G[Recommendation: Maintain Price]
    E --> H[Recommendation: Raise Price to Maximize Revenue]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Block Diagram Logic:** In management engineering and operations research, these block diagrams parallel *control-system feedback loops*. The "Pricing Method Selection Engine" mirrors a closed-loop control system where price is the output, market/cost/competition data are inputs, and customer response is the feedback signal.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
*Define "Pricing Objective." List any four major categories of pricing objectives.*

**Model Answer:**

A **Pricing Objective** is a specific, measurable goal that a firm aims to achieve through its pricing strategy over a defined planning horizon. It defines *why* a firm is setting a particular price.

**Four major categories of pricing objectives:**

1. **Profit-Oriented Objectives:** Profit maximization, target return on investment.
2. **Sales-Oriented Objectives:** Sales growth, sales volume maximization.
3. **Market-Share Oriented Objectives:** Market penetration, market skimming.
4. **Competitor-Oriented Objectives:** Pricing to match or counter competitors.
5. **Image / Positioning Objectives:** Premium image, value-for-money image.
6. **Customer-Oriented Objectives:** Customer retention, status-quo pricing.

*(Any four well-explained points fetch 3 marks.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - June 2024, Supplementary]**
*Differentiate between **Cost-Plus Pricing** and **Perceived-Value Pricing**.*

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Cost-Plus Pricing | Perceived-Value Pricing |
|-----------|------------------|------------------------|
| **Basis** | Internal production cost | Customer's perceived value of product |
| **Formula** | $P = C \times (1 + m)$ | Based on customer surveys and segmentation |
| **Customer Focus** | Low — backward looking | High — forward looking |
| **Risk** | Ignores demand & competition | Requires extensive market research |
| **Best For** | Commodities, standardized goods | Differentiated, branded, premium products |
| **Flexibility** | Low — cost-driven | High — value-driven |

Cost-plus is *supplier-oriented*, whereas perceived-value is *customer-oriented* and is increasingly preferred in modern markets (SaaS, luxury, healthcare).

---

### 5.2 Part B — Descriptive Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **KTU 2024 Exam Pattern:** Each 14-mark question has **two sub-parts of 7 marks each**. The internal choice (Q-A or Q-B) is *within the same module* and is *not optional between full questions* — both sub-parts must be answered.

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Main Exam]**

**(a)** Explain the major **pricing objectives** a firm may adopt. Discuss how the choice of objective influences the pricing method selected. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A manufacturing company produces a product at a variable cost of ₹30 per unit. Its fixed costs are ₹2,00,000 per year. The firm expects to sell 8,000 units annually. The firm desires a 25% markup on cost.
- (i) Compute the selling price per unit. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Calculate the break-even quantity in units. **(3 Marks)**
- (iii) If the firm wants a target profit of ₹80,000, what quantity must it sell? **(1 Mark)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Pricing Objectives and Their Influence on Pricing Method:**

Pricing objectives are the *end-states* a marketer aims to achieve. The major categories are:

1. **Profit-Oriented Objectives:** Firms aiming for *profit maximization* often use *demand-based* methods such as *price skimming* (for innovative products) or *variable pricing*. Firms targeting a *specific ROI* use *target return pricing*.

2. **Sales-Oriented Objectives:** Firms focusing on *sales growth or volume maximization* typically employ *penetration pricing* (low initial price) or *psychological pricing* (₹99, ₹499).

3. **Market-Share Objectives:** To *build market share*, firms use *penetration pricing* or *going-rate pricing* (matching competitor). To *skim* the high-end segment, *price skimming* is used.

4. **Competitor-Oriented Objectives:** Firms aiming for *competitive stability* use *going-rate pricing* or *sealed-bid pricing* in tendering.

5. **Image / Positioning Objectives:** Premium brands use *prestige pricing*; value brands use *psychological pricing* and *price lining*.

6. **Customer-Oriented Objectives:** Retention-led firms use *loyalty discounts, captive-product pricing,* and *bundling*.

**Influence of Objective on Method (Summary Table for 3 Marks):**

| Objective | Recommended Method |
|-----------|--------------------|
| Profit Maximization | Skimming, Perceived-Value |
| Target ROI | Target Return Pricing |
| Market Share | Penetration, Going-Rate |
| Premium Image | Prestige, Perceived-Value |
| Survival | Cost-Plus, Break-Even |

**Conclusion:** The pricing *objective* acts as the *input parameter* that selects the appropriate pricing *method*, which then determines the final *price*. This three-step flow is the *strategic pricing hierarchy* examined in all major KTU questions. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Selling Price per Unit (3 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute unit cost.**
$$C = VC + \frac{FC}{Q} = 30 + \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{8{,}000} = 30 + 25 = ₹55$$

**Step 2: Apply cost-plus pricing.**
$$P = C \times (1 + m) = 55 \times (1 + 0.25) = 55 \times 1.25 = ₹68.75 \text{ per unit}$$

**Step 3: Conclude.**
$$\boxed{P = ₹68.75 \text{ per unit}}$$

---

**(ii) Break-Even Quantity (3 Marks):**

**Step 1: Apply break-even formula.**
$$Q_{BE} = \frac{FC}{P - VC} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{68.75 - 30} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000}{38.75}$$

**Step 2: Compute.**
$$Q_{BE} = 5{,}161.29 \approx 5{,}162 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Conclude.**
$$\boxed{Q_{BE} \approx 5{,}162 \text{ units}}$$

---

**(iii) Target Profit Quantity (1 Mark):**

**Step 1: Apply target profit formula.**
$$Q = \frac{FC + \text{Target Profit}}{P - VC} = \frac{2{,}00{,}000 + 80{,}000}{68.75 - 30} = \frac{2{,}80{,}000}{38.75}$$

**Step 2: Compute.**
$$Q = 7{,}225.8 \approx 7{,}226 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3: Conclude.**
$$\boxed{Q_{target} = 7{,}226 \text{ units}}$$

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks)**
**[KTU University Exam - June 2023, Supplementary]**

**(a)** Discuss the major **pricing methods** in detail. Compare cost-based, demand-based, and competition-based methods with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The demand function for a product is given by $Q_d = 1{,}200 - 40P$.
- (i) Find the price elasticity of demand at $P = ₹15$. **(3 Marks)**
- (ii) Is the demand elastic or inelastic at this price? Justify. **(2 Marks)**
- (iii) If the firm wants to maximize revenue, should it raise or lower the price from ₹15? Why? **(2 Marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Major Pricing Methods — Detailed Discussion:**

**1. Cost-Based Methods (Internal-Focus):**

- **Cost-Plus Pricing:** $P = C(1+m)$. Simple, ensures cost recovery. Used in *manufacturing, retail, government contracts*.
- **Break-Even Pricing:** $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P-VC}$. Used in *project appraisal* and *capacity planning*.
- **Target Return Pricing:** Used by *public utilities* and *capital-intensive industries* (e.g., power, telecom) to ensure a specified ROI.

*Example: A furniture maker adding 30% markup on ₹2,000 cost charges ₹2,600.*

**2. Demand-Based Methods (Customer-Focus):**

- **Skimming Pricing:** High initial price targeting innovators. *Example: iPhone launch at ₹1,40,000*.
- **Penetration Pricing:** Low initial price to gain market share. *Example: Jio 4G at ₹399 plan in 2016*.
- **Perceived-Value Pricing:** Price based on customer perception. *Example: Maggi, Coca-Cola, premium coffee brands*.
- **Price Lining:** Limited price points for product variants. *Example: T-shirts at ₹500, ₹1,000, ₹2,000*.

**3. Competition-Based Methods (Market-Focus):**

- **Going-Rate Pricing:** Price set equal to market leader. Common in *oligopolies* (cement, steel, telecom).
- **Sealed-Bid Pricing:** Price quoted competitively. *Example: Government tenders, construction bids*.
- **Customary Pricing:** Price fixed by convention. *Example: ₹5 chocolate, ₹10 cold drink*.

**Comparison Table (for 3 marks):**

| Aspect | Cost-Based | Demand-Based | Competition-Based |
|--------|-----------|--------------|-------------------|
| Anchor | Internal cost | Customer willingness to pay | Market/competitor |
| Information Needed | Low | High | Medium |
| Strategic Agility | Low | High | Medium |
| Risk | Demand rejection | Cost under-recovery | Margin erosion |

**Conclusion:** Modern firms use a *blended approach*, often starting with *value-based* pricing and adjusting using *cost and competition* benchmarks. **(7 Marks)**

---

**(b) Numerical Solution:**

**(i) Price Elasticity at P = ₹15 (3 Marks):**

**Step 1: Compute $Q_d$ at $P = 15$.**
$$Q_d = 1{,}200 - 40(15) = 1{,}200 - 600 = 600 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2: Compute the derivative $\dfrac{dQ}{dP}$.**
$$\frac{dQ}{dP} = -40$$

**Step 3: Apply the point elasticity formula.**
$$E_p = \frac{dQ}{dP} \times \frac{P}{Q} = -40 \times \frac{15}{600} = -40 \times 0.025 = -1.0$$

**Step 4: Conclude.**
$$\boxed{E_p = -1.0}$$

---

**(ii) Elasticity Interpretation (2 Marks):**

Since $\vert E_p \vert = 1.0$, demand is **unit-elastic** at ₹15. A 1% increase in price leads to exactly a 1% decrease in quantity demanded. **Total revenue remains constant** in the immediate vicinity of this price.

---

**(iii) Pricing Recommendation (2 Marks):**

At unit elasticity, total revenue is at its **maximum possible value**. Any price change in either direction (up or down) will *decrease* total revenue.

**Recommendation:** The firm should **maintain the price at ₹15** because at this point revenue is already maximized. Lowering it below ₹15 will make demand elastic (revenue falls), and raising it above ₹15 will make demand inelastic (revenue also falls).

**Mathematical Justification (for higher marks):**
$$TR = P \times Q = P(1200 - 40P) = 1200P - 40P^2$$
$$\frac{d(TR)}{dP} = 1200 - 80P = 0 \implies P^* = ₹15$$
$$\text{Maximum } TR = 15 \times 600 = ₹9{,}000 \;\checkmark$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Warning:**
> 1. **Confusing markup on cost with markup on selling price** — these give *different* percentages. Always read the question carefully.
> 2. **Forgetting to interpret elasticity** — a numerical value *without* "elastic / inelastic / unit-elastic" wording loses 1 mark.
> 3. **Mixing up objective, policy, and method** — examiners ask "define pricing objective" but students often write the *method* definition. Memorize the hierarchy: Objective (Why) → Policy (What rules) → Method (How).
> 4. **Not showing derivation steps** — even for a 3-mark problem, *showing the formula and substitution* earns full marks. A bare answer "₹68.75" with no working may get partial credit only.
> 5. **Ignoring the choice question's intent** — Question A and Question B in Part B are *alternatives*, but each has *two sub-parts* that *both* must be answered. Do not skip sub-part (b).

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ **Pricing is the only revenue-generating "P"** of the marketing mix.
- ✅ The **three-tier hierarchy** is: **Objective → Policy → Method**. Always answer in this logical flow.
- ✅ The **six pricing objectives** are: Profit, Sales, Market Share, Competitor, Customer, Image.
- ✅ The **three major pricing policies** are: Price-Flexibility, Price-Level, and Price-Change policies.
- ✅ The **three broad pricing methods** are: Cost-Based, Demand-Based, Competition-Based (with Value-Based as a modern fourth).
- ✅ **Cost-Plus Formula:** $P = C(1+m)$. Watch for "markup on cost" vs. "markup on selling price."
- ✅ **Break-Even Formula:** $Q_{BE} = \dfrac{FC}{P - VC}$.
- ✅ **Target Return Formula:** $P = \dfrac{FC + (ROI \times I)}{Q} + VC$.
- ✅ **Elasticity Formula:** $E_p = \dfrac{dQ}{dP} \times \dfrac{P}{Q}$. Always interpret: $\vert E_p \vert > 1$ = elastic, $= 1$ = unit-elastic, $< 1$ = inelastic.
- ✅ **Profit Maximization Rule:** $MR = MC$ (or equivalently, $E_p = -1$).
- ✅ **Skimming** = high initial price; **Penetration** = low initial price. Know at least one real-world example for each.
- ✅ **Psychological pricing** (₹99, ₹499) works because of the *left-digit effect* in consumer perception.
- ✅ **Captive-product pricing** (low main product, high consumables) is the basis of business models like HP printers, Gillette, and gaming consoles.
- ✅ In KTU 14-mark answers, use a **comparison table** for the 3-mark portion of any "compare" or "differentiate" sub-question — tables are a top-scoring technique.
- ✅ For numericals, always show: (1) given data, (2) formula, (3) substitution, (4) final boxed answer, (5) interpretation.
- ✅ Common KTU traps: confusing policy with method; listing methods without classification; not stating the *objective* when explaining a *method*.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Understanding Industrial and Consumer Buyer Behavior
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Understanding Industrial and Consumer Buyer Behavior

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# Module 4 — Understanding Industrial and Consumer Buyer Behavior

## 1.1 Core Definitions & Intuitive Overview

### A. Consumer Buyer Behavior (B2C Context)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Consumer Buyer Behavior** refers to the *buying behavior* of final consumers — individuals and households who buy goods and services for personal consumption. It studies *how* consumers make decisions to spend their available resources (time, money, effort) on consumption-related items, and *why* they act in particular ways during the buying process.

**The phrase "final consumers" is critical here** — it distinguishes consumer behavior from industrial behavior. A consumer purchases a product *for self-use or family use*, not to manufacture, resell, or operate another business.

**Intuitive Analogy — "The College Student's Phone Purchase"**

Imagine *Ananya*, a final-year B.Tech student in Kochi, deciding to buy a smartphone worth ₹25,000. She does not just "buy a phone." She:

- Reads YouTube reviews for **two weeks** (information search)
- Asks her *friends and sister* which phone has a good camera (social influence)
- Feels her *parents* expect her to pick a "value-for-money" brand (family/cultural influence)
- Ultimately chooses the *Samsung Galaxy A55* because of brand trust built over years (psychological: attitude, perception)
- Then feels a mild **post-purchase doubt** — "Did I make the right choice?" (cognitive dissonance)

This *entire chain of mental, emotional, and behavioral events* — from recognizing a need to post-purchase evaluation — is **Consumer Buyer Behavior**.

---

### B. Industrial (Organizational / B2B) Buyer Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition**
> **Industrial Buyer Behavior** (also called *Organizational Buyer Behavior* or *B2B Buyer Behavior*) refers to the decision-making process by which *formal organizations* establish the need for purchased products and services, and then identify, evaluate, and choose among alternative brands and suppliers.

The buyer may be a manufacturing firm, a reseller (wholesaler/retailer), a government body, an institution (hospital, school), or any organization buying goods/services to:
- Produce other goods (raw materials, components)
- Operate the organization (office supplies, IT systems)
- Resell to others (a retailer buying stock)

**Intuitive Analogy — "The IT Manager's Server Purchase"**

Imagine *Mr. Rajiv*, an IT Manager at a private hospital in Trivandrum, who must procure **10 new servers** for a hospital information system. Notice how this differs from Ananya:

- The decision takes **6 months**, involves **multiple people** (IT Manager, CFO, Vendor Committee, Hospital Director), and follows a *formal purchase policy*.
- The buying rationale is **rational-economic + technical**, not emotional.
- Specifications, warranty terms, service-level agreements (SLAs), and *bulk discounts* dominate — *brand image* is secondary.
- Vendors submit *tenders*, *quotations*, and *technical bids* — a structured process.
- Even after purchase, *vendor performance* is continuously monitored.

This *formal, multi-person, evidence-driven, long-gestation decision process* is **Industrial Buyer Behavior**.

---

### C. Why Marketers Study Buyer Behavior — The Core Premise

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**
> The marketer's fundamental premise is that **consumers/organizations will be more satisfied and the organization will be more profitable if the marketer truly understands buyer behavior**. Understanding the "why" behind the "buy" is the central task of modern marketing management.

**Three Ws of Buyer Behavior Study:**

| Question | Implication for the Marketer |
|---|---|
| **Who** buys? | Identify the buyer, the decider, the influencer, the user |
| **What** do they buy? | Understand product features, perceived benefits, bundle composition |
| **Why** do they buy? | Decode motivations, attitudes, perceptions, cultural context, situational triggers |

Only after answering these can a marketer craft the right *Product*, set the right *Price*, choose the right *Place*, and design the right *Promotion* — i.e., the **4 Ps of Marketing**.

---

### D. The Two-Market Classification Framework

Buyer behavior studies split the entire market into **two broad categories**:

$$
\text{Total Market Demand} = \text{Consumer Market (B2C)} + \text{Business / Industrial Market (B2B)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Industrial market in most countries is LARGER than consumer market** — even though it has far fewer buyers. In India, B2B transactions contribute to roughly **65–70% of total economic activity** by value (KTU-recommended figure for conceptual understanding).

The fundamental differences between these two markets are listed in Section 5.

---

### E. Buyer Decision Process — A Universal 5-Stage Model

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal KTU Model (Kotler & Keller, widely adopted in KTU syllabus)**
> Both consumer and industrial buyers traverse a **five-stage decision process**. The model applies universally but the *duration, complexity, and participants* differ dramatically between B2C and B2B.

$$
\text{Need Recognition} \rightarrow \text{Information Search} \rightarrow \text{Evaluation of Alternatives} \rightarrow \text{Purchase Decision} \rightarrow \text{Post-Purchase Behavior}
$$

This model is developed in detail in Section 3.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

## 1.2 Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### A. Models of Consumer Buyer Behavior (Theoretical Foundations)

Marketing theorists have proposed several models to explain *why consumers buy*. KTU examiners frequently ask about **at least three models** in descriptive questions.

#### 1. The Economic Model (Marshallian / Economic Man)

**Logic:** Consumer is a **rational economic decision-maker** who always chooses the product that delivers the *highest utility per rupee spent*.

$$
\text{Decision Choice} = \max_{i \in \text{Alternatives}} \left( \frac{U_i}{P_i} \right)
$$

Where $U_i$ = utility (satisfaction) from alternative $i$, $P_i$ = price of alternative $i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Limitation:** Real consumers are *not* purely rational — emotions, habits, brand loyalty, and social pressure violate this assumption. Hence other models were developed.

#### 2. The Learning Model (Stimulus-Response / Behavioral)

**Logic:** Consumer behavior is a **learned response** to marketing stimuli. The formula is:

$$
\text{Behavior} = f(\text{Drives, Cues, Response, Reinforcement})
$$

The four learning variables:
- **Drive:** A strong internal stimulus (e.g., hunger → food need)
- **Cues:** Minor stimuli that direct drive (e.g., a McDonald's hoarding)
- **Response:** The action taken (entering McDonald's)
- **Reinforcement:** Positive outcome increases repeat behavior

#### 3. The Psychoanalytic Model (Freudian / Motivational Research)

**Logic:** Buyer behavior is shaped by **subconscious motivations, hidden fears, repressed desires, and childhood experiences**. Consumers often *cannot articulate* why they buy.

**Real-world example:** A man buying an *expensive sports car* may rationalize "faster commute," but the *real* psychological drivers could be: status, mid-life expression, childhood fantasy, sexual symbolism.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Tip:** This model explains *motivational research* and *projective techniques* (word association, sentence completion, story-telling tests).

#### 4. The Sociological Model

**Logic:** Buyer behavior is shaped by **social roles, group influences, and cultural pressures** rather than individual psychology.

#### 5. The Howard-Sheth Model (1969)

**Comprehensive integrative model** combining inputs → perceptual learning → outputs:

$$
\text{Inputs} \rightarrow \text{Perceptual Constructs} \rightarrow \text{Learning} \rightarrow \text{Outputs} \rightarrow \text{Exterior Variables}
$$

#### 6. The Nicosia Model (1966)

Field 1: Manufacturer's message → Consumer's attitude
Field 2: Search and evaluation
Field 3: Act of purchase
Field 4: Feedback to manufacturer

#### 7. The Engel-Kollat-Blackwell (EKB) Model

A four-stage *information-processing* model that became the foundation of modern consumer decision theory.

---

### B. The Four Sets of Factors Influencing Consumer Behavior (Kotler — Most Important)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD FRAMEWORK — KOTLER'S 4 FACTORS**
> The marketer must study four sets of factors that influence consumer behavior. This is the *single most frequently asked* framework in KTU Marketing Management exams.

**The Master Equation:**

$$
C = f(C, S, P, Ps)
$$

Where:
- $C$ = Consumer Behavior (dependent variable)
- $C$ = **Cultural** factors
- $S$ = **Social** factors
- $P$ = **Personal** factors
- $Ps$ = **Psychological** factors

#### B.1 Cultural Factors (Most Powerful & Deep-Rooted)

| Sub-factor | Explanation | KTU Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Culture** | The most basic cause of a person's wants and behavior — set of values, perceptions, preferences learned from family and society | Indian preference for *gold* during weddings vs. Western preference for diamonds |
| **Subculture** | Smaller cultures within a culture — religion, nationality, regional groups, age groups | Kerala (Onam Sadya demand spikes), Tamil Nadu (Pongal), Punjab (Baisakhi) |
| **Social Class** | Relatively permanent & ordered divisions in society whose members share similar values, interests, behavior | Upper-middle class prefers premium FMCG, working class prefers value brands |

> [!NOTE]
> Cultural factors operate at the **deepest subconscious level** and are the **most difficult for a marketer to change**.

#### B.2 Social Factors

| Sub-factor | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Reference Groups** | Groups that have direct/indirect influence on attitudes/behavior — membership groups (family, friends, colleagues) and aspirational groups (celebrities, role models) |
| **Family** | Most influential consumer-buying organization in society. Two types: *Family of Orientation* (parents, siblings) and *Family of Procreation* (spouse, children) |
| **Roles and Status** | A person occupies multiple positions in groups — each role conveys a status that influences consumption (e.g., a CEO's car vs. an employee's car) |

#### B.3 Personal Factors

| Sub-factor | Explanation |
|---|---|
| **Age & Life-Cycle Stage** | A 22-year-old bachelor and a 45-year-old father of three have drastically different consumption baskets |
| **Occupation** | A doctor's clothing ≠ a factory worker's clothing |
| **Economic Situation** | Disposable income, savings, borrowing capacity, attitude toward spending |
| **Lifestyle** | A person's pattern of living expressed through activities, interests, opinions (AIOs) |
| **Personality & Self-Concept** | Self-confidence, dominance, sociability, autonomy, defensiveness — each maps to brand choices |

#### B.4 Psychological Factors (The 4 Os)

| Sub-factor | Core Question |
|---|---|
| **Motivation** | *Why* is the person driven to act? Maslow's hierarchy of needs |
| **Perception** | *How* does the person interpret information? Selective attention, distortion, retention |
| **Learning** | What *experiences* shape behavior? Drives, cues, responses, reinforcement |
| **Attitude & Belief** | What *consistent evaluations* does the person hold? Cognitive, affective, behavioral components |

> [!TIP]
> **Perception has four stages: Selective Exposure → Selective Attention → Selective Distortion → Selective Retention.** Remember the acronym **EADS** (Exposure-Attention-Distortion-Retention).

**Maslow's Hierarchy (from bottom to top):**

$$
\text{Physiological} \rightarrow \text{Safety} \rightarrow \text{Social} \rightarrow \text{Esteem} \rightarrow \text{Self-Actualization}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Trick:** Maslow's theory helps marketers identify *which level of need* their product addresses — Coca-Cola = Social, Volvo = Safety, Rolex = Esteem, Art classes = Self-Actualization.

---

### C. Industrial Buyer Behavior — Core Characteristics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Definition — Industrial Buyer Behavior**
> Industrial buyer behavior refers to the *decision-making process* by which formal organizations establish the need for purchased products and services, identify, evaluate, and choose among alternative brands and suppliers.

#### C.1 Structural Features of the Industrial Market (KTU High-Yield Table)

| Feature | Consumer Market (B2C) | Industrial Market (B2B) |
|---|---|---|
| **Number of buyers** | Mass market — millions | Few, large buyers |
| **Market value** | Smaller per transaction, huge in volume | Larger per transaction, fewer in volume |
| **Buyer-seller relationship** | Anonymous, transactional | Close, long-term, partnership |
| **Geographic concentration** | Spread nationwide | Concentrated in industrial belts (e.g., Mumbai-Pune, Bangalore, Chennai, Coimbatore) |
| **Demand nature** | Independent / volatile | **Derived demand** from consumer demand |
| **Demand fluctuation** | Minor swings | **Accelerator effect** — small change in consumer demand causes large swing in industrial demand |
| **Purchase volume** | Small, frequent | Bulk, infrequent |
| **Decision-making unit** | Individual or family | Buying Center / DMU (multiple people) |
| **Decision complexity** | Low to moderate | High — formal procedures |
| **Negotiation & risk** | Low | High — risk of supply disruption |

#### C.2 Derived Demand — The Defining Concept

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept**
> **Derived Demand** states that demand for industrial goods is ultimately *derived* from the demand for consumer goods. If consumer demand for cars falls, demand for steel, rubber, glass, and paint *also falls* — even though consumers don't buy these directly.

$$
D_{\text{industrial}} = f(D_{\text{consumer}})
$$

#### C.3 The Accelerator Effect (Demand Volatility)

$$
\Delta D_{\text{industrial}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta D_{\text{consumer}}
$$

Where $\alpha$ > 1 (the multiplier/accelerator). A 10% drop in consumer car demand may cause a 40–50% drop in demand for tires.

#### C.4 Buying Situations (Three Types — Robinson-Farrell Model)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD — Three Buying Situations in Industrial Buying**

1. **Straight Rebuy** — Routine, repetitive purchase from the same supplier with no modification (e.g., office stationery reorder). Lowest complexity, lowest risk.
2. **Modified Rebuy** — Buyer wants to modify product specs, price, terms, or suppliers. Moderate complexity.
3. **New Task** — First-time purchase requiring major decisions. Highest complexity, longest decision time, most risk, maximum marketer opportunity.

---

### D. The Decision-Making Unit (DMU) / Buying Center

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Critical Concept**
> A *Buying Center* or **Decision-Making Unit (DMU)** is the group of individuals who participate in the industrial buying decision process. They play different roles.

#### D.1 Seven Roles in the DMU (Webster-Wind Model)

| Role | Definition | Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Initiator** | Person who first suggests buying the product | Production Manager |
| **User** | Person who will actually use the product | Machine Operator |
| **Influencer** | Person whose views affect the decision | Quality Engineer |
| **Decider** | Person who finally chooses | Plant Head |
| **Approver** | Person who authorizes the purchase | Director / CFO |
| **Buyer** | Person who has formal authority to execute the purchase | Purchase Manager |
| **Gatekeeper** | Person who controls information flow | Receptionist, Secretary, Purchase Officer |

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Memory Trick: "I-U-I-D-A-B-G"** → **"I Used Impulse Decisions, A Boy Gets"** (Initiator, User, Influencer, Decider, Approver, Buyer, Gatekeeper).

#### D.2 Who Makes the Buying Decision?

The **decider** is not always obvious. In some organizations the *CFO* decides on a ₹5 lakh machine, in others the *Plant Manager* does. The marketer must identify and target *all seven role-holders* through tailored communication.

---

### E. Factors Influencing Industrial Buyer Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD — Four Sets of Factors Influencing Industrial Buying**
> These factors map to the consumer model but operate differently.

$$
B_{\text{ind}} = f(E, O, I, In)
$$

Where:
- $E$ = **Environmental** factors
- $O$ = **Organizational** factors
- $I$ = **Interpersonal** factors
- $In$ = **Individual** factors

#### E.1 Environmental Factors

- Level of primary demand (the overall market outlook)
- Economic outlook (GDP, inflation, interest rates)
- Rate of technological change (e.g., AI transformation)
- Political and regulatory environment (FDI rules, GST, environmental norms)
- Competitive developments
- Social and cultural shifts (ESG, sustainability)

#### E.2 Organizational Factors

- Objectives and strategies of the organization
- Policies and procedures (purchase policy, vendor approval)
- Organizational structure (centralized vs. decentralized purchasing)
- Systems and processes (ERP, e-procurement)

#### E.3 Interpersonal Factors

- Authority, status, empathy, risk-taking ability of participants
- Group dynamics — negotiation tactics, coalition building

#### E.4 Individual Factors

- Age, education, job position, personality, risk attitude
- Personal motivations and incentives

---

### F. The Industrial Buying Process (8 Stages — KTU Mandatory)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Industrial Buying Decision Process — 8 Stages (Robinson-Farrell / Webster-Wind)**

| Stage | Activity |
|---|---|
| **1. Problem Recognition** | Someone in the firm identifies a need (machine breakdown, new project) |
| **2. General Need Description** | Determine the characteristics and quantity of the needed item |
| **3. Product Specification** | Technical specs are written — what features the product must have |
| **4. Supplier Search** | Identify potential vendors |
| **5. Proposal Solicitation** | Invite qualified vendors to submit proposals/tenders |
| **6. Supplier Selection** | Evaluate proposals, visit plants, negotiate terms |
| **7. Order-Routine Specification** | Final order with terms (quantity, delivery, warranty, payment) |
| **8. Performance Review** | Evaluate vendor's actual performance, give feedback |

> [!NOTE]
> In **straight rebuy** situations, some stages are skipped. In **new task** situations, all 8 stages are completed rigorously.

---

### G. KTU High-Yield Formula / Concept Sheet (Master Reference)

| # | Concept / Equation | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | $C = f(C, S, P, Ps)$ | Consumer Behavior Master Equation |
| 2 | $\text{Consumer 5-Stage} = N \rightarrow I \rightarrow E \rightarrow P \rightarrow B$ | Need → Info Search → Eval Alternatives → Purchase → Post-purchase |
| 3 | $B_{\text{ind}} = f(E, O, I, In)$ | Industrial Buyer Behavior Master Equation |
| 4 | $D_{\text{ind}} = f(D_{\text{consumer}})$ | Derived Demand |
| 5 | $\Delta D_{\text{ind}} = \alpha \cdot \Delta D_{\text{consumer}}, \; \alpha > 1$ | Accelerator Effect |
| 6 | $\max_i \left( \frac{U_i}{P_i} \right)$ | Economic Model Decision Rule |
| 7 | $\text{Behavior} = f(\text{Drive, Cues, Response, Reinforcement})$ | Learning Model |
| 8 | Maslow: $\text{Phys} \rightarrow \text{Safe} \rightarrow \text{Soc} \rightarrow \text{Esteem} \rightarrow \text{SA}$ | Motivation Hierarchy |
| 9 | Perceptual filter: $\text{EADS} = \text{Exp} \rightarrow \text{Att} \rightarrow \text{Dist} \rightarrow \text{Ret}$ | Selective Perception |
| 10 | $\text{Industrial Buying Stages} = 8$ | Problem Recognition → Performance Review |

---

### H. Real-World Utility of These Concepts

- **Consumer 4-Factor Model** → Used in STP (Segmentation, Targeting, Positioning). Knowing culture, age, lifestyle helps marketers define *target segments*.
- **Industrial DMU Model** → Used in B2B Key Account Management. Salespeople identify *champions, influencers, blockers* inside each customer.
- **Derived Demand** → Used by industrial goods manufacturers to forecast demand by tracking consumer demand signals.
- **Buying Situations (Straight/Modified/New Task)** → Used to design the *selling process* and *negotiation strategy*.
- **Maslow's Hierarchy** → Used in *advertising copy* to position products at the right need level.

## 1.3 Step-by-Step Buyer Decision Process & Analytical Matrix

### A. The 5-Stage Consumer Buyer Decision Process (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

The Kotler 5-stage model is universal but interpreted differently in B2C vs B2B contexts. Below is the exhaustive breakdown with reasoning at every step.

---

#### Stage 1: Need Recognition

**Definition:** The buyer recognizes a *problem or need* triggered by internal or external stimuli.

**Internal Stimuli:** Hunger, thirst, fatigue, sadness, status-seeking urge.
**External Stimuli:** Advertisement, friend's new phone, weather change (rain → umbrella), packaging display.

**Step-by-step logic:**

$$
\text{Stimulus (Internal/External)} \rightarrow \text{Awareness of Gap} \rightarrow \text{Need Recognition} \rightarrow \text{Buyer State: "I want/need this"}
$$

**KTU Real-World Example:**
*Trigger:* Ananya's phone screen cracked during a fall → Internal stimulus (frustration) + External stimulus (friends showing new phones).
*Need recognized:* "I need a new smartphone."

**Marketer's Role:** Use advertising, packaging, in-store displays, social media trends to **activate needs** — especially dormant ones.

---

#### Stage 2: Information Search

**Definition:** The buyer *searches for information* about how to satisfy the need. There are two levels of search:

$$
\text{Information Search} = 
\begin{cases} 
\text{Heightened Attention (mild search)} \\
\text{Active Information Search (deliberate search)}
\end{cases}
$$

**Sources of Information:**

| Source Type | Examples | Influence Level |
|---|---|---|
| **Personal** | Family, friends, neighbors | **Highest** trust |
| **Commercial** | Advertising, salespersons, packaging, websites | Moderate |
| **Public** | Mass media, consumer review platforms, rating agencies | Moderate |
| **Experiential** | Handling, testing, using the product | **High** for expensive items |

**Marketer's Role:** Make information *abundant, accessible, and credible*. Identify *which sources* the target segment trusts.

---

#### Stage 3: Evaluation of Alternatives

**Definition:** The buyer evaluates *brands, products, and services* against a set of evaluative criteria.

**The Evaluative Process Logic:**

$$
\text{Choice} = \max_{i \in \text{Evoked Set}} \left[ \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot a_{ik} \right]
$$

Where:
- $i$ = brand in the evoked set
- $w_k$ = importance weight of attribute $k$
- $a_{ik}$ = perceived performance of brand $i$ on attribute $k$

**Concepts to know:**

- **Evoked Set:** The handful of brands the consumer actively considers
- **Total Set:** All brands the consumer is aware of
- **Inept Set:** Brands the consumer excludes
- **Inert Set:** Brands known but not considered

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield:** Marketing wants its brand in the **Evoked Set**, not just the Total Set.

---

#### Stage 4: Purchase Decision

**Definition:** The buyer chooses a brand and executes the purchase. *Two factors can intervene between evaluation and decision:*

$$
\text{Attitude} \times \text{Others' Opinions} \times \text{Unanticipated Situational Factors} \rightarrow \text{Purchase Decision}
$$

- **Attitude of others:** Negative opinion of family reduces purchase probability
- **Unexpected situational factors:** Product out of stock, salesperson rude, friend offers to lend theirs
- **Perceived risk:** Higher risk → more deliberation

**Marketer's Role:** Reduce *perceived risk* through guarantees, free trials, testimonials, easy returns.

---

#### Stage 5: Post-Purchase Behavior

**Definition:** After purchase, the buyer experiences either **satisfaction** or **dissonance**, which influences future behavior.

**Satisfaction Model:**

$$
\text{Satisfaction} = f(\text{Perceived Performance}, \text{Expectations})
$$

$$
\text{Outcome} = 
\begin{cases} 
\text{Positive Disconfirmation (Performance > Expectation)} \rightarrow \text{Satisfaction} \\
\text{Simple Confirmation (Performance = Expectation)} \rightarrow \text{Satisfaction} \\
\text{Negative Disconfirmation (Performance < Expectation)} \rightarrow \text{Dissatisfaction}
\end{cases}
$$

**Cognitive Dissonance (Post-Purchase Doubt):** A state of anxiety after a high-value purchase ("Did I choose the right brand?") → Customer seeks reassurance.

**Marketer's Role:** Follow up post-purchase with thank-you notes, instructions, reassurance, warranty information, customer care calls. This reduces dissonance and encourages *brand loyalty*.

---

### B. The 8-Stage Industrial Buying Decision Process (Exhaustive Walkthrough)

Below is the complete industrial buying decision process in logical order, with reasoning and marketer implications at each step.

---

#### Stage 1: Problem Recognition

**Trigger Sources:**
- Internal (firm-level): Machine breakdown, expired equipment, scaling production
- External: Supplier salesperson, trade show, competitor innovation

**Logic:**

$$
\text{Internal/External Stimulus} \rightarrow \text{Gap Identified} \rightarrow \text{Formal Need Acknowledged}
$$

**Example:** A textile mill's boiler breaks down → Need for a new boiler recognized.

---

#### Stage 2: General Need Description

**Activity:** The buying firm defines the *general characteristics* and *quantity* of the needed item.

**Logic:**

$$
\text{Need} \rightarrow \text{Translates into General Specification}
$$

**Example:** Need = "A high-pressure boiler with 10-ton/hour capacity, running on natural gas, with 95% thermal efficiency."

**Marketer's Role:** Help the buyer *articulate* the need through whitepapers, technical consultation, benchmark studies.

---

#### Stage 3: Product Specification

**Activity:** The technical team converts the general need into **detailed engineering specifications**.

**Logic:**

$$
\text{General Need} \rightarrow \text{Technical Specifications} \rightarrow \text{Tender Document Drafted}
$$

**Example:** Boiler specifications: Pressure (10 bar), Temperature (180°C), Fuel type (natural gas), Capacity (10 TPH), Material (SA 516 Grade 70 steel), Safety compliance (IBR norms).

---

#### Stage 4: Supplier Search

**Activity:** Identify potential vendors through trade directories, internet, trade shows, referrals.

$$
\text{Specification} \rightarrow \text{Long List of Suppliers} \rightarrow \text{Short List of Qualified Suppliers}
$$

---

#### Stage 5: Proposal Solicitation

**Activity:** Invite qualified suppliers to submit detailed proposals / tenders / quotations.

$$
\text{Short List} \rightarrow \text{RFP (Request for Proposal)} \rightarrow \text{Vendor Proposals Received}
$$

---

#### Stage 6: Supplier Selection

**Activity:** Evaluate proposals on multiple criteria and select the best vendor.

**Decision Criteria (Weighted Scoring Model):**

$$
\text{Vendor Score} = \sum_{k=1}^{n} w_k \cdot s_{ik}
$$

Where $w_k$ = weight of criterion $k$, $s_{ik}$ = score of vendor $i$ on criterion $k$.

**Common Selection Criteria:**

| Criterion | Typical Weight (Industrial Purchase) |
|---|---|
| Price | 20–30% |
| Technical capability | 15–25% |
| Reliability & reputation | 10–15% |
| After-sales service | 10–15% |
| Delivery capability | 5–10% |
| Warranty & guarantees | 5–10% |
| Past relationship | 5–10% |

---

#### Stage 7: Order-Routine Specification

**Activity:** Final order is placed with detailed terms — quantity, specifications, delivery schedule, payment terms, warranty, penalty clauses.

$$
\text{Vendor Selected} \rightarrow \text{Purchase Order} \rightarrow \text{Contract Signed}
$$

---

#### Stage 8: Performance Review

**Activity:** The buying firm continuously *monitors* the chosen vendor's performance and may give formal feedback.

$$
\text{Vendor Performance} \rightarrow \text{Review Against SLA} \rightarrow \text{Future Vendor Relationship}
$$

**Marketer's Role:** Provide excellent post-sale support because the next review is the gateway to *repeat orders, modified rebuys, or new tasks*.

---

### C. Analytical Matrix — Consumer vs. Industrial Buyer Behavior

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU HIGH-YIELD COMPARISON TABLE** — *This table is a very common 14-mark or 7-mark question in KTU.*

| Dimension | Consumer Buyer Behavior (B2C) | Industrial Buyer Behavior (B2B) |
|---|---|---|
| **Market structure** | Many buyers, mass market | Few buyers, concentrated |
| **Demand nature** | Direct, independent | **Derived** from consumer demand |
| **Volume per buyer** | Small | Large |
| **Purchase frequency** | Frequent, recurring | Infrequent, periodic |
| **Buyer-seller interaction** | Transactional, impersonal | Long-term, partnership-based |
| **Number of decision makers** | 1–2 (individual/family) | **Multiple (Buying Center / DMU)** |
| **Decision process length** | Hours to weeks | Months to years |
| **Decision rationale** | Emotional + rational + social | Mostly **rational-economic + technical** |
| **Information sources** | Personal + commercial + media | Trade publications, exhibitions, vendor reps, technical data |
| **Negotiation intensity** | Low (fixed price) | High (negotiated contracts) |
| **Post-purchase evaluation** | Personal satisfaction/dissonance | Formal performance review |
| **Reciprocity** | Absent | Often present (buy from each other) |
| **Leasing option** | Rare | Common |
| **Risk & impact** | Personal, low magnitude | Organizational, high magnitude |
| **Promotional tools** | Mass advertising, digital, social media | Personal selling, exhibitions, technical journals |

---

### D. Analytical Matrix — Three Buying Situations in Industrial Buying

| Dimension | Straight Rebuy | Modified Rebuy | New Task |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Decision complexity** | Low | Medium | Very high |
| **Number of participants** | Few (1–2) | Moderate | Many (entire buying center) |
| **Information needed** | Routine operational data | Spec changes, alternatives | Detailed technical & commercial analysis |
| **Time taken** | Days to weeks | Weeks to months | Months to over a year |
| **Supplier status** | Existing approved vendor | Mix of existing + new | Open to new vendors |
| **Marketer's role** | Ensure on-time delivery, no stock-outs, service | Highlight improvements & benefits | Aggressive prospecting, deep engagement |
| **Risk level** | Low | Moderate | High |
| **Opportunity for vendor change** | Very low | Moderate | **Highest** |

flowchart TD

    node2["STAGE 2: INFORMATION SEARCH<br/>Personal, Commercial,<br/>Public, Experiential Sources"]
    node3["STAGE 3: EVALUATION OF ALTERNATIVES<br/>Evaluate Evoked Set on<br/>Multiple Weighted Criteria"]
    node4["STAGE 4: PURCHASE DECISION<br/>Select Brand and Execute Buy<br/>Influenced by Attitude, Others,<br/>Situational Factors"]

    node4 --> node5

> **Reading the diagram:** Notice the *feedback loop* from Stage 5 to Stage 1. Post-purchase experience directly influences future need recognition (positive experience → repeat purchase, negative experience → search for alternatives).
