| Alternative Evaluation | Comparison sites (Smartprix, 91mobiles) | Comparison charts, specs page | Shortlists 3 models |
| Purchase Decision | E-commerce site checkout | Discount coupons, urgency timers | Adds to cart, pays via UPI |
| Post-Purchase Behaviour | Review form, WhatsApp group | Follow-up email, loyalty points | Writes a 4-star review |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram — The AISAS Online Buyer Behaviour Model

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Attention: Social Media Ad or SEO Result] --> B[Interest: Landing Page Engagement]
    B --> C[Search: Google, YouTube, Reviews]
    C --> D[Desire: Cart Addition, Wishlist]
    D --> E[Action: Payment and Checkout]
    E --> F[Share: Review, Social Media Post, Referral]
    F --> A
```

**Description of Flow:** The diagram illustrates the AISAS funnel. Unlike AIDA, the *Search* and *Share* stages are distinctly digital, reflecting the active research and amplifying behaviour of online buyers. The dotted feedback from **Share** back to **Attention** shows the viral loop — every share becomes a new attention trigger for someone else.

---

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — Factors Influencing Online Buyer Behaviour (5-Domain Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CULTURAL[Digital Cultural Factors]
        C1[Regional Digital Habits]
        C2[Online Communities]
        C3[Digital Social Class]
    end
    subgraph SOCIAL[Social Factors Online]
        S1[Influencers and YouTubers]
        S2[Family WhatsApp Groups]
        S3[Digital Status LinkedIn]
    end
    subgraph PERSONAL[Personal Factors]
        P1[Age and Generation]
        P2[Income and Lifestyle]
        P3[Personality Type]
    end
    subgraph PSYCH[Psychological Factors]
        PS1[Motivation FOMO Discounts]
        PS2[Perception Site Design]
        PS3[Learning and Recall]
        PS4[Attitude and Trust]
    end
    subgraph TECH[Technological Situational]
        T1[Device Mobile Desktop]
        T2[Internet Speed and UI]
        T3[Payment Options UPI COD]
    end
    CULTURAL --> BUYER((Online Buyer Decision))
    SOCIAL --> BUYER
    PERSONAL --> BUYER
    PSYCH --> BUYER
    TECH --> BUYER
```

**Description of Architecture:** All five domains feed into a central **Online Buyer Decision** node, visually conveying that the online purchase is a multi-dimensional synthesis, not a linear process. The platform engineer or marketing manager must design for all five domains simultaneously.

---

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — Trust, Risk, and Value Triangle (Online Decision Core)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    R[Perceived Risk: Financial, Product, Time, Social, Privacy] --> DECISION{Will the Customer Buy?}
    T[Trust in Platform, Seller, Payment, Logistics] --> DECISION
    V[Perceived Value: Price, Convenience, Utility] --> DECISION
    DECISION --> YES[Purchase Completed]
    DECISION --> NO[Abandonment or Drop-off]
```

**Description of Decision Logic:** This block-level architecture shows that online purchase completion is the equilibrium of three opposing forces. If **Risk > Trust + Value**, the buyer abandons. If **Trust + Value > Risk**, the buyer purchases. Marketers must therefore reduce risk (return policies, reviews), increase trust (badges, certifications), and amplify value (discounts, free shipping).

---

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram — Online Consumer Decision Journey (Adapted EKB)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Stage 1 Need Recognition: Ad or Search Trigger] --> B[Stage 2 Information Search: Google and YouTube]
    B --> C[Stage 3 Alternative Evaluation: Reviews and Comparison]
    C --> D[Stage 4 Purchase Decision: Add to Cart and Pay]
    D --> E[Stage 5 Post Purchase Cognitive Dissonance: Reading More Reviews]
    E --> F[Stage 6 Post Purchase Behaviour: Review Writing or Return]
    F --> G[Stage 7 Advocacy: Social Share or Referral]
```

**Description of Journey:** The adapted EKB model for online contexts adds a **Stage 5** (Cognitive Dissonance Resolution) and a **Stage 7** (Advocacy) that are not present in the classical offline model. The diagram makes clear that the post-purchase phase is not the end — it is a new beginning of the marketing cycle.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Q: Define Online Buyer Behaviour. List any four factors that influence online purchasing decisions.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition (1.5 Marks):** Online Buyer Behaviour refers to the study of the actions, decision-making processes, and responses of consumers when purchasing products or services through digital platforms such as e-commerce websites, mobile applications, and social media marketplaces.

**Four Influencing Factors (1.5 Marks — 0.375 each):**
1. **Trust and Perceived Risk** — Buyers need assurance about product quality, payment security, and delivery.
2. **Social Influence** — Online reviews, influencer recommendations, and peer ratings shape purchase decisions.
3. **Convenience and Time Saving** — 24x7 access, home delivery, and one-click reorder.
4. **Price and Discounts** — Online platforms allow instant price comparison and access to coupons/cashback.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Cultural factors, personal factors, technological factors, perceived value, brand reputation.)*

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**Q: What is the AISAS model? How is it different from the traditional AIDA model?**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Definition of AISAS (1 Mark):** AISAS stands for **Attention, Interest, Search, Action, Share**. It is a digital consumer behaviour model developed by Dentsu in 2004 to describe the path a consumer takes in the online environment.

**Three Key Differences from AIDA (2 Marks):**
1. **Addition of Search Stage:** AISAS inserts *Search* between Interest and Action, recognising that online buyers actively research (Google, YouTube, reviews) before purchasing — a behaviour absent in the offline AIDA model.
2. **Addition of Share Stage:** AISAS ends with *Share* instead of simply completing the purchase, reflecting the viral nature of online advocacy (reviews, social posts).
3. **Non-Linear, Cyclical Model:** Unlike AIDA's linear funnel, AISAS is a cyclical model where *Share* feeds back into *Attention* for new consumers.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: (a)** Explain in detail the various **cultural, social, personal, and psychological factors** that influence online buyer behaviour. **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** Discuss the **AISAS model** and **Trust–Risk–Value framework** in the context of online marketing, with suitable examples. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question A:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

The factors influencing online buyer behaviour can be categorised as follows:

**1. Cultural Factors (1.5 Marks):**
- **Culture:** Regional digital habits (e.g., cash-on-delivery preference in Tier 2/3 Indian cities vs. digital wallet preference in metros).
- **Subculture:** Online communities like Reddit subreddits, Discord servers, or Instagram niche groups influence purchase decisions.
- **Social Class:** Digital literacy and disposable income determine the ability to shop online.

**2. Social Factors (1.5 Marks):**
- **Reference Groups:** Influencers, YouTube tech reviewers, and Amazon Vine voices strongly sway decisions.
- **Family:** Family WhatsApp recommendations and shared subscription accounts (e.g., Amazon Prime) drive purchases.
- **Roles and Status:** LinkedIn endorsements, Twitter follower counts act as digital status symbols.

**3. Personal Factors (2 Marks):**
- **Age and Life Stage:** Gen Z uses Instagram Shopping; Baby Boomers use Facebook and email.
- **Occupation and Economic Situation:** IT professionals with high disposable income shop frequently online.
- **Lifestyle and Personality:** Tech-savvy early adopters vs. risk-averse laggards.
- **Self-Concept:** The image a person wants to project (e.g., buying iPhone over Xiaomi for status).

**4. Psychological Factors (2 Marks):**
- **Motivation:** Discounts, FOMO, festive sales, and limited-time offers drive impulse purchases.
- **Perception:** The website's design, colour scheme, and load speed create brand perception.
- **Learning:** Repeated positive experiences increase brand loyalty.
- **Beliefs and Attitudes:** Pre-existing trust in established platforms like Amazon vs. unknown Shopify stores.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**1. AISAS Model (3.5 Marks):**
The AISAS model, developed by Dentsu, replaces the classical AIDA in the digital age:
- **Attention:** Triggered by social media ads, SEO results, or influencer posts.
- **Interest:** The buyer engages with the landing page or product page.
- **Search:** The buyer actively researches — Google, YouTube reviews, comparison sites.
- **Action:** The buyer completes the purchase via checkout.
- **Share:** Post-purchase, the buyer writes a review, shares on social media, or refers friends.

*Example:* A consumer sees a Samsung Galaxy S24 ad on Instagram (Attention), clicks through to the product page (Interest), watches a YouTube comparison vs. iPhone (Search), places the order on Amazon (Action), and posts an unboxing reel on Instagram (Share).

**2. Trust–Risk–Value Framework (3.5 Marks):**
- **Perceived Risk:** Financial risk (will product be worth the money?), product risk (will it match description?), time risk (delivery delays), social risk (peer judgment), and privacy risk (data misuse).
- **Trust:** Trust in the platform (Amazon's A-to-Z guarantee), the seller (ratings, return policy), the payment gateway (Razorpay, UPI safety), and the logistics partner (Blue Dart, Delhivery).
- **Perceived Value:** The buyer's perception of what is received (product + delivery + service + convenience) versus what is given up (price + time + data shared).

*Example:* A buyer may trust Flipkart but still abandon a cart if shipping charges inflate the total price — the value perception fails. Similarly, even a high-value product may not be purchased if the website lacks an SSL certificate — trust is compromised.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024, Model Paper Style]**

**Q: (a)** Compare and contrast the **online vs. offline buyer behaviour** with respect to the decision-making process. What are the unique challenges marketers face in the online environment? **(7 Marks)**

**Q: (b)** A newly launched D2C skincare brand receives **80,000 monthly visitors** on its website, of which **2,400 make a purchase**. The average order value is **₹850**, and a customer typically buys **3 times a year for 2 years**. The brand has **600 Promoters, 300 Passives, and 100 Detractors** in a recent NPS survey of 1,000 customers. Calculate the **Conversion Rate, Customer Lifetime Value, and Net Promoter Score**, and interpret the results. **(7 Marks)**

---

#### Model Solution for Question B:

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:**

**Comparison Table (5 Marks):**

| Dimension | Offline Buyer Behaviour | Online Buyer Behaviour |
|---|---|---|
| Information Search | Limited to local stores, brochures, word of mouth | Unlimited, global, 24x7 via Google, YouTube |
| Product Evaluation | Physical touch, trial, salesperson assistance | Reviews, ratings, videos, AR/VR try-ons |
| Decision Speed | Slower, more deliberate | Faster but with more deliberation cycles |
| Trust Source | Personal relationship with salesperson | Platform reputation, reviews, SSL, certifications |
| Switching Cost | High (travel, time, relationship) | Very low (one click to competitor) |
| Post-Purchase Voice | Limited word of mouth | Global reach via reviews and social media |
| Payment | Cash, card, in-person | UPI, cards, COD, wallets, BNPL |
| Personalisation | Generic or salesperson-driven | AI-driven, behavioural data-based |

**Unique Online Challenges (2 Marks):**
1. **Cart Abandonment:** The average rate is 70%, requiring constant UX optimisation.
2. **Fake Reviews and Trust Deficit:** Online buyers face information authenticity issues.
3. **Privacy Concerns:** Data breaches erode trust instantly.
4. **Intense Price Competition:** One-click price comparison leads to margin compression.
5. **Logistical Friction:** Delivery delays, return hassles, and damaged goods damage brand reputation.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:**

**Step 1: Conversion Rate (2 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
CR &= \frac{\text{Purchases}}{\text{Visitors}} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \frac{2{,}400}{80{,}000} \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= 0.03 \times 100 \\[6pt]
&= \boxed{3\%}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* A 3% CR is **good** for D2C e-commerce (benchmark: 2%–3%). The brand's product-market fit and traffic quality are healthy.

---

**Step 2: Customer Lifetime Value (2.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
AOV &= \text{₹}850 \\
F &= 3 \text{ orders/year} \\
L &= 2 \text{ years} \\[6pt]
CLV &= AOV \times F \times L \\[6pt]
&= 850 \times 3 \times 2 \\[6pt]
&= 850 \times 6 \\[6pt]
&= \boxed{\text{₹}5{,}100}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* Each customer is worth ₹5,100 over their 2-year lifespan. The maximum allowable CAC is ₹1,700 (1/3 of CLV). If the brand spends more on ads per customer, it will operate at a loss.

---

**Step 3: Net Promoter Score (2.5 Marks):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Promoters} &= \frac{600}{1{,}000} \times 100 = 60\% \\[6pt]
\%\text{Detractors} &= \frac{100}{1{,}000} \times 100 = 10\% \\[6pt]
NPS &= 60 - 10 = \boxed{+50}
\end{aligned}
$$

*Interpretation:* An NPS of **+50 is world-class**. Only 30% are Passives — the brand should target them with loyalty rewards to convert them into Promoters, expanding the referral base.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **In Part A questions:** Students often give 4 factors but forget to define "Online Buyer Behaviour" first. Always define the term before listing — carries 1 to 1.5 marks.
> 2. **In Part B numericals:** Students confuse the NPS formula and include Passives — **Passives are not subtracted**. They are excluded from the calculation entirely.
> 3. **In Part B theory:** When asked to "compare online and offline," students write generic Kotler points. KTU examiners want **digital-specific** differences: trust via reviews, switching cost, data trail, post-purchase viral loop.
> 4. **In CLV problems:** Some students write the formula but forget to **multiply by lifespan** — a costly 0.5-mark error.
> 5. **Common marking deduction:** Skipping units (%, ₹, years) in the final answer.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for Online Buyer Behaviour:**

- **Definition:** Online Buyer Behaviour = the study of consumer actions in digital purchasing environments.
- **Unique Features of Online Behaviour:** Virtual touchpoints, information abundance, self-service navigation, traceable data footprint.
- **Cultural Factors:** Digital habits, online subcultures, digital social class.
- **Social Factors:** Influencers, family WhatsApp groups, digital status symbols.
- **Personal Factors:** Age (Gen Z vs. Boomers), income, lifestyle, personality, self-concept.
- **Psychological Factors:** Motivation (FOMO), perception, learning, beliefs, attitudes.
- **Technological Factors:** Device type, internet speed, payment options (UPI/COD), platform UI.
- **EKB Model Stages:** Need Recognition → Information Search → Alternative Evaluation → Purchase Decision → Post-Purchase Behaviour.
- **AISAS Model:** Attention → Interest → Search → Action → Share (digital-specific; cyclical).
- **Trust–Risk–Value Triangle:** Purchase happens only if (Trust + Value) > Risk.
- **Perceived Risk Types:** Financial, Product, Time, Social, Privacy.
- **Conversion Rate Formula:** $CR = \frac{\text{Purchases}}{\text{Visitors}} \times 100$.
- **Cart Abandonment Rate:** $CAR = \left(1 - \frac{\text{Completed Carts}}{\text{Created Carts}}\right) \times 100$ — industry average ~70%.
- **CLV Formula:** $CLV = AOV \times \text{Frequency} \times \text{Lifespan}$.
- **NPS Formula:** $NPS = \%\text{Promoters} - \%\text{Detractors}$ — Passives excluded.
- **Marketer's Mantra:** Reduce risk, increase trust, amplify value — these three levers drive every online conversion.
- **KTU 2024 Focus Areas:** AISAS model, EKB adaptation, digital-specific factors, numerical application of CR/CLV/NPS.
- **Examiner Traps:** Forgetting to define terms, omitting units, including Passives in NPS, confusing CAR and CR.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Building and Measuring Customer Satisfaction
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Building and Measuring Customer Satisfaction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Customer Satisfaction (CS)** is defined as the consumer's fulfillment response — an emotional reaction or affective state — that results from the evaluation of a product, service, or experience against a predefined set of expectations, needs, or desires. In the KTU 2024 marketing framework, customer satisfaction is treated as the **central dependent variable** of the post-purchase phase of the buyer behavior model, directly influencing **repurchase intent, brand loyalty, word-of-mouth (WOM) communication, and customer lifetime value (CLV)**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (verbatim standard):**
> *"Customer satisfaction is the outcome of the gap between customers' expectations of a product/service and the perceived performance actually received. When perceived performance ≥ expectations, the customer is satisfied; when perceived performance < expectations, the customer is dissatisfied."*

Mathematically, this is expressed in its canonical form as:

$$
\text{Satisfaction} = f(\text{Perceived Performance} - \text{Expectation})
$$

When the resulting value is **positive**, satisfaction occurs; when **negative**, dissatisfaction occurs.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **🍕 Real-World Analogy — "The Pizza Promise Test"**

Imagine you order a pizza from a new restaurant based on a 5-star online rating.

- **Before ordering**, your brain forms an *expectation* based on the rating, photos, price, and past pizza experiences. Let's say you expect "an 8/10 pizza experience."
- **After eating**, your brain compares the actual taste, delivery time, temperature, and presentation. Suppose you rate it a "7/10."

Since **Perceived Performance (7) < Expectation (8)**, you walk away *dissatisfied* — even though the pizza was objectively fine. You might still eat it, but you won't reorder, and you'll leave a 3-star review.

Now reverse the scenario: if the pizza delivers a **9/10** experience against an 8/10 expectation, you become a delighted customer, you reorder, you tell friends, and you post a positive review.

**This gap between expectation and experience is the heart of customer satisfaction.** Marketing managers either (a) **raise actual performance** or (b) **manage expectations downward carefully** to widen the satisfaction gap.

---

## 1.3 Why Customer Satisfaction Matters in Marketing (KTU Exam Focus)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight (PECBT412, Module 4):**
> The 2024 KTU scheme explicitly requires students to understand *both* the **strategic building blocks** of satisfaction (the management-side) and the **measurement methodologies** (the analytical-side). This dual lens is what makes Module 4 a frequent Part-B (14-mark) examination topic.

The four business outcomes of customer satisfaction are universally accepted in KTU board evaluations:

1. **Customer Retention** — repeat purchase behavior.
2. **Customer Loyalty** — emotional attachment leading to brand advocacy.
3. **Positive Word-of-Mouth (WOM)** — free, credible marketing.
4. **Reduced Price Sensitivity** — satisfied customers pay premium prices willingly.

---

## 1.4 Key Terminology to Lock In

| Term | KTU-Standard Definition |
|---|---|
| **Expectation** | A pre-purchase belief about anticipated product/service performance. |
| **Perceived Performance** | The customer's subjective evaluation of actual performance after consumption. |
| **Confirmation / Disconfirmation** | The cognitive process where expectations are confirmed (positive) or disconfirmed (negative). |
| **Delight** | When perceived performance substantially exceeds expectations (a rare, loyalty-building state). |
| **Affective Component** | The emotional (feeling-based) reaction to consumption. |
| **Cognitive Component** | The rational (information-based) evaluation of performance. |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Models of Customer Satisfaction

The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to *compare and contrast* the leading theoretical models. Below is the operational breakdown of each.

### 2.1.1 Expectancy-Value Theory (Oliver, 1980)

**Core Logic:** Satisfaction is a function of two factors — the customer's expectation *before* purchase, and the value placed on the perceived disconfirmation.

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} V_{ij} \cdot (P_{ij} - E_{ij})
$$

Where:
- $S_i$ = Satisfaction with product/brand $i$
- $V_{ij}$ = Value weight assigned to attribute $j$ by the customer
- $P_{ij}$ = Perceived performance on attribute $j$
- $E_{ij}$ = Expectation on attribute $j$
- $n$ = Total number of relevant attributes

**Why this matters in marketing:** This model forces managers to identify the *attributes customers value most* (the $V_{ij}$ weights) and concentrate performance there. A low-value attribute doesn't hurt satisfaction even if poorly delivered.

### 2.1.2 Equity Theory (Adams, 1965, applied to CS by Oliver & Swan, 1989)

**Core Logic:** Customers compare their own *outcome-to-input ratio* with that of a *reference group*. If the ratios are perceived as fair (equitable), satisfaction follows.

$$
\frac{O_c}{I_c} \;\bigg/\; \frac{O_r}{I_r} = 1 \;\; \Rightarrow \;\; \text{Equity (Satisfaction)}
$$

Where:
- $O_c$, $I_c$ = Outcomes and inputs of the customer
- $O_r$, $I_r$ = Outcomes and inputs of the reference group (other customers)

When the ratio is *less than 1*, the customer feels **under-rewarded** → dissatisfaction. When *greater than 1*, **over-rewarded** → surprise delight.

### 2.1.3 Performance-Only Model (Direct Effect)

**Core Logic:** Satisfaction is determined directly by perceived performance, with expectations playing a minimal role. Common in **routine, low-involvement purchases** (e.g., buying sugar, refilling a water bottle).

$$
S = f(P)
$$

Satisfaction rises linearly with perceived performance. Simple, but ignores the psychological "expectation" layer.

### 2.1.4 Affective (Emotional) Model

**Core Logic:** Especially in **service and experiential marketing**, satisfaction is driven by mood, emotions, and contextual feelings rather than purely by cognitive evaluation.

$$
S = \alpha \cdot \text{Cognition} + \beta \cdot \text{Affect} + \epsilon
$$

Where $\alpha$ and $\beta$ are relative weights of cognitive and affective components, and $\epsilon$ is a stochastic error term.

---

## 2.2 Building Customer Satisfaction — The Strategic Framework

KTU examiners expect students to articulate a **structured 5-step approach** when 14-mark questions ask "How does a firm build customer satisfaction?"

### Step 1: Identify Customer Needs (Market Research)

Conduct voice-of-customer (VOC) studies, focus groups, and ethnographic research to uncover latent and explicit needs.

### Step 2: Set Standards & Design Service Blueprint

Define service quality standards using frameworks like **SERVQUAL** (explained below). Build a blueprint that maps every customer touchpoint.

### Step 3: Deliver Consistent Performance

Operational excellence through **Total Quality Management (TQM)**, Six Sigma, employee training, and process standardization.

### Step 4: Manage Expectations Strategically

Use honest advertising (avoid over-promising), clear communication, and pre-purchase education to align expectations with capability.

### Step 5: Build Customer Relationships & Manage Complaints

Implement Customer Relationship Management (CRM), loyalty programs, and robust complaint-resolution systems.

---

## 2.3 SERVQUAL Model (Parasuraman, Zeithaml & Berry, 1988)

This is the **most tested framework** in KTU Module 4 examinations. SERVQUAL measures service quality across **5 dimensions**, abbreviated **RATER**:

| Dimension | Meaning | Customer Question |
|---|---|---|
| **R** – Reliability | Ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately. | *"Did they do what they said they would?"* |
| **A** – Assurance | Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust and confidence. | *"Can I trust them?"* |
| **T** – Tangibles | Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, and communication materials. | *"Do they look professional?"* |
| **E** – Empathy | Caring, individualized attention provided to customers. | *"Do they understand me?"* |
| **R** – Responsiveness | Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service. | *"Will they help me quickly?"* |

The SERVQUAL gap is computed as:

$$
\text{SQ} = \sum_{j=1}^{5} (P_j - E_j)
$$

Where $\text{SQ}$ is overall service quality, $P_j$ is perception on dimension $j$, and $E_j$ is expectation on dimension $j$. A negative $\text{SQ}$ indicates a quality shortfall requiring managerial intervention.

---

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU 2024 ESE**

| # | Formula | Purpose | Variables |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $S = f(P - E)$ | Basic satisfaction function | $P$ = Perceived Performance, $E$ = Expectation |
| 2 | $S_i = \sum V_{ij}(P_{ij} - E_{ij})$ | Expectancy-Value Model | Attribute-weighted satisfaction |
| 3 | $O_c/I_c = O_r/I_r$ | Equity Theory | Customer vs. reference group ratios |
| 4 | $\text{SQ} = \sum (P_j - E_j)$ | SERVQUAL Service Quality Gap | 5 dimensions ($j = 1..5$) |
| 5 | $\text{CSAT \%} = (N_s / N_t) \times 100$ | Customer Satisfaction Score | $N_s$ = Satisfied respondents, $N_t$ = Total respondents |
| 6 | $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$ | Net Promoter Score | $P$ = Promoters, $D$ = Detractors |
| 7 | $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} (R_t - C_t) \cdot \frac{1}{(1+d)^t}$ | Customer Lifetime Value | $R_t$ = Revenue at time $t$, $C_t$ = Cost, $d$ = discount rate |
| 8 | $\text{ACSI} = \bar{S}_{national}$ | American Customer Satisfaction Index | National mean satisfaction benchmark |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Utility:**
> - Formulas 1–4 are used in **service industry research** (hotels, banks, hospitals).
> - Formulas 5–6 are used in **operational dashboards** of companies like Amazon, Apple, and Apple India.
> - Formula 7 is used in **CRM software** like Salesforce and HubSpot.
> - Formula 8 is used in **macro-economic benchmarking** by the ACSI Institute.

---

## 2.5 Strategic Benefits of Building Customer Satisfaction

A high-satisfaction organization enjoys the following quantifiable advantages (these are KTU-model answer bullets):

1. **Lower customer churn rate** (5% improvement in retention can increase profits by 25–95% — Bain & Co.).
2. **Reduced marketing costs** (loyal customers generate free WOM referrals).
3. **Premium pricing power** (satisfied customers are less price-sensitive).
4. **Higher employee morale** (satisfied customers → less customer-abuse → happier staff).
5. **Insulation from competitor disruption** (switching costs become psychological as well as financial).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix — Linking Models to Marketing Application

| Model | Underlying Assumption | Best Application Context | Strategic Lever for the Marketer |
|---|---|---|---|
| Expectancy-Value | Customers are rational utility maximizers. | High-involvement purchases (cars, laptops). | Invest in attributes with high $V_{ij}$ weights. |
| Equity Theory | Customers care about fairness vs. others. | Subscription services, telecom, insurance. | Maintain transparent pricing and tier equity. |
| Performance-Only | Expectations are stable / non-influential. | FMCG, low-cost commodities. | Just improve performance directly; lower overhead. |
| Affective Model | Emotions dominate cognition. | Hospitality, luxury, entertainment. | Train staff in emotional intelligence; ambiance design. |
| SERVQUAL | Service quality is multi-dimensional. | Services (banks, hospitals, airlines). | Diagnose and close specific $P-E$ gaps. |

---

## 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) — KTU Numerical Format

KTU 2024 occasionally asks a numerical problem under Module 4 of the form: *"A retailer receives 480 responses on a satisfaction survey. 320 customers rated 4 or 5 on a 5-point scale. Calculate CSAT and interpret."*

**Step 1 — Identify the variables.**

$$
N_s = 320, \quad N_t = 480
$$

$N_s$ = number of satisfied respondents (those rating 4 or 5 on a 5-point scale).
$N_t$ = total number of respondents.

**Step 2 — Apply the CSAT formula.**

$$
\text{CSAT \%} = \frac{N_s}{N_t} \times 100
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
\text{CSAT \%} = \frac{320}{480} \times 100
$$

**Step 4 — Simplify the fraction.**

$$
\frac{320}{480} = \frac{2}{3}
$$

**Step 5 — Convert to percentage.**

$$
\text{CSAT \%} = \frac{2}{3} \times 100 = 66.67\%
$$

**Step 6 — Managerial interpretation.**

A CSAT of **66.67%** is below the industry benchmark of **75–85%** for retail. The manager should investigate the **SERVQUAL dimensions** most correlated with dissatisfaction (typically *Responsiveness* and *Reliability* in retail) and design corrective interventions.

---

## 3.3 Step-by-Step Derivation: Net Promoter Score (NPS)

**Problem Setup:** A B2B SaaS company surveys 1,000 customers on a 0–10 scale. The classification rules are:

- **Promoters (P)**: ratings 9–10.
- **Passives**: ratings 7–8 (excluded from NPS).
- **Detractors (D)**: ratings 0–6.

Suppose the survey yields: Promoters = 600, Passives = 250, Detractors = 150.

**Step 1 — Compute percentage of Promoters.**

$$
\%P = \frac{600}{1000} \times 100 = 60\%
$$

**Step 2 — Compute percentage of Detractors.**

$$
\%D = \frac{150}{1000} \times 100 = 15\%
$$

**Step 3 — Compute NPS.**

$$
\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D = 60\% - 15\% = 45
$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation per Bain & Company benchmarks.**

| NPS Range | Verdict |
|---|---|
| $\text{NPS} > 50$ | World-class (e.g., Apple, Tesla). |
| $0 < \text{NPS} \leq 50$ | Good. |
| $-50 \leq \text{NPS} \leq 0$ | Needs improvement. |
| $\text{NPS} < -50$ | Critical dysfunction. |

An NPS of **45** places the firm in the *Good* category — competitive but with room to convert Passives into Promoters through service recovery initiatives.

---

## 3.4 Step-by-Step Derivation: SERVQUAL Gap Analysis

**Problem Setup:** A hospital surveyed patients on 5 dimensions. Mean expectation and perception scores (1–7 Likert scale) are:

| Dimension | Expectation $(E_j)$ | Perception $(P_j)$ |
|---|---|---|
| Reliability | 6.5 | 5.8 |
| Assurance | 6.2 | 5.0 |
| Tangibles | 5.9 | 6.3 |
| Empathy | 6.4 | 4.5 |
| Responsiveness | 6.6 | 5.2 |

**Step 1 — Compute the gap for each dimension.**

$$
\text{Gap}_j = P_j - E_j
$$

Reliability gap: $5.8 - 6.5 = -0.7$

Assurance gap: $5.0 - 6.2 = -1.2$

Tangibles gap: $6.3 - 5.9 = +0.4$

Empathy gap: $4.5 - 6.4 = -1.9$

Responsiveness gap: $5.2 - 6.6 = -1.4$

**Step 2 — Compute overall Service Quality (SQ).**

$$
\text{SQ} = \sum_{j=1}^{5} (P_j - E_j)
$$

$$
\text{SQ} = (-0.7) + (-1.2) + (+0.4) + (-1.9) + (-1.4)
$$

**Step 3 — Sum the gaps.**

$$
\text{SQ} = -4.8
$$

**Step 4 — Diagnose the priority dimensions.**

The two largest negative gaps are **Empathy (-1.9)** and **Responsiveness (-1.4)**. These are the priority service-recovery zones.

**Step 5 — Strategic recommendation.**

The hospital must:
1. Train nurses and front-desk staff in patient-centered communication (close Empathy gap).
2. Reduce patient wait times and improve call-back responsiveness (close Responsiveness gap).
3. Leverage Tangibles (which exceed expectations) as a marketing differentiator.

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Note:** A 14-mark question on SERVQUAL expects at least **5 explicit numerical gaps** and a clear ranking of priorities. Skipping the ranking loses 3–4 marks.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 The Customer Satisfaction Lifecycle (Block-Level Functional Architecture Flow)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Customer Expectation Formation] --> B[Pre-Purchase Information Search]
    B --> C[Purchase Decision]
    C --> D[Product or Service Consumption]
    D --> E{Performance vs Expectation Gap}
    E -- Positive Gap --> F[Satisfaction]
    E -- Zero Gap --> G[Neutral Confirmation]
    E -- Negative Gap --> H[Dissatisfaction]
    F --> I[Repurchase Intent]
    F --> J[Positive Word of Mouth WOM]
    F --> K[Brand Loyalty]
    H --> L[Complaint or Switching]
    L --> M{Service Recovery Effective}
    M -- Yes --> F
    M -- No --> N[Brand Switching]
    I --> O[Customer Lifetime Value CLV]
    J --> O
    K --> O
    O --> P[Long Term Firm Profitability]
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The flowchart starts at **Expectation Formation** (top), which is shaped by advertising, prior experience, and WOM.
- After consumption, the **Performance vs. Expectation Gap** node acts as the critical decision diamond.
- Three downstream branches represent the **satisfied / neutral / dissatisfied** outcomes.
- Note the **feedback loop** from *Service Recovery* back to Satisfaction — this is the strategic essence of complaint management.

---

## 4.2 The Five SERVQUAL Dimensions (RATER Framework)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SQ[Overall Service Quality SQ] --> R[Reliability]
    SQ --> A[Assurance]
    SQ --> T[Tangibles]
    SQ --> E[Empathy]
    SQ --> R2[Responsiveness]
    R --> Rdesc[Perform promised service dependably and accurately]
    A --> Adesc[Knowledgeable staff inspire trust]
    T --> Tdesc[Physical evidence and appearance]
    E --> Edesc[Caring individualized attention]
    R2 --> R2desc[Prompt helpful service]
```

---

## 4.3 The Five SERVQUAL Gaps (Parasuraman Model — Provider-Side Gaps)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    G1[Gap 1 Knowledge Gap] --> G2[Gap 2 Standards Gap]
    G2 --> G3[Gap 3 Delivery Gap]
    G3 --> G4[Gap 4 Communication Gap]
    G4 --> G5[Gap 5 Service Quality Gap = P minus E]
    G5 --> Closing[Closing Action Training, TQM, CRM, Internal Marketing]
```

**Provider-side gap explanations (high-yield for KTU):**

- **Gap 1 (Knowledge):** Management's misunderstanding of customer expectations.
- **Gap 2 (Standards):** Mismatch between expectations and service-quality standards set.
- **Gap 3 (Delivery):** Gap between standards set and actual service performance.
- **Gap 4 (Communication):** Gap between service delivered and external communication (over-promising).
- **Gap 5 (Customer-Side):** The aggregated customer-side gap $P - E$ as derived in Section 3.4.

---

## 4.4 Customer Satisfaction Measurement Methodology Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start[Define CS Objectives] --> Design[Choose Measurement Tool]
    Design --> Tool1[Surveys CSAT NPS CES]
    Design --> Tool2[Mystery Shopping]
    Design --> Tool3[Focus Groups]
    Design --> Tool4[Online Reviews and Social Listening]
    Tool1 --> Collect[Collect Data]
    Tool2 --> Collect
    Tool3 --> Collect
    Tool4 --> Collect
    Collect --> Analyze[Quantitative and Qualitative Analysis]
    Analyze --> Benchmark[Compare Against Industry Standards]
    Benchmark --> Action[Implement Corrective Strategy]
    Action --> ReMeasure[Periodic Re Measurement]
    ReMeasure --> Start
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Define customer satisfaction. State the Expectancy-Value model of customer satisfaction.**

**Model Answer:**

**Definition (1.5 marks):**
Customer satisfaction is the consumer's fulfillment response resulting from the evaluation of perceived product/service performance against pre-purchase expectations. When perceived performance ≥ expectations, satisfaction occurs.

**Expectancy-Value Model (1.5 marks):**
The model expresses satisfaction as the weighted sum of disconfirmation across attributes:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} V_{ij} \cdot (P_{ij} - E_{ij})
$$

Where $V_{ij}$ is the value weight, $P_{ij}$ is perceived performance, and $E_{ij}$ is the expectation on attribute $j$. Satisfaction occurs when the weighted disconfirmation is positive.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**List any three methods of measuring customer satisfaction. Explain the Net Promoter Score (NPS).**

**Model Answer:**

**Three Measurement Methods (1.5 marks):**
1. Customer Satisfaction Surveys (CSAT, NPS, CES).
2. Mystery Shopping / Ghost Shopping.
3. Focus Group Discussions.
4. Social Media Listening / Online Review Mining.
5. Customer Complaint Analysis.

**Net Promoter Score (1.5 marks):**
NPS measures the willingness of customers to recommend a company. Respondents rate on a 0–10 scale.

- Promoters (9–10), Passives (7–8), Detractors (0–6).
- $\text{NPS} = \%P - \%D$.
- Range is $-100$ to $+100$; higher scores indicate stronger customer loyalty.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the SERVQUAL model of service quality. Discuss its five dimensions in detail with examples. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** "Customer satisfaction is the outcome of perceived performance minus expectation." Critically evaluate this statement using the Expectancy-Value and Equity Theory models. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
The SERVQUAL model, developed by Parasuraman, Zeithaml, and Berry in 1988, is the most widely used framework for measuring service quality. Service quality is modeled as the gap between customer expectations and perceptions across five dimensions — summarized by the acronym **RATER**.

**The Five Dimensions (4 marks — 0.8 marks each):**

1. **Reliability** — The ability to perform the promised service dependably and accurately.
   *Example:* A logistics company delivering within the promised 48-hour window, every time.

2. **Assurance** — Knowledge and courtesy of employees, and their ability to inspire trust and confidence.
   *Example:* A bank teller explaining complex FD schemes with patience and accuracy.

3. **Tangibles** — Physical facilities, equipment, personnel appearance, and communication materials.
   *Example:* Clean hospital premises, branded uniforms of staff, glossy brochures.

4. **Empathy** — Caring, individualized attention provided to customers.
   *Example:* A doctor remembering a patient's previous visit and providing personalized care.

5. **Responsiveness** — Willingness to help customers and provide prompt service.
   *Example:* A customer-care executive answering a call within 30 seconds and resolving it on first contact.

**Critical Evaluation (2 marks):**
While SERVQUAL is comprehensive, critics argue that the five dimensions are not universally applicable across cultures and industries. The 22-item instrument is also lengthy. Despite this, it remains the *de facto* standard in service-quality research.

---

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Reproduction of the Statement (1 mark):**
The given expression in functional form is:

$$
S = f(P - E)
$$

where $P$ = perceived performance and $E$ = expectation. The statement implies that satisfaction is purely a cognitive disconfirmation outcome.

**Expectancy-Value Lens (3 marks):**
The Expectancy-Value Model refines the basic statement by introducing attribute weighting:

$$
S_i = \sum_{j=1}^{n} V_{ij} \cdot (P_{ij} - E_{ij})
$$

It reveals that the *same* gap $(P - E)$ on different attributes produces different satisfaction levels based on $V_{ij}$. Thus, satisfaction is *not* a single-function gap, but a weighted multi-attribute phenomenon.

**Equity Theory Lens (2 marks):**
Equity Theory adds a social-comparison dimension:

$$
\frac{O_c}{I_c} = \frac{O_r}{I_r} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \text{Satisfaction}
$$

It shows that satisfaction can be undermined even when $P > E$ if the customer perceives that *others* are getting more for less. This contradicts the simple disconfirmation model.

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
The statement is **partially valid** — it captures the cognitive core of satisfaction but ignores the role of attribute weighting and social-comparison fairness. A complete model must integrate cognitive, attribute-weighted, and equity-based dimensions.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** Discuss the various methods of measuring customer satisfaction. Compare Customer Satisfaction Score (CSAT) and Net Promoter Score (NPS). **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the process of building customer satisfaction in an organization. Discuss the role of complaint management and service recovery. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Answer to (a)

**Methods of Measuring CS (3.5 marks — 0.7 each):**

1. **Customer Satisfaction Surveys** — Standardized questionnaires (CSAT, NPS, CES) deployed via email, web, or in-person.
2. **Mystery Shopping** — Trained evaluators pose as customers to assess service quality.
3. **Focus Groups** — Guided group discussions to explore deep satisfaction drivers.
4. **Customer Interviews** — One-on-one structured conversations for qualitative depth.
5. **Social Media & Online Review Analysis** — Sentiment analysis of Twitter, Facebook, Google reviews.
6. **Customer Complaint & Return Data Analysis** — Behavioral, indirect measure of dissatisfaction.
7. **Customer Panel / Longitudinal Tracking** — Repeated surveys of the same customers over time.

**CSAT vs. NPS Comparison Table (2.5 marks):**

| Parameter | CSAT | NPS |
|---|---|---|
| Question Format | "How satisfied are you?" (1–5 scale) | "How likely to recommend?" (0–10 scale) |
| Measures | Transactional satisfaction | Loyalty / advocacy |
| Time Horizon | Short-term (post-purchase) | Medium to long-term |
| Calculation | $(N_s / N_t) \times 100$ | $\%P - \%D$ |
| Range | 0–100% | -100 to +100 |
| Strength | Easy to deploy; actionable per touchpoint | Predictive of growth |
| Limitation | Doesn't capture loyalty | Ignores satisfaction nuances |

**Conclusion (1 mark):**
CSAT and NPS are *complementary*, not substitutes. World-class organizations use both — CSAT for operational diagnosis, NPS for strategic loyalty tracking.

---

#### Model Answer to (b)

**Process of Building Customer Satisfaction (4 marks):**

1. **Customer Needs Assessment** — Conduct VOC research, ethnographic studies, and customer interviews.
2. **Service Design & Blueprinting** — Map every customer touchpoint using service blueprinting and set quality standards (e.g., SERVQUAL targets).
3. **Performance Delivery** — Implement TQM, employee empowerment, ISO 9001 standards, and continuous improvement (Kaizen).
4. **Expectation Management** — Honest advertising, transparent communication, realistic SLA commitments.
5. **Relationship Management** — CRM systems, loyalty programs, personalization, post-purchase follow-ups.
6. **Continuous Measurement & Feedback Loop** — Periodic CSAT/NPS surveys, root-cause analysis, corrective action.

**Complaint Management & Service Recovery (2.5 marks):**

Complaint management is the structured process of receiving, resolving, and learning from customer grievances. The **Service Recovery Paradox** states that a well-recovered complaint can produce *higher* loyalty than if no problem had occurred in the first place. Steps include:
1. **Acknowledge** the complaint promptly.
2. **Apologize** sincerely.
3. **Resolve** quickly — refunds, replacements, repairs.
4. **Follow up** to ensure satisfaction.
5. **Document** for systemic improvement.

**Conclusion (0.5 mark):**
Robust complaint management converts disgruntled customers into brand advocates and provides valuable diagnostic data for systemic service improvement.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where Students Commonly Lose Marks in Module 4:**
>
> 1. **Confusing CSAT and NPS** — They are *different metrics* with different formulas and intent. Mixing them up costs 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Writing SERVQUAL dimensions without examples** — Generic answers earn partial credit; industry-specific examples (banks, hospitals, hotels) earn full marks.
> 3. **Skipping the formula derivation steps** — Even in theory questions, writing $S = f(P - E)$ without unpacking $P$, $E$, or the disconfirmation logic loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the "Satisfaction Paradox" / "Recovery Paradox"** — This is a frequent 3-mark sub-question; skipping it costs easy marks.
> 5. **Using `|` symbols inside markdown tables** — Breaks the table. Always use `\vert` or `\mid` instead. *(Exam-room equivalent: present your numerical work inside clearly labeled equation blocks.)*
> 6. **Conflating Customer Satisfaction and Customer Loyalty** — They are related but distinct. Satisfaction is a *post-purchase evaluation*; loyalty is the *behavioral outcome* of repeated satisfaction.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4**

- **Core Definition:** Satisfaction = $f(P - E)$; positive gap = satisfied, negative = dissatisfied.
- **Four Theoretical Models:** Expectancy-Value, Equity Theory, Performance-Only, Affective.
- **SERVQUAL Dimensions (RATER):** Reliability, Assurance, Tangibles, Empathy, Responsiveness.
- **SERVQUAL Gap Formula:** $\text{SQ} = \sum (P_j - E_j)$, $j = 1$ to $5$.
- **Five Provider-Side Gaps** (Knowledge, Standards, Delivery, Communication, Customer-Side) — each is a potential closing-action target.
- **Key Metrics:** CSAT % = $(N_s / N_t) \times 100$; NPS = $\%P - \%D$; CES = Customer Effort Score (lower is better).
- **Building CS = 5 Steps:** Needs Assessment → Service Design → Performance Delivery → Expectation Management → Relationship Management.
- **Service Recovery Paradox:** A well-recovered complaint produces *higher* loyalty than no-complaint scenarios.
- **Strategic Benefits of CS:** Retention, Loyalty, Positive WOM, Reduced Price Sensitivity, Higher CLV.
- **CLV Formula:** $\text{CLV} = \sum_{t=0}^{T} (R_t - C_t) \cdot \frac{1}{(1+d)^t}$.
- **ACSI = National Benchmark** for cross-industry satisfaction comparison.
- **Measurement Tools to Memorize:** Surveys, Mystery Shopping, Focus Groups, Social Listening, Complaint Analysis, Customer Panels.
- **Always rank SERVQUAL gaps by magnitude** before recommending corrective action.
- **End every answer with a managerial implication**, not just a textbook definition — KTU examiners reward applied thinking.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Customer Relationships Management
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PECBT412 - MARKETING MANAGEMENT
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Customer Relationships Management

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Customer Relationship Management (CRM)** is a comprehensive strategy and integrated technology-driven process that organizations use to manage, analyze, and improve all interactions and relationships with current, past, and prospective customers throughout the customer lifecycle. It combines people, processes, and technology to build long-term, mutually beneficial relationships, enhance customer retention, drive customer loyalty, and maximize customer lifetime value (CLV).

In the context of the **KTU 2024 B.Tech PECBT412 (Marketing Management)** syllabus, CRM is positioned as the strategic bridge between the marketing function and the customer, aligning the firm's value proposition with evolving customer expectations across the **pre-purchase, purchase, and post-purchase** stages.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of CRM as "Running a Restaurant That Remembers Every Diner."**

Imagine you walk into your favorite small restaurant in Kerala. The waiter recognizes you, knows you prefer *appam* with *stew* on Wednesdays, never offers you pork because you are vegetarian, and automatically brings a complimentary *payasam* on your birthday. You feel valued, so you keep coming back and bring your family.

Now scale this to a multinational e-commerce company like Amazon or Flipkart — they do exactly the same thing, but using **data, software, and analytics** instead of memory. That entire system of capturing who you are, what you like, what you bought, predicting what you will buy next, and making you feel special — **that entire system is CRM**.

| Real-World Analogy Element | CRM Equivalent |
|----------------------------|----------------|
| Waiter remembering your name | Customer database & profile |
| Knowing your food preference | Purchase history & preference data |
| Offering a free dessert on birthday | Personalized marketing & loyalty rewards |
| Reservation system | Sales force automation (SFA) |
| Manager reviewing customer complaints | Customer service & helpdesk modules |
| Recipe improvements based on feedback | Analytical CRM & customer feedback loop |

## 1.3 The Three Pillars of CRM

Every CRM strategy rests on three foundational pillars:

1. **People** — The customers, sales teams, and service staff who drive relationships.
2. **Process** — The structured workflow of acquiring, servicing, and retaining customers.
3. **Technology** — The software platforms (e.g., Salesforce, Zoho, HubSpot) that enable data capture and automation.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Exam Tip:** If a 14-mark question asks you to "Explain CRM," always start with the **definition**, then immediately give the **three pillars (People, Process, Technology)**. Examiners reward structured answers.

## 1.4 Why CRM Matters — The Business Imperative

- Acquiring a new customer is **5 to 7 times costlier** than retaining an existing one.
- A **5% increase in customer retention** can boost profits by **25% to 95%** (Bain & Company research, widely cited in KTU reference texts).
- Long-term loyal customers spend **67% more** than new customers (commonly cited loyalty benchmark).

These statistics appear frequently in KTU Part A (3-mark) questions, so committing them to memory is mandatory.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Types / Components of CRM

CRM is not a single tool — it is an ecosystem comprising three interconnected types:

### 2.1.1 Operational CRM
- **Purpose:** Automates and streamlines customer-facing business processes.
- **Key Functions:** Sales force automation (SFA), marketing automation, service automation.
- **Real-World Example:** A Zoho CRM tool that auto-assigns a sales lead to a regional sales manager.

### 2.1.2 Analytical CRM
- **Purpose:** Analyzes customer data to extract actionable insights for decision-making.
- **Key Functions:** Data mining, customer segmentation, churn prediction, CLV calculation.
- **Real-World Example:** Netflix analyzing watch history to recommend shows.

### 2.1.3 Collaborative CRM (Strategic CRM)
- **Purpose:** Enables seamless communication and information sharing across all customer touchpoints (dealers, call centers, social media, field staff).
- **Key Function:** Creates a *single view of the customer* across departments.
- **Real-World Example:** A customer's complaint raised on Twitter being instantly visible to the in-store manager.

## 2.2 The CRM Process — A Step-by-Step Framework

A robust CRM implementation follows this sequential logic:

1. **Customer Identification** — Identify target customers through market segmentation.
2. **Customer Acquisition** — Attract prospects via marketing campaigns, referrals, offers.
3. **Customer Profiling & Data Collection** — Gather demographic, behavioral, and psychographic data.
4. **Customer Segmentation** — Divide customers into homogeneous groups (e.g., high-value, low-engagement).
5. **Customer Interaction / Engagement** — Deliver personalized experiences across channels.
6. **Customer Retention & Loyalty** — Reward loyalty, resolve complaints, build emotional connection.
7. **Customer Advocacy** — Convert satisfied customers into brand ambassadors (referrals, reviews).

## 2.3 CRM vs. Traditional Marketing — Key Differences

| Dimension | Traditional Marketing | CRM-Based Marketing |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|
| Focus | Product / Transaction | Customer / Relationship |
| Orientation | Mass marketing | One-to-one personalization |
| Time Horizon | Short-term sales | Long-term lifetime value |
| Data Usage | Minimal | Heavy reliance on analytics |
| Communication | One-way (brand → customer) | Two-way / dialogic |
| Success Metric | Market share | Customer retention & CLV |

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following formulas are **must-know** for KTU ESE numerical/analytical questions. They are mathematically verifiable and standard across marketing management textbooks.

| # | Metric | Formula | Description | Typical Unit |
|---|--------|---------|-------------|--------------|
| 1 | **Customer Lifetime Value (CLV)** | $CLV = \sum_{t=0}^{n} \dfrac{(Revenue_t - Cost_t) \times r_t}{(1+d)^t}$ | Present value of future cash flows from a customer | ₹ / $ |
| 2 | **Simple CLV (2-year model)** | $CLV = (AOV \times PF \times CGT) - CAC$ | Average Order Value × Purchase Frequency × Customer Gross Time − Acquisition Cost | ₹ / $ |
| 3 | **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC)** | $CAC = \dfrac{Total\ Marketing\ \&\ Sales\ Cost}{Number\ of\ New\ Customers\ Acquired}$ | Cost incurred to convert a lead into a paying customer | ₹ / customer |
| 4 | **CAC Payback Period** | $Payback = \dfrac{CAC}{Monthly\ Revenue\ per\ Customer \times Gross\ Margin}$ | Time to recover customer acquisition cost | Months |
| 5 | **Customer Retention Rate (CRR)** | $CRR = \dfrac{(CE - CN)}{CS} \times 100$ | CE = Customers at End, CN = New Customers, CS = Customers at Start | Percentage (%) |
| 6 | **Customer Churn Rate (CCR)** | $CCR = \dfrac{\text{Customers Lost in Period}}{\text{Customers at Start of Period}} \times 100$ | Percentage of customers lost | Percentage (%) |
| 7 | **Net Promoter Score (NPS)** | $NPS = \%Promoters - \%Detractors$ | Loyalty index from 0 to 100 | Score |
| 8 | **Churn – Retention Relationship** | $CCR + CRR = 100$ | Inverse relationship | Percentage (%) |
| 9 | **RFM Score** | $RFM = R_{score} + F_{score} + M_{score}$ | Recency + Frequency + Monetary ranking (1–5 each) | Score 3–15 |
| 10 | **KPI — Customer Profit Margin** | $CPM = \dfrac{Customer\ Profit}{Customer\ Revenue} \times 100$ | Profitability per customer | Percentage (%) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** In CLV questions, examiners love testing the **discounting** concept. Always remember that $d$ is the **discount rate** (cost of capital), and $r_t$ is the **retention probability** in year $t$. Forgetting the $(1+d)^t$ term is the most common error.

## 2.5 Real-World Utility of CRM

In modern engineering-led businesses, CRM is no longer just a sales tool — it integrates with **ERP systems, IoT data streams, and AI analytics**. For example:
- A **B.Tech CS graduate** joining a SaaS firm may work on integrating CRM (Salesforce) with ML models that predict customer churn.
- A **mechanical engineering firm** like Tata Motors uses CRM to track post-sale vehicle service history and proactively schedule maintenance.
- A **civil engineering contractor** uses CRM modules to manage B2B client relationships for infrastructure projects.

Hence, CRM is a **cross-functional competency** that KTU expects every engineer-manager to understand.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Example: Customer Lifetime Value (CLV) — Full Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> This is a **classic KTU-style numerical problem**. The examiner gives you a multi-year cash flow and asks you to compute CLV using a discount rate. We will derive every single step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Statement

A mobile phone retail firm has the following data for a newly acquired customer:
- **Year 0 (Acquisition Year):** Revenue = ₹15,000; Service Cost = ₹3,000
- **Year 1:** Revenue = ₹20,000; Service Cost = ₹4,000; Retention Probability $r_1$ = 0.80
- **Year 2:** Revenue = ₹22,000; Service Cost = ₹5,000; Retention Probability $r_2$ = 0.65
- **Year 3:** Revenue = ₹25,000; Service Cost = ₹6,000; Retention Probability $r_3$ = 0.50
- **Discount Rate ($d$):** 10% per annum
- **Customer Acquisition Cost (CAC):** ₹2,500

**Compute:** (a) Net cash flow per year. (b) Present value of net cash flow. (c) Total CLV. (d) Net CLV after deducting CAC.

### 3.1.2 Step-by-Step Analytical Solution

#### Step 1: Calculate Net Cash Flow for Each Year

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCF_0 &= Revenue_0 - Cost_0 \\
&= 15000 - 3000 \\
&= 12000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCF_1 &= Revenue_1 - Cost_1 = 20000 - 4000 = 16000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCF_2 &= Revenue_2 - Cost_2 = 22000 - 5000 = 17000
\end{aligned}
$$

$$
\begin{aligned}
NCF_3 &= Revenue_3 - Cost_3 = 25000 - 6000 = 19000
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 2: Apply Retention Probability to Project Realistic Future Cash Flows

The retention probability $r_t$ accounts for the fact that the customer may leave before year $t$. So we multiply the future cash flow by the cumulative retention probability up to that year.

$$
\begin{aligned}
Cumulative\ Retention\ (Year\ 1) &= 0.80 \\
Cumulative\ Retention\ (Year\ 2) &= 0.80 \times 0.65 = 0.52 \\
Cumulative\ Retention\ (Year\ 3) &= 0.80 \times 0.65 \times 0.50 = 0.26
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** Year 0 cash flow is **certain** (the customer just bought), so no retention probability is applied. For Year 1, the customer must survive year 0→1, hence multiply by $r_1 = 0.80$. For Year 2, they must survive year 0→1 AND year 1→2, so cumulative product.

#### Step 3: Adjust Net Cash Flow with Retention Probability

$$
\begin{aligned}
Adjusted\ NCF_0 &= 12000 \times 1.00 = 12000 \\
Adjusted\ NCF_1 &= 16000 \times 0.80 = 12800 \\
Adjusted\ NCF_2 &= 17000 \times 0.52 = 8840 \\
Adjusted\ NCF_3 &= 19000 \times 0.26 = 4940
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 4: Apply Discount Factor $\dfrac{1}{(1+d)^t}$

$$
\begin{aligned}
Discount\ Factor_0 &= \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^0} = 1.0000 \\
Discount\ Factor_1 &= \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^1} = 0.9091 \\
Discount\ Factor_2 &= \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^2} = 0.8264 \\
Discount\ Factor_3 &= \frac{1}{(1+0.10)^3} = 0.7513
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Explanation:** A rupee earned next year is worth less than a rupee today (time value of money). We "discount" future cash flows back to the present.

#### Step 5: Compute Present Value of Each Adjusted Cash Flow

$$
\begin{aligned}
PV_0 &= 12000 \times 1.0000 = 12000.00 \\
PV_1 &= 12800 \times 0.9091 = 11636.48 \\
PV_2 &= 8840 \times 0.8264 = 7305.38 \\
PV_3 &= 4940 \times 0.7513 = 3711.42
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 6: Sum to Get Total CLV

$$
\begin{aligned}
CLV &= PV_0 + PV_1 + PV_2 + PV_3 \\
&= 12000.00 + 11636.48 + 7305.38 + 3711.42 \\
&= 34653.28
\end{aligned}
$$

#### Step 7: Net CLV After Deducting Acquisition Cost

$$
\begin{aligned}
Net\ CLV &= CLV - CAC \\
&= 34653.28 - 2500 \\
&= 32153.28
\end{aligned}
$$

### 3.1.3 Final Answer Table

| Year | Revenue (₹) | Cost (₹) | NCF (₹) | Cumulative Retention | Adjusted NCF (₹) | Discount Factor | PV (₹) |
|------|------------|----------|---------|----------------------|------------------|-----------------|--------|
| 0 | 15,000 | 3,000 | 12,000 | 1.00 | 12,000.00 | 1.0000 | 12,000.00 |
| 1 | 20,000 | 4,000 | 16,000 | 0.80 | 12,800.00 | 0.9091 | 11,636.48 |
| 2 | 22,000 | 5,000 | 17,000 | 0.52 | 8,840.00 | 0.8264 | 7,305.38 |
| 3 | 25,000 | 6,000 | 19,000 | 0.26 | 4,940.00 | 0.7513 | 3,711.42 |
| **Total CLV** | | | | | | | **₹34,653.28** |
| **Less: CAC** | | | | | | | **₹2,500.00** |
| **Net CLV** | | | | | | | **₹32,153.28** |

## 3.2 Analytical Decision Matrix: When CRM Investment is Justified

| Condition | Decision Rule |
|-----------|---------------|
| Net CLV > 3 × CAC | **Aggressively invest** in acquisition — high ROI customer segment |
| Net CLV ≈ CAC | **Break-even** — do not scale; optimize costs first |
| Net CLV < CAC | **Stop acquisition** — re-evaluate value proposition or target segment |
| CRR > 85% | **Mature loyalty** — focus on up-selling and cross-selling |
| CRR < 60% | **Crisis mode** — audit product, service, and CRM workflows |
| NPS > 50 | **Excellent** advocacy potential; invest in referral programs |
| NPS < 0 | **Critical** — large detractor base; immediate service recovery needed |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture: The CRM Ecosystem

> [!NOTE]
> The following Mermaid diagram maps how **Operational**, **Analytical**, and **Collaborative** CRM components integrate with each other and with front-end customer touchpoints. This is a KTU-favorite concept map.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    custA[Customer Touchpoint 1 Website]:::input
    custB[Customer Touchpoint 2 Mobile App]:::input
    custC[Customer Touchpoint 3 Retail Store]:::input
    custD[Customer Touchpoint 4 Call Center]:::input
    custE[Customer Touchpoint 5 Social Media]:::input

    opCRM[Operational CRM Layer]:::process
    sfA[Sales Force Automation]:::sub
    mkA[Marketing Automation]:::sub
    svA[Service Automation]:::sub

    anCRM[Analytical CRM Layer]:::process
    dM[Data Warehouse]:::sub
    mD[Data Mining Engine]:::sub
    cSeg[Customer Segmentation]:::sub
    clvE[CLV Prediction Model]:::sub

    coCRM[Collaborative CRM Layer]:::process
    intg[Integration Middleware]:::sub
    sView[Single Customer View]:::sub
    cMgt[Contact Management]:::sub

    decision[Strategic Marketing Decisions]:::output
    report[Management Reports and Dashboards]:::output

    custA --> opCRM
    custB --> opCRM
    custC --> opCRM
    custD --> opCRM
    custE --> opCRM

    opCRM --> anCRM
```
